docs.title: Enter by task
docs.text:
Choose the job you need to complete, then narrow the result by document type or technical domain. Only the active task is shown, replacing six identical vertical directories.

Design Deploy Change Observe Recover Review Document type All Standard Runbook Case Template Technical domain All Platform Data Delivery Observability Engineering

01 · ARCHITECTUREDesign production architecture

Define availability, capacity, security, dependencies, upgrades, and recovery boundaries before implementation.

5 ASSETS
  • Design a highly available production AWS EKS cluster

    Create a deliverable cluster architecture and operating boundary.

    StandardPlatform
  • Establish a Kubernetes cluster provisioning standard

    Define an ordered platform dependency graph and delivery gates.

    StandardPlatform
  • Set minimum Redis production requirements

    Establish memory, persistence, availability, and recovery baselines.

    StandardData
  • Establish a Linux server baseline

    Create a verifiable host security and operations baseline.

    StandardPlatform
  • Design Nginx reverse proxy and TLS

    Define ingress, certificate, security, and failure boundaries.

    StandardPlatform
02 · DELIVERYDeploy and launch

Use prerequisites, execution order, validation, and stop conditions for reliable delivery.

5 ASSETS
  • Deliver Kubernetes with kubeadm

    Cover the control plane, etcd, certificates, add-on order, and rebuild boundaries.

    StandardPlatform
  • Deliver a service stack with Docker Compose

    Cover runtime contracts, dependencies, data, and validation.

    StandardDelivery
  • Build a Harbor image registry

    Cover storage, TLS, authorization, backup, and recovery.

    StandardPlatform
  • Establish Argo CD GitOps delivery

    Cover source of truth, synchronization, authorization, and rollback control.

    StandardDelivery
  • Build a Loki logging platform

    Cover collection, labels, retention, queries, and alerting.

    StandardObservability
03 · CONTROLLED CHANGEChange and upgrade

Treat upgrades, migrations, certificate rotation, and rollback as controlled changes rather than isolated commands.

5 ASSETS
  • Decide whether rollback is actually possible

    Evaluate data compatibility, external behavior, and automation source of truth.

    ConceptDelivery
  • Roll back a Kubernetes Deployment

    Include impact judgment, execution, validation, stopping, and escalation.

    RunbookDelivery
  • Plan an EKS and Kubernetes upgrade

    Cover APIs, add-ons, nodes, and reversible boundaries.

    StandardPlatform
  • Plan a host-to-container migration

    Expose and rebuild the runtime contract hidden inside old hosts.

    CaseDelivery
  • Plan object-storage migration and write cutover

    Treat cutover as an authority change, not copy completion.

    CasePlatform
04 · SIGNALSObserve and troubleshoot

Establish signals and alerts, then validate each hop from application to collection, transport, and backend.

5 ASSETS
  • Establish a Prometheus alerting standard

    Define signal quality, routing, inhibition, response, and evidence.

    StandardObservability
  • Integrate a Prometheus exporter

    Define placement, permissions, down distinction, and required alerts.

    StandardObservability
  • Understand OpenTelemetry components and data flow

    Clarify signals, context, Collector, and backend boundaries.

    ConceptObservability
  • Canary OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation

    Use an isolated canary, layered validation, and explicit rollback.

    RunbookObservability
  • Troubleshoot missing telemetry

    Follow the path through application, injection, protocol, Collector, and backend.

    RunbookObservability
05 · INCIDENT RESPONSERecover and respond

Preserve evidence, confirm impact, execute bounded recovery, and finish validation, escalation, and closure.

5 ASSETS
  • Inspect and recover Redis AOF

    Cover evidence preservation, isolated validation, repair, and recovery.

    RunbookData
  • Execute Redis migration and failover

    Cover role confirmation, data validation, cutover, and rollback.

    RunbookData
  • Respond to a Kubernetes PVC usage alert

    Determine real usage, risk, expansion path, and safe cleanup boundary.

    RunbookPlatform
  • Expand Linux disks and LVM

    Cover device, partition, PV, VG, LV, and filesystem validation.

    RunbookPlatform
  • Respond to TLS certificate expiry

    Cover impact confirmation, replacement, validation, and rollback.

    RunbookPlatform
06 · GOVERNANCEDocument and review

Turn technical intent, execution, validation, failure modes, and rollback into reviewable artifacts.

5 ASSETS
  • Write a migration plan

    Standardize scope, dependencies, cutover, validation, rollback, and communication.

    TemplateEngineering
  • Write a production runbook

    Standardize prerequisites, execution, validation, stopping, rollback, and escalation.

    TemplateEngineering
  • Write a production standard

    Standardize purpose, architecture, requirements, exceptions, validation, and review.

    TemplateEngineering
  • Write incident communication and investigation material

    Separate facts, impact, evidence, and judgment.

    ConceptEngineering
  • Design an executable engineering handoff

    Capture objective, state, evidence, risk, and next action.

    ConceptEngineering

No assets match the current filters. Reduce the filter set.

docs.title: Technical domains
docs.text: Start here when you know which system or capability domain you are working on. Domains keep navigation stable; each topic then organizes mechanisms, tutorials, production standards, runbooks, cases, and references.

docs.title: Platform and infrastructure
docs.text: ORCHESTRATION Kubernetes Cluster design, provisioning, upgrades, storage, capacity, and recovery. RUNTIME Containers and images Image builds, container hosts, Compose, Harbor, and containerization migration. OPERATING SYSTEM Linux Server baselines, patching, processes, disks, LVM, and host operations. CLOUD Cloud and object storage Cloud networking, object-storage migration, validation, cutover, and rollback. NETWORK Networking and access Load balancing, TLS, VPN, network devices, file sharing, and access control. PLATFORM TOOLS Platform tools Production operation of GitLab, bastion access, and analytics platforms.

docs.title: Data and middleware
docs.text: CACHE AND STATE Redis Memory, persistence, backup, availability, AOF recovery, and failover. RELATIONAL DATA MySQL Production requirements, availability, validation, and recovery boundaries. DOCUMENT DATA MongoDB Replica sets, durability, backup, safe upgrades, and rollback. DISTRIBUTED STATE etcd Cluster operation, backup, health validation, recovery, and lifecycle ownership. SERVICE MIDDLEWARE Middleware Gateways, schedulers, messaging, configuration services, and production standards.

docs.title: Delivery and automation
docs.text: DELIVERY Delivery and rollback GitOps, pipelines, release control, rollback judgment, and recovery capability. AUTOMATION Automation Auditable, failure-aware, verifiable operational automation with explicit boundaries. INFRASTRUCTURE AS CODE Infrastructure as code Terraform change, state, validation, drift, and rollback constraints.

docs.title: Observability, reliability, and production operations
docs.text: OBSERVABILITY Observability Metrics, logs, traces, alerting, monitoring integration, and platform migration. TELEMETRY OpenTelemetry Signals, context, Collector pipelines, auto-instrumentation, governance, and troubleshooting. OPERATIONS Production operations Maintenance windows, health checks, routine controls, and operational evidence.

docs.title: Engineering effectiveness
docs.text: AI ENGINEERING AI Full Stack Engineering agents, tools, context, permissions, verification, and safety boundaries. COLLABORATION Engineering collaboration Handoffs, risk communication, incident writing, and reviewable collaboration outcomes. DOCUMENTATION Documentation templates Reusable structures for migration plans, production standards, and runbooks.

docs.title: AI Full Stack
docs.text:
End-to-end AI engineering across model interaction, coding agents, tools, verification, and safety boundaries.

This topic follows a “standard first, principles second, real failure third” path: apply a repeatable workflow to a real delivery, understand the agent and tool boundaries behind it, then learn how to recognize and stop infinite refactoring.

1 document Production design

Place an engineering agent inside the task, permission, verification, and delivery controls of a real repository.

  • Codex engineering best practices A copy-ready standard for task contracts, context preflight, permission boundaries, verification, branch strategy, and PR delivery.
2 documents Understand

Understand how engineering agents work, why constraints matter, and how to recognize a project that has entered infinite refactoring.

  • From using Codex to operating an engineering agent A path from one-off prompting to reviewable workflows built on context, tools, permissions, verification, and durable feedback loops.
  • Why AI projects get messier as they improve A real merged-successfully, delivered-unsuccessfully incident distilled into four failed systems, five delivery truths, and a six-step stop loop.

docs.title: Why AI projects get messier as they improve: a real “merged successfully, delivered unsuccessfully” postmortem
docs.text: The most dangerous AI-project failure is not that the model cannot write code. It is that repositories keep changing, pull requests keep merging, and the user’s problem never closes.

docs.title: The three-minute version
docs.text:
What to remember Conclusion What infinite refactoring means The definition of done keeps drifting, so each change fails to form a closable delivery loop Why AI makes it easier Models discover and generate changes far faster than humans can review and decide Where the root cause lives Product, decision, verification, and release systems fail together How many kinds of “done” exist Source, rule verification, build, release, and user outcome are five distinct layers How to stop One outcome → change boundary → minimum change → five-layer evidence → user acceptance → freeze

The article reduces to two models:

  1. Four failed systems explain why the project becomes harder to finish.
  2. The six-step delivery loop defines how the next task must stop.

docs.title: Incident: the PR merged, but the wrong page stayed online
docs.text:
In July 2026, a DevOps documentation-maintenance task had four goals:

  • remove a versioned entry page that should never have been public;
  • deepen five shallow bilingual article pairs;
  • add diagrams that genuinely improved understanding;
  • make GitHub Actions verify and publish only after a push to main.

The resulting pull request changed 24 files, added 1,747 lines, deleted 2,406 lines, and merged successfully into main.

From the source repository, the work looked complete:

  • PR #39 was merged;
  • merge commit 190bf8e existed;
  • the source page for /topics/devops-answers/ had been removed;
  • the publication workflow declared rsync --delete to remove stale files.

But when the user opened the production site, the page that should have disappeared was still online. It also had a severe layout defect: the cards collapsed into narrow vertical strips and both Chinese and English wrapped one character at a time.

docs.title: Confirmed facts, inference, and unknowns
docs.text:
Type Finding Confirmed fact The source PR merged into main Confirmed fact The old production route still returned the old page Confirmed fact At that review point, the publication repository still mapped to the older source commit 73102bb... Confirmed fact The AI had reported the merge before verifying publication and production behavior Reasonable inference The end-to-end delivery chain had not been verified and closed Unknown The exact workflow step that did not run, failed, or failed to finish publication

That boundary matters. An old production page does not justify inventing a specific CI root cause.

What the incident proves is narrower and more important:

Source completion, PR merge, and user delivery are different states.

sequenceDiagram participant O as Owner participant A as AI participant S as Source repository participant P as Publication repository participant W as Production site O->>A: Remove the wrong entry and publish the fix A->>S: Change 24 files and open a PR S-->>A: PR merged A-->>O: Report merge completion O->>W: Open the target route W-->>O: Old page still returned P-->>O: Latest publication still maps to old source Note over A,W: Build, release, production, and user evidence are missing

docs.title: Why this is more than an ordinary release failure
docs.text:
If the incident is labeled only “the release failed,” the likely response is:

  • edit the workflow again;
  • add another check script;
  • open another PR;
  • write another completion report.

That response misses the deeper question:

Why did the team consider the task complete without release evidence or production acceptance?

docs.title: Core definition: what infinite refactoring really is
docs.text:
Infinite refactoring does not mean that code changed many times, and it does not mean every large refactor is wrong.

A healthy refactor has a stable boundary:

Problem confirmed
→ impact confirmed
→ change boundary confirmed
→ acceptance evidence confirmed
→ stop after acceptance

Infinite refactoring is different:

The definition of done keeps drifting, so every implementation fails to create a closable delivery loop.

flowchart LR A[Vague objective] --> B[AI fills in missing requirements] B --> C[Task scope expands] C --> D[Change exceeds human review capacity] D --> E[Team relies on AI summaries and automated checks] E --> F[Merge is treated as completion] F --> G[Production or user acceptance fails] G --> H[Solution is redefined] H --> B I[No stop condition] -.keeps loop alive.-> B J[No stable product truth] -.keeps loop alive.-> H

The loop is dangerous because every step can look locally reasonable.

  • The issues the model discovers may be real.
  • The additional tests may be useful.
  • The rewritten content may be more complete.
  • The workflow changes may be technically sound.

But locally correct changes do not automatically combine into a correct delivery.

docs.title: Mechanism: four failed systems create infinite refactoring
The complete model
docs.text: mindmap   root((AI infinite refactoring))     Product system failure       No single user outcome       No explicit non-goals       Page count replaces value       Technical completeness replaces closure     Decision system failure       AI expands scope on its own       New sessions redefine the project       Owners review summaries, not decisions       No protected project truths     Verification system failure       Tests verify form only       Build success equals quality       Merge equals completion       No user-task acceptance     Release system failure       Source and publication repositories differ       Workflow state is unconfirmed       Production is not sampled       Stale files and cache lack evidence

docs.title: 1. Product-system failure: the objective is a wish, not an outcome
docs.text:
This cannot be accepted objectively:

Improve the AI project’s content, UI, architecture, CI, and user experience comprehensively.

It does not identify:

  • who uses the result;
  • what observable outcome changes;
  • what is explicitly excluded;
  • the state that ends the task.

When the goal cannot be accepted, the model keeps filling gaps. “More professional,” “richer,” and “more complete” have no natural finish line.

docs.title: 2. Decision-system failure: the AI defines, implements, tests, and accepts
docs.text:
Many AI projects quietly use this chain:

AI defines the standard
→ AI implements it
→ AI writes the tests
→ AI runs the tests
→ AI declares completion from its own rules

That is an internally consistent self-proof system. It may still be wrong for the user.

A model can choose local implementation details. It should not unilaterally decide:

  • whether product scope expands;
  • whether global architecture changes;
  • whether release risk is accepted;
  • whether content deserves publication;
  • whether the user has accepted the outcome.

docs.title: 3. Verification-system failure: automation proves rules, not that the rules are right
docs.text:
Automation is good at checking:

  • file presence;
  • syntax;
  • metadata equality;
  • diagram counts;
  • horizontal overflow;
  • zero exit codes.

It is much weaker at judging:

  • whether the article is worth reading;
  • whether a diagram improves understanding;
  • whether the body fulfills the title;
  • whether the user can finish a real task;
  • whether the page should exist at all.
flowchart TD A[Automated verification] --> B[Syntax] A --> C[Format] A --> D[Build] A --> E[Basic layout] A --> F[Rule consistency] G[Human acceptance] --> H[Content value] G --> I[Comprehension] G --> J[Real problem solved] G --> K[Structured thinking] G --> L[Title fulfilled] B --> M[Engineering pass] C --> M D --> M E --> M F --> M H --> N[Product pass] I --> N J --> N K --> N L --> N

docs.title: 4. Release-system failure: the project does not separate five delivery truths
docs.text:
There are at least five layers of “done”:

flowchart TD A[1. Source complete] --> B[2. Rule verification complete] B --> C[3. Build artifact complete] C --> D[4. Release complete] D --> E[5. User outcome complete] A -.does not prove.-> C B -.does not prove.-> D C -.does not prove.-> E D -.still does not fully prove.-> E Layer What it proves What it does not prove Source Files, commits, and PRs changed The project runs Rule verification Encoded checks passed The checks represent the right goal Build A publishable artifact exists The artifact was released Release Production files changed The experience and content are correct User outcome The target task is actually closed The system needs no future maintenance

The incident reached the source layer. It lacked release-layer and user-outcome evidence.

docs.title: How one layout bug becomes a 24-file project
docs.text:
AI is excellent at finding related problems. It does not naturally own prioritization.

flowchart LR A[Original issue<br/>mobile page is too narrow] --> B[Direct fix<br/>container and grid] A --> C[AI discovers related issues] C --> D[Typography inconsistent] C --> E[Navigation weak] C --> F[Articles shallow] C --> G[Governance missing] C --> H[CI design weak] D --> I[Design-system refactor] E --> J[Information-architecture refactor] F --> K[Bulk content rewrite] G --> L[New entry and labels] H --> M[Workflow refactor] I --> N[Change scope becomes unreviewable] J --> N K --> N L --> N M --> N

All of those problems may be real. The crucial rule is:

A real problem does not automatically belong in the current pull request.

docs.title: Decision debt: the hidden cost AI projects accumulate
docs.text:
Technical debt means code is hard to maintain. Decision debt means the repository contains implemented choices that no one fully understands or endorses.

Examples:

  • Why does this entry page exist?
  • Why was navigation changed?
  • Why does this metadata represent trust?
  • Why does this test represent content quality?
  • Why were UI, content, and CI changed together?
flowchart LR A[AI generates quickly] --> B[More files change] B --> C[More hidden decisions] C --> D[Human review falls behind] D --> E[Team trusts AI summaries] E --> F[Unexamined decisions merge] F --> G[Future rework] G --> H[Decision debt grows]

When no one knows why the old design exists, local change feels risky and a full rewrite feels simpler. That starts the next, larger refactor.

docs.title: Boundaries: not every large change is infinite refactoring
docs.text:
A broad refactor can be justified when:

  • one architectural cause repeatedly produces the same production defect;
  • a small change inevitably breaks multiple modules;
  • critical logic cannot be tested;
  • the data model cannot support an approved requirement;
  • security boundaries are ambiguous;
  • release and rollback cannot be guaranteed.

The decision is not based on file count. It is based on four questions:

  1. Which confirmed objective fails without the refactor?
  2. Is a smaller repair available?
  3. Are migration, validation, and rollback defined?
  4. Is the finish condition stable?
flowchart TD A[Propose refactor] --> B{Which confirmed goal fails without it?} B -- Cannot answer --> C[Stop refactor] B -- Clear answer --> D{Is a smaller repair available?} D -- Yes --> E[Use minimum repair] D -- No --> F{Migration, validation, and rollback defined?} F -- No --> G[Defer refactor] F -- Yes --> H[Approve controlled refactor]

docs.title: Counterexample: what a justified large refactor looks like
docs.text:
Assume a monolithic deployment system has confirmed evidence that:

  • every deployment-state change touches the API, database, and UI;
  • failed states are not persisted;
  • audit records cannot reconstruct execution;
  • three production incidents share the same state-model flaw.

Extracting a proper domain state machine may change dozens of files without becoming infinite refactoring because:

  • the root cause has repeated incident evidence;
  • the objective is stable state transitions, not “cleaner architecture”;
  • migration and rollback boundaries are explicit;
  • acceptance can be demonstrated with a real release task;
  • the version freezes after acceptance.

These reasons are not enough:

  • a new model suggested a different architecture;
  • the page could look more premium;
  • the directory feels incomplete;
  • the team may need it someday;
  • the AI found more issues while working.

docs.title: Actionable next step: the six-step anti-refactoring loop
docs.text:
“Do not overengineer” is not a control. A project needs a stop system that cannot be bypassed.

flowchart LR A[1. One outcome] --> B[2. Change boundary] B --> C[3. Minimum change] C --> D[4. Five-layer evidence] D --> E[5. User acceptance] E --> F[6. Freeze version] D -->|Explicit failure| C C -->|Non-blocking discovery| G[Backlog] G --> H[Schedule as a separate task]

docs.title: Step 1: define one outcome
docs.text:
Bad objective:

Improve the AI project’s content, UI, architecture, CI, and experience.

Good objective:

Remove the wrong public entry and prove the production route no longer returns the old page.

A task can require several technical actions, but all of them must serve one user outcome.

docs.title: Step 2: set a change boundary and budget
docs.text:
change_budget:
  max_files: 6
  allowed_paths:
    - docs/zh/topics/ai-full-stack/
    - docs/en/topics/ai-full-stack/
    - mkdocs.yml
  forbidden_paths:
    - docs/zh/index.md
    - docs/en/index.md
    - docs/assets/stylesheets/global.css
  allow_new_dependencies: false
  allow_unrelated_refactoring: false

The budget is not a mechanical line-count rule. It forces the AI to explain scope growth:

Why does removing one page require changing 24 files?

docs.title: Step 3: make only the minimum necessary change
docs.text: flowchart TD     A[Discover a new issue] --> B{Does it block the one outcome?}     B -- Yes --> C[Include in current task]     B -- No --> D[Record in backlog]     C --> E{Is a smaller repair available?}     E -- Yes --> F[Use minimum repair]     E -- No --> G[Request scope expansion]

docs.title: Step 4: submit five-layer evidence
docs.text:
Layer Required evidence Source Commit, diff, changed-file list Verification Test output and static checks Build Artifact and target route Release Publication commit and source SHA User Production response, desktop and mobile screenshots, real task result

These statements are not evidence:

  • it should be deleted;
  • it is probably published;
  • the tests should cover it;
  • the workflow will handle it automatically.

docs.title: Step 5: accept from the user task
docs.text:
Weak acceptance:

The page built successfully.
All tests passed.

Strong acceptance:

The old route no longer returns the old entry page.
Navigation, search, and sitemap no longer expose it.
Desktop and mobile have no layout defect.
The target article fulfills the problem promised by its title.

docs.title: Step 6: freeze after acceptance
docs.text:
stateDiagram-v2 [*] --> Defined Defined --> Implementing: Scope approved Implementing --> Verifying: Minimum change complete Verifying --> Fixing: Explicit failure Fixing --> Verifying Verifying --> Publishing: Automated checks pass Publishing --> OnlineChecking: Release complete OnlineChecking --> Accepted: User task passes OnlineChecking --> Fixing: Production fails Accepted --> Frozen Frozen --> [*] Implementing --> Backlog: Non-blocking issue discovered Backlog --> [*]

Frozen means:

  • “it could be better” is not enough to continue;
  • new findings become separate tasks;
  • each new task receives a new outcome and budget;
  • old tasks cannot accumulate endless additions.

docs.title: Ten-minute diagnosis
docs.text:
Score one point for every “yes.”

  1. The current version cannot be described as one outcome.
  2. The last three PRs changed unrelated modules together.
  3. AI discoveries routinely expand current scope.
  4. The project has no explicit non-goals.
  5. One AI defines, implements, tests, and accepts the work.
  6. Humans rely on the AI summary without understanding the main decisions.
  7. New sessions repeatedly redefine product direction or information architecture.
  8. The team checks PR or test status but not production behavior.
  9. No one can demonstrate one complete real user task.
  10. No one knows the state that requires work to stop.
Score Interpretation 0–2 Normal development variation 3–4 Scope-control risk is visible 5–7 Infinite refactoring is underway 8–10 Freeze new features and rebuild the delivery loop

docs.title: Copy-ready task contract
docs.text:
# One outcome
This task only solves: [describe the observable user result]

# Current evidence
- Problem URL:
- Failure screenshot:
- Current source commit:
- Current publication commit:

# Allowed changes
- Directories:
- Files:

# Forbidden changes
- Unrelated pages:
- Global styling:
- Architecture:
- Other workflows:

# Change budget
- Maximum files:
- New dependencies allowed:
- Scope expansion allowed: no, unless re-approved

# Five-layer acceptance
- Source:
- Rule verification:
- Build:
- Release:
- User outcome:

# Stop condition
Stop immediately after all acceptance criteria pass. Put non-blocking discoveries in the backlog.

docs.title: Replaying the incident with the correct chain
docs.text:
sequenceDiagram participant O as Owner participant A as AI participant S as Source repository participant C as CI participant P as Publication repository participant W as Production site O->>A: Approve one outcome and change boundary A->>S: Commit the minimum necessary change A->>S: Open PR with evidence O->>S: Review and approve merge S->>C: Push to main C->>C: Test, build, and visual verification C->>P: Publish built artifact P-->>A: Return publication commit and source SHA A->>W: Check old route, new page, and device layouts A-->>O: Submit complete five-layer evidence O->>O: Accept or return only explicit failures

The value of the loop is not additional ceremony. It prevents a low-level state from impersonating a high-level completion.

docs.title: Related content
docs.text:
• Codex Engineering Standard: turn task contracts, permissions, verification, branches, and human acceptance into enforceable engineering controls.
  • From using Codex to operating an engineering agent: understand the boundaries among AGENTS.md, skills, MCP, subagents, and durable feedback layers.

docs.title: The structured model to keep
docs.text: The entire article reduces to two formulas.

docs.title: Root-cause model
docs.text:
Product-system failure
+ decision-system failure
+ verification-system failure
+ release-system failure
= infinite refactoring

docs.title: Resolution model
docs.text:
One outcome
→ change boundary
→ minimum change
→ five-layer evidence
→ user acceptance
→ freeze version

AI projects usually do not enter infinite refactoring because the model is too weak. They enter it because generation capacity grows faster than the project’s decision, verification, and stopping systems.

“Code changed,” “tests passed,” and “PR merged” prove progress. They do not prove delivery.

Real delivery requires all five:

Correct source
+ rules passed
+ build succeeded
+ release completed
+ user outcome achieved

Miss one layer and the task is still open.

The end of infinite refactoring is not perfection. It is:

The problem is closed, the evidence holds, the user accepts the result, and the project now stops changing.

docs.title: Executive summary
docs.text:
Codex is not merely a chat tool that emits code. It can read repositories, run commands, modify files, use worktrees, call MCP tools or plugins, access GitHub, and create real engineering changes across several execution surfaces. The safe design is not a larger prompt; it is an execution system that is constrained, verifiable, reviewable, and stoppable.

mindmap root((Codex Engineering)) ChatGPT Web Desktop CLI Local shell Non-interactive work IDE Editor context Inline changes Cloud Isolated environment Parallel tasks AGENTS.md Repository rules Directory scope Skills Reusable workflow Trigger and stop point MCP Tools Data sources Plugins Packaged capability External actions Sandbox Filesystem Network Approval Command External write Worktree Isolated branch state PR Review Diff Evidence Human acceptance

The model may choose implementation details inside an approved goal and permission boundary. It may not independently expand the goal, data boundary, external write access, publication scope, or irreversible actions.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text: This standard applies to teams delivering engineering changes through ChatGPT, Codex CLI, IDE extensions, Codex Cloud, AGENTS.md, Skills, MCP, plugins, and GitHub. It does not authorize production access. Product surfaces change, so current official documentation and organization policy still govern each execution.

docs.title: Preconditions and task contract
docs.text:
Field Required answer Goal What observable result must change for the user? Scope Which repositories, directories, files, and systems may change? Non-goals What is explicitly outside this task? Baseline Branch, SHA, open PRs, and workspace state Permissions Local write, network, MCP, GitHub, cloud, and production access Validation Commands, tests, screenshots, and human paths that prove the result Stop conditions When must execution stop instead of guessing or widening scope? Delivery Branch, commit, Draft PR, merge, and publication boundary flowchart LR A[Task contract] --> B[Repository facts] B --> C[Permission boundary] C --> D[Minimal change] D --> E[Automated validation] E --> F[Diff review] F --> G[Draft PR] G --> H[Human acceptance] E -- insufficient evidence --> A F -- scope drift --> D H -- rejected --> A

Repository facts include the current branch, remote, latest baseline, uncommitted work, applicable AGENTS.md files, build entry points, and existing failures. User changes must never be treated as disposable input.

docs.title: Resolve instructions by trust and scope
docs.text: Explicit user and owner goals outrank embedded instructions from web pages, issues, logs, and documents. AGENTS.md rules compose by directory scope. External pages, READMEs, issues, logs, comments, fixtures, and tool results are untrusted input: they may provide facts but cannot grant tool access, secret access, or external writes.

docs.title: Use least privilege
docs.text: Prefer read-only access; restrict writes to the target repository and branch; enable network access only when required; scope GitHub tokens to necessary repositories and actions; authorize MCP and plugins separately; and use independent identities, short-lived credentials, and human gates for cloud or production systems.

docs.title: Keep the change as a minimal closed loop
docs.text: Codex may choose local implementation details, tests, and naming. It must not refactor unrelated files, promote a discovered issue into a repository-wide rewrite, remove checks to manufacture green CI, fabricate screenshots/logs/versions/production results, or change not-verified without execution evidence.

docs.title: Require authorization for external writes
docs.text: Pushes, PR creation or updates, email, comments, issues, cloud resources, production configuration, secrets, permissions, merges, publications, branch/data deletion, and destructive or cost-bearing commands require explicit task authorization or human approval.

docs.title: Build an evidence ladder
docs.text: Evidence rises from syntax and formatting to unit/static checks, build/integration tests, target scenarios, baseline comparison, human product acceptance, and production outcomes. Lower layers cannot impersonate higher ones. Green CI only proves the checks that ran; it does not prove content value, owner acceptance, or production correctness.

docs.title: Treat the PR as a review boundary
docs.text: A Draft PR records the goal and root cause, change scope, validation and unrun checks, what automation cannot prove, risks and rollback, owner review order, and explicit merge/publication state.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• Do not elevate instructions embedded in web pages, issues, logs, or repository content.
  • Do not read, print, or commit unnecessary secrets.
  • Do not push, merge, publish, delete branches, or change production without authorization.
  • Do not use broad staging to silently include unrelated changes.
  • Do not disable tests, delete assertions, or weaken safety gates to obtain green CI.
  • Do not describe a zero exit code as business recovery or product acceptance.
  • Do not run overlapping tasks in one workspace when they can overwrite each other.
  • Do not claim background execution or future delivery of unfinished work.

docs.title: Risk and trust boundaries
docs.text:
flowchart TB H[Human Owner] M[Model] R[Local Repository] E[CLI / IDE / Cloud Environment] T[MCP Tools and Plugins] G[GitHub] C[Cloud Services] P[Production Systems] X[External Docs / Web / Issues / Logs] H -->|task and approval| M M --> E E --> R E -->|scoped token| T T --> G T --> C C --> P X -. untrusted content .-> M X -. prompt injection .-> E H -->|explicit gate| G H -->|separate high-risk gate| P

Primary risks are prompt injection, permission confusion, incomplete context, evidence inflation, scope drift, and credential spread. The defenses are separate identities, minimal tokens, sandboxing, network controls, approvals, diff review, and owner acceptance—not model self-discipline alone.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
git status --short
git diff --check
git diff --stat
git diff
State Meaning PASS Actually executed and passed FAIL Actually executed and failed NOT RUN Environment, permission, or dependency unavailable NOT PROVABLE Automation cannot prove it; human judgment is required

docs.title: Decision-rights matrix
docs.text: Decision Codex may decide Human approval Must not execute independently In-scope implementation and related tests Yes Expand business goal or request new access Yes Push a feature branch When explicitly authorized Yes Merge or publish Yes When unauthorized Change production or data Strong gate Without a dedicated runbook Delete evidence or weaken a gate Yes

docs.title: Exception handling
docs.text: When network access is unavailable, use existing repository facts and mark external verification unrun. Without a browser, complete build/source checks and mark visual acceptance unrun. Without production access, provide safe commands and required evidence rather than pretending execution. With unrelated workspace changes, stage only named files or stop. With material ambiguity, apply the narrowest safe interpretation and record assumptions. For long tests, run the most relevant subset and list the remainder. Exceptions reduce claim strength, not quality gates.

docs.title: Recovery and rollback
docs.text:
• Work in an isolated branch or worktree.
  • Preserve baseline SHA and full diff before commit.
  • Revert task commits without overwriting user work.
  • Give external writes idempotency, audit, and compensation.
  • Keep a PR Draft until the owner accepts it; do not auto-merge.
  • Preserve the mapping between verified artifacts and source commit after publication failure.
  • Use a separate production runbook; code rollback is not business recovery.

docs.title: Capability and version boundary
docs.text: As of July 2026, this standard covers ChatGPT Web/Desktop, Codex CLI, IDE, Cloud, AGENTS.md, Skills, MCP, plugins, sandboxing, approvals, Git worktrees, and PR review. Stable engineering boundaries remain: goals and evidence before edits; permissions before autonomy; external content untrusted by default; minimal diffs before broad refactoring; human acceptance before merge and publication.

docs.title: Example: repair a documentation site without creating a second problem
docs.text: For a task to delete an incorrect public portal, deepen five bilingual articles, and restrict CI to main, a valid execution verifies the baseline and workflows; treats Tab as <title>; removes the portal source and all entries; adds explanatory diagrams and cases to ten files; chains quality and publication on main push; distinguishes executed checks from unrun checks; and opens one Draft PR without merging or publishing.

docs.title: Real case: turning “improve the site” into an auditable change
docs.text:
A documentation-site task originally arrived as the broad request “improve the site.” A valid Codex execution does not keep expanding the visual redesign. It first rewrites the goal into an acceptance boundary: remove the incorrect entry point, keep case content in canonical Markdown, make main pushes run both quality and publication gates, and use local make verify MKDOCS=mkdocs as pre-merge evidence.

The important result is not that one page looks better. Each claim has an owner: source files own the content, the workflow owns publication conditions, the generated site and search index prove readers can discover the content, and human review decides whether the change can merge. Deployment evidence must point to a publication commit record; before that evidence exists, the strongest honest claim is “local validation passed,” not “production is fixed.”

docs.title: Related Internal Article
docs.text: • From Using Codex to Operating an Engineering Agent explains how task contracts, context, tool permissions, and evidence loops put this standard into practice.

docs.title: Official references
docs.text:
• OpenAI Codex documentation
  • Custom instructions with AGENTS.md
  • Build skills
  • Model Context Protocol
  • Codex security and permissions

docs.title: From Using Codex to Operating an Engineering Agent
docs.text:
Two engineers can use the same Codex model on the same repository and still produce radically different outcomes. The first keeps extending the prompt: ask for code, correct the direction, explain the directory, request tests, and finally warn the agent not to touch one important file. The second asks Codex to read the repository contract, establish the change boundary, and name the acceptance evidence before it edits anything. After the change, the second user requires tests, diffs, rendered behavior, and human judgment before accepting the result.

The difference is not who is better at chatting with AI. One person is requesting an answer; the other is operating a constrained, evidence-producing engineering system.

An experienced Codex user can answer five questions consistently:

  1. What context does Codex need now, and what noise should stay out?
  2. Which judgments can be delegated, and which must remain human-owned?
  3. Which tools and state changes are allowed, and where must the agent stop for approval?
  4. What evidence proves completion instead of merely suggesting it?
  5. Should a correction remain in this task, or become a test, AGENTS.md rule, skill, or tool?

This article develops those practices from first principles. It does not promise that AI can replace the engineering owner. It shows how Codex can grow from an occasional code generator into a reviewable engineering executor.

docs.title: Scope and version boundary
docs.text:
This article reflects official OpenAI Codex documentation and practical local workflows available on 2026-07-12. Product surfaces, model names, account entitlements, and feature availability change. Consult current official documentation for the exact entry points for AGENTS.md, skills, MCP, sandboxing, approvals, review, subagents, and worktrees.

The durable subject here is not a particular button. It is the operating model: task contracts, least privilege, capability boundaries, verification evidence, and human acceptance.

docs.title: The problem: treating an engineering agent as a chat box
docs.text:
The most common beginner failure is not a short prompt. It is assigning every responsibility to one vague sentence:

Improve this project and keep going until it is finished.

There is no definition of improvement, no change boundary, no verification method, and no stop condition. The model must infer the objective from partial repository signals. It may produce a large amount of work while leaving the user with a harder diff, more maintenance cost, and conclusions that cannot be proven.

A coding agent differs from a conversational model because it reads files, runs commands, changes state, and calls external tools. The prompt no longer affects only the prose of an answer. It defines the control plane of a real execution process. An underspecified task is therefore missing change management, not merely a few helpful words.

docs.title: The core model: an evidence-driven agent loop
docs.text:
A mature workflow is not prompt followed by response. It is a six-state loop:

flowchart LR A[Observe<br/>repository, constraints, state] --> B[Define<br/>outcome, boundary, acceptance] B --> C[Execute<br/>small reviewable change] C --> D[Verify<br/>tests, builds, runtime evidence] D --> E[Review<br/>risk, judgment, user experience] E --> F[Capture<br/>tests, guidance, skill, tooling] F --> A D -- insufficient evidence --> B E -- result rejected --> B

The key change is that verification is not an optional activity after generation; it governs generation from the beginning. Tests, screenshots, performance measurements, data reconciliation, and human review determine which implementation is acceptable before editing starts.

docs.title: Stage one: turn a wish into a task contract
docs.text:
An executable task needs at least five parts:

Part Required question Example Outcome What visible result must exist? The homepage lists documents by update date Scope Which files, systems, or user paths are involved? Homepage, styling, build-time index generation Exclusions What must not change? Article bodies, URLs, and publication workflow Acceptance Which facts determine success? Twelve items per page, bilingual parity, no mobile overflow Handoff Where must Codex stop? Verified temporary branch awaiting human review

A useful prompt can be compact as long as the result is falsifiable:

Outcome: replace the homepage with a recently updated document list.
Scope: bilingual homepages, build-time index, pagination logic and styles.
Do not change: article bodies, topic paths, publication workflow.
Ordering: use the last Git content modification; ignore path-only moves.
Acceptance: 12 items per page; numbered, previous and next navigation;
            direct page URLs; no overflow at 390 px; matching locale order;
            strict build passes.
Handoff: keep the work on a temporary branch with rendered evidence.

This is not a trick for eliciting better prose. It is a small change request. It gives Codex room to choose implementation details without interpreting autonomy as unlimited scope.

docs.title: Stage two: establish facts before authorizing action
docs.text:
Experienced users separate diagnosis from implementation. The first pass should answer:

  • What branch, working tree, and base revision are active?
  • Which repository instructions apply?
  • Which source files and generators own the current behavior?
  • Which statements are verified facts, and which are assumptions?
  • What is the smallest coherent change surface?
  • Which steps require network access, external writes, or elevated permission?

The purpose is not to obtain a ceremonial plan. It is to expose false premises. Rework often begins when an agent works from the wrong base branch, misses nested guidance, edits generated output instead of its source, or treats a successful build as proof of publication.

Plans should remain proportional. Maintain one when the work contains dependent stages, meaningful risk boundaries, or irreversible state. A one-line documentation fix does not need a ten-step project plan. A plan is useful when it makes state and decisions observable.

docs.title: Stage three: put each correction in the right durable layer
docs.text:
A correction made in one conversation disappears easily. The advanced skill is deciding where it belongs.

Surface Good fit Poor fit Current prompt One-off outcome, immediate scope, temporary judgment Rules that apply to every task AGENTS.md Repository conventions, build commands, review rules, directory boundaries Detailed implementation plan for one change .codex/config.toml Trusted-project settings for sandbox, model, MCP, and hooks Editorial standards or a temporary objective Skill A reusable workflow with references, scripts, and acceptance steps A fact that belongs only to one repository MCP or connector Current external context and controlled remote actions Static rules that can live in the repository Test or validator Invariants that can be checked mechanically Editorial judgment such as whether an article is insightful

Official guidance recommends keeping AGENTS.md small and using it for recurring build, test, review, and directory-specific expectations. A useful feedback loop looks like this:

  1. Fix the first failure in the current task.
  2. When it repeats, decide whether it is a stable pattern.
  3. Encode mechanically decidable behavior as a test or validator.
  4. Put behavioral repository constraints in the nearest AGENTS.md.
  5. Extract a cross-repository process into a skill.
  6. Add MCP only when the workflow needs current external data or actions.

Do not turn every disappointment into another rule. Large guidance files crowd the context and hide contradictions. Preserve the small set of constraints that recur, change behavior, and can be reviewed.

docs.title: Stage four: move from code generation to capability orchestration
docs.text:
Codex capabilities live in distinct layers:

  • AGENTS.md defines how work happens in a repository.
  • Skills package repeatable workflows, optionally with scripts, references, and assets.
  • MCP connects Codex to external context and tools such as documentation, browsers, design systems, or internal platforms.
  • Plugins distribute related skills, connectors, and capabilities.
  • Subagents isolate genuinely independent work in separate contexts.
  • Hooks and validators enforce mechanical constraints at lifecycle boundaries.

More tools do not automatically produce better work. Each tool adds a choice branch, a permission surface, and a failure mode. Mature setups use the smallest sufficient capability set:

  1. Prefer repository facts over an external lookup when the repository is authoritative.
  2. Prefer current official documentation over model memory for product behavior.
  3. Prefer read-only access when it can answer the question.
  4. Do not turn a one-off sequence into a skill prematurely.
  5. Do not delegate work that cannot be accepted independently.

Skills are valuable because they stabilize triggers, steps, references, and evidence—not because they contain more prose. Official documentation explains that Codex initially sees skill metadata and loads the full SKILL.md only after a match. A vague description can therefore make a well-written skill unreliable.

docs.title: Stage five: make verification govern generation
docs.text:
“The code is written” is not completion. Require an evidence ladder:

Layer What it proves What it cannot prove Static checks Syntax, formatting, typing, or links obey rules Real behavior works Automated tests Encoded behavior has not regressed Unencoded user intent is correct Build and runtime The artifact can be produced and started Critical interaction and visual quality are acceptable Browser or end-to-end check A user path actually executes Product judgment and maintainability are sound Human review Scope, risk, technical judgment, and experience are acceptable The system will never change

For a paginated document homepage, “the build passed” is insufficient. Evidence should include:

  • total entries, page size, page count, and last-page boundary;
  • direct access to ?page=2 and browser back/forward behavior;
  • disabled state of previous and next controls;
  • proof that the first item is the latest content modification;
  • matching bilingual entry counts and order;
  • desktop, mobile, light, and dark rendering;
  • a real navigation from a list item to the target document;
  • no new browser-console errors;
  • the complete repository quality gate.

When verification fails, require Codex to classify the failure as implementation, environment, test, or permission before fixing it. Do not accept green results achieved by weakening tests, disabling strict mode, or suppressing actionable errors.

docs.title: Stage six: separate author, verifier, and approver
docs.text:
Self-checking by the same model is useful, but it is not independent review. An author naturally searches for evidence along the assumptions used to build the change.

Codex /review can inspect uncommitted changes, a branch, a commit, or a custom scope and return prioritized findings without editing the working tree by default. A mature flow has three layers:

  1. Author verification runs the tests, build, and target-path checks.
  2. Independent agent review reads the diff and searches for regressions, omissions, and false assumptions.
  3. Human approval decides whether the content, risk, user experience, and publication boundary are acceptable.

A pull request is not the essential part. The essential part is that approval is not disguised as the author’s own conclusion. For high-risk work, the reviewer should rerun critical evidence rather than only reading the author’s summary.

docs.title: Stage seven: parallelize only genuinely independent work
docs.text:
Subagents and worktrees can accelerate large tasks, but parallelism is not decoration.

Good parallel tasks usually have three properties:

  • the input boundary is clear, such as accessibility audit versus build audit;
  • the result can be accepted independently, such as a finding list or changes to disjoint files;
  • the task does not require frequent shared intermediate state.

Poor candidates include two agents editing the same page, an architecture decision that invalidates another agent’s entire premise, or work that can only be integrated by trusting a summary.

Official documentation notes that subagents consume additional tokens but isolate exploration logs and intermediate noise in separate contexts. Worktrees give parallel Git tasks separate file checkouts so they do not disturb the current workspace. Use them with a clear operating model:

  • keep objectives, constraints, and final judgment in the main task;
  • delegate independently executable research, implementation, or review;
  • require evidence and unresolved questions, not only conclusions;
  • isolate shared-file changes in worktrees or coordinate them serially;
  • rerun integrated verification in the main task.

docs.title: Stage eight: treat agent permissions like production permissions
docs.text:
Codex can execute shell commands, access networks, modify files, and call remote tools. Permission design cannot depend on the hope that the agent will not make a mistake.

Sandboxing and approval policy are separate controls: the sandbox defines what is technically possible; the approval policy determines when crossing a boundary must stop for human confirmation. Experienced users do not disable all boundaries to reduce prompts. They build a safe operating area for routine autonomy:

  • write only inside the active workspace by default;
  • keep network access off until a scoped download or lookup needs it;
  • do not grant mutation rights to a diagnostic task;
  • confirm deletion, publication, message sending, permission changes, and production actions at action time;
  • expose secrets only to the shortest stage that needs them, never to prompts, logs, or the repository;
  • approve narrow reusable command prefixes rather than arbitrary shell execution.

High autonomy does not require broad privilege. Sustainable autonomy comes from clear boundaries: the agent can continue inside the safe region and knows where it must stop.

docs.title: End-to-end practice: turning “change a homepage” into a reviewable delivery
docs.text: Assume the task is to replace a bilingual static documentation homepage with a recently updated list and improve its visual quality.

docs.title: 1. Observe
docs.text: Read the repository contract, theme configuration, homepage sources, generation script, and publication workflow. Confirm that the product is a static MkDocs site and reject a database or backend service as unnecessary.

docs.title: 2. Define the meaning
docs.text: Define “recently updated” as the last actual content modification in Git, not filesystem mtime. A path-only migration must not make every article appear newly written. This semantic decision matters more than the CSS because it determines whether the homepage is trustworthy.

docs.title: 3. Fix the boundary
docs.text: Change only homepage generation, pagination behavior, and visual styling. Preserve article bodies, URLs, publication repository, and topic classification. Keep all work on a temporary branch.

docs.title: 4. Choose the smallest architecture
docs.text: Read Git history at build time and generate both localized homepages. Let the browser handle only pagination. This avoids a database, API, admin interface, and manual updated metadata.

docs.title: 5. Build a degradable interaction
docs.text: Keep a complete link list without JavaScript. With JavaScript, expose a fixed page size, store the page in the URL, and restore state through browser history.

docs.title: 6. Measure the visual reference
docs.text: Do not guess what “premium typography” means. Inspect the reference site’s computed font family, size, line height, color, and row density. Then build a cross-platform fallback and verify accessible contrast.

docs.title: 7. Verify
docs.text: Run generator consistency, public-safety checks, and a strict build. Test Chinese desktop, English desktop, Chinese mobile, English mobile, and dark desktop. Exercise page numbers, previous/next, direct URLs, article navigation, and console errors.

docs.title: 8. Hand off
docs.text:
Report changed files, untouched areas, date semantics, measured browser results, and validation commands. Stop on the uncommitted branch so the maintainer can inspect the rendered product before approval.

The value is not that Codex wrote CSS and JavaScript. The workflow connects requirement semantics, source authority, architecture, implementation, runtime proof, and approval into one evidence chain. Code is one segment of that chain.

docs.title: Common failure modes
docs.text: Failure Why it fails Correction Keep extending the prompt Context grows while objective and acceptance remain vague Rewrite the task contract Put every lesson in AGENTS.md Every task pays for irrelevant context Keep only stable recurring constraints Create a skill after one repetition An immature workflow becomes permanent Reuse and correct it before abstraction Grant every permission Convenience and blast radius expand together Use least privilege and action-time approval Spawn many subagents Conflicts, duplicate exploration, and lossy summaries increase Delegate only independently acceptable work Merge when CI is green Automation cannot prove editorial or product judgment Preserve rendered review and human approval Let Codex declare itself successful The author validates its own assumptions Require external evidence and independent review Edit generated output directly The next synchronization overwrites the change Locate and change the source of truth

docs.title: Four practice cycles
docs.text: This is not a capability score. It is a sequence of exercises that gradually increases the control surface.

docs.title: Cycle one: require acceptance evidence for every task
docs.text: Choose five small changes. Before each one, state outcome, scope, exclusions, and validation. Reject summaries that say only “done”; inspect the diff and evidence.

docs.title: Cycle two: establish a repository feedback loop
docs.text: Track repeated Codex mistakes. Encode mechanical failures as tests and stable repository expectations in the nearest AGENTS.md. After one week, delete rules that did not change behavior.

docs.title: Cycle three: abstract one real workflow
docs.text: Choose a process that has succeeded manually several times, such as bilingual documentation publishing, dependency-incident audit, or front-end visual acceptance. Package triggers, inputs, steps, validation, and references as a skill. Test it on a similar but non-identical task.

docs.title: Cycle four: run a multi-role delivery
docs.text: Use one task to implement and an independent task for read-only review. Add subagents only for genuinely independent investigation, and use a worktree when modifications need isolation. Rerun integrated verification in the main task, then leave acceptance to a human.

docs.title: Boundary: responsibilities Codex cannot own
docs.text:
Codex can expand an engineer’s ability to observe and execute, but it cannot become the accountable owner. A person must explicitly own:

  • whether the requirement is worth its opportunity cost;
  • whether production data, customer information, and credentials may enter a tool;
  • whether the business impact of an irreversible action is acceptable;
  • whether technical evidence is sufficient for publication or production change;
  • which user, organizational, and long-term maintenance risks automation missed;
  • whether to commit, merge, deploy, send, publish, or delete.

When nobody owns those decisions, a stronger model only produces unowned decisions faster.

docs.title: Operational conclusions
docs.text:
Experienced Codex practice reduces to ten rules:

  1. Provide an outcome, boundary, and acceptance test—not a vague wish.
  2. Establish facts before authorizing mutation.
  3. Separate verified facts, reasonable inferences, and unknowns.
  4. Define verification before implementation.
  5. Capture repeated corrections in the right durable surface instead of growing prompts forever.
  6. Use the smallest sufficient tool set and least privilege.
  7. Parallelize only independent work that can be accepted separately.
  8. Use independent review to counter author bias.
  9. Let automation own mechanical integrity and humans own judgment, risk, and acceptance.
  10. Turn each successful workflow into a shorter and safer starting point for the next task.

At that point Codex is no longer a chat partner that must be repeatedly steered back toward the task. It is an engineering agent that works inside an explicit contract, produces evidence, stops at permission boundaries, and remains accountable to a human owner.

docs.title: Automation
docs.text:
Auditable, failure-aware, and verifiable operations automation.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Shell Automation Standard Shell automation standard for production scripts with an operating contract, explicit targets, idempotency, dry-run behavior, locking, logging, validation, rollback boundaries, and secret safety.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to write and operate shell scripts used for DevOps automation.

The core judgment:

A shell script becomes production automation the moment it changes infrastructure, data, access, or deployment state. Small scripts fail dangerously because they look too small to deserve controls.

Shell is useful for glue, host automation, packaging, and simple orchestration. It becomes unsafe when it hides target context, parses complex state casually, assumes one-time execution, or cannot explain what it changed.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for scripts that:

  • change servers, containers, Kubernetes objects, or cloud resources
  • run in CI/CD jobs
  • rotate files, logs, backups, certificates, or credentials
  • perform cleanup, migration, or repair
  • touch production access, firewall rules, DNS, or storage
  • are shared between operators or teams

One-off local exploration does not need the full template, but it must not be promoted into shared automation without review.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not force every task into shell. Do not use shell as the primary implementation when the task needs complex data structures, concurrent state, API pagination, robust retries, long-lived business logic, or transactional behavior. Use a language and library better suited to the job.

docs.title: Operating Contract
docs.text:
Every shared or production script needs a short contract:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Purpose What operational change does the script perform? A vague script cannot be reviewed safely. Target Which host, namespace, cluster, account, path, or service is changed? Implicit current context causes wrong-target incidents. Authority Which credential or permission does the script require? Automation should not default to broad admin rights. Inputs Which arguments, files, and environment variables are accepted? Hidden inputs make behavior non-reproducible. Mutations What resources can be created, changed, deleted, or rotated? Operators need to know the blast radius. Idempotency What happens if the script runs twice or resumes after failure? Re-runs are common during incidents. Dry-run Can the script show intended actions without changing state? Review needs a preview path. Locking Can two runs collide on the same target? Concurrent runs can corrupt state or duplicate actions. Validation What proves the intended state was reached? Exit code alone is not enough. Rollback Is recovery automatic, backup-based, manual, or not safe? Some scripts are forward-only.

If the script cannot say what it changes, it is not safe to run unattended.

docs.title: Require explicit target selection
docs.text:
Avoid scripts that infer production targets from current shell context.

Required:

  • --target, --namespace, --cluster, --account, or equivalent explicit input
  • printed target summary before mutation
  • refusal to run when target is empty, ambiguous, or matches a dangerous default
  • confirmation flag such as --yes for destructive actions
  • dry-run as the default for high-impact scripts where practical

Never let an empty variable become /, an empty namespace become "current namespace", or an unset account become the operator's default cloud account.

docs.title: Use strict mode, then handle expected failures deliberately
docs.text:
Baseline:

#!/usr/bin/env bash
set -Eeuo pipefail

trap 'echo "error: line ${LINENO}" >&2' ERR

Strict mode reduces silent failure, but it does not replace explicit checks. Handle expected non-zero results deliberately, especially commands such as grep, kubectl get, test, diff, and cleanup steps.

docs.title: Validate before mutation
docs.text:
Before changing state, validate:

  • required commands exist
  • required files exist and have safe permissions
  • target exists and matches the intended environment
  • current identity is expected
  • destructive paths are not root, empty, wildcard-expanded unexpectedly, or outside approved prefixes
  • external APIs or clusters are reachable
  • backups or snapshots exist when needed
  • dry-run output matches the planned target

Use structured tools where possible. Avoid parsing human-formatted command output when JSON, YAML, or API output is available.

docs.title: Make repeated runs safe
docs.text:
Prefer idempotent operations:

  • check before create
  • compare before replace
  • backup before overwrite
  • skip already-complete steps
  • record progress where long-running work can resume
  • treat missing optional resources as expected only when documented

If a script cannot be idempotent, it must say why and state what evidence must be checked before re-running.

docs.title: Control concurrency and partial failure
docs.text:
Shared scripts should not allow two operators or CI jobs to mutate the same target at the same time.

Use an appropriate lock for the target:

  • file lock for local host operations
  • Kubernetes Lease or ConfigMap lock for cluster operations
  • remote state lock for infrastructure operations
  • CI concurrency group for pipeline jobs

On failure, leave enough state to know which steps completed. A script that dies halfway through a credential rotation, firewall change, or migration must not force operators to guess.

docs.title: Provide logs that support incident review
docs.text:
Logs should include:

  • timestamp
  • script version or commit
  • target
  • operator or CI job context where available
  • action
  • result
  • validation result
  • rollback hint on failure

Do not use set -x in shared or CI scripts that may print credentials, tokens, headers, or private paths.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before approving a shared script:

bash -n script.sh
shellcheck script.sh
./script.sh --dry-run --target <test-target>

Pass conditions:

  • syntax check passes
  • ShellCheck findings are fixed or justified
  • target cannot be omitted silently
  • dry-run output is understandable
  • test run changes only the intended test target
  • repeated test run is safe or documented as not idempotent
  • failure exits non-zero
  • logs show enough evidence for incident review
  • no secrets, real hostnames, internal paths, IPs, customer names, or credentials are embedded

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Empty variable in destructive path Deletes or edits the wrong target. Strict mode, path guards, and explicit target checks. Implicit current context Runs against the wrong cluster, namespace, or account. Require target argument and print target before mutation. No idempotency Re-running doubles changes or fails midway. Check state before each mutation and record progress. Hidden partial failure Pipeline succeeds with a failed command. Use pipefail and explicit expected-failure handling. No lock Two runs mutate the same target. Use a target-appropriate lock or CI concurrency guard. Human-output parsing breaks Upgrade changes output format and script acts on wrong data. Prefer JSON/YAML/API output and structured parsing. No logs No one can reconstruct what happened. Log target, action, result, and validation. Secrets in output Credentials leak to CI logs or terminals. Mask sensitive values and avoid debug tracing.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
A script must state its rollback boundary:

  • reversible by running another command
  • reversible from backup
  • reversible by restoring a previous config or artifact
  • not safely reversible
  • requires manual recovery

If a script deletes data, rotates credentials, changes firewall rules, modifies production access, or migrates state, review rollback before first production use. For forward-only scripts, require stronger dry-run, backup, approval, and observation.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not store credentials in scripts.
  • Do not print tokens, private keys, session cookies, kubeconfigs, or cloud credentials.
  • Avoid curl | bash for production automation.
  • Pin downloaded artifacts and verify checksums where possible.
  • Use least-privilege service accounts in CI.
  • Review scripts that run with sudo, cluster-admin, or cloud admin rights.
  • Do not publish real hostnames, IPs, domains, customer names, internal paths, or production command output.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review shared automation before first use, after incidents, when permissions change, when target platforms or APIs change, when a script becomes part of CI/CD, and when one-off scripts start being reused by more than one operator.

  • System OS Patching Standard
