docs.title: Cloud and object storage
docs.text:
Cloud networking and object-storage migrations with validation and recovery boundaries.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard Cloud network and object storage migration standard for authority by phase, route and identity validation, object metadata proof, read/write cutover, rollback, and reconciliation.
1 document Cases

Extract reusable decisions from anonymous real-world constraints, evidence, and results.

  • Anonymous Object Storage Migration Case Anonymous case study of a cross-provider object storage migration that treated write cutover as an authority change, not a copy-job finish line.

docs.title: Anonymous Object Storage Migration Case
docs.text: All identifiers in this case are generic. No original bucket names, domains, regions, customer data, access keys, prefixes, or application names are preserved.

docs.title: What This Case Is Really About
docs.text:
The migration looked like a data-copy problem. It was really an authority-change problem.

Before the write cutover, the source bucket was still the system of record. A failed attempt could usually roll back by pointing reads back to the source. After the write cutover, new objects and updates began landing on the target. From that moment, rollback stopped being a routing change and became reconciliation.

That single boundary shaped the whole plan. The team moved all dangerous uncertainty before the write cutover and made the cutover the last small step, not the celebration after a large copy job.

docs.title: Decision Frame
docs.text:
Decision question Why it mattered Required answer What is the source of truth at each phase? Without an authority model, rollback language becomes vague. Source is authoritative before write cutover; target becomes authoritative only after explicit approval. How will correctness be proven? Object count and total bytes can match while specific objects, metadata, or access behavior are wrong. Manifest, delta ledger, checksum sampling, metadata checks, and application reads. What changes after writes move? Write cutover changes recovery from repointing to reconciling. A rollback and forward-reconciliation plan exists before target writes begin. How are fresh writes kept visible during read cutover? Reading from target while writing to source can hide newly written objects if replication lag is ignored. Fresh-write paths are fenced, read-through/fallback-enabled, or explicitly outside the read-after-write contract until the final delta closes.

The case became safe when the team stopped asking "did the sync finish?" and started asking "which store currently has the right to accept truth?"

docs.title: Starting Conditions
docs.text:
• The application continuously read and wrote objects on the source provider.
  • Object prefixes carried application meaning.
  • Some objects had metadata and access behavior that mattered to the application, not just bytes.
  • There was no maintenance window long enough for a full freeze-copy-switch migration.
  • Long-lived migration credentials were not acceptable.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• The application had to remain available.
  • Data inconsistency had to be bounded and observable.
  • Credentials used for migration had to be temporary and revocable.
  • Source and target layout differences had to be reviewed before the first bulk copy.
  • The source had to remain available as a rollback path until after the write cutover observation period.

docs.title: Migration Model
docs.text:
The team used three ledgers. This was the most important design choice.

Ledger Purpose Failure it prevents Inventory manifest Declares which buckets, prefixes, object classes, metadata, and access rules are in scope. Copying the wrong shape of data and discovering missing prefixes late. Sync ledger Records bulk-copy completion, delta windows, retries, mismatches, and unresolved objects. Treating "command finished" as equivalent to "migration correct." Application validation ledger Records real application reads, sampled writes, metadata behavior, and permission checks. Migrating bytes that the application still cannot use.

The ledgers were not bureaucracy. They were the only way to know whether a rollback would lose data or merely change routing.

docs.title: Execution Sequence
docs.text:
1. Build the inventory manifest from source buckets, prefixes, object counts, byte totals, metadata requirements, lifecycle policies, and access rules.
  2. Create target buckets, policies, lifecycle behavior, encryption settings, and temporary migration credentials.
  3. Run the initial bulk sync while the application continued reading and writing from the source.
  4. Record mismatches and retry failures in the sync ledger instead of hiding them in tool logs.
  5. Run delta sync for objects changed during the bulk copy, tracking create, update, delete, overwrite, and tombstone or versioning semantics.
  6. Validate counts and sizes, then perform stratified checksum sampling across prefixes, object sizes, object ages, and storage classes.
  7. Run application read tests against the target without moving write authority.
  8. Move reads to the target only after fresh-write visibility is handled through fast enough replication, read-through fallback, write fencing, or an explicit statement that the affected path has no read-after-write requirement.
  9. Freeze, fence, or tightly sequence writes for the final delta window.
  10. Move writes to the target only after unresolved mismatches were accepted or cleared.
  11. Keep the source read-only through the rollback window and revoke temporary credentials after closure.

docs.title: Validation Evidence
docs.text: Evidence Why count and size were not enough Prefix-level manifest comparison A global count can hide a missing or misplaced prefix. Stratified checksum sampling Total bytes can match while selected objects differ. Sampling by prefix, size, and age catches more than random spot checks alone. Metadata comparison Content-Type, cache headers, encryption, and ACL behavior can break applications even when bytes match. Mutation semantics Deletes, overwrites, tombstones, object versions, and list consistency decide whether the target represents current truth or only copied history. Application reads SDK behavior, signed URLs, range reads, redirects, and permission paths are not proven by storage CLI output. Final delta ledger The dangerous objects are the ones changing during the migration, not the cold archive that copied cleanly. Write-cutover observation New writes must be readable from the target, visible to downstream jobs, and absent from unexpected source paths.

docs.title: Count and Size Matched While a Sample Failed
docs.text:
The first comparison looked clean at aggregate level. A checksum sample found mismatches in a small set of objects. The issue was not large enough to move the global totals, but it was large enough to prove that the team could not trust aggregate numbers alone.

Those objects were re-synced and the sample strategy was widened. The team kept the mismatch record instead of erasing it from memory, because that record later justified why the write cutover could proceed.

docs.title: Prefix Mapping Was a Product Contract
docs.text:
One prefix assumption did not match how the target layout had been prepared. If the team had discovered it after bulk sync, the cost would have been a large re-copy. If it had discovered it after write cutover, the cost would have included reconciliation and possible application confusion.

The inventory manifest made prefix mapping a pre-copy decision. This was the right place to find the mistake.

docs.title: Metadata Was Part of the Data
docs.text:
Some application behavior depended on metadata and access semantics rather than only object bytes. A byte-perfect migration could still have broken responses, caching behavior, or client downloads.

The team added application read tests before write cutover, which caught issues a storage-level diff would not have seen.

docs.title: Credentials Needed an End Date
docs.text: Temporary migration access was convenient enough that it could have become permanent by accident. The team treated credential revocation as part of the migration closure, not as a cleanup someday.

docs.title: Reversibility Boundary
docs.text:
The migration had two rollback regimes:

Phase Authority Rollback cost Before read cutover Source accepts reads and writes. No customer-facing rollback; target can be rebuilt. After read cutover, before write cutover Source still accepts writes; target serves reads. Repoint reads to source and investigate target behavior. After write cutover Target accepts new writes, updates, deletes, and overwrites. Rollback requires reverse sync or reconciliation; routing alone is not enough, and source read-only by itself does not preserve target-side mutations.

The final write cutover was therefore treated as the smallest, most controlled step. Everything possible was proven before it.

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
The migration completed without extended outage and without detected data loss. More importantly, the team knew why it was safe to continue at each phase. Counts, sizes, checksums, metadata checks, application reads, and delta reconciliation all answered different questions.

The lasting lesson was that object storage migration should be designed around authority and evidence. Copy tools move bytes; migration plans move responsibility for truth.

docs.title: Reusable Review Questions
docs.text:
• Which store is authoritative before, during, and after each phase?
  • Which prefixes or object classes would be expensive to discover late?
  • Are metadata, ACLs, lifecycle rules, encryption settings, and signed URL behavior part of correctness?
  • How are creates, updates, deletes, overwrites, tombstones, and object versions tracked during bulk copy?
  • During read cutover, what guarantees that a newly written object remains visible to the reader?
  • What test proves the application can read from the target, not merely that the target contains bytes?
  • What exactly happens if the write cutover fails after new writes have landed?
  • When are temporary migration credentials revoked?

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
  • Migration Plan Template
  • Terraform IaC Standard

docs.title: Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to plan and execute cloud network and object storage migrations across accounts, regions, or providers.

The core judgment:

A cloud migration fails less often because copying is impossible, and more often because authority moved without proof: routes, identity, DNS, object metadata, write ownership, and rollback were treated as afterthoughts.

Network migration and object storage migration should be planned together when application traffic, private connectivity, and data paths change at the same time.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • workloads move between cloud accounts, regions, or providers
  • VPC/VNet network boundaries are rebuilt
  • object storage buckets, prefixes, metadata, or lifecycle policies are migrated
  • DNS, private connectivity, NAT, egress, or firewall paths change
  • application reads and writes move to a new location
  • rollback after cutover requires data reconciliation rather than only route reversal

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not cover database migration internals, application refactoring, or vendor-specific console steps. It defines the control model for migration safety.

docs.title: Migration Contract
docs.text:
Before migration starts, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Authority by phase Which system is authoritative before read cutover, after read cutover, and after write cutover? Rollback changes meaning when new truth begins on the target. Network boundary Which routes, DNS names, private endpoints, firewall rules, and egress paths move? Copying data does not prove applications can reach the right target. Identity mapping Which roles, policies, keys, service accounts, or workload identities can read, write, and migrate? A sync job can succeed while the application role fails. Object semantics Are metadata, content type, encryption, versioning, delete markers, overwrite behavior, and lifecycle preserved? Object count and size alone are not enough. Cutover owner Who can decide read switch, write switch, rollback, or stop-the-line? Cutover without authority becomes debate during outage. Final delta rule How are fresh writes handled between last bulk sync and write cutover? This is where data divergence usually begins. Validation evidence Which manifests, checksums, sampled reads, logs, and app tests prove success? Sync summaries are not migration evidence. Reconciliation rule What happens to mismatches or writes after target-side mutation begins? After write cutover, rollback may require merging truth, not flipping DNS.

docs.title: Separate migration planes
docs.text:
Plan three planes independently:

Plane Scope Failure signal Network CIDR, routes, firewall rules, private links, DNS, NAT, egress. Application cannot connect, connects to old target, or bypasses policy. Data Object count, size, checksum, metadata, encryption, versioning, delete and overwrite semantics. Missing object, wrong metadata, access denied, stale read, inconsistent delete behavior. Application Config, endpoint selection, read/write switch, cache, URL generation, retry behavior. Writes split across systems or users read inconsistent data.

Do not declare migration complete because data copied once. Completion requires traffic, identity, and ownership to move safely.

docs.title: Use placeholders in public documentation
docs.text: Public docs must not include real cloud account IDs, VPC IDs, subnet IDs, bucket names, domains, private endpoints, IP addresses, access policies, or migration logs. Use placeholders such as <source-vpc>, <target-bucket>, and <private-service-endpoint>.

docs.title: Build route and identity matrices
docs.text:
Before data movement, document:

  • source and target network boundaries
  • required application connections
  • DNS names and where they resolve
  • route tables and private connectivity
  • security groups or firewall policies
  • NAT and egress path
  • source and target access identities
  • migration job identity
  • application read identity
  • application write identity
  • denied paths that must remain denied

Validate from the workload perspective, not only from a bastion host. The application identity should prove the path it will use after cutover.

docs.title: Move object storage in phases
docs.text:
Use phased migration:

  1. Inventory source buckets or prefixes.
  2. Classify data sensitivity, retention, public-access posture, encryption, and lifecycle.
  3. Create target bucket or prefix with encryption, lifecycle, logging, versioning, and access policy.
  4. Run initial bulk sync.
  5. Run incremental sync until drift is understood and small.
  6. Validate manifests, checksums, metadata, and application reads.
  7. Plan final delta using write freeze, write fencing, queue drain, or a design-approved dual-write.
  8. Cut reads when stale-read behavior is acceptable and observable.
  9. Move write authority last.
  10. Observe target writes and decide whether reconciliation is needed.
  11. Retain the source through the rollback or reconciliation window.

Avoid permanent dual-write unless the application has conflict resolution and ownership rules. Dual-write without conflict rules is data divergence with two destinations.

docs.title: Treat write cutover as the risk boundary
docs.text:
Before write cutover, the source usually remains authoritative. Rollback is often route or configuration reversal plus another sync.

After target writes begin, new truth may exist on the target. Rollback can require:

  • stopping target writes
  • replaying or reversing writes
  • reconciling source and target manifests
  • preserving target-only objects
  • resolving metadata conflicts
  • communicating partial rollback limits

This means write cutover needs a stricter approval bar than read cutover.

docs.title: Network validation
docs.text:
Validate from workload identities and runtime locations:

curl -fsS https://<service-endpoint>/healthz
dig <service-name>
nc -vz <private-service-endpoint> <port>

Expected evidence:

  • DNS resolves to the intended target
  • private endpoints are reachable from approved networks only
  • denied paths are actually denied
  • egress follows the intended path
  • logs show the expected source identity
  • application identity can access the target
  • migration identity can no longer overreach after cutover

docs.title: Object validation
docs.text:
Validate more than count and size:

  • prefix-level manifest comparison
  • total logical size
  • stratified checksum sampling
  • metadata and content type
  • encryption state
  • object versioning, delete markers, overwrite, and tombstone behavior
  • lifecycle and retention rules
  • access policy from application read and write roles
  • application reads against representative paths
  • final delta ledger
  • unresolved mismatch disposition
  • target-write observation after write cutover
  • reconciliation requirement after target-side mutation begins

For large stores, use deterministic sampling plus inventory comparison rather than trusting a single sync command summary.

docs.title: Cutover
docs.text:
Cutover must have:

  • read switch owner
  • write switch owner
  • final delta or write fencing plan
  • DNS or config switch owner
  • application smoke test
  • monitoring owner
  • mismatch disposition owner
  • rollback decision window
  • communication to affected teams

Do not cut over if the rollback condition is vague. The team must know which signal triggers rollback, who decides, and whether rollback is still a route change or now a data reconciliation.

docs.title: Rollback and Reconciliation
docs.text:
Rollback is easiest before target writes begin and while the source remains intact.

Rollback options:

  • repoint DNS or config to source
  • re-enable source route
  • replay accepted writes if captured
  • restore from retained source copy
  • stop target writes and reconcile target-only changes

Rollback is risky when:

  • writes occurred in both source and target
  • target-side mutations already became user-visible
  • object versioning or metadata changed incompatibly
  • source lifecycle policy deleted old data
  • application URL generation or cache behavior changed
  • reconciliation owner and ledger are missing

If write cutover has occurred, do not describe rollback as "switch back" unless reconciliation has been proven.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention CIDR or route overlap Traffic reaches wrong target or no target. Validate route matrix before cutover. DNS TTL ignored Some clients keep old endpoint. Lower TTL and test resolver behavior. IAM mismatch Sync succeeds but application cannot read or write. Validate with application identities, not only migration identity. Metadata lost Downloads, caching, content type, or encryption behavior breaks. Compare metadata and semantics, not only bytes. Fresh writes not fenced Source and target diverge during final delta. Freeze, fence, drain, or design-approved dual-write. Source deleted early Rollback becomes impossible or expensive. Retain source through rollback and reconciliation window. Permanent dual-write Inconsistent objects and unclear authority. Prefer single writer and explicit conflict rules. Mismatch disposition missing Known differences are ignored until users find them. Assign owner and outcome for every unresolved mismatch class.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Use temporary migration roles with least privilege.
  • Encrypt objects in transit and at rest.
  • Do not copy public access policies by accident.
  • Log data access during migration.
  • Remove migration roles and temporary routes after cutover.
  • Protect manifests and checksum logs when they reveal object names or business structure.
  • Keep migration evidence generic in public documentation.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard before any migration wave, after each cutover, after unresolved mismatches, after reconciliation work, and after any route, DNS, IAM, object metadata, lifecycle, or rollback incident.

docs.text:

  • Terraform IaC Standard
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Containers and images
docs.text:
Image builds, container hosts, Compose, Harbor, and containerization migrations.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

5 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • BuildKit Build Standard BuildKit build standard for reproducible container builds, cache safety, secret handling, provenance, validation, and rollback.
  • ChartMuseum Helm Repository Standard ChartMuseum Helm repository standard for operating a bounded chart repository as a release input with publishing control, immutability, backup, validation, and migration boundaries.
  • Docker Compose Stack Standard Standard for operating Docker Compose stacks as small, transitional, or single-host services with explicit ownership, state, validation, and rollback.
  • Docker Host Operations Standard Docker host operations standard for bounded single-host container workloads with daemon configuration, data-root capacity, log limits, cleanup safety, network recovery, socket protection, validation, and rollback.
  • Harbor Registry Standard Harbor registry standard for operating container images as release inputs with access control, immutable identity, scanning, retention, replication, backup, and recovery evidence.
1 document Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • Harbor Certificate Rotation Runbook Runbook for rotating TLS certificates on Harbor or an equivalent private container registry with key safety, client trust, login, pull, push, node, and CI validation.
1 document Cases

Extract reusable decisions from anonymous real-world constraints, evidence, and results.

  • Anonymous Application Containerization Migration Case Anonymous case study of moving a host-managed application into containers by first exposing the runtime contract hidden inside the old hosts.

docs.title: Anonymous Application Containerization Migration Case
docs.text: All identifiers in this case are generic. No original service names, hostnames, domains, images, repositories, or credentials are preserved.

docs.title: What This Case Is Really About
docs.text:
The useful lesson in this migration was not "write a Dockerfile and move the service." The useful lesson was that the old hosts were already part of the application.

They provided local directories, shell profiles, package versions, certificates, cron jobs, startup timing, log paths, and a small amount of undocumented operator memory. The team could not make the deployment repeatable until those host behaviors were turned into an explicit runtime contract.

That changed the sequence of the migration. Image build was not the first serious milestone. The first serious milestone was proving that everything the old host quietly did for the application had a named owner in the new model.

docs.title: Decision Frame
docs.text:
The migration was accepted only if three conditions stayed true:

Decision question Required answer before production traffic What state can disappear on container restart? Every writable path is either externalized, rebuilt deterministically, or declared disposable. What made the old process start successfully? Ports, dependencies, startup order, config files, certificates, and background jobs are documented and tested. Where is the rollback boundary? The original host deployment remains live until the containerized service proves read, write, job, log, and alert parity.

This forced the team to treat "containerization" as a contract extraction exercise, not a packaging exercise.

docs.title: Starting Conditions
docs.text:
• Long-lived hosts had been patched and configured by hand.
  • Deployment scripts captured only part of the runtime behavior.
  • Some application files were written to local disk and assumed persistent.
  • A few settings existed in host files or shell startup files rather than in source-controlled configuration.
  • Scheduled jobs and maintenance tasks ran beside the main process, not inside the deploy pipeline.
  • Startup order between services was enforced by habit and timing, not by readiness checks.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
• The service had to remain available during the migration.
  • Credentials could not be baked into images or copied as plaintext files.
  • The new deployment had to support gradual traffic movement.
  • The old hosts had to stay usable as rollback until the new runtime contract was proven.
  • No unrelated application change was allowed in the same window.

docs.title: Migration Contract
docs.text:
Before a service moved, the team filled a small contract instead of starting with the Dockerfile:

Contract area Question that had to be answered Example evidence Process What command starts the service and what must exist before it starts? Entrypoint, readiness probe, dependency check. Configuration Which values vary by environment and where are they stored? Config map or environment inventory, no secrets in image layers. Secrets Which credentials are required at runtime and how are they rotated? Secret manager reference and revocation procedure. State Which paths are writable and which must survive restart? Volume mapping, object storage path, or explicit disposable cache. Jobs Which cron or side jobs are part of the product behavior? Job schedule, owner, retry rule, and alert. Observability How will the service be seen after it moves? Logs, metrics, traces, dashboards, and alert routing. Rollback What makes returning to the host safe? Host deployment remains compatible with the same data authority.

Only after this table had owners did the image build become useful.

docs.title: Execution Sequence
docs.text:
1. Inventory the running host with process list, open ports, mounted paths, package versions, certificates, scheduled tasks, and startup files.
  2. Classify every writable path as persistent data, cache, generated artifact, or accidental state.
  3. Move configuration into explicit environment/config objects and move credentials into secret management.
  4. Build pinned images from repeatable inputs; no floating base tags, no ad hoc packages installed during startup.
  5. Add startup, liveness, and readiness checks that model real dependency readiness, not just process existence.
  6. Recreate side jobs as scheduled jobs with the same ownership and alert path as the main service.
  7. Run staging with production-shaped config and dependency failures injected.
  8. Shift a small slice of production traffic while the old host deployment remains writable authority.
  9. Expand traffic only after parity checks pass for reads, writes, jobs, logs, and alerts.

docs.title: Validation Evidence
docs.text:
The team did not accept "the container starts" as evidence. The acceptance checks were closer to this:

Check Why it mattered Pass condition Read path parity The new runtime had to reach every dependency the host reached. Same functional read tests passed from host and container. Write path durability Container restart must not delete product state. Restart test preserved required files or recreated disposable ones. Startup gating Parallel scheduling would reveal hidden timing assumptions. Dependency unavailable test kept the service out of ready state. Job parity Cron-like behavior was part of production, even if it was not in the app server. Scheduled jobs ran once, retried correctly, and alerted on failure. Log and metric path A working service without visibility is not production-ready. Logs, metrics, and key alerts appeared under the new labels. Rollback drill A rollback plan that is not executed once is only a hope. Traffic returned to host deployment without data reconciliation.

docs.title: Local State Was Product State
docs.text:
One component wrote files to a path that looked like scratch space. On the old host the path survived restarts for years, so the team had stopped noticing it. In the container it disappeared immediately after rescheduling.

The fix was not "mount everything." The path was split into durable product data, generated cache, and accidental residue. Only the durable part moved to managed storage. The rest became rebuildable or disposable.

docs.title: Startup Order Was a Hidden API
docs.text:
The old stack started in a familiar sequence. The container platform scheduled components in parallel. A service that previously "usually started after its dependency" began crash-looping.

The durable fix was readiness gating and dependency checks, not sleep loops. The service was allowed to start, but it was not allowed to receive traffic until the dependency behavior it required was present.

docs.title: Cron Was Outside the Deployment Story
docs.text:
Several small maintenance tasks were not part of the application release artifact. They were easy to miss because they were not on the critical request path, yet customers would have noticed their absence later.

Those jobs were migrated as first-class workload objects with retry behavior, ownership, and alerts. This was the point where the team stopped treating the web process as the whole service.

docs.title: Configuration Was a Memory Leak
docs.text:
Some settings existed only because a host had been adjusted during an earlier incident. They were not malicious or dramatic. They were simply not represented anywhere durable.

The team found them by comparing runtime behavior, environment, config files, and startup logs between host and container. Any setting that could not be explained was treated as a release blocker, not as a harmless difference.

docs.title: Reversibility Boundary
docs.text:
Rollback was easy only while the old host deployment remained compatible with the same data authority. The team therefore kept the first production phase deliberately narrow:

  • Traffic could move between host and container.
  • Writes still landed in a data path both versions understood.
  • No schema, storage layout, or credential rotation made the host version unusable.
  • The observation window ended only after scheduled jobs and alerting had run under real conditions.

The moment a migration step would make the host version unable to serve the same data, that step was moved out of the first cutover and treated as a separate change.

docs.title: Outcome
docs.text:
The service moved without sustained outage because the team discovered the unsafe parts before the traffic cutover. The most valuable artifact was not the final image. It was the runtime contract that made later services faster to move and easier to review.

The case also changed the team's definition of "ready for containers": ready meant that the service could explain its configuration, state, jobs, dependencies, and observability without borrowing facts from a long-lived host.

docs.title: Reusable Review Questions
docs.text:
• Which parts of the old host are being treated as invisible infrastructure?
  • What would be lost if the container restarted on a different node?
  • Which background jobs are required for the product to remain correct?
  • Which dependency failures should keep the service unready rather than crash-looping?
  • Can traffic return to the old host without reconciling data?
  • Which settings exist only because someone once fixed an incident by hand?

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Docker Host Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to use BuildKit and Docker Buildx for container image builds without leaking secrets, producing unreproducible images, or hiding supply-chain risk.

The core judgment:

A container build is a supply-chain event, not a packaging shortcut.

BuildKit improves speed, caching, multi-platform output, and secret handling. Those benefits matter only when build inputs, secrets, cache sources, image tags, and validation are controlled.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Building container images in CI/CD.
  • Enabling docker buildx or remote BuildKit builders.
  • Using build cache across pipelines.
  • Building multi-platform images.
  • Passing private dependency credentials to a build.
  • Publishing images to a shared registry.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define application Dockerfile style in full, registry governance, or runtime Kubernetes deployment policy. It only covers build-time controls.

docs.title: Separate build inputs from deployment state
docs.text:
The build should consume source code, Dockerfile, lockfiles, build arguments, and approved secrets. It should not depend on mutable host state, local developer files, or runtime .env.

Required inputs:

  • Dockerfile path.
  • Build context.
  • Base image tag or digest.
  • Dependency lockfiles.
  • Target platforms.
  • Image tag and immutable digest output.
  • Build secret names, not secret values.

docs.title: Pin base images and dependencies
docs.text:
Use explicit base image versions. For highly controlled environments, pin by digest and refresh through a scheduled update process.

Avoid:

  • FROM ubuntu:latest
  • Unpinned package managers in release builds.
  • Downloading scripts from arbitrary URLs during build.
  • Embedding environment-specific configuration into image layers.

docs.title: Use secret mounts, not build args
docs.text:
Build arguments become visible in image history and build metadata. Use BuildKit secret mounts for private dependency tokens or package repository credentials.

Pattern:

# syntax=docker/dockerfile:1.7
RUN --mount=type=secret,id=package_token \
    ./scripts/install-private-deps.sh

Build command:

docker buildx build \
  --secret id=package_token,env=PACKAGE_TOKEN \
  --tag registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0 \
  --push \
  .

Do not print secret values in build logs.

docs.title: Treat cache as shared state
docs.text:
Cache improves speed but can carry stale or unsafe assumptions.

Cache Type Use Rule Local cache Developer builds. Never relied on for release correctness. Registry cache CI speed-up. Namespace per project or trust boundary. Inline cache Rebuild optimization. Publish only for approved images.

Invalidate cache deliberately when base images, package indexes, lockfiles, or security patches change.

docs.title: Produce traceable output
docs.text:
Every release image should have:

  • Immutable digest.
  • Source revision.
  • Build timestamp.
  • Builder identity.
  • Target platform.
  • SBOM or equivalent dependency inventory where available.
  • Vulnerability scan result or exception record.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before publishing a release image:

docker buildx version
docker buildx build --platform linux/amd64 --tag registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0 --push .
docker buildx imagetools inspect registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0

Pass conditions:

  • Build completes from a clean runner.
  • Image digest is recorded.
  • Target platforms match the release plan.
  • No secret values appear in logs or image history.
  • Vulnerability scan result is attached to the release record.
  • A smoke test runs against the built image, not a local container.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Secret passed as build arg Secret can leak through history or metadata. Use BuildKit secret mounts. Unpinned base image Rebuild changes without source change. Pin versions or digests. Shared cache across trust boundaries One project influences another build. Scope cache by repository and trust domain. Multi-platform image untested Wrong architecture fails at runtime. Inspect manifest and smoke-test target platform. Build downloads mutable scripts Supply-chain drift. Vendor scripts or verify checksums. Image pushed without scan Known vulnerabilities ship silently. Make scan part of release gate.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback uses the previous approved image digest. Do not rebuild the old tag and assume it is identical.

Keep:

  • Previous digest.
  • Dockerfile revision.
  • SBOM or dependency inventory.
  • Registry retention long enough for rollback.

If a base image vulnerability triggers rebuild, treat the new image as a new release even when application code did not change.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not use privileged remote builders without an owner and isolation model.
  • Keep build credentials scoped to the registry or package repository they need.
  • Avoid mounting the host Docker socket into untrusted build jobs.
  • Do not copy .git, local credentials, or developer caches into the image context.
  • Keep .dockerignore reviewed.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard when enabling a new builder, changing cache strategy, adding private dependencies, adding a platform, or changing registry promotion rules.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Docker Host Operations Standard
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate ChartMuseum or a similar self-hosted Helm chart repository.

The core judgment:

A Helm repository is a release input, not a file share. If chart versions are mutable, unaudited, or easy to overwrite, the deployment system can no longer prove what it installed.

ChartMuseum is useful for bounded internal chart distribution. It should not become an unowned artifact platform, a permanent substitute for the platform registry, or a place where release evidence disappears.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use ChartMuseum when:

  • A team needs a simple private Helm chart repository.
  • Existing deployment tooling still expects an HTTP Helm repository.
  • OCI chart storage is not yet available, approved, or integrated.
  • Chart producers and consumers are internal and bounded.
  • Rollback depends on keeping exact historical chart versions available.

Prefer OCI registry-based chart storage when the platform registry already supports chart artifacts, access control, scanning, retention, and audit in one standard path.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not define:

  • Helm chart authoring style.
  • Application release approval policy.
  • A full artifact registry strategy.
  • Public chart distribution.
  • Secrets management inside chart values.

It covers repository operation, publishing control, release trust, and recovery.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before ChartMuseum is used by delivery automation, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Publishing authority Which CI job or release owner can publish charts? Laptop publishing removes release accountability. Version immutability Can a chart version ever be overwritten? Mutable versions make rollbacks and audits unreliable. Artifact mapping How does chart version map to source revision, app version, and validation result? A deployed chart must be traceable to reviewed inputs. Storage ownership Where are chart packages and index.yaml stored and backed up? Losing the index or packages can break deploys and rollback. Read/write access Which identities can read, publish, and administer? Shared credentials hide who changed release inputs. Retention How long are rollback versions kept? Aggressive cleanup can remove the only recoverable release. Migration path When should the repository move to OCI or a managed registry? ChartMuseum should not quietly become permanent technical debt.

docs.title: Publish only from controlled automation
docs.text:
Only CI/CD or approved release automation should publish charts. Human laptop publishing should be an exception with a recorded reason.

Each published chart version should record:

  • chart name
  • chart version
  • application version
  • source revision
  • release owner or pipeline identity
  • validation result
  • publish timestamp

Do not publish a chart that cannot be tied back to source and validation evidence.

docs.title: Treat chart versions as immutable
docs.text:
Do not overwrite an existing chart version to fix a mistake. Publish a new version.

If a repository backend allows overwrites, block them through policy, pipeline checks, or review gates. At minimum, alert on an overwrite attempt.

Immutability is what lets a release tool prove that example-service-1.2.3.tgz means the same thing today that it meant during the incident.

docs.title: Protect read and write paths separately
docs.text:
Use TLS and authentication for private charts. Separate:

  • read credentials used by deployment systems
  • write credentials used by publishing automation
  • administrative credentials used for repository operation

Do not reuse a shared write credential across teams. If a write credential leaks, every chart in the repository becomes a release-integrity risk.

docs.title: Back up both packages and index
docs.text:
Backup must include chart packages and repository index state. Backing up only object files without index recovery can still leave deployments unable to resolve versions.

The recovery drill should prove:

  • the repository index can be rebuilt or restored
  • an exact historical version can be pulled
  • a rollback chart inside the retention window still exists
  • permissions still prevent unauthorized publish after restore

docs.title: Keep ChartMuseum bounded
docs.text:
ChartMuseum is acceptable when its scope is explicit. Review migration to OCI or a managed registry when:

  • multiple teams depend on it
  • audit requirements grow
  • chart retention becomes long-term
  • registry credentials and image credentials need unified control
  • release tooling already supports OCI charts

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Use placeholder endpoints in public examples:

helm repo add example https://<chart-repository>
helm repo update
helm search repo example
helm pull example/<chart-name> --version <chart-version>

Pass conditions:

  • Repository index is reachable over the approved HTTPS path.
  • Exact chart version can be pulled.
  • Unauthorized publish is rejected.
  • Authorized publish comes only from the approved CI or release identity.
  • Existing chart version cannot be overwritten, or overwrite attempts are detected and blocked.
  • A restore drill proves chart packages and index state can be restored or rebuilt.
  • An exact historical chart can be pulled from the restored path.
  • Unauthorized publish remains rejected after restore.
  • A previous rollback chart still exists within the retention window.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Chart version overwritten Release is not reproducible. Treat versions as immutable and block overwrites. Shared publish credential No accountability for changed release input. Use CI identity or named release owners. No TLS or weak auth Charts and credentials can be exposed or modified. Require HTTPS and separate read/write credentials. Retention deletes rollback chart Rollback is blocked during incident response. Keep rollback versions through the recovery window. Index corrupt or missing Deployments cannot resolve chart versions. Back up and test restore for packages and index. Chart embeds secrets Values or templates leak credentials. Keep secrets in approved secret management, not charts. Unbounded repository scope A small utility becomes an ungoverned artifact platform. Define owner, consumers, retention, and migration path.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Application rollback requires both:

  • the previous chart version
  • compatible values and application artifacts

Do not roll back by overwriting the current chart version. Deploy the previous version explicitly.

Repository recovery may require:

  • restoring chart packages
  • restoring or rebuilding index.yaml
  • validating repository read access
  • validating publish restrictions
  • confirming deployment tooling can pull the exact rollback chart

If the repository itself is unreliable, route deployments to a restored repository or approved registry path before changing application releases.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not embed secrets in chart templates, default values, or public examples.
  • Protect write credentials and rotate them after suspected exposure.
  • Avoid real internal domains, repository paths, project names, or environment defaults in public docs.
  • Review chart dependencies before publishing.
  • Limit repository administration to platform or release owners.
  • Keep audit logs for publish and delete operations where available.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • when adding a new publisher
  • after a failed chart deployment
  • after a rollback misses a chart version
  • when changing storage backend
  • when changing retention
  • when migrating to OCI registry
  • after credential exposure or unauthorized publish attempts

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Harbor Registry Standard
  • Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • BuildKit Build Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines when Docker Compose is an acceptable operating model and how to run Compose stacks without turning them into undocumented production platforms.

The core judgment:

Docker Compose is useful for bounded stacks, not for hidden orchestration.

Compose is strongest when the service boundary is small, the host is explicit, the data model is understood, and recovery is documented. It becomes risky when teams use it as an invisible replacement for scheduling, high availability, secrets management, and production deployment control.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use Docker Compose for:

  • Local development and integration environments.
  • Demonstrations and disposable test stacks.
  • Small internal services with low availability requirements.
  • Transitional workloads before Kubernetes or a managed service migration.
  • Single-host tools where the failure domain is accepted.

Do not use it for workloads that require multi-node scheduling, automated failover, horizontal autoscaling, strong tenant isolation, or complex release governance.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not replace Kubernetes, service mesh, managed database, or full platform operations standards. It also does not approve publishing real .env files, production hostnames, private registry URLs, or credentials.

docs.title: Define the stack contract
docs.text:
Every Compose stack needs a short contract:

Area Required Decision Owner Team or person responsible for deployment, logs, backup, and restore. Host Where the stack runs and whether it is disposable. State Which volumes contain business data and which are cache-like. Exposure Which ports are reachable and from where. Secrets Where runtime secrets come from; never commit .env. Recovery How to restart, restore, or rebuild the stack. Upgrade How images are pinned and promoted.

If nobody owns restore, the stack is not production-ready even if the containers are running.

docs.title: Pin images and keep build separate from run
docs.text:
Use pinned image tags or digests for services. Do not let a production-like stack pull latest.

Compose may build images for development, but production-like stacks should consume images produced by a controlled build pipeline. This keeps build credentials, source code, and runtime deployment separate.

docs.title: Treat volumes as data contracts
docs.text:
Named volumes and bind mounts must be documented.

Volume Type Use Requirement Named volume Normal single-host persistent data. Backup and restore path required. Bind mount Host-integrated files, configs, or uploads. Exact host path must be owned and permissioned. tmpfs Temporary sensitive or disposable runtime data. Must not hold business data.

Do not delete a volume until the data class and owner are known.

docs.title: Keep network exposure deliberate
docs.text:
Avoid publishing every container port to the host. Prefer an internal Compose network and expose only the edge service.

Document:

  • Host ports.
  • Protocols.
  • Allowed clients.
  • Reverse proxy or TLS boundary.
  • Health endpoint.

docs.title: Standard service pattern
docs.text:
services:
  app:
    image: registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0
    restart: unless-stopped
    env_file:
      - .env
    depends_on:
      db:
        condition: service_healthy
    ports:
      - "8080:8080"
    volumes:
      - app-data:/var/lib/app
    logging:
      driver: json-file
      options:
        max-size: "100m"
        max-file: "3"
    healthcheck:
      test: ["CMD", "wget", "-qO-", "http://localhost:8080/healthz"]
      interval: 30s
      timeout: 5s
      retries: 3

volumes:
  app-data:

Publish .env.example with placeholder keys. Never publish the actual .env.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Run before deployment or after change:

docker compose config
docker compose pull
docker compose up -d
docker compose ps
docker compose logs --tail=100 app

Pass conditions:

  • docker compose config renders without surprises.
  • All intended services are healthy or explicitly one-shot.
  • Only intended ports are exposed.
  • Logs are bounded and readable.
  • State volumes are visible and backup scope is known.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention latest image drift Restarts deploy unreviewed versions. Pin tags or digests. Undocumented bind mount Data loss during host cleanup. Inventory host paths and owners. No log limits Host disk fills. Set log rotation options. Port sprawl Internal services become reachable. Expose only edge ports. .env committed Secret leakage. Commit only .env.example; use local secret distribution. No restore test Backup confidence is false. Run restore verification for stateful services.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback is safe only when the previous image and data format are compatible.

Use:

docker compose pull
docker compose up -d

after changing the image tag back to the approved previous version.

Escalate before rollback when the release changed database schema, volume layout, or file format. In those cases, recovery may require restoring data, not only reverting containers.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Keep .env files out of git.
  • Avoid privileged containers unless there is a documented host integration reason.
  • Do not mount the Docker socket into application containers.
  • Run containers as non-root where images support it.
  • Limit host port exposure.
  • Keep registry credentials outside the Compose file.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review a Compose stack before first production-like use, before image major upgrades, after host incidents, and before migrating the workload to Kubernetes or a managed service.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Docker Host Operations Standard
  • BuildKit Build Standard
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Docker on a host that runs single-host workloads, build jobs, internal tools, or transitional services.

The core judgment:

A Docker host is a failure domain. If its disk, daemon, bridge, socket, or volumes are unmanaged, containers only make the outage easier to restart, not easier to understand.

Docker is acceptable for bounded workloads. It should not become hidden orchestration, unowned production state, or a shortcut around scheduler, registry, backup, and access controls.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • single-host Docker workloads
  • development or integration hosts
  • lightweight internal tools
  • CI/build machines
  • transitional services before Kubernetes or managed platforms
  • host-managed container stacks with explicit owner and recovery path

Prefer Kubernetes, a managed scheduler, or a managed service when workloads require multi-node scheduling, automated failover, horizontal autoscaling, strong tenant isolation, or formal release governance.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not approve running critical multi-service production platforms on one Docker host. It does not replace registry governance, image build standards, Compose stack standards, or Kubernetes operations.

docs.title: Host Contract
docs.text:
Before a Docker host runs shared or production-like workloads, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Owner Who owns daemon config, cleanup, patching, incidents, and retirement? Docker hosts without owners become forgotten production dependencies. Workload class Build runner, internal tool, transient app, stateful service, or migration bridge? Cleanup, restart, and backup rules differ. Data boundary Which volumes or bind mounts hold business data? Which are disposable? docker volume prune can delete state. Image source Which registry and tags/digests are approved? Rollback depends on recoverable images. Log policy How are container logs bounded and collected? JSON logs can fill the host filesystem. Network boundary Which bridge CIDRs, ports, and host routes are in use? Docker networks can collide with VPN, cloud, or Kubernetes ranges. Socket boundary Who can reach the Docker socket or daemon API? Docker socket access is effectively host-level control. Recovery path Is recovery restart, image rollback, volume restore, host rebuild, or migration away? Restarting containers does not recover lost state.

docs.title: Configure the daemon before workloads arrive
docs.text:
Create a reviewed daemon configuration before long-lived containers run:

{
  "log-driver": "json-file",
  "log-opts": {
    "max-size": "100m",
    "max-file": "3"
  },
  "storage-driver": "overlay2"
}

Apply and validate:

sudo systemctl daemon-reload
sudo systemctl enable --now docker
docker info

Set the Docker data root on storage with enough capacity for images, layers, logs, build cache, and volumes. If /var/lib/docker is on the root filesystem, disk pressure becomes host pressure.

docs.title: Treat volumes and bind mounts as data contracts
docs.text:
Before running or cleaning a workload, record:

  • named volumes
  • bind mount paths
  • whether each path is business data, cache, or disposable
  • backup owner
  • restore command or rebuild path
  • retention expectation

Do not treat all Docker data as cache. Some of it may be the only copy of application state.

docs.title: Bound logs before disk pressure starts
docs.text:
Container logs should be bounded before the first long-lived workload starts. If centralized logging exists, still keep local log growth bounded.

Minimum controls:

  • log rotation in daemon config or per container
  • disk usage alert for Docker data root
  • owner for cleanup
  • evidence that application logs are available elsewhere if local logs are pruned

docs.title: Use guarded cleanup in risk order
docs.text:
Use cleanup when build cache, old images, stopped containers, or unused networks consume disk.

Preconditions:

  • docker system df reviewed
  • critical containers identified
  • stopped containers reviewed for writable-layer data, troubleshooting evidence, and logs that are not retained elsewhere
  • stateful volumes identified and excluded unless approved
  • current rollback images confirmed locally or in the registry
  • active deployment or rollback window checked

Cleanup order, from lower to higher risk after the preconditions are met:

docker container prune -f
docker network prune -f
docker image prune -f
docker builder prune -f

Higher-risk cleanup:

# High risk: removes all images not used by running containers.
# Confirm rollback images are available in the registry before use.
docker image prune -a

Avoid volume cleanup unless the volume owner and data lifecycle are known:

# High risk: only with explicit approval and known-disposable volumes.
docker volume prune

Validation:

  • expected disk space reclaimed
  • running containers remain healthy
  • rollback images still available locally or in the registry
  • disk alert clears
  • no stateful volume was removed without approval

docs.title: Recover Docker networking before deleting state
docs.text:
Symptoms of Docker network issues include name-resolution failures between containers, bridge CIDR conflicts, unreachable services after IP range changes, or a corrupted default bridge.

Low-risk checks:

docker network ls
docker network inspect bridge
ip addr
iptables -S

Low-risk recovery:

  1. Stop affected non-critical containers.
  2. Remove only unused Docker networks.
  3. Restart Docker.
  4. Recreate containers from reviewed Compose or deployment files.

Use a full bridge reset only when the bridge is corrupted, conflicts with host routing, or uses an incorrect CIDR.

Preconditions:

  • maintenance window approved
  • running containers identified
  • /etc/docker/daemon.json backed up
  • new bridge CIDR checked against host, VPN, Kubernetes, and cloud ranges
  • remote access does not depend on the Docker bridge being reset

Procedure:

  1. Stop Docker cleanly.
  2. Confirm no critical container is expected to keep running.
  3. Update daemon.json with approved bridge CIDR and log rotation settings.
  4. While Docker is still stopped, remove a stale docker0 only if it still exists and blocks the approved CIDR:
sudo ip link set docker0 down
sudo ip link delete docker0
  1. Start Docker and let it rebuild the default bridge from config.
  2. Validate bridge address, container networking, NAT, and service reachability.

Do not delete /var/lib/docker as a normal repair step. Data deletion is a separate action with its own backup and approval gate.

docs.title: Protect the Docker socket
docs.text:
Access to /var/run/docker.sock is effectively control of the host. A container with the Docker socket mounted can start privileged containers, read host files through mounts, and change running workloads.

Required controls:

  • no application container mounts the Docker socket unless the host-control role is explicit
  • CI runners using Docker socket are isolated from untrusted jobs
  • daemon TCP API is disabled or tightly protected
  • Docker group membership is treated as privileged access
  • socket access is reviewed after personnel or runner changes

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
docker version
docker info
docker run --rm <approved-registry>/<project>/<smoke-test-image>@<digest>
docker system df

Pass conditions:

  • Docker daemon starts from reviewed config
  • storage driver and data root match the host contract
  • log limits are active
  • disk usage is visible and alertable
  • running containers are healthy
  • volume and bind mount ownership is documented
  • Docker socket access is limited to approved operators or workloads
  • network CIDR does not conflict with host, VPN, Kubernetes, or cloud ranges

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Docker data root fills root filesystem Host instability or outage. Put data root on planned storage and alert on usage. Logs unbounded Container JSON logs fill disk. Configure log rotation before workloads run. Volume pruned as cleanup Stateful data loss. Inventory volume owners and exclude stateful volumes. Rollback image removed Fast rollback unavailable during incident. Confirm previous image digest exists locally or in registry before cleanup. Docker bridge conflicts with network ranges Containers or host routes fail unpredictably. Reserve and validate bridge CIDR. Docker socket exposed Container or user gains host-level control. Restrict socket and Docker group access. /var/lib/docker deleted during repair Images, volumes, and container state lost. Treat data deletion as separate approved recovery. Unreviewed Compose file runs critical service Hidden production platform appears. Require stack owner, state, exposure, backup, and rollback contract.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery may mean:

  • restart a failed container
  • restore previous daemon config
  • roll back to previous image digest
  • restore a named volume from backup
  • restore Docker bridge configuration
  • rebuild the host and redeploy workloads
  • migrate the workload to a scheduler or managed platform

If stateful volume data is lost, image rollback is not enough. Restore data first or decide that the workload is disposable.

For daemon configuration rollback, restore the previous /etc/docker/daemon.json, reload systemd, restart Docker, and validate workloads. For image rollback, use the previous approved digest, not a mutable tag.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Install Docker from trusted repositories.
  • Keep Docker and the host OS patched.
  • Limit Docker group membership.
  • Avoid privileged containers unless there is a documented host-integration reason.
  • Do not mount the Docker socket into ordinary application containers.
  • Keep registry credentials outside Compose files and shell history.
  • Do not publish real hostnames, registry URLs, image names, volume paths, private IP ranges, credentials, or production command output.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review Docker hosts after disk-pressure incidents, daemon or network changes, cleanup mistakes, socket-access changes, host patching, runner onboarding, and before a single-host workload becomes long-lived or business-critical.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• BuildKit Build Standard
  • Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Linux Server Baseline Standard

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when the TLS certificate for Harbor, or an equivalent self-hosted container registry, must be rotated on scheduled renewal, before expiry, after endpoint migration, or after certificate or key compromise.

For upstream detection and expiry tracking, see the TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook.

Core judgment:

A registry certificate is part of the delivery path. If clients stop trusting it, deployment recovery can stop too.

Harbor TLS failure can break docker login, CI pushes, Kubernetes image pulls, node scale-out, rollback that needs an older image, and incident recovery that depends on pulling a patched image. Treat rotation as a delivery-path change, not a cosmetic certificate swap.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before rotation, confirm:

  • registry owner, TLS owner, and rollback owner
  • maintenance window and expected reload impact
  • exact TLS termination point: Harbor, reverse proxy, ingress, or load balancer
  • new certificate, private key, and full chain in the required format
  • certificate SANs cover every registry endpoint clients use
  • private key matches the certificate
  • current certificate, key, and config backup location
  • client classes that must trust the certificate: developer machines, CI runners, Kubernetes nodes, image scanners, replication peers, and automation

Do not continue if the private key source, chain order, or termination point is unclear.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
• A wrong certificate or chain breaks login, pull, and push.
  • A mismatched private key prevents TLS termination from starting.
  • Missing intermediate certificates may affect only some clients.
  • Restarting the wrong component can interrupt registry, portal, jobservice, chart, scan, or replication flows.
  • Client trust-store changes may be required for internal CAs.

If the registry is used by production clusters, make sure current workloads are not depending on immediate image pulls during the reload window.

docs.title: Key Permission Rule
docs.text:
Some older notes suggest making certificate files broadly readable or world-writable to "make it work." Do not do this. A registry private key that is world-readable or world-writable is a credential leak.

Safe rule:

  • private key readable only by the service account or root-controlled process that needs it
  • private key mode 600 or 640 owned by the service group
  • certificate and chain mode 644
  • no world-writable permission on any TLS file
  • ownership matches the TLS-terminating process
# Private key: owner read/write only
sudo chmod 600 /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.key
sudo chown harbor:harbor /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.key

# Certificate and chain: readable is fine, writable is not
sudo chmod 644 /etc/harbor/cert/registry.example.com.crt

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
1. Identify the TLS termination point and the exact certificate/key paths or certificate-manager object.
  2. Validate the new certificate:
openssl x509 -in registry.example.com.crt -noout -issuer -subject -dates -ext subjectAltName
  1. Confirm the key matches the certificate.

Compare the certificate public key and private-key public key with SHA-256:

openssl x509 -in registry.example.com.crt -noout -pubkey \
  | openssl pkey -pubin -outform DER \
  | openssl dgst -sha256
openssl pkey -in registry.example.com.key -pubout -outform DER \
  | openssl dgst -sha256

The two SHA-256 digests must match.

  1. Check chain presentation from the served endpoint:
openssl s_client -connect registry.example.com:443 -servername registry.example.com -showcerts -verify_return_error </dev/null
  1. Back up the current certificate, key, chain, and relevant TLS config.
  2. Install the new certificate and key at the approved path or object.
  3. Apply restrictive permissions.
  4. Reload or restart only the TLS-terminating component where possible.
  5. Validate before announcing completion.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Served certificate:

openssl s_client -connect registry.example.com:443 -servername registry.example.com -showcerts -verify_return_error </dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -issuer -subject -dates -ext subjectAltName

Registry operations:

docker login registry.example.com
docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0

Validate the write path only through the same approved CI or release identity that normally publishes images, and only against a disposable validation project or repository. Do not run an ad hoc docker push to a production project from an operator shell as a certificate test.

Representative client trust:

# Run from a Kubernetes node or equivalent container-runtime host.
crictl pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0

# Run from the same runner class that publishes images.
docker pull registry.example.com/example/app:1.0.0

Pass conditions:

  • served certificate is the expected new certificate
  • SAN covers the registry hostname used by clients
  • full chain is trusted by intended clients
  • login succeeds
  • pull succeeds from a typical runtime client
  • push succeeds from a CI-like client
  • Kubernetes nodes or container runtime hosts can pull an image
  • CI runners can push and pull through the same endpoint they use in pipelines
  • no trust or chain errors appear in registry, proxy, node, or runner logs
  • certificate expiry tracking is updated

Use placeholder hostnames and image names in public docs. Do not publish real registry endpoints, project names, tokens, or credentials.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
Rollback:

  1. Restore the previous certificate, key, chain, and TLS config from the backup.
  2. Reapply restrictive permissions.
  3. Reload or restart the TLS-terminating component.
  4. Validate served certificate, login, pull, push, and node pull again.
  5. Keep rollback files until the observation window passes.

Do not roll back to a compromised private key unless an incident commander accepts the temporary risk and a replacement path is already active.

Escalate when:

  • private key may have leaked
  • clients still fail with a confirmed-good chain
  • internal CA trust store needs node or runner rollout
  • Kubernetes nodes cannot pull images
  • CI runners cannot push release images
  • replication or scanner integrations fail TLS validation

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Key does not match certificate TLS terminator fails or serves old cert. Compare public key before install. Missing intermediate Some clients reject the registry. Validate full chain from client classes. Node trust store not updated Pods fail to start or scale. Test from node/container runtime path. CI trust store not updated Pipelines cannot push release images. Test from CI-like client. World-readable private key Registry credential leak. Enforce restrictive ownership and mode. Whole platform restarted unnecessarily Rotation creates avoidable outage. Reload only TLS terminator where possible. Old key retained after compromise Revoked trust can be reused. Destroy old key after rollback window if compromised.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Private key permissions are restrictive and ownership matches the running service.
  • No certificate or key material was committed, pasted into a ticket, or sent in chat.
  • The new expiry date is recorded in certificate tracking.
  • Client trust-store work is closed or tracked with owner and deadline.
  • Temporary test image or tag is removed if not needed.
  • If rotation followed compromise, old key destruction or revocation is recorded.

docs.title: Related Standards
docs.text:
• Harbor Registry Standard
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Harbor or a similar private container registry as part of the production delivery path.

The core judgment:

A registry is not just image storage. It is the place where a release becomes pullable, attributable, scannable, and recoverable.

If Harbor loses identity, retention, access control, or restore evidence, deployment automation may still run, but it can no longer prove which image reached production or whether rollback will be able to pull the previous image.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Teams push private container images for deployment.
  • CI/CD, GitOps, Kubernetes nodes, or emergency rollback paths depend on Harbor.
  • Robot accounts, replication, retention, vulnerability scanning, or registry backup need a shared rule.
  • Harbor is used as a source for production Kubernetes image pulls.

Do not treat Harbor as a general file share, unmanaged backup store, or place for personal image publishing.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not define:

  • Application Dockerfile style.
  • Kubernetes deployment strategy.
  • Full incident response for compromised images.
  • TLS rotation details; use the Harbor certificate rotation runbook for that.

It covers registry operation, image trust, access, retention, and recovery.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before Harbor is used for production delivery, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Project ownership Which team owns each Harbor project and its retention rules? Unowned projects keep risky images forever or delete rollback images too early. Publishing authority Which CI identity may push release images? Laptop pushes and shared accounts break attribution. Image identity Which tag and digest are recorded in the release record? Tags are convenient; digests prove what was pulled. Access boundary Which users, robot accounts, CI jobs, and clusters can pull or push? Overbroad access turns a registry weakness into a production-change weakness. Scan policy Which vulnerability results block promotion, and who can accept exceptions? Scans that only log do not change delivery risk. Retention How long are release, rollback, and incident images kept? Cleanup can remove the only recoverable artifact. Backup and restore What metadata and blob storage are backed up and restore-tested? A registry backup is not useful until an exact image can be pulled after restore. Replication Which direction is authoritative, and how are conflicts handled? Replication can copy stale, deleted, or compromised artifacts if the authority model is unclear.

docs.title: Publish only through controlled automation
docs.text:
Production release images should be pushed by CI/CD or approved release automation. Human pushes to production projects should be exceptions with a recorded reason.

Each release image should record:

  • project and repository
  • immutable tag
  • digest
  • source revision
  • build pipeline identity
  • scan result or exception
  • publish time
  • promotion or approval reference

Do not promote an image that cannot be tied back to source, build, and validation evidence.

docs.title: Separate push, pull, and admin identities
docs.text:
Use named users for humans and scoped robot accounts for automation. Separate:

  • push identity used by CI
  • pull identity used by Kubernetes or deployment automation
  • replication identity
  • admin identity

Robot accounts must have narrow project scope, expiry where supported, and a rotation owner. Never publish robot account tokens, kubelet pull secrets, or registry credentials.

docs.title: Treat tags as labels and digests as proof
docs.text:
Tags can move unless policy prevents movement. Release records and rollback plans should capture the digest.

Recommended behavior:

  • use immutable release tags or policy checks that block overwrite
  • record digest after push
  • deploy or validate by digest where the platform supports it
  • alert or block when a release tag is overwritten

Rollback should pull the previous approved digest, not rebuild a tag and hope it is identical.

docs.title: Make scanning a gate, not decoration
docs.text:
Define which severity and exploitability levels block promotion. Exceptions must have an owner, expiry, and mitigation.

Scanning does not replace dependency governance, but it catches one class of obvious release risk. A scan that cannot stop promotion is only inventory.

docs.title: Protect retention windows
docs.text:
Retention must not delete images still needed for:

  • current production
  • rollback window
  • incident investigation
  • rebuild avoidance during an outage
  • regulated retention or audit

Apply retention per project and repository. Test the cleanup policy on non-production or report-only mode before enabling deletion.

docs.title: Back up metadata and blob storage together
docs.text:
Harbor recovery depends on both registry content and metadata:

  • image blobs and manifests
  • project settings
  • robot accounts and access configuration
  • scan metadata where required
  • replication configuration
  • audit or event logs where available

A restore drill should prove that an exact historical image digest can be pulled and that unauthorized push remains blocked after restore.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Use placeholder endpoints in public examples:

docker login <registry-host>
docker pull <registry-host>/<project>/<image>:<tag>
docker image inspect <registry-host>/<project>/<image>:<tag> --format '{{index .RepoDigests 0}}'

Pass conditions:

  • Login works only for approved identities.
  • Pull works for deployment identities and fails for unauthorized identities.
  • Write-path validation, when required, is performed only by an approved CI or release identity against a disposable test project or test repository, never by pushing a production image or tag from a generic operator shell.
  • Release image tag and digest are recorded.
  • Scan result is available and promotion policy is applied.
  • Retention policy keeps rollback images through the recovery window.
  • Backup restore can recover an exact image digest and project metadata.
  • Replication, if used, preserves the intended authority direction.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Shared push account Image changes cannot be attributed. Use CI or named release identities. Mutable release tag Rollback or incident review cannot prove the artifact. Record digest and block tag overwrite. Aggressive retention Previous image disappears during incident response. Protect rollback and investigation windows. Scan only logs Known vulnerable image reaches production. Make scan policy a promotion gate. Robot token leaks Unauthorized push or pull becomes possible. Scope, expire, rotate, and audit robot accounts. Backup excludes metadata Restored blobs cannot be used correctly. Back up blobs and Harbor metadata together. Replication has unclear authority Stale or compromised image propagates. Define source of truth and conflict handling. TLS or trust store breaks CI push, node pull, and rollback fail. Validate certificate chain from every delivery path.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Application rollback depends on Harbor in two ways:

  1. the previous image digest must still exist and be pullable
  2. the target cluster must still trust and reach the registry

Before closing a release, confirm the previous approved image remains inside the retention window. Before closing a Harbor restore, prove:

  • the exact digest pulls successfully
  • Kubernetes image pull succeeds from a representative node
  • CI can push only through approved identity
  • unauthorized push and pull are rejected
  • retention rules are restored and do not delete rollback images

If Harbor itself is unavailable, the release process should have an emergency pull-through, replica, or restored registry path that is narrower than normal access and recorded in the incident plan.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real registry domains, robot tokens, pull secrets, project names, or replication credentials.
  • Protect admin accounts with SSO/MFA where available.
  • Scope robot accounts by project and purpose.
  • Rotate credentials after suspected exposure, employee offboarding, or vendor access expiry.
  • Keep image signing, provenance, or admission controls aligned with registry policy when used.
  • Treat exports, backups, and replication config as sensitive internal topology.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • when adding a Harbor project
  • when adding a publisher, robot account, or replication target
  • after failed image pull, failed rollback, or registry outage
  • after changing retention, scanning, or backup policy
  • after credential exposure or unauthorized image activity
  • before relying on Harbor for a new production cluster

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Harbor Certificate Rotation Runbook
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook
  • BuildKit Build Standard
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard

docs.title: Delivery and rollback
docs.text:
GitOps, pipelines, release controls, and verifiable rollback capability.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.
