1 document Understand

Build the core mental model: how the components cooperate, why the system is designed this way, and where its boundaries are.

  • Rollback Is a Product Capability, Not a Command Explains why rollback depends on data compatibility, external behavior, automation state, and user-path recovery rather than only redeploying an older version.
2 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Argo CD GitOps Standard Standard for operating Argo CD as a GitOps controller — sync-policy by risk tier, secret handling, drift control, disaster recovery, and rollback.
  • Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard Standard for Jenkins pipelines that build and deploy to Kubernetes — immutable image tags, secret handling, gated promotion, verified rollout, and rollback.
1 document Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • Deployment Rollback Runbook Operational runbook for safely rolling back a Kubernetes Deployment, including trigger, impact check, commands, validation, escalation, and closure.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Argo CD as a GitOps deployment controller for Kubernetes: what belongs in Git, how aggressively to let Argo CD reconcile, and how to recover both an application and Argo CD itself.

The core judgment of this standard:

GitOps moves the risk from "who ran the command" to "what is in Git and how fast it reconciles." selfHeal and prune are not convenience flags — they decide whether a bad commit or a manual hotfix repairs itself or gets reverted under you. Choose them per risk tier, deliberately.

A healthy Argo CD setup has two clocks: the human review clock in Git and the reconciliation clock in the cluster. Most GitOps incidents happen when those clocks are treated as the same thing. A reviewed commit can still be wrong, and a fast reconciler can turn one wrong line into a fleet-wide outage before anyone reads the diff.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Deploy to Kubernetes and want Git to be the source of truth for cluster state.
  • Run, or plan to run, Argo CD as the reconciliation controller.
  • Need a defensible sync policy, secret-handling model, and recovery plan for GitOps.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • CI (building and testing artifacts). GitOps is the CD half; see the Jenkins-to-Kubernetes pipeline standard for the CI half.
  • Manifest templating choice (Helm vs Kustomize vs plain YAML) beyond how it interacts with sync.
  • Multi-cluster fleet design and ApplicationSets generators beyond the single-Application model shown here.

docs.title: GitOps Principles
docs.text:
1. Git is the desired state. Cluster changes flow through reviewed commits, not kubectl edit.
  2. Secrets are referenced through a secret-management process, never committed in plain text.
  3. Every Application has a deliberate sync policy matched to its risk tier.
  4. Drift is visible and handled by policy, not silently overwritten everywhere.
  5. Notifications expose status, not webhook secrets or tokens.

docs.title: Application Release Contract
docs.text:
Before an Application becomes production-managed by Argo CD, write down the release contract it implies:

Contract item Required decision Source of truth Which repo, branch, path, chart, or generated manifest is allowed to define production state. Review authority Who may approve changes to this path, and whether the approver also owns production impact. Destination boundary Which cluster, namespace, resource kinds, and AppProject allow-list this Application may touch. Reconciliation speed Whether sync is manual, automated, self-healing, or allowed to prune resources. Emergency path How to stop reconciliation, revert Git, or apply a temporary fix without fighting the controller. Secret boundary Which external secret mechanism is authoritative, and what must never appear in Git. Recovery evidence Which status, health, metric, and user-path checks prove the new desired state is safe.

If any row is unknown, the Application is not ready for automatic production sync. GitOps does not remove the need for release ownership; it makes missing ownership faster to expose.

docs.title: Installation Model
docs.text:
Use the official manifests or Helm chart. For production:

  • Run Argo CD in high-availability mode.
  • Keep Argo CD in its own namespace, separate from application namespaces.
  • Restrict who can create/modify Application and AppProject resources via RBAC.
  • Scope AppProject to the repos, destinations, and resource kinds each team may deploy — this is the real blast-radius control.
  • Keep cluster credentials, repo credentials, and admin tokens in the approved secret-management path, not in exported examples or shared runbooks.
  • Treat Argo CD upgrades as production delivery-system changes: back up config, check release notes, upgrade outside high-risk release windows, and verify existing Applications after the controller restarts.

Validation:

kubectl get pods -n argocd
kubectl get applications -n argocd
argocd app list

docs.title: Sync Policy by Risk Tier
docs.text:
This is the central decision. Match the policy to the service, not the convenience of the operator.

Tier automated selfHeal prune Use for Production / stateful off (manual sync) off off High-risk services; a human approves each sync. Standard service on on off Most stateless services; drift is auto-corrected, deletions stay manual. Mature, well-tested on on on Strong review + rollback; Git fully owns lifecycle including deletion.

Manual sync is not a sign of immaturity; for data-bearing or shared-infrastructure services it is often the correct way to keep the human approval clock slower than the controller clock. Automated sync is appropriate only when the team has already proven the validation and rollback loop outside the happy path.

apiVersion: argoproj.io/v1alpha1
kind: Application
metadata:
  name: example-service
  namespace: argocd
spec:
  project: example-team
  source:
    repoURL: https://git.example.com/example/example-repo.git
    targetRevision: main
    path: deploy/kubernetes
  destination:
    server: https://kubernetes.default.svc
    namespace: example-namespace
  syncPolicy:
    automated:
      prune: false      # deletions stay manual for this tier
      selfHeal: true    # revert out-of-band drift

Two flags worth understanding before you enable them:

  • selfHeal: true reverts any change made directly to the cluster. That is the point — but it also means an emergency kubectl hotfix will be undone within minutes. The correct emergency path is a commit, or temporarily disabling auto-sync, not fighting the controller.
  • prune: true lets Argo CD delete resources that disappear from Git. Powerful for lifecycle hygiene, dangerous if someone deletes a manifest by mistake. Reserve it for services with strong review.

docs.title: Secret Handling
docs.text:
Plain secrets must never live in Git. Choose one model and apply it consistently:

  • Sealed Secrets (encrypted in Git, decrypted in-cluster), or
  • An external secret operator pulling from a secret manager, or
  • A CSI secrets driver mounting from a vault.

Argo CD Application manifests themselves contain repo URLs, project names, and cluster references — treat exports as sensitive topology.

docs.title: Drift, Rollback, and Recovery
docs.text:
Drift is not always bad. A direct cluster change may be an emergency patch, an attacker, a failed controller, or a human bypass. The operating question is who is allowed to decide whether Git or the live cluster wins.

For production Applications:

  • Ignore differences only for fields that are known to be controller-owned, noisy, or safely externalized.
  • Alert on unexpected drift in production even when selfHeal will correct it.
  • Record who disabled auto-sync, why, and when it must be restored.
  • Keep a break-glass path narrower than normal access; emergency access should not become a second deployment system.

docs.title: Rollback an application
docs.text:
Because Git is the source of truth, rollback is a Git operation:

# Preferred: revert the commit, let Argo CD reconcile.
git revert <bad-commit> && git push

For Applications with automated sync or self-heal enabled, do not start with argocd app rollback. Argo CD cannot roll back an Application while automated sync is enabled, and a self-healing controller may move the live cluster back toward the bad Git state. Use one of these paths first:

  • revert or patch Git and let Argo CD reconcile to the recovered state, or
  • suspend automated sync through the platform-approved path, recording owner, reason, and restore deadline.

Only after the source of truth is controlled should you use a direct rollback as an emergency bridge:

argocd app history example-service
argocd app rollback example-service <revision>

A direct argocd app rollback is an emergency bridge, not the final state. Re-enable automation only after Git matches the recovered state and the application has passed service-level validation.

docs.title: Disaster recovery of Argo CD itself
docs.text:
Argo CD is a production dependency; plan for losing it.

The export contains sensitive deployment topology. Create it only into an approved encrypted/internal backup path with restrictive permissions, and do not attach, commit, or share the raw export.

umask 077
argocd admin export > <encrypted-internal-backup-path>/argocd-export-<yyyy-mm-dd>.yaml
# restore:
argocd admin import - < <encrypted-internal-backup-path>/argocd-export-<yyyy-mm-dd>.yaml

Because desired state lives in Git, a rebuilt Argo CD plus the Git repos can reconstruct the cluster — but only if the Application/AppProject definitions are also captured (in Git or in the export).

Do not treat the export as an ordinary backup artifact. It can expose repositories, projects, cluster destinations, and operational topology. Test import in an isolated environment before relying on it for incident recovery.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
argocd app get example-service
kubectl get applications -n argocd

Confirm:

  • Sync status is Synced and health is Healthy for changed apps.
  • The synced revision matches the intended Git commit.
  • No unexpected OutOfSync resources (drift) remain after sync.
  • For prune-enabled apps, nothing was deleted that you did not intend.
  • The AppProject allow-list still matches the intended team boundary.
  • The service-level smoke test or synthetic check passes after sync, not only Argo CD health.
  • Notifications route to the team that owns the release and the team that owns the platform controller.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Check / response App stuck OutOfSync Live drift, ignored diffs, or generated-manifest mismatch argocd app diff; reconcile or add ignoreDifferences deliberately. Sync fails RBAC, missing namespace, missing CRD, admission policy Read the sync error; create prerequisites; check AppProject allow-lists. Emergency fix keeps reverting selfHeal is reverting out-of-band changes Commit the fix, or temporarily disable auto-sync; don't fight the controller. Health stays Unknown Custom resource without health assessment Add a resource health customization. Repo connection fails Bad credentials, network, or unknown host key Re-check repo credentials and connectivity. Unexpected deletion prune: true plus a removed/misgenerated manifest Restore via Git revert; reconsider prune for that tier. Manual rollback keeps being undone Git still points at the bad desired state Revert or patch Git before re-enabling automation. Controller outage blocks delivery Argo CD dependency not included in release continuity plan Use the documented emergency path, then restore controller state and reconcile Git.

docs.title: Rollback (Operational Summary)
docs.text:
1. Prefer git revert of the offending commit; let Argo CD reconcile back to good state.
  2. If automated sync or self-heal is enabled, revert or patch Git first, or suspend automation with owner, reason, and deadline before any direct rollback.
  3. For emergency speed after the source of truth is controlled, argocd app rollback to a known-good revision, then reconcile Git to match.
  4. If Argo CD itself is the problem, disable auto-sync on critical apps to freeze the cluster while you recover the controller.
  5. Restore Argo CD from a protected export; verify apps return to Synced/Healthy.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never commit secrets, webhook URLs, or chat tokens in plain text.
  • Scope AppProject to allowed repos, destinations, and resource kinds — this bounds blast radius.
  • Use SSO and least-privilege RBAC; restrict who can sync production-tier apps.
  • Treat argocd admin export output as sensitive internal topology; store it encrypted.
  • Pin Argo CD versions and patch promptly; it has cluster-wide reconciliation power.
  • Review Application manifests as production-change authority, not as harmless deployment metadata.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/delivery team.
  • Re-review each Application's risk tier and sync flags on significant change.
  • Test Argo CD disaster recovery (export/import) on a cadence, not just on paper.
  • Re-check AppProject allow-lists when a team's deployment scope changes.
  • Review disabled auto-sync and drift exceptions as a recurring operational debt list.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Argo CD documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This runbook defines how to roll back a Kubernetes Deployment when a release causes user impact, reliability degradation, or operational risk.

The core judgment is:

A rollback is a controlled recovery action, not a panic command.

kubectl rollout undo is only one step. A safe rollback also freezes further change, captures current state, verifies that the previous version is healthy, prevents the bad version from being redeployed, and leaves enough evidence for a later fix.

The first question is not "which command rolls back?" It is "what state will still be true after the command?" If the release changed data shape, feature exposure, queue messages, client contracts, or the GitOps source of truth, an older ReplicaSet may start successfully while the product remains broken.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when any of the following happens after a deployment:

  • Error rate, latency, saturation, or restart count increases beyond the release guardrail.
  • Readiness or liveness failures affect serving capacity.
  • A critical user journey fails after release.
  • A dependency or configuration change makes the new pods unhealthy.
  • The release owner, incident commander, or service owner decides that recovery speed is more important than continuing the rollout.

Do not use rollback as a substitute for diagnosis when the current version is not related to the symptom. First confirm that the release is a plausible cause.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before executing rollback, confirm:

  • You know the namespace and deployment name.
  • You have permission to update the deployment.
  • The previous ReplicaSet or image revision is known or can be inferred from rollout history.
  • The previous version is expected to be safe.
  • The previous image still exists in the registry and the target nodes can pull it.
  • Data, configuration, feature flags, and external contracts are compatible with the previous version, or the release-specific recovery plan covers the gap.
  • No concurrent automation will immediately reapply the bad version.
  • Someone is watching application health while the rollback runs.

If the deployment is managed by GitOps, decide whether the rollback will be performed through Git revert, image tag change, or emergency kubectl operation followed by Git reconciliation.

docs.title: Recovery Boundary
docs.text:
Classify the rollback before touching the workload:

Boundary Meaning Decision Deployment-only The failed change is limited to image, command, env, or manifest fields. rollout undo, set image, or Git revert is usually safe after validation. Config or secret The same bad config may affect both old and new versions. Repair config/secret first; rollback may not recover. Data-writing The new version may already have written state the old version cannot read. Stop and use service-owner approval or release-specific recovery. Traffic/client Clients, queues, caches, or downstream services may have observed the new behavior. Validate user path and contract compatibility, not only pods. Automation GitOps, CI/CD, or an operator may reapply the bad state. Correct or suspend the source of truth before closing recovery.

This classification should take minutes, not become a meeting. The point is to avoid running a correct Kubernetes command against the wrong recovery model.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
Record impact before changing the workload:

kubectl -n <namespace> get deploy <deployment> -o wide
kubectl -n <namespace> get rs -l app=<app-label>
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -l app=<app-label> -o wide
kubectl -n <namespace> describe deploy <deployment>

Capture:

  • Current image or release version.
  • Desired, updated, available, and unavailable replicas.
  • Failing pod names.
  • Last deployment time.
  • Primary symptom and affected user path.

This does not need to delay recovery. A short command transcript is enough.

docs.title: Rollback Decision
docs.text:
Choose the action:

Condition Action New version is clearly causing impact and previous version is known good. Roll back immediately. Rollout is still progressing and only a small subset is affected. Pause rollout, verify impact, then roll back or resume. Bad config or secret caused all versions to fail. Fix config or secret; rollback alone will not recover. Database migration is incompatible with the previous app version. Stop and escalate; use the release-specific recovery plan. Unknown root cause but user impact is severe. Roll back if previous version is likely safe, then investigate.

If the rollback could make data incompatible, do not proceed without the service owner or incident commander.

docs.title: 1. Freeze further changes
docs.text:
Stop the automation that could reapply the bad release:

  • Stop or block the failing pipeline promotion.
  • Suspend or update GitOps automation according to the platform process.
  • Disable the feature flag or traffic exposure if that is the real source of user impact.

If a rollout is still progressing and you need a short decision window, you may pause it temporarily:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout pause deployment/<deployment>

Do not treat a paused Deployment as the normal rollback state. Resume or update the source of truth before executing the rollback path that should converge the workload. A manual rollback that GitOps immediately overwrites is not recovery.

docs.title: 2. Review rollout history
docs.text:
kubectl -n <namespace> rollout history deployment/<deployment>
kubectl -n <namespace> rollout history deployment/<deployment> --revision=<revision>

Confirm the target revision. If rollout history lacks useful change cause, identify the previous image only from ReplicaSets that belong to the target workload:

kubectl -n <namespace> get rs -l app=<app-label> -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.metadata.ownerReferences[*].name}{"\t"}{.spec.template.spec.containers[*].image}{"\n"}{end}'

Use only rows whose owner reference is the target Deployment. In a shared namespace, a namespace-wide ReplicaSet listing can point you at another service's image under pressure.

docs.title: 3. Execute rollback
docs.text:
If the Deployment was paused only to stop a progressing rollout, resume it before using rollout history:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout resume deployment/<deployment>

Rollback to the previous revision:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout undo deployment/<deployment>

Rollback to a specific revision:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout undo deployment/<deployment> --to-revision=<revision>

Then watch status:

kubectl -n <namespace> rollout status deployment/<deployment> --timeout=10m
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -l app=<app-label> -o wide

If rollout history is unreliable, or the release system tracks images more accurately than ReplicaSet revisions, set the Deployment to the previous known-good immutable image:

kubectl -n <namespace> set image deployment/<deployment> <container>=<image>:<previous-sha>
kubectl -n <namespace> rollout status deployment/<deployment> --timeout=10m

Use the path that matches your source of truth. In GitOps-managed services, the live operation is an emergency bridge; the Git state still must be corrected.

docs.title: 4. Verify service health
docs.text:
Validate both Kubernetes state and user-facing behavior:

kubectl -n <namespace> get deploy <deployment>
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -l app=<app-label>
kubectl -n <namespace> logs deploy/<deployment> --tail=100

Check the service dashboards for:

  • Error rate returning to baseline.
  • Latency returning to baseline.
  • Restart count stabilizing.
  • Successful readiness.
  • Successful business smoke test.
  • Ingress or gateway 5xx rate dropping.
  • Queue depth, background-job failures, or downstream error rate stabilizing if the release touched asynchronous paths.
  • Feature flags, caches, or client-visible behavior returning to the intended state.

docs.title: 5. Prevent redeployment of the bad version
docs.text:
After recovery, block the bad version from being applied again:

  • Revert or amend the GitOps commit.
  • Disable the failed pipeline promotion.
  • Mark the failed image or release as blocked in the release record.
  • Notify the service owner and platform owner.

Do not close the incident while the automation source still points at the bad release.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The rollback is successful only when all conditions are met:

Check Pass Condition Deployment status Desired replicas equal available replicas. Pods New pods are running the intended rollback image or revision. Readiness No sustained readiness failure. Logs The release-related error pattern has stopped or returned to baseline. Metrics Error rate, latency, saturation, and restart metrics are back within guardrail. User path The affected critical path passes a smoke test. Automation GitOps or CI/CD will not redeploy the bad version. Data and clients No known data compatibility, cache, queue, or client-contract issue remains unresolved.

docs.title: Rollback Is Not Enough When
docs.text:
Rollback alone may fail or be unsafe when:

  • The release included an irreversible or unreviewed database migration.
  • The failure is caused by an external dependency, not the application version.
  • A shared config, secret, or feature flag is broken across versions.
  • Data written by the new version is incompatible with the old version.
  • The previous image has been removed from the registry.
  • The failure is resource exhaustion caused by current traffic, not the release.
  • A controller, operator, or GitOps process will reconcile the system back to the failed state.
  • The product has already exposed a new contract to clients or downstream consumers that the old version cannot honor.

In these cases, escalate to the incident commander and service owner. The recovery path may require config repair, traffic shedding, feature flag disablement, database recovery, or image rebuild.

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text:
Escalate immediately when:

  • Rollback does not complete within the expected window.
  • Available replicas stay below the service minimum.
  • Error rate does not recover after the old version is running.
  • Data compatibility is uncertain.
  • Multiple services are affected.
  • You lack permission to update the deployment or source of truth.

Escalation should include namespace, deployment, failed version, target rollback version, current symptoms, and commands already executed.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
After recovery:

  • Record the failed version, rollback version, time to detect, and time to recover.
  • Attach the command transcript or dashboard links to the incident or change record.
  • Create a follow-up item for the root fix.
  • Add or tighten release guardrails if the failure escaped detection.
  • Review whether the deployment strategy, probes, or canary policy should change.
  • Confirm the bad version is blocked in the source of truth and cannot be redeployed by the next routine pipeline run.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines a safe Jenkins pipeline pattern for building container images and deploying them to Kubernetes: how to keep builds reproducible, how to handle credentials, and how to make production deploys gated, verified, and reversible.

The core judgment of this standard:

The pipeline's job is not "to deploy" — it is to make every deploy reproducible, attributable, and reversible. The single decision that delivers most of that is using an immutable image tag (the commit SHA), so what you tested is exactly what you ship and exactly what you can roll back to.

A green Jenkins build is weak evidence unless it preserves custody: source commit, build inputs, image digest, scan result, approval, target environment, rollout result, and rollback artifact. Without that custody chain, the pipeline can be fast and still be unable to answer the only incident question that matters: "What exactly changed?"

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Build container images and deploy to Kubernetes from Jenkins.
  • Need a consistent stage model, credential model, and rollback path across services.
  • Want CI (this standard) to hand off to GitOps CD, or to deploy directly with verification.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • GitOps reconciliation — if Argo CD owns cluster state, the pipeline pushes the image and updates Git, and Argo CD deploys (see the Argo CD GitOps standard).
  • Jenkins controller/agent infrastructure and scaling.
  • Build-tool specifics for a given language.

docs.title: Pipeline Stages
docs.text:
Use a predictable stage model so failures are localized and promotion is explicit:

checkout → test → build image → scan → push image →
deploy to staging → smoke test → manual approval → deploy to production → verify

Each stage must fail the build on error; a failing test or image scan must stop promotion, not warn and continue.

docs.title: Release Contract
docs.text:
Every production pipeline must emit or link to a compact release record:

Contract item Required evidence Source Git repository, commit SHA, branch or tag, and build number. Artifact Immutable image tag and digest, plus the registry location that production can still pull during rollback. Quality gates Test result, image scan result, policy exception if any, and who accepted the exception. Target Kubernetes context, namespace, deployment, and the account used to deploy. Approval Human approval, GitOps PR approval, or change window reference. Runtime proof Rollout status, pod image digest, readiness, metrics, and smoke-test result. Recovery proof Previous known-good artifact, rollback command or Git revert, and the automation source that must be corrected.

If the release record cannot prove the artifact identity and target boundary, do not promote to production. A missing record is not a documentation inconvenience; it means the change cannot be reconstructed under incident pressure.

docs.title: Immutable image tags
docs.text:
Tag images with the Git commit SHA (and optionally a release tag), never a moving tag like latest. A moving tag means "what ran in staging" and "what runs in prod" can silently differ, and rollback has nothing precise to point at.

Record the image digest after push and validate the deployed pod image against the intended tag or digest. Tags are human-friendly; digests are the stronger proof when a registry or rebuild path is under investigation.

docs.title: Credential handling
docs.text:
Store all credentials in Jenkins credentials or an external secret manager — never hardcoded in Jenkinsfile, scripts, or docs:

  • Git, registry, Kubernetes deploy, notification, and cloud credentials.
  • Inject at the stage that needs them via Jenkins credential bindings or a native registry wrapper.
  • Never print secret values. If a CLI requires stdin, disable shell tracing and keep command output free of credential material.

docs.title: Verified, gated promotion
docs.text:
• Deploy to staging and run a real smoke test before any production stage.
  • Gate production behind a manual approval (or a GitOps PR), recording who approved.
  • After the production rollout, verify — do not declare success on "kubectl applied".

docs.title: Direct deploy or GitOps handoff
docs.text:
Choose one production authority per service:

  • Direct deploy: Jenkins changes the live Kubernetes workload and must own rollout status, rollback, and source-of-truth correction.
  • GitOps handoff: Jenkins builds and pushes the image, then opens or updates the Git change that Argo CD will reconcile. Jenkins must not also mutate the same production Deployment directly.

Mixing both paths without an explicit emergency rule creates a recovery trap: Jenkins rolls back the live Deployment, then GitOps replays the failed desired state.

docs.title: Example Jenkinsfile skeleton
docs.text:
pipeline {
  agent any
  environment {
    IMAGE             = 'registry.example.com/example/app'
    DEPLOYMENT        = 'example-service'
    APP_LABEL         = 'app=example-service'
    STAGING_CONTEXT   = 'staging-context'
    STAGING_NAMESPACE = 'example-namespace-staging'
    PROD_CONTEXT      = 'prod-context'
    PROD_NAMESPACE    = 'example-namespace'
  }
  stages {
    stage('Checkout') { steps { checkout scm } }
    stage('Test')     { steps { sh 'make test' } }
    stage('Build')    { steps { sh 'docker build -t ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT} .' } }
    stage('Scan')     { steps { sh 'trivy image --exit-code 1 --severity HIGH,CRITICAL ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}' } }
    stage('Push') {
      steps {
        script {
          docker.withRegistry('https://registry.example.com', 'registry-credentials') {
            sh 'docker push ${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}'
          }
        }
        sh '''
          set -eu
          skopeo inspect docker://${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT} --format '{{.Digest}}' > image-digest.txt
        '''
        archiveArtifacts artifacts: 'image-digest.txt', fingerprint: true
      }
    }
    stage('Deploy staging') {
      steps {
        withCredentials([file(credentialsId: 'kubeconfig-staging', variable: 'KUBECONFIG')]) {
          sh '''
            set -eu
            test "$(kubectl config current-context)" = "${STAGING_CONTEXT}"
            kubectl -n "${STAGING_NAMESPACE}" set image deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} app=${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}
            kubectl -n "${STAGING_NAMESPACE}" rollout status deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} --timeout=5m
            kubectl -n "${STAGING_NAMESPACE}" get pods -l "${APP_LABEL}" -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[*].imageID}{"\n"}{end}'
          '''
        }
        sh 'make smoke-test ENV=staging'
      }
    }
    stage('Approve') { steps { input message: 'Promote to production?' } }
    stage('Deploy production') {
      steps {
        withCredentials([file(credentialsId: 'kubeconfig-production', variable: 'KUBECONFIG')]) {
          sh '''
            set -eu
            test "$(kubectl config current-context)" = "${PROD_CONTEXT}"
            kubectl -n "${PROD_NAMESPACE}" set image deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} app=${IMAGE}:${GIT_COMMIT}
            kubectl -n "${PROD_NAMESPACE}" rollout status deployment/${DEPLOYMENT} --timeout=5m
            kubectl -n "${PROD_NAMESPACE}" get pods -l "${APP_LABEL}" -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[*].imageID}{"\n"}{end}'
          '''
        }
      }
    }
  }
}

The image scan (trivy ... --exit-code 1) blocks promotion on HIGH/CRITICAL vulnerabilities — a scan that only logs is theater.

docs.title: Pre-Production Deploy Checklist
docs.text:
Before the production stage:

  • Image tag is immutable (SHA) and is the exact artifact tested in staging.
  • Image digest is recorded after push and visible in the release record.
  • The target Kubernetes context and namespace are correct.
  • The Deployment has readiness probes and a sane rollout strategy (maxUnavailable/maxSurge).
  • The rollback command is known and tested.
  • The previous known-good image still exists in the registry and is pullable by the target cluster.
  • Any database migration, feature flag, or config change has an explicit reversibility decision.
  • Monitoring dashboard and alert ownership exist for the service.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deployment/example-service --timeout=5m
kubectl -n example-namespace get pods -l app=example-service
kubectl -n example-namespace get pods -l app=example-service -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{.spec.containers[*].image}{"\t"}{.status.containerStatuses[*].imageID}{"\n"}{end}'

Confirm:

  • Rollout completed; new pods are Ready with the expected image SHA.
  • Runtime imageID matches the recorded registry digest, or the workload was deployed by digest directly.
  • Smoke test passed in staging; post-deploy checks (error rate, latency) are within threshold in production.
  • No crash-looping or readiness failures after the rollout window.
  • The deployment target is the intended cluster and namespace, not a similarly named environment.
  • If GitOps owns the service, the Git desired state now points at the same artifact that was verified.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Staging ≠ production Works in staging, breaks in prod Moving tag (latest) Use immutable SHA tags end to end. Credentials leaked Secret in logs/repo Hardcoded in Jenkinsfile/echoed Use withCredentials; rotate the exposed secret. "Deployed" but broken Pipeline green, service failing Declared success on apply, not verified Use rollout status + smoke/post-deploy checks. Scan ignored Vulnerable image shipped Scan logs but doesn't fail build Make the scan fail on HIGH/CRITICAL. Rollout hangs Stuck at rollout status Bad readiness probe or insufficient capacity Roll back; fix probe/capacity in staging. Wrong target changed Unexpected namespace or cluster modified Context injected implicitly or reused from an agent Print and verify context/namespace before production actions; scope credentials. Rollback image unavailable ImagePullBackOff during rollback Previous image was garbage-collected or tag was mutable Retain rollback artifacts and validate pull access before promotion. GitOps replays failure Manual rollback succeeds, then bad version returns Git desired state still points at failed artifact Revert or patch Git before closing recovery. Data change is forward-only Old app starts but corrupts or rejects new state Migration/config/feature flag not covered by release contract Stop rollback and use the release-specific recovery plan.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout undo deployment/example-service
kubectl -n example-namespace rollout status deployment/example-service --timeout=5m
  1. Because tags are immutable SHAs, rolling back means redeploying the previous known-good SHA (or rollout undo to the prior ReplicaSet).
  2. If the pipeline updates Git (GitOps), roll back by reverting the commit so the controller does not re-apply the bad version.
  3. Confirm the previous image can still be pulled before declaring rollback available.
  4. Verify after rollback exactly as after a deploy — confirm pods, image SHA or digest, source of truth, and service health.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Keep all credentials in Jenkins/secret manager; never in Jenkinsfile, scripts, or notifications.
  • Scope deploy credentials to the target namespace; do not use cluster-admin in pipelines.
  • Block promotion on image scans; pin base images.
  • Do not include webhook URLs, tokens, or sensitive logs in notification templates — notify with job, build, commit, environment, status, and a runbook link only.
  • Treat Jenkins logs and artifacts as audit material; mask secrets, restrict retention access, and avoid storing raw kubeconfigs.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the delivery/platform team.
  • Re-review the gate and scan thresholds when risk tolerance changes.
  • Confirm rollback works (test it) on a cadence, not just on paper.
  • Re-check deploy-credential scope when namespaces or clusters change.
  • Sample recent release records and verify that artifact, approval, target, and recovery evidence can be reconstructed without asking the person who ran the job.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Problem: reverting an image is not business recovery
docs.text:
kubectl rollout undo can restore a historical Pod template, but a bad change may already have crossed the container boundary. If a database contains new state, queues contain a new event format, notifications have been sent, and GitOps still declares the new release, reverting the image can create a second failure.

mindmap root((Product recovery capability)) Application artifacts Images and binaries Charts and dependencies Configuration ConfigMap and Secret Feature flags Database Schema Written data Messages and jobs Event formats Queues and offsets Caches and indexes Cache keys Search indexes External side effects Payments Notifications Webhooks Client compatibility API contracts Mobile versions GitOps source of truth Git Pipelines IaC state Business outcome Critical journeys Data integrity SLOs

Every branch can hold authoritative state. Restoring only the image leaves a split system: old code operating on new facts.

docs.title: Core definition
docs.text:
Rollback capability is the ability to choose the lowest-risk action after a bad change has partially taken effect and return critical user journeys to a known acceptable state.

There are five distinct actions:

Action What it solves Typical prerequisite Rollback Restore an older artifact or configuration The old version still understands current data and protocols Disable Stop new writes or side effects A feature flag controls reads, writes, and external calls Recover Restore an authoritative state from a healthy replica or backup The recovery point and RPO/RTO are approved Compensate Correct an external or business fact that already occurred The operation is idempotent, auditable, and replayable Forward Fix Keep current state and repair it with a small change Reverse migration is riskier than a forward repair

docs.title: Decision mechanism
docs.text:
flowchart TD A[Bad change confirmed] --> B{Can further spread be stopped now} B -- Yes --> C[Disable writes or remove traffic] B -- No --> D[Freeze changes and escalate] C --> E{Can the old version read current data and messages} E -- Yes --> F{Did external side effects occur} E -- No --> G{Is there a healthy replica or backup within RPO} F -- No --> H[Rollback] F -- Yes --> I[Rollback or Disable plus Compensate] G -- Yes --> J[Recover] G -- No --> K[Forward Fix] H --> L[Update the GitOps source of truth] I --> L J --> L K --> L L --> M[Validate user journeys and data]

The order matters:

  1. Contain first by disabling writes, removing traffic, or pausing consumers.
  2. Identify compatibility and authority: can old code read new state, and which copy is authoritative?
  3. Choose the action and update both runtime and declarative state.
  4. Validate outcomes, not only Pods and HTTP probes.

docs.title: Worked case: why an image-only rollback fails
docs.text:
Assume order service v2 is live:

  • the schema was expanded and remains readable by v1;
  • v2 produced a new event format;
  • a consumer sent customer notifications;
  • GitOps still declares v2;
  • no payment occurred, but notifications are external facts.
sequenceDiagram participant G as GitOps participant A as Application v2 participant D as Database participant Q as Queue participant N as Notification service G->>A: Deploy v2 A->>D: Write compatible extension fields A->>Q: Produce new event format Q->>N: Send notification Note over N: External fact already exists A-->>G: Operator reverts only the image G->>A: Reconcile back to v2

The safe response is:

  1. disable the writer feature flag;
  2. change the GitOps source to the approved version;
  3. confirm that v1 reads the expanded schema;
  4. drain or isolate events with the new format through a compatible consumer;
  5. execute a defined customer-compensation workflow for incorrect notifications;
  6. validate ordering, reads, queue lag, and notification state.

docs.title: Boundaries and irreversible points
docs.text:
A direct rollback is usually no longer the default after:

  • a destructive schema migration removed old fields or constraints;
  • the new version wrote a format that the old version cannot parse;
  • uncontrolled clients adopted a new protocol;
  • payments, refunds, messages, or third-party resources were created;
  • multiple data sources contain unique writes;
  • GitOps, Terraform, or a release controller will recreate the bad state.

Design for recovery before release with immutable artifacts, expand-contract migrations, overlapping message/API compatibility, write-controlling feature flags, idempotency keys, compensating actions, synchronized GitOps state, and user-centered validation.

docs.title: Counterexamples: when not to roll back
docs.text: Situation Unsafe reaction Safer direction New data uses an irreversible format Force-start the old version Forward Fix or recover data Database authority is unknown Enable writes on several candidates Freeze writes, preserve evidence, identify authority A third-party payment completed Revert code and retry Contain and run idempotent compensation GitOps still declares the bad release Patch the Deployment by hand Fix the source of truth first A small repair is available quickly Reverse several systems Controlled forward repair

docs.title: Actionable next step: the release recovery contract
docs.text:
Before release, answer:

  1. Which components support Rollback, Disable, Recover, Compensate, or Forward Fix?
  2. Can the previous version read every state and event the new version can create?
  3. Which action crosses an irreversible boundary?
  4. What is the authoritative source for GitOps, configuration, database, and messages?
  5. Which user journeys, data samples, and operating signals prove recovery?
  6. What condition stops the attempt and escalates it to a data or business incident?

A release without these answers is not rollback-capable.

docs.title: Related standards and runbooks
docs.text:
• Deployment rollback runbook
  • Kubernetes cluster provisioning standard
  • Argo CD GitOps standard

docs.title: Official references
docs.text:
• Kubernetes: Rolling Back a Deployment
  • Kubernetes: kubectl rollout undo
  • Argo Rollouts: Rollback windows

docs.title: Documentation templates
docs.text:
Reusable templates for migration plans, standards, and runbooks.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

3 documents Reference

Look up versions, commands, parameters, templates, and verified official facts.

  • Migration Plan Template Public-safe migration plan template for production changes, including scope, risk, validation, rollback, communication, and post-migration review.
  • Runbook Template Production runbook template for alerts, incidents, and operational procedures with triage, decision paths, validation, rollback, and closure criteria.
  • Standard Document Template Template for DevOps standards that require purpose, architecture, product placement, procedure, validation, failure modes, rollback, and review.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this template to plan a production migration before any irreversible network, data, platform, or application cutover.

The core judgment:

A migration plan is a decision record for risk, not a task list for copying resources.

It should make the target state, safety checks, rollback boundary, and ownership clear enough that another engineer can review the plan without knowing the original environment.

docs.title: How to Use
docs.text: Fill every section with public-safe placeholders. Do not include real customer names, domains, IP addresses, account IDs, bucket names, credentials, private repository URLs, or raw production evidence.

docs.title: Migration Summary
docs.text: Field Value Migration name <short-name> Owner <team-or-role> Migration family Network / storage / database / application / platform / mixed Source state <generic-source-state> Target state <generic-target-state> Planned window <window> Rollback decision owner <role>

docs.title: Background
docs.text:
Explain why the migration is needed:

  • Current limitation.
  • Target outcome.
  • Business or reliability driver.
  • Constraints that shape the design.

Do not start with commands. Start with the operational problem.

docs.title: In Scope
docs.text:
• Systems or data included.
  • Traffic paths included.
  • Teams affected.
  • Validation responsibilities.

docs.title: Out of Scope
docs.text:
• Systems intentionally excluded.
  • Follow-up work that must not block the migration.
  • Adjacent modernization that is not part of this cutover.

docs.title: Current State
docs.text:
Describe the current architecture with generic labels:

  • Components.
  • Data flows.
  • Ownership.
  • Known risks.
  • Current monitoring.
  • Current rollback path.

docs.title: Target State
docs.text:
Describe the intended state:

  • Components.
  • Data flows.
  • Ownership changes.
  • Security changes.
  • Monitoring changes.
  • Decommission plan for source resources.

docs.title: Risk Register
docs.text: Risk Probability Impact Detection Mitigation Rollback Trigger Example: DNS clients keep old endpoint Medium High Resolver test and traffic logs Lower TTL before cutover More than expected traffic remains on old path

docs.title: Migration Sequence
docs.text:
1. Prepare target environment.
  2. Validate target access and observability.
  3. Sync data or configuration.
  4. Run pre-cutover smoke tests.
  5. Freeze or drain source writes if required.
  6. Execute cutover.
  7. Run post-cutover validation.
  8. Observe for the agreed window.
  9. Decide rollback, continue, or complete.
  10. Decommission source only after rollback window ends.

docs.title: Validation Plan
docs.text: Layer Check Expected Result Owner Platform Target resources are healthy. No critical platform errors. <role> Network Required paths work and denied paths fail. Route behavior matches plan. <role> Data Counts, size, checksum, or sample reads match. Drift is within accepted threshold. <role> Application Critical user path passes. Smoke test succeeds. <role> Monitoring Alerts and dashboards reflect target. Signals are visible. <role>

docs.title: Rollback Plan
docs.text:
Define:

  • Rollback trigger.
  • Rollback decision owner.
  • Latest safe rollback time.
  • Steps to return traffic or reads to source.
  • How writes are reconciled.
  • Data that must be preserved.
  • Conditions where rollback is unsafe.

If rollback requires restoring data, state the restore source and expected recovery time.

docs.title: Communication Plan
docs.text:
Include:

  • Change announcement.
  • Maintenance window.
  • Status update cadence.
  • Escalation channel.
  • Completion notice.
  • Rollback notice template.

docs.title: Post-Migration Review
docs.text:
After completion, record:

  • What changed.
  • What validation proved.
  • What failed or surprised the team.
  • Follow-up actions.
  • Decommission status.
  • Monitoring or runbook updates.

docs.title: Quality Gate
docs.text:
The migration plan is not ready if:

  • Rollback trigger is vague.
  • Validation checks are only infrastructure-level and ignore user paths.
  • Data ownership is unclear.
  • Source deletion happens before rollback window.
  • The plan contains environment-specific secrets or identifiers.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
  • Object Storage Migration Case
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this template for alerts, incidents, and repeatable operational procedures.

The core judgment:

A runbook is a decision path under pressure, not a command archive.

It should help an operator decide what is happening, what is safe to do, when to stop, and how to prove recovery.

docs.title: Runbook Summary
docs.text: Field Value Runbook name <name> Service or platform area <area> Owner <team-or-role> Severity range <severity> Last reviewed <date> Escalation owner <role>

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
State exactly when to use this runbook:

  • Alert name.
  • User symptom.
  • Maintenance task.
  • Operational request.
  • Manual detection condition.

docs.title: Impact
docs.text:
Describe possible impact:

  • User impact.
  • Data impact.
  • Platform impact.
  • Security impact.
  • Blast radius.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
List what the operator needs before acting:

  • Access.
  • Tools.
  • Target namespace, host, cluster, or account.
  • Required approval.
  • Backup or snapshot requirement.
  • Known rollback owner.

docs.title: Initial Triage
docs.text:
Use commands that identify the target and risk before changing state:

example status command
example describe command
example recent-events command

Record current state, current symptom, and whether users are affected.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Symptom Check Likely Cause Next Path Example symptom Example check Example cause Path A

docs.title: Path A: Low-Risk Recovery
docs.text:
Use when impact is limited and the action is reversible.

  1. Step.
  2. Validate.
  3. Continue or escalate.

docs.title: Path B: High-Risk Recovery
docs.text:
Use when data, security, or broad availability is at risk.

  1. Stop and notify owner.
  2. Preserve evidence.
  3. Execute approved mitigation.
  4. Validate.
  5. Decide rollback or escalation.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Define healthy evidence:

example validation command

Pass conditions:

  • Service health recovered.
  • Error or symptom cleared.
  • User path works.
  • No new critical alert.
  • Owner accepts the result.

docs.title: Rollback or Escalation
docs.text:
State:

  • What can be rolled back.
  • What cannot be rolled back.
  • When to escalate.
  • Who approves risky actions.
  • What evidence to attach.

docs.title: Closure Criteria
docs.text:
Close only when:

  • Triggering alert is resolved or accepted.
  • Validation passes.
  • User impact is over.
  • Follow-up owner and due date exist.
  • Incident or change record is updated.

docs.title: Post-Incident Follow-Up
docs.text:
Record:

  • Root cause or suspected cause.
  • Prevention action.
  • Monitoring gap.
  • Runbook improvement.
  • Owner.
  • Due date.

docs.title: Quality Gate
docs.text:
The runbook is not ready if it:

  • Starts with destructive commands.
  • Has no impact check.
  • Has no rollback or escalation boundary.
  • Does not define healthy state.
  • Assumes one environment-specific target.
  • Contains secrets or real operational identifiers.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Standard Document Template
  • Migration Plan Template
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this template for public DevOps standards and operational references.

A standard should explain the operating decision and system boundary, not only document commands.

A complete standard helps the reader understand when to use the guidance, how the system is structured, how it fits into a product, how to validate it, and where it can fail.

docs.title: Title
docs.text:
Use a literal, searchable title:

<Technology or Capability> Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Explain:

  • What problem the standard solves.
  • Which operational decision it helps.
  • The core judgment in one sentence.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text: List situations where the standard applies. Include enough detail for the reader to self-select.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: State what the standard does not cover.

docs.title: Architecture and Visual Model
docs.text:
Use diagrams when topology, ownership, traffic flow, data flow, migration, failure propagation, or change sequencing matters.

A substantial standard should normally include:

  1. Architecture or topology view — components, control plane, data plane, ownership, and evidence path.
  2. Decision, sequence, or recovery flow — how the system changes state and where it must stop.
flowchart LR User[User or operator] --> Control[Control plane] Control --> Runtime[Runtime system] Runtime --> Evidence[Metrics, logs, and change evidence] Inventory[Asset and ownership catalog] --> Control

Diagrams must explain an engineering relationship. Do not add decorative images that do not improve understanding.

docs.title: Product Integration
docs.text:
Explain how the standard fits into a DevOps or platform product.

Product capability Managed object Required behavior Asset inventory System, owner, environment, dependency Establish ownership and scope. Change management Revision, plan, approval, rollback window Coordinate execution and decision authority. Identity and access Runtime identity and access reference Enforce least privilege. Observability Metrics, logs, health, evidence Prove readiness and detect regression. Recovery Known-good state and recovery owner Make failure response executable.

Use only relevant rows and add domain objects such as topics, state keys, certificates, routes, databases, or clusters.

docs.title: Recommended Standard
docs.text:
Describe:

  • Ownership.
  • Architecture or process boundary.
  • Required controls.
  • Safe defaults.
  • Exceptions.
  • Trade-offs.

docs.title: Procedure
docs.text:
Present operational steps in a safe order:

  1. Prepare.
  2. Capture baseline.
  3. Execute.
  4. Validate.
  5. Observe.
  6. Roll back or close.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Define how to prove success:

  • Commands.
  • Metrics.
  • Logs.
  • User journeys.
  • Expected healthy state.
  • Observation window.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Example failure Example result Example prevention

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Explain:

  • What is reversible.
  • What is not safely reversible.
  • Who approves rollback.
  • What state or data must be preserved.
  • When to escalate.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text: Describe access, exposure, audit, data sensitivity, and public-documentation redaction boundaries using placeholders rather than real environment values.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: State when the standard must be reviewed: after incidents, upgrades, ownership changes, or new workload classes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text: Link to nearby standards, runbooks, cases, and templates.

docs.title: Quality Gate
docs.text:
The page is not ready if it:

  • Is only an installation note.
  • Has no reader task.
  • Has no architecture or visual model when topology or change sequencing matters.
  • Does not explain product-module placement or operational ownership.
  • Has no validation or failure modes.
  • Has no rollback or escalation boundary where risk exists.
  • Contains real environment identifiers.
  • Exists in only one language.

docs.title: Engineering collaboration
docs.text:
Handoffs, risk communication, and technical writing for incident conditions.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

3 documents Understand

Build the core mental model: how the components cooperate, why the system is designed this way, and where its boundaries are.

  • Collaboration Is Takeover-Ability Collaboration is not meetings or sync. It is whether someone who is not you can safely change what you shipped while you are not there. This essay reframes collaboration as the takeover-ability of an artifact, and distinguishes it from the topology seam between technical layers.
  • Translating Risk in Both Directions An engineer's core communication skill is not explaining clearly. It is bidirectional translation of risk — turning technical fact into consequence for the business, and turning organizational pressure back into the specific constraint that cannot be relaxed. This essay argues most engineers do only one direction, or neither.
  • Writing for the Reader at 3 a.m. Good engineering writing is not measured by a calm reader who wants to learn. It is measured by a degraded reader mid-incident at 3 a.m. This essay inverts the goals of technical writing — against completeness, against elegant introductions — toward effectiveness on a person in their worst cognitive state.

docs.title: Collaboration Is Takeover-Ability
docs.text:
There is a quiet test that every system you ship eventually takes, and you are never in the room when it happens. You are on a plane, on leave, asleep, or gone to another company. Something you built breaks, or needs to change. A person who is not you sits down in front of your code, your pipeline, your Terraform, your dashboard — and tries to change it safely without you. Whether they can is the entire measure of how well you collaborated. Everything else we call collaboration — the standups, the sync meetings, the "let's get aligned" — is rehearsal for that moment, and most of it does not actually prepare for it.

This is the reframe: collaboration is not an activity you perform with people who are present. It is a property of the artifacts you leave behind for people who are absent. Your collaboration capability is the takeover-ability of what you ship — how safely someone who is not you can change it while you are not there.

docs.title: This is not the topology argument
docs.text:
It would be easy to read this as a restatement of the Capability Topology, which says failures live in the seams between layers. It is not, and the difference is the whole point.

Topology is about contracts between technical layers: the pipeline meets the cluster, observability is supposed to feed delivery, the platform is supposed to encode judgment. The actors there are systems. The seam is the undocumented assumption between two pieces of machinery, and the failure is a service that degrades because nobody designed the handoff between two technical concerns.

This essay is about a different seam: the one between two people across time. The actors here are not systems; they are you and the person who inherits your artifact. The contract is not "how does the pipeline hand off to the cluster," it is "what did the author know that the artifact does not say, and that the next person will have to rediscover under pressure." A perfectly designed technical seam — layers that integrate flawlessly — can still be a collaboration disaster if the integration only works because one specific human holds the context. Topology asks whether the layers connect; this asks whether a person can take over the connection without the original author. You can pass one and fail the other, and the fixes are different in kind: topology debt is an architecture problem, takeover debt is a knowledge-residence problem.

docs.title: Where knowledge lives is the real variable
docs.text:
The takeover-ability of an artifact is decided by one thing: where the knowledge required to change it safely lives. It lives in exactly one of two places. Either it is in the artifact — readable in the code, the comments, the docs, the structure, the tests — or it is in a human head. Every piece of context in the second category is a collaboration debt, payable at the worst possible time by someone who does not know they owe it.

This is why the symptom of bad collaboration is never "we don't meet enough." It is always a specific scene, and the people who have carried production work through handoff recognize all three immediately.

The code only its author can change. It runs fine. It has been in production for a year. But it is structured around a model that exists only in the author's memory — the variable names are abbreviations only they expand, the control flow encodes business rules nobody wrote down, the one function that looks deletable is load-bearing for a reason discoverable only by deleting it and watching production break. A new engineer can read every line and still not be able to change it safely, because reading the lines does not recover the model. This code is not an asset. It is a hostage situation with the author as the ransom.

The deploy that needs the one person who knows. The release "works," but the real procedure has steps that live nowhere — the flag you flip first, the cache you warm, the one service you restart in a specific order, the thing you check before you proceed. None of it is written, because the person who does it has done it forty times and it is obvious to them. The danger is that this looks like competence: the team trusts that person because only they can do it, and rewards the very dependency it should be dissolving. A deploy that one person can always do and no one else can ever learn is not hard-won judgment being applied; it is hard-won judgment being trapped in one person's memory. (The maturity essay reaches the same place from the other side: a process that only works when its author is present is not maturity, it is single-person dependency with better documentation.)

The doc that assumes the context only the writer has. This is the subtlest, because it fails while looking finished. The document is well-written and complete to the author, who reads it through the full context already in their head and sees a clear, sufficient page. The next person reads the identical words without that context and falls into every gap — and here is the trap that makes it nearly impossible to self-correct: the author cannot see the gaps, because seeing them would require un-knowing what they know, and no one can read their own writing as a stranger. The completeness is an illusion projected by the reader who needs it least. (This is the same blindness the reader at 3 a.m. page attacks from the operational side — a document is only proven by a reader who does not share the author's head.)

docs.title: Why we mistake activity for collaboration
docs.text:
The reason this stays invisible is that the activity we call collaboration is legible and the property that is collaboration is not. Meetings are visible, schedulable, and feel like teamwork. Takeover-ability is invisible until someone actually attempts the takeover, which by definition happens when the author is absent and cannot be graded on it.

So organizations optimize the visible proxy. They add sync meetings, alignment sessions, and status updates — and feel more collaborative while shipping artifacts that are less takeover-able than ever, because all the context transfer happened verbally in rooms and none of it landed in the artifact. A team can be in constant communication and produce a codebase nobody but the original authors can safely touch. The meetings transferred context between the people who were present; they did nothing for the person who joins next year, which is the person collaboration actually has to serve.

The honest test cuts past the activity entirely. It is not "do we communicate well." It is: if this person went on leave for a month with no contact, what could not be safely changed until they came back? Every artifact on that list is a collaboration failure with that person's name on it, no matter how good they are in meetings. And the most dangerous version is the engineer who is brilliant and generous in person — always happy to explain, always available — because their helpfulness is exactly what lets the artifact stay unowned by anyone else. They are not collaborating. They are making themselves necessary, and calling it teamwork.

docs.title: The takeover test
docs.text:
There is a practical way to measure this without arguing about culture. Choose one artifact that will outlive the current sprint, and ask an engineer who did not build it to make a harmless change while the author stays silent. Do not grade whether they succeed eventually. Watch the first fact they need from the author's head: why a flag exists, which workspace is authoritative, which metric proves the change is safe, which rollback is forbidden, which dependency cannot be restarted.

That missing fact is the exact unit of collaboration that failed to leave the author. The repair is not another meeting. The repair is to move that fact into the artifact: a name, a test, a comment that explains why, a runbook guard, a dashboard annotation, a deployment invariant. Retest until the next person can change the thing without borrowing the author's memory.

docs.title: Counterexamples: not everything must be handed off
docs.text:
Takeover-ability is a cost, and like any cost it is worth paying in proportion to risk. Pushing it to the maximum everywhere is its own failure.

  • A throwaway script or a one-off migration that runs once and is deleted does not need to be takeover-able. Investing in its handoff is waste.
  • A genuine spike or prototype is built to answer a question and be thrown away. Demanding it be ownable by a stranger defeats the purpose of moving fast to learn.
  • Deep specialist work — a kernel tuning, a tricky scheduler change — sometimes legitimately requires context that cannot be fully resident in the artifact, and the right move is not to pretend otherwise but to name the bus-factor explicitly and decide deliberately to carry it, the way a payments team deliberately carries some risks and not others.

The line is the same one maturity draws: invest in takeover-ability where the artifact will outlive your attention and someone will have to change it without you, and refuse the investment where it will not. The skill is not maximizing handoff-readiness; it is knowing which artifacts will be inherited and making exactly those ones inheritable.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Measure your collaboration by absence, not presence. Ask of anything you own: if I vanished for a month, what could nobody safely change until I returned? That list is your real collaboration scorecard.
  • Move knowledge out of your head and into the artifact. Context that lives only in you is debt the next person pays under pressure. Comments that explain why, tests that pin the non-obvious invariant, docs that name the unwritten step — these are the payments.
  • Validate handoff with a stranger, never with yourself. You cannot un-know what you know; only someone without your context can find the gaps your context hides.
  • Notice when "being helpful" is really being necessary. If you are the only one who can do a thing and you keep being the one who does it, you are accumulating dependency and calling it collaboration. Hand it off on purpose.
  • Decide deliberately what does not need to be takeover-able, and name the bus-factor you are choosing to carry instead of pretending it away.

Distinguish this from the topology seam every time: there, the question is whether two layers connect; here, it is whether a person who is not you can take the wheel while you are not there. A team full of strong engineers who each ship un-takeover-able artifacts is not a strong team. It is a collection of single points of failure that happen to attend the same meetings.
