docs.title: Translating Risk in Both Directions
docs.text:
An engineer once stood up in a planning meeting and said, correctly, "our database is at 80% connection pool utilization and we have no headroom." It was true, it was important, and it was completely ignored. Two weeks later, a traffic spike exhausted the pool, the service went down for forty minutes during a sales event, and the post-incident review noted — accurately — that the engineer had "raised the concern." Everyone in the room had heard a fact about a connection pool. Nobody had heard "we are one busy Tuesday away from losing the checkout flow during the campaign we are all in this room to plan." The warning was not unclear. It was untranslated.

This is the skill we mislabel. We call it "communication" and imagine it means explaining technical things clearly. But clarity was never the problem in that room — the engineer was perfectly clear. The problem was that a true technical statement is in the wrong language for the person who has to act on it, and the engineer's real job was to translate it. An engineer's core communication capability is not explaining clearly. It is the bidirectional translation of risk: from the language of technical fact into the language of consequence, and back. Most engineers do one direction. Many do neither. The rare ones who do both are the ones whose warnings get heard and whose deadlines get sane.

docs.title: The two languages
docs.text:
There are two languages spoken around every engineering decision, and they do not share vocabulary.

One is the language of technical fact: connection pools, p99 latency, replication lag, certificate expiry, cardinality, blast radius. It is precise, it is true, and it is inert to anyone who does not already live in it. A statement in this language carries no force to a product manager or an executive, not because they are unintelligent, but because the statement does not contain the thing they steer by.

The other is the language of consequence: what happens if we do this or don't, who is affected, what it costs, when, and how likely. Revenue, customers lost, hours of outage, regulatory exposure, the campaign that fails. This is the language business decisions are actually made in. An engineer who can only speak the first language is, from the business's point of view, providing weather data in a language nobody on the bridge reads, and then being surprised the ship hit the rock they accurately described.

Translation is not dumbing down. It is the genuinely hard cognitive work of taking "80% pool utilization, no headroom" and computing the consequence: under the planned campaign traffic, this fails, here is roughly when, here is what it takes down, here is what that costs. That computation is engineering judgment. Skipping it and stating the raw fact is not honesty; it is offloading the translation onto someone who cannot perform it.

docs.title: Upward translation: fact into consequence
docs.text:
The first direction is the one the connection-pool engineer missed: upward and toward the business, every technical fact must arrive as a consequence or it does not arrive at all.

The failure mode is specific and common. The engineer states the risk accurately, in technical terms, considers themselves to have "raised it," and is then genuinely aggrieved when it is deprioritized. From their side, they warned everyone and were ignored. From the business's side, they were handed a fact with no decision attached and reasonably moved on to risks that came with consequences. Both are telling the truth. The gap is the missing translation, and it is the engineer's to close, because the engineer is the only person in the room who can compute the consequence from the fact.

The translated version of the same warning sounds entirely different: "If we run the campaign on the current database without adding connection headroom, I expect the checkout path to fail under peak load — probably within the first hour of the promotion — taking down the exact flow this campaign drives traffic to. Buying the headroom is two days of work. Not buying it risks the campaign's revenue." Same fact. Now it is a decision the business can make, with a cost on both sides, in the language it decides in. It might still get deprioritized — but now that is a real choice someone owns, not a warning that evaporated.

docs.title: Downward translation: anxiety into constraint
docs.text:
The second direction is rarer, harder, and the one almost nobody names. It runs the other way: a pressure expressed in the language of consequence has to be translated back into the specific technical constraint it is allowed to move — and the one it is not.

The scene: leadership is anxious. "We have to ship by the end of the quarter, the board is watching, this date is not negotiable." That is a real statement in the language of consequence, and it arrives as undifferentiated pressure on the whole engineering effort. The untranslated response is to absorb the pressure evenly — cut corners across the board, including the one corner that must not be cut. The deadline crushes the load test, or the rollback rehearsal, or the migration's backup step, because all of engineering felt the squeeze equally and the most boring safety task lost.

The translation that should have happened runs backward: take "the date cannot move" and resolve it into which specific technical constraints can flex and which one cannot. "We can hit the date. To do it we cut the new dashboard, defer the refactor, and reduce the test matrix — those are safe to compress. But the database migration's rollback path is not on the table, because if it fails without one, the outage costs us more than the slipped date ever could." That sentence translates anxiety into a constraint, and it does something the raw pressure could not: it tells leadership exactly what their own deadline is buying and at what price, so they can decide whether the price is acceptable. Pressure that is never translated into specific constraints lands on the wrong one by default, because the boring, invisible safety task is always the cheapest thing to sacrifice and the most expensive thing to lose.

Notice that the downward direction also protects the upward one. The engineer who can say "this constraint cannot move, here is the consequence if it does" is doing the same translation, pointed the other way — and an organization that has learned to hear that translation is an organization whose warnings get acted on.

docs.title: Why most engineers do only one direction, or neither
docs.text:
The two translations require different temperaments, which is why people who do one rarely do both.

Upward translation requires a willingness to leave the safety of pure technical truth and make a claim about consequence — a forecast, with a number, that could be wrong. Many strong engineers refuse this. They will state the fact, which is unfalsifiable and safe, but not the consequence, which exposes them to being wrong about the business. So they stay accurate and unheard, and mistake the first for having done their job.

Downward translation requires the opposite move: the willingness to push back up against expressed pressure, to tell an anxious executive "no, not that constraint," which feels like insubordination and risks the relationship. The engineers who can do upward translation are often the most accommodating, which is exactly why they fold under downward pressure: the same desire to be useful that makes them translate facts into business value makes them absorb deadlines without defending the one line that matters. And the ones who can hold the line are often too blunt to translate their facts into consequence in the first place. Doing both means being persuasive toward the business and immovable on the one constraint that earns it. The rarity of that combination is why so many technically excellent engineers have no influence: the accommodating ones translate facts but fold on the constraint, the blunt ones hold the constraint but never translate the facts, and an organization only changes course for the engineer who does both. It is a learnable skill, not a temperament you were issued at birth.

docs.title: The translation test
docs.text:
Before a decision meeting ends, write two sentences on the page.

  • Fact to consequence: "If we ignore <technical-fact>, <user-or-business-path> fails under <condition>, causing <impact>."
  • Pressure to constraint: "To hit <business-goal>, we can move <safe-scope>, but not <non-negotiable-constraint>, because moving it creates <failure-mode>."

If the room cannot produce both sentences, risk has not been communicated; it has only been mentioned. The test is intentionally mechanical, because mechanical language is often what saves a decision from becoming theater. It forces the technical side to own the consequence and the business side to own exactly which constraint it is choosing to protect or relax.

docs.title: Counterexamples: not every fact needs translating
docs.text:
Translation is for risk that crosses the boundary between the two languages. Inside one language, it is overhead.

  • Engineer-to-engineer, the language of technical fact is the shared language. Translating "80% pool utilization" into business consequence for a fellow engineer who can compute it themselves is condescending and slow. Speak fact to people who speak fact.
  • A purely technical decision with no business-visible consequence — which library, which internal naming convention — does not need a consequence translation, because there is no consequence at the business layer to translate to. Manufacturing one is theater.
  • When leadership is already fluent in the technical risk — a deeply technical founder, an SRE-literate director — the upward translation is redundant and can read as managing them. Translate the gap that exists, not the one the template says should.

The skill is recognizing which statements cross the boundary between fact and consequence and translating exactly those, in the direction the listener needs. Over-translating inside a single language is its own failure of judgment.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Before raising a technical risk upward, compute its consequence: what fails, when, for whom, at what cost, how likely. If you state the fact without the consequence, assume it will be ignored — and that the gap was yours, not theirs.
  • Treat "I raised it" as not yet having done the job. You raised it only when it arrived as a decision someone could own, in the language they decide in.
  • When pressure arrives as undifferentiated anxiety, translate it backward before you absorb it: which constraints can flex, and which single one cannot, and what the consequence is if it moves. Make leadership spend their deadline on purpose, not on the cheapest safety task by default.
  • Build both directions. If you only translate facts into consequence, you will be heard and then steamrolled. If you only hold the line, you will be right and unheard. Influence needs both.
  • Translate across the boundary, not within it. Speak fact to engineers, consequence to the business, and stop translating when the listener already shares your language.

The engineer whose accurate warnings keep getting ignored, and the engineer whose deadlines keep crushing the one thing that mattered, usually believe they have a communication problem and a clarity problem. They have a translation problem, in two directions, and it is the most demanding communication skill in the building precisely because almost no one is doing both.

docs.title: Writing for the Reader at 3 a.m.
docs.text:
Picture the actual person who opens your runbook. They are not at a desk on a quiet afternoon deciding to deepen their knowledge. It is 3 a.m. A pager woke them. Revenue is dropping, a customer is escalating, or a region is dark. Their working memory is half what it normally is, their hands are slightly shaky, and they are reading your document for one reason: to make the alarm stop without making it worse. They will read the first screen, maybe the second. If the answer is not there, they will stop reading and start guessing.

That person — not the curious learner — is the real reader of almost everything operational you will ever write. And once you accept who the reader is, the standard for good writing changes completely. The question is no longer "is this clear?" It is: can a person in their worst cognitive state use this correctly under pressure? Those are not the same test, and optimizing for the first quietly fails the second.

docs.title: Why the default goals are wrong
docs.text:
Most of what we are taught about good writing was tuned for a reader who has patience and intact cognition: be complete, give context before detail, build the argument, be elegant. Each of those is actively harmful to the 3 a.m. reader.

Completeness becomes a hazard. A document that lists every possible cause of an alert, every caveat, every edge case, is more complete and less usable. Under stress, more options is not more help — it is more decisions, and decision-making is exactly the faculty that degrades first. The complete runbook hands a panicking person a menu when they needed an instruction. The cruelest version is the troubleshooting guide that is technically exhaustive and operationally useless, because the one path that resolves 80% of cases is buried on equal footing with nineteen paths that almost never apply.

The "Background and Context" section becomes a wall. Writers love to open with how the system works, why this component exists, what the architecture is. For the learner, that is generous. For the incident reader, it is a barrier between them and the action — paragraphs they must skip, suspecting the answer is hidden somewhere inside them, unsure if skipping will cost them. Context placed before action does not inform the reader; it delays them and makes them doubt. The mid-incident reader does not want to understand the system. They want to stop the bleeding and understand it tomorrow.

Elegance costs scanning speed. Flowing prose, where each sentence depends on the last, reads beautifully and scans terribly. A degraded reader does not read; they scan, jumping to the first thing that looks like a command or a number. Connected paragraphs force linear reading on someone who has no patience for it. The elegant runbook is the one nobody can find the rollback step in.

docs.title: The inversion, made concrete
docs.text:
So the rules invert. Here is what that actually looks like, because the principle is worthless without the specific moves it forces.

Rollback goes first, not last. The instinct is to order a runbook the way the incident unfolds: detect, diagnose, identify root cause, then remediate, then — at the bottom, as a fallback — roll back. That ordering optimizes for understanding. The 3 a.m. reader needs the opposite ordering, optimized for stopping harm: the fastest action that ends customer impact comes first, and diagnosis comes after the bleeding stops. A runbook that puts "how to roll back this deploy" in step one and "how to figure out what went wrong" in step six is correctly ordered for its real reader, even though it reads backwards to its author. (This is the operational twin of the argument in Rollback Is a Product Capability: rollback only helps if the person under pressure can reach it.)

The first screen must be self-sufficient. Assume the reader sees only what fits above the fold and never scrolls. That screen should answer: am I in the right place, what is the single most likely fix, and what is the one command that is dangerous. Everything else is below, for the reader who has bought themselves time by acting on the top.

You delete to make it safer. Writing for incidents is mostly subtraction. Every sentence that does not change what the reader does is competing for the attention of someone who has almost none. The mature move is to cut the explanatory paragraph, cut the third alternative remediation, cut the caveat that applies once a year — not because they are wrong, but because their presence dilutes the steps that are right. A runbook is not improved by adding; it is improved by removing everything the panicking reader would have to read past.

Dangerous commands get a guard, not a footnote. The command that drops a table, scales to zero, or force-deletes a namespace cannot rely on a warning three lines above it that the reader will skip. The destructive step needs its precondition welded to it — what must be true before you run this, stated immediately adjacent, so the guard cannot be skimmed past separately from the action it guards.

docs.title: A test you can actually run
docs.text:
There is a single, brutal acceptance test for operational writing, and it is not "does it read well." It is: hand the document to someone who has never seen this system, simulate the incident, and watch where they hesitate. Every pause, every re-read, every "wait, which one?" is a defect — not in the reader, in the document. The places a fresh reader stalls are exactly the places the real reader will stall at 3 a.m., except the real reader will be more degraded and the stakes will be live.

This reframes a thing teams get backwards. We say a runbook "wasn't followed" during an incident, as if the reader failed. Far more often the runbook was unusable by a degraded reader and the reader correctly abandoned it. The document was written for the author, who has full context and a calm mind, and tested by the author, who cannot simulate not knowing what they know. An operational document validated only by its author is the writing equivalent of code only its author can change — see Collaboration Is Takeover-Ability.

docs.title: Counterexamples: not everything is a 3 a.m. document
docs.text:
This standard is specific to writing that gets read under pressure. It is the wrong standard elsewhere, and applying it everywhere produces its own damage.

  • A design document or an architecture decision record is read deliberately, by someone who wants the context and the trade-offs. Stripping it to terse steps would destroy its purpose. Completeness and reasoning are the point there.
  • A tutorial or onboarding guide is read by exactly the patient learner the runbook must ignore. Background-first is correct; the reader has time and wants the model, not just the move.
  • A postmortem is read after the adrenaline, to change the system. It needs the full narrative the runbook had to delete.

The line is simple: the more likely the reader is to be degraded and in a hurry when they read it, the more these inversions apply. A runbook, an alert annotation, an on-call dashboard description, an error message, a "break glass" procedure — these have a 3 a.m. reader. Treat them accordingly. Most other writing does not, and should not be flattened to fit.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Name the reader before you write. If the answer is "someone mid-incident," you are writing for a degraded reader and the normal rules of good writing are now liabilities.
  • Order operational documents by fastest path to stopping harm, not by the chronology of diagnosis. Rollback and mitigation go up top; understanding goes down below.
  • Make the first screen self-sufficient. Assume no scroll. Put the most likely fix and the most dangerous command where they cannot be missed.
  • Edit by deletion. Cut every sentence that does not change what a panicking reader does next; the rare caveat costs you the common case.
  • Weld each destructive command to its precondition, adjacent, so the guard cannot be skimmed past.
  • Validate with a stranger under a simulated incident, not by re-reading your own work. Treat every hesitation as a defect in the document, never in the reader.

Clear writing serves a reader who has time to be taught. Operational writing serves a reader who has none, and the difference between the two is the difference between a document that wins awards and a document that ends an outage.

docs.title: etcd
docs.text:
etcd cluster operations, backups, validation, and recovery boundaries.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • etcd Operations Standard Standard for operating etcd as critical cluster state — quorum, defrag/alarm handling, snapshot backup proven by restore, and disaster recovery.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate etcd safely as the source of truth for Kubernetes and other distributed systems: how quorum determines availability, why disk latency and database size quietly cause outages, and how to take a snapshot you can actually restore.

The core judgment of this standard:

etcd is not "a database the cluster uses" — for Kubernetes it is the cluster's memory. Losing quorum stalls the control plane; losing the data loses the cluster. A snapshot you have never restored is not a recovery plan.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run a self-managed etcd cluster (standalone or as the Kubernetes control-plane store).
  • Need a backup/restore and disaster-recovery procedure for cluster state.
  • Plan an etcd or Kubernetes control-plane upgrade or member change.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Managed Kubernetes, where the provider owns control-plane etcd. You still need application-level backups (Velero etc.); etcd internals are the provider's.
  • Application data modeling on top of etcd.
  • Tuning beyond the production-critical settings below.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Treat etcd as critical state with strict change control.
  2. Use an odd number of members (3 or 5) so the cluster keeps quorum when one member is lost.
  3. Put etcd on fast, low-latency disks — etcd is fsync-bound, and slow disks cause leader changes and request timeouts.
  4. Take a verified snapshot before any cluster change.
  5. Monitor leader changes, disk fsync/backend-commit latency, database size, and raised alarms.

docs.title: Quorum and member count
docs.text: etcd needs a majority (quorum) of members to serve writes. With 3 members it tolerates 1 failure; with 5 it tolerates 2. Even member counts add cost without improving fault tolerance and can split votes. Spread members across failure domains.

docs.title: Disk and size are the silent killers
docs.text:
• etcd commits every write to disk; high fsync/backend-commit latency manifests as frequent leader elections and etcdserver: request timed out.
  • The database has a quota (--quota-backend-bytes, default ~2 GiB). When exceeded, etcd raises a NOSPACE alarm and goes read-only — a cluster-wide outage. Stay well under the quota; compact and defragment on a schedule.
# compaction (keep current revision), then defragment to reclaim space:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl compact <rev>
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl defrag
# if a NOSPACE alarm is set, clear it after reclaiming space:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl alarm list
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl alarm disarm

Defrag is disruptive per member (it blocks that member briefly); do it one member at a time, starting with followers.

docs.title: Backup: Snapshot, Proven by Restore
docs.text:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl --endpoints=<endpoint> \
  --cacert=<ca> --cert=<cert> --key=<key> \
  snapshot save /backup/etcd-snapshot.db

# verify the snapshot is consistent:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl snapshot status /backup/etcd-snapshot.db --write-out=table
  • Take snapshots on a schedule and before every cluster change (upgrade, member add/remove).
  • Store snapshots encrypted and off the etcd hosts; they contain all cluster state, including Secrets.
  • Restore-test on a schedule into an isolated environment. A snapshot that has never been restored is a hope, not a backup.

docs.title: Disaster Recovery (Restore)
docs.text:
Restoring etcd is destructive and must be coordinated — for a Kubernetes control plane, the API server must be stopped while you restore.

  1. Stop the etcd (and dependent API server) processes on the target.
  2. Restore the snapshot into a new data directory on each member with matching peer URLs.
  3. Start etcd as a new cluster from the restored data, then restart the API server.
  4. Validate health and that cluster state matches the snapshot point in time.

Do this from a runbook, with an owner, never improvised during an incident.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint health --cluster
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl endpoint status --cluster --write-out=table
ETCDCTL_API=3 etcdctl alarm list

Confirm:

  • All endpoints are healthy; exactly one leader; no excessive leader changes.
  • Database size is well under quota; no NOSPACE/CORRUPT alarms.
  • For Kubernetes, the API server is responsive and controllers reconcile normally.
  • The latest snapshot passed a restore test within its interval.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Lost quorum Writes fail; control plane stalls Too many members down / even count Restore a member or recover from snapshot; use odd counts. NOSPACE / read-only Cluster refuses writes DB exceeded backend quota Compact, defrag, then alarm disarm; raise quota deliberately. Frequent leader elections Timeouts, instability Slow disk fsync, network latency Move to fast disks; check network; isolate noisy neighbors. Snapshot won't restore Restore fails Never restore-tested / inconsistent snapshot snapshot status to verify; schedule restore tests. Cert/peer mismatch Members won't join Wrong peer URLs or certs on restore Restore with matching peer URLs; protect/rotate certs.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Always snapshot before a cluster change so you have a known-good point.
  2. To roll back a failed member change, recover the member from the pre-change snapshot and rejoin.
  3. To roll back catastrophic data loss, perform the disaster-recovery restore from the most recent verified snapshot.
  4. Protect and rotate etcd peer/client certificates after any recovery.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never publish real etcd endpoints, certificates, keys, snapshots, or member names.
  • Snapshots contain all cluster state including Kubernetes Secrets — encrypt and access-control them like secrets.
  • Enable mutual TLS between peers and clients; restrict who can reach the client port.
  • Limit who can run snapshot, defrag, alarm, and member operations.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/cluster team.
  • Verify a successful snapshot restore test each interval.
  • Re-check database size, quota headroom, and disk latency on a regular cadence.
  • Re-confirm the DR runbook before each control-plane upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
