  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • etcd disaster recovery documentation

docs.title: Infrastructure as code
docs.text:
Terraform changes, state, validation, and recovery constraints.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Terraform IaC Standard Standard for safe Terraform usage — remote locked state, plan-review gates, secret and drift discipline, and controlled rollback and recovery.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to use Terraform so that infrastructure changes are reproducible, reviewable, and recoverable — and so that the two most dangerous Terraform failure modes, damaged state and an unintended destructive apply, are designed out.

The core judgment of this standard:

Configuration defines the intended infrastructure; state records Terraform's binding to real objects. Protect both, and never apply a plan you have not read.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Provision cloud or on-prem infrastructure with Terraform/OpenTofu.
  • Work in a team where more than one person or pipeline can run apply.
  • Need an auditable, rollback-aware infrastructure change process.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Module design patterns for a specific provider in depth.
  • Choosing a cloud provider or designing the target architecture.
  • CI system specifics; this defines the Terraform discipline that any pipeline must enforce.

docs.title: Principles
docs.text:
1. Store state remotely, encrypted, versioned, and with locking enabled.
  2. Lock state during every write operation so two operators cannot race.
  3. Keep secrets out of .tf files and, as far as possible, out of state.
  4. Use modules and pinned versions for repeatable infrastructure.
  5. Read every plan before applying; gate apply behind review for shared environments.
  6. Treat state restoration as an incident-recovery action, not a routine undo button.

docs.title: Architecture and Control Flow
docs.text:
flowchart LR Git[Engineer and Git] --> PlanJob[CI plan job] Inventory[Asset and ownership catalog] --> PlanJob PlanJob --> Plan[Reviewed plan artifact] Plan --> Gate[Policy and human approval] Gate --> Runner[Controlled apply runner] Runner --> API[Cloud or platform API] Runner <--> State[(Remote state)] State --- Lock[(Lock file)] Runner --> Evidence[Change evidence store] API --> Drift[Post-apply drift check] Drift --> Evidence

The architecture separates four responsibilities:

  • Intent: versioned Terraform configuration in Git.
  • Decision: plan analysis, policy checks, and accountable approval.
  • Execution: a controlled runner with scoped credentials.
  • Evidence: remote state, change logs, and post-apply drift results.

docs.title: Change Decision Flow
docs.text: flowchart TD     A[Configuration change] --> B[fmt, validate, and plan]     B --> C{Delete, replacement, or policy violation?}     C -- Yes --> D[Stop, redesign, or require recovery proof]     C -- No --> E[Approve the exact saved plan]     D --> B     E --> F[Apply saved plan]     F --> G[Run post-apply plan]     G --> H{Unexpected drift or failure?}     H -- No --> I[Close change and retain evidence]     H -- Yes --> J[Freeze writers and execute controlled recovery]

docs.title: Product Integration
docs.text:
This standard belongs in a DevOps platform as a cross-module contract, not as an isolated command page.

Product capability Managed object Required behavior Asset inventory Workspace, backend key, account, owner, critical resources Make ownership and state boundaries visible before planning. Change management Commit, plan artifact, reviewer, approval window Approve the exact plan that will be applied. Secret and identity management Runner identity and backend credentials Supply short-lived, least-privilege access without storing secrets in code. Policy engine Delete, replacement, public exposure, encryption rules Stop unsafe plans before execution. Evidence store Plan, apply output, state serial, post-apply plan Preserve enough evidence to explain what changed and why. Recovery center State versions, known-good revision, recovery owner Guide controlled state repair or forward recovery.

docs.title: Remote, locked, encrypted state
docs.text:
Local state on a laptop is a single point of failure and cannot be shared safely. Use a remote backend with locking, encryption, and object versioning.

The example below is scoped to Terraform 1.15.x. Revalidate the backend arguments before adopting a different Terraform or S3-compatible implementation.

terraform {
  required_version = "~> 1.15.0"

  backend "s3" {
    bucket       = "example-tf-state"     # placeholder; never publish the real name
    key          = "env/prod/terraform.tfstate"
    region       = "example-region"
    use_lockfile = true
    encrypt      = true
  }
}
  • Enable versioning on the state store so accidental deletion or overwrite can be investigated and recovered.
  • Use a separate state key per environment; never share one state across prod and non-prod.
  • Grant the runner the required state-object permissions and GetObject, PutObject, and DeleteObject on the .tflock object.
  • The locking mechanism is not optional for a shared state.

Current Terraform S3 backend documentation marks DynamoDB-based locking as deprecated. Existing estates may temporarily configure both mechanisms while older runners are upgraded, but new standards should use use_lockfile = true.

docs.title: Pin versions
docs.text: Pin both required_version and provider versions. Unpinned providers mean an unrelated dependency change can silently alter a plan. Commit the .terraform.lock.hcl lock file.

docs.title: Plan, review, apply
docs.text:
terraform fmt -check
terraform init -input=false
terraform validate
terraform plan -out=plan.tfout
terraform show -no-color plan.tfout
terraform apply plan.tfout

Saving the plan and applying that file binds apply to the reviewed action set. It does not eliminate provider-side failures, expired credentials, policy changes, or changes outside Terraform between plan and apply. Keep the interval short and reject stale plans in CI.

In the plan output, the symbols that matter most:

  • + create and ~ update in place still require review.
  • -/+ means destroy and recreate. Stop and confirm before applying it to a database, volume, identity-bearing resource, or anything externally referenced.

Use prevent_destroy selectively as a final guard for critical resources, not as a substitute for plan review or recovery design.

docs.title: Keep secrets out of .tf and state
docs.text:
• Prefer workload identity, environment injection, or a secret manager over literals in .tf.
  • Many resources write sensitive values into state even when input injection is safe.
  • Mark sensitive outputs sensitive = true, while remembering that this only redacts normal CLI display; it does not remove the value from state.
  • Never commit state, saved plans, or secret-bearing variable files.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
terraform validate
terraform plan -detailed-exitcode
terraform state list

Confirm:

  • A second writer cannot acquire the same state lock.
  • The reviewed plan has no unexplained delete or replacement.
  • A post-apply plan is empty, or every remaining drift item has an owner and decision.
  • State-store versioning, encryption, and access logging are enabled.
  • The expected resources are tracked in the expected backend key and workspace.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response State lost or corrupted apply errors; resources appear missing or are proposed for recreation Wrong backend/workspace, state loss, or unsafe manual state change Freeze applies; identify the real resources and correct state lineage before recovery. Concurrent apply Lock error or conflicting state updates Locking disabled, bypassed, or an abandoned lock Stop extra writers; investigate ownership before any force-unlock. Unintended destroy A resource is deleted or recreated Forced replacement from an immutable attribute Stop; redesign sequencing or require explicit approval with backup and recovery proof. Drift Plan shows changes nobody intended through code Out-of-band changes or provider behavior Import, revert, or deliberately codify the change; do not blindly apply. Secret in state/repo Credential exposed Literal secret, committed state, or saved plan artifact Revoke and rotate; remove exposure; redesign injection and artifact handling. Provider upgrade surprise Plan changes after no intended code change Unpinned provider or module Pin versions and review the dependency update separately.

docs.title: Destroy and Rollback
docs.text:
Terraform has no universal undo. Rollback is normally forward-applying a previous known-good configuration after reviewing the reverse plan.

  1. Guard critical stateful resources with reviewed lifecycle and policy controls.
  2. To reverse a configuration change, check out the previous revision and review its plan; reversal can itself force replacement.
  3. Use terraform plan -destroy to preview teardown; never destroy a shared environment without explicit approval and recovery proof.
  4. If state is suspected to be damaged, stop all writers and preserve the current state and logs.
  5. Before restoring an older state object, compare its serial, lineage, resource identities, and relationship to the real infrastructure.
  6. After any state repair or restore, run and review terraform plan before applying.

Blindly restoring an older state can make Terraform forget live resources or replay obsolete relationships. State restoration is therefore a controlled recovery procedure, not the first response to a failed apply.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never publish real account IDs, resource IDs, backend bucket names, regions, state files, plans, or provider credentials.
  • Treat state and saved plans as secret-bearing artifacts: encrypt them, restrict access, and audit reads.
  • Use least-privilege identities for Terraform runners and separate plan from apply authorization.
  • Review module sources; pin them to trusted versions or commits, not floating references.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/infrastructure team.
  • Re-pin and review Terraform, provider, and module versions on a regular cadence.
  • Confirm state-store versioning, encryption, locking, and runner permissions each quarter.
  • Re-review destructive-change controls when the architecture changes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Terraform S3 backend documentation

docs.title: Kubernetes
docs.text:
Cluster design, provisioning, upgrades, storage, capacity, and incident handling.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

6 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • AWS EKS Production High Availability Cluster Design and Delivery Standard AWS EKS production HA cluster standard covering control plane, nodes, networking, IAM, ingress, storage, delivery, observability, upgrades, and recovery.
  • EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard Standard for upgrading a Kubernetes cluster (EKS or self-managed) without an unplanned outage — three coupled upgrades in sequence, a pre-flight API-compatibility check, a reversible node model, and rollback designed around the layers that can actually be reversed.
  • kubeadm Installation Standard kubeadm installation standard for self-managed Kubernetes control-plane contracts, etcd recovery, certificates, add-on order, validation, rebuild boundaries, and lifecycle ownership.
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard Production standard for provisioning Kubernetes clusters as an ordered platform dependency graph instead of a one-time cluster creation command.
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard Production standard for Kubernetes storage and ingress as platform contracts covering CSI, StorageClass, IngressClass, TLS, validation, and failure modes.
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management Standard for managing NFS-backed Kubernetes PVC capacity by joining PVC metadata with real backend filesystem usage, ownership, thresholds, and response rules.
1 document Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • PVC Usage Alert Runbook Runbook for responding to Kubernetes PVC usage alerts by validating real usage, impact, data criticality, expansion path, cleanup safety, and escalation.
1 document Cases

Extract reusable decisions from anonymous real-world constraints, evidence, and results.

  • Anonymous Kubernetes Upgrade Case Anonymous Kubernetes minor-version upgrade case showing how add-on compatibility, node replacement, workload validation, and rollback boundaries determine the upgrade sequence.

docs.title: An Anonymous Kubernetes Upgrade Case
docs.text: This case is based on a real but anonymized production upgrade. Original minor releases, provider, cluster, add-on versions, dates, and workload names are not public. It is not execution evidence for Kubernetes 1.34, 1.35, or 1.36.

docs.title: Context and limits
docs.text:
A production cluster with a managed control plane required a minor upgrade. The provider owned the control plane; the team owned node pools, CNI/CSI, ingress, observability, and workloads. Surviving records prove that baseline checks, control-plane work, node migration, and anomaly handling occurred, but they do not reconstruct every version, timestamp, or metric.

mindmap root((Upgrade-case evidence model)) Confirmed facts Managed control plane Minor upgrade occurred Nodes and workloads required migration Anomaly occurred Engineering inferences Add-on compatibility may affect nodes Capacity and PDB affect drain Recovery after control-plane upgrade favors forward action Missing evidence Original versions Exact dates Add-on matrix Complete metrics Cannot confirm Single root cause Exact impact duration Every business-path result Current re-evaluation API-deprecation gate Frozen compatibility matrix Irreversible-boundary record Stage-by-stage evidence

docs.title: Symptoms
docs.text: After the control-plane operation, some nodes or workloads did not stabilize as expected. The team paused later batches and checked system components and workload health. Node Ready alone did not explain every symptom, and recovery required node/workload action rather than a simple control-plane downgrade. These observations prove upgrade friction, not a specific root cause.

docs.title: Evidence and timeline
docs.text: timeline     title Anonymized upgrade sequence     Pre-upgrade baseline : Record control plane, nodes, and workloads                          : Confirm window and recovery entry points     Compatibility review : Review APIs and critical add-ons                          : Confirm spare capacity and PDBs     Control-plane action : Upgrade managed control plane                          : Cross a boundary not safely downgraded by assumption     Node handling : Replace or upgrade nodes in batches                   : Cordon, drain, observe scheduling     Anomaly found : Some nodes or workloads remain unstable                   : Pause later batches     Decision and recovery : Preserve evidence                           : Repair or replace affected nodes                           : Validate business paths     Result : Serviceable state restored            : Exact versions, dates, and full metrics remain private or missing Evidence What it supports What it does not prove Provider operation record Control-plane action occurred All workloads were healthy Node/Pod/Event snapshots Resource state at a time Full business impact CNI/CSI/Ingress logs Component-observed errors A unique root cause PDB, requests, capacity Whether drain could block Actual impact of every eviction Business smoke/SLO Critical-path recovery Every background job and datum Change record Decision and execution order Unrecorded actions

docs.title: Confirmed facts
docs.text:
1. A managed Kubernetes control plane received a minor upgrade.
  2. Control plane and node/workload layers had different ownership.
  3. The change had compatibility, control-plane, node, and business-validation stages.
  4. An anomaly required a pause and intervention.
  5. Recovery relied on node/workload handling, not assumed control-plane downgrade.
  6. Original versions, dates, add-ons, and metrics cannot be responsibly invented.

docs.title: Engineering inferences
docs.text:
PDBs, spare capacity, image pulling, or system-component compatibility may have affected batches. CNI, CSI, ingress, admission webhooks, operators, or monitoring agents may have influenced the result. After a control-plane upgrade, forward repair, a compatible node pool, or workload migration is usually more realistic than downgrade. Node Ready may hide DNS, network, storage, and business failures.

flowchart LR F[Confirmed facts] --> D[Handling decision] I[Engineering inference] -. hypothesis only .-> D M[Missing evidence] --> U[Cannot confirm] U --> G[New gates and evidence requirements] D --> L[Reusable lesson] G --> L

The dotted edge marks inference: it can guide checks but cannot be published as fact.

docs.title: Unknowns
docs.text: Unknown items include source and target minors; provider, region, and scale; CNI/CSI/Ingress/CoreDNS/monitoring versions; exact anomaly and recovery times; removed APIs or webhook/CRD conversion issues; a single root cause; exhaustive business-path validation; and whether requests or data were lost. These are credibility boundaries, not blanks to fill with plausible details.

docs.title: Reasoning process
docs.text: The correct sequence is to stop later batches; confirm control-plane availability without equating it to business health; inspect system components, nodes, CNI/DNS/CSI/ingress, workloads, then business paths; compare against the baseline; identify irreversible boundaries; choose forward repair or node replacement; and close only with business and data validation.

docs.title: Gates for a current maintained-line execution
docs.text: Stage Required gate Plan Target latest patch, skew, add-on matrix, owners, window Before control plane Deprecated APIs, webhooks/CRDs, CNI/CSI/Ingress support Before nodes New image/runtime, spare capacity, PDBs, drain plan After each batch System, DNS, network, storage, ingress, and business SLI Continue Current batch stable with no new high-severity alert Stop Data risk, critical-path failure, insufficient compatibility evidence Complete Change record, matrix, results, and residual risk updated

docs.title: Decision
docs.text: Do not continue node batches blindly after a control-plane upgrade. Preserve affected nodes and logs, restore capacity with a new pool or controlled repair, cordon/drain in batches, validate DNS/CNI/CSI/ingress and business paths after each batch, and leave unexplained anomalies explicitly unconfirmed rather than publishing a fabricated RCA.

docs.title: Result
docs.text:
The defensible result is limited: the process returned the platform to a serviceable state; required node/workload handling was completed; and control-plane success was shown to be insufficient as platform-upgrade acceptance. Missing versions, dates, metrics, and full validation prevent claims of zero data loss, exact recovery duration, or a named component root cause.

verification_status: not-verified remains because this is a structured review of anonymized historical evidence.

docs.title: Reusable lessons
docs.text:
1. A Kubernetes minor upgrade is a compatibility migration, not one click.
  2. Control plane, add-ons, nodes, workloads, and business outcomes need separate evidence.
  3. Control-plane upgrade is an important irreversible boundary; prepare forward repair and node replacement.
  4. Add-on matrix, PDBs, and spare capacity must be closed before the window.
  5. Node Ready, Pod Running, and green CI do not replace business validation.
  6. Mark missing evidence; do not convert reasonable inference into fact.

docs.title: Related documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes cluster provisioning standard
  • Deployment rollback runbook
  • Kubernetes PVC usage alert runbook

docs.title: Official references
docs.text:
• Kubernetes releases
  • Kubernetes version skew policy
  • Upgrading kubeadm clusters

docs.title: AWS EKS Production High Availability Cluster Design and Delivery Standard
Executive Summary
docs.text:
Building a production-grade highly available EKS cluster on AWS is not mainly about increasing node count or creating a Kubernetes control plane. The real work is putting control plane access, worker capacity, networking, identity, ingress, storage, delivery, observability, upgrades, and recovery into one verifiable operating model.

A credible production EKS design should provide:

  • Workload placement across multiple Availability Zones.
  • Scheduling continuity when one AZ loses capacity.
  • Recoverable and scalable worker capacity.
  • Workload-level AWS permissions instead of broad node-level credentials.
  • Clear ownership for ingress, DNS, certificates, logs, metrics, and alerts.
  • Validation and rollback paths for each critical change.

If a guide stops at eksctl create cluster, it proves that a cluster can be created. It does not prove that the platform can carry production traffic.

docs.title: When To Use This Standard
docs.text:
Use this document when:

  • Creating a new production Amazon EKS cluster.
  • Migrating workloads from virtual machines or self-managed Kubernetes to EKS.
  • Hardening an existing EKS cluster for availability, security, observability, and release safety.
  • Building a reusable delivery checklist for platform or DevOps teams.

Do not use this as:

  • A beginner Kubernetes tutorial.
  • A short-lived proof-of-concept guide.
  • A replacement for multi-Region disaster recovery architecture.
  • A complete regulated-industry compliance blueprint.

Multi-AZ inside one Region is not the same as multi-Region resilience. Treat those as different architecture problems.

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
This standard assumes:

  • One AWS Region with at least three Availability Zones.
  • Mostly stateless services, with state placed in managed data services or carefully designed persistent storage.
  • GitOps or CI/CD based delivery.
  • Public or internal ingress traffic.
  • Least-privilege access, auditability, and rollback requirements.

For a small low-risk environment, reduce the design: two capacity classes, one ingress path, basic metrics, basic logs, and explicit backup. Do not add every production component just to make the platform look mature.

docs.title: Target Architecture
docs.text:
Users
  |
Route 53
  |
AWS Load Balancer Controller
  |
Ingress / Service
  |
Application Pods
  |
Managed data services / persistent storage

Control and operations plane:

EKS managed control plane
  - multi-AZ managed by AWS

Worker capacity
  - managed node groups or Karpenter
  - spread across at least 3 AZs
  - separate system and application capacity

Identity
  - IAM roles for cluster operators
  - EKS Pod Identity or IRSA for workload permissions

Observability
  - metrics
  - logs
  - events
  - alerts
  - audit trail

The key principle: AWS manages the EKS control plane, but application availability still depends on your VPC, subnets, node groups, scheduling rules, Pod replicas, disruption budgets, storage, ingress, and release process.

docs.title: 1. Multi-AZ Is The Baseline
docs.text:
A production EKS cluster should normally use at least three AZs. Two AZs can be acceptable for lower-risk systems, but capacity recovery and maintenance flexibility are weaker. A one-AZ EKS cluster should not be described as production high availability.

Check:

  • The VPC spans at least three AZs.
  • Each AZ has private subnets for worker nodes.
  • Node groups or Karpenter provision capacity across AZs.
  • Application replicas are spread with topology spread constraints or anti-affinity.
  • Storage classes are understood in terms of AZ binding.

Common failure mode: the cluster is configured with three AZ subnets, but actual worker capacity only exists in one AZ. The architecture diagram says multi-AZ; the scheduler experiences a single-AZ platform.

docs.title: 2. Worker Capacity Should Be Layered
docs.text:
Do not run every Pod on the same worker pool. A minimum production model has:

  • System capacity: CoreDNS, ingress controllers, log agents, monitoring agents.
  • Application capacity: user-facing and internal services.
  • Batch or Spot capacity: retryable jobs and interruptible workloads.

The purpose is not complexity. The purpose is to prevent business traffic or batch spikes from evicting the components that keep the cluster observable and reachable.

Recommended principles:

  • Keep system components on stable capacity.
  • Select instance families based on workload resource profiles.
  • Use Spot only for workloads that tolerate interruption and retry.
  • Use PodDisruptionBudgets for critical services.

docs.title: 3. Pods Should Not Inherit Broad Node Permissions
docs.text:
Production workloads should not rely on the node IAM role for AWS API access. Use EKS Pod Identity or IRSA so each workload gets the minimum IAM role it needs.

Acceptance criteria:

  • Each workload receives only the AWS permissions it needs.
  • Secrets, object storage, queues, and container registry permissions are separated.
  • Permission changes are auditable.
  • Removing an application also removes or disables its cloud permissions.

Common failure mode: S3, ECR, CloudWatch, and Secrets Manager permissions are all attached to the node role. It is convenient at first, but any compromised Pod can inherit excessive lateral movement capability.

docs.title: 4. Ingress Is More Than An Ingress Object
docs.text:
A production ingress path needs decisions for:

  • DNS ownership.
  • TLS issuance and renewal.
  • Public or internal load balancers.
  • WAF requirements.
  • Stable health check paths.
  • Traffic shifting for rollback or canary releases.

If a design only contains an Ingress YAML file, the ingress layer is not finished.

docs.title: 5. Default To Managed Services For State
docs.text:
EKS can run stateful systems, but production teams should not default to placing every database, queue, search engine, or cache inside the cluster. Prefer managed services such as RDS, ElastiCache, MSK, OpenSearch, and S3 where they fit.

Before running stateful services in the cluster, answer:

  • Where are backups stored?
  • How does recovery work across AZ failure?
  • How are failed upgrades rolled back?
  • What happens when a node is lost?
  • Does the team really have operational ownership of this component?

docs.title: Phase 1: Network And Account Boundary
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • VPC, subnets, route tables, NAT Gateway or an approved alternative.
  • Cluster IAM role.
  • Node IAM role.
  • Operator and CI/CD access boundaries.
  • Tagging standards.

Validate:

aws sts get-caller-identity
aws ec2 describe-subnets --filters "Name=vpc-id,Values=<vpc-id>"
aws eks describe-cluster --name <cluster-name>

Risks: NAT Gateway cost is often underestimated; private cluster access is often forgotten; overly broad administrator access tends to become the default.

docs.title: Phase 2: Create The EKS Control Plane
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • EKS cluster.
  • Kubernetes version selection.
  • API server endpoint access policy.
  • Control plane logging.
  • Encryption configuration.

Validate:

aws eks update-kubeconfig --name <cluster-name> --region <region>
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get nodes
kubectl get ns

Note: kubectl get nodes can be empty immediately after the control plane is created. A healthy control plane does not mean worker capacity has joined.

docs.title: Phase 3: Worker Capacity And System Components
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • Managed Node Groups or Karpenter.
  • Verified versions for CoreDNS, kube-proxy, and VPC CNI.
  • Scheduling separation for system and application components.
  • Cluster Autoscaler or Karpenter scale-out behavior.

Validate:

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl -n kube-system get pods -o wide
kubectl describe node <node-name>
kubectl top nodes

Failure mode: nodes are Ready, but business Pods stay Pending because subnet IP capacity or CNI configuration is exhausted. The broken object is not the Deployment; it is the network capacity model.

docs.title: Phase 4: Identity, Permissions, And Secrets
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • EKS Pod Identity or IRSA.
  • Least-privilege IAM policies.
  • Secret management rules.
  • CI/CD deployment permissions.

Validate:

kubectl -n <namespace> describe serviceaccount <service-account>
kubectl -n <namespace> exec deploy/<deployment-name> -- aws sts get-caller-identity

The identity visible from the business Pod should be the workload role, not the node role.

docs.title: Phase 5: Ingress, Certificates, And Release Path
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • AWS Load Balancer Controller.
  • IngressClass.
  • DNS records.
  • TLS certificates.
  • Health checks.
  • Canary or blue-green release strategy.

Validate:

kubectl -n kube-system get deploy aws-load-balancer-controller
kubectl -n <namespace> get ingress
kubectl -n <namespace> describe ingress <ingress-name>
curl -I https://<service-domain>

Failure mode: the Ingress object is created, but no load balancer appears. The usual causes are controller permissions, subnet tags, or incorrect annotations.

docs.title: Phase 6: Observability And Alerting
docs.text:
Deliver:

  • Node metrics.
  • Pod metrics.
  • Application logs.
  • Kubernetes Events.
  • API server or audit logs.
  • Critical alerts.

Minimum alert set:

  • Node NotReady.
  • Pod CrashLoopBackOff.
  • Deployment replica shortage.
  • PVC high usage.
  • Ingress 5xx increase.
  • HPA unable to scale for a sustained period.
  • CoreDNS error rate increase.

Validate:

kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp
kubectl top pods -A
kubectl -n <monitoring-namespace> get pods

docs.title: Rollback Strategy
docs.text:
EKS rollback cannot rely only on kubectl rollout undo. Roll back by layer.

Layer Rollback Method Application release Revert image tag, Helm release, or GitOps commit Ingress Restore old rule, old certificate, or old load balancer Worker nodes Revert launch template or create a previous-version node group Permissions Revert IAM policy or ServiceAccount binding Cluster add-ons Revert to a compatible add-on version or temporarily scale old capacity Kubernetes version Usually cannot be downgraded in place; recover through a new cluster migration plan

The Kubernetes version upgrade path is often underestimated. Before upgrading production, confirm compatibility for add-ons, node AMIs, controllers, and critical CRDs. For the full upgrade procedure and rollback triggers, follow the EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard, which is the canonical source for the upgrade sequence.

docs.title: Production Acceptance Checklist
docs.text:
Before production traffic:

  • The cluster spans three AZs.
  • Private subnet IP capacity is sufficient.
  • Worker capacity spans AZs and can recover automatically.
  • System and application components have capacity separation.
  • CoreDNS has enough replicas and is spread across nodes.
  • Critical applications have multiple replicas, PDBs, requests, and limits.
  • Ingress has DNS, TLS, health checks, and rollback.
  • Workloads use least-privilege identity.
  • Secrets are not stored in images, ConfigMaps, or Git.
  • Logs, metrics, events, and alerts are available.
  • CI/CD permissions are auditable.
  • Backup and restore have been tested.
  • Upgrade and failure drills are recorded.

docs.title: Mistake 1: Treating The Managed Control Plane As Application HA
docs.text: AWS reduces control plane operational burden, but application Pods, node capacity, ingress, storage, delivery, and monitoring remain your responsibility. Application availability is designed and verified, not purchased by cluster creation.

docs.title: Mistake 2: Counting Nodes Without Checking Placement
docs.text: Six nodes in one AZ still create an AZ-level failure risk. Replica count and node count only matter when combined with topology distribution.

docs.title: Mistake 3: Migrating Stateful Services First
docs.text: Start migrations with stateless services. Use them to prove image standards, release flow, ingress, logging, metrics, and rollback. Stateful services come later because their hardest problems are data consistency, recovery time, and recovery point.

docs.title: Mistake 4: Leaving Permissions To The End
docs.text: If permissions are handled last, temporary exceptions become permanent patterns. Pod identity and deployment access boundaries should exist from the start.

docs.title: Q&A Assistant Blocks
docs.text:
Question: Is a Managed Node Group enough for EKS high availability?

No. Managed Node Groups help with worker lifecycle management, but high availability also depends on multi-AZ subnets, scheduling spread, Pod replicas, PDBs, ingress, storage, permissions, monitoring, and rollback.

Question: How many AZs should a production EKS cluster use?

Usually at least three AZs. Two AZs can work for lower-risk systems, but recovery and maintenance flexibility are weaker. A one-AZ cluster should not be called production high availability.

Question: How should Pods access AWS services?

Use EKS Pod Identity or IRSA and bind each workload to a least-privilege IAM role. Do not let application Pods inherit broad permissions from the node role.

Question: Should stateful services run inside EKS?

Prefer managed AWS services by default. Run stateful systems inside EKS only when the team owns backup, recovery, upgrade, monitoring, and incident handling for that component.

docs.title: Official References
docs.text:
• Amazon EKS Best Practices Guide: Reliability
  • Amazon EKS Pod Identities
  • Amazon EKS security and IAM
  • Amazon EKS networking

docs.title: Executive Summary
docs.text:
A Kubernetes minor-version upgrade is not one upgrade. It is three coupled upgrades that must happen in order — control plane, then add-ons, then nodes — plus one compatibility check that has to run before any of them. The control-plane version is the most visible part, but the application-impacting failures usually surface through add-on skew, APIs removed in the target version, admission or conversion webhooks, PDBs, or node migration.

The incidents come from the couplings. An add-on left at the old version after the control plane moves produces flaky DNS, networking, or storage that looks like a control-plane problem and is not. A workload still calling an API the new version removed breaks the moment its controller restarts — which may be hours later, long after the upgrade was declared "done." A PodDisruptionBudget that blocks all eviction stalls the node drain halfway through, with some workloads on the new version and some on the old.

The last fully reversible point is before the control-plane upgrade starts. A pre-flight compatibility check finds removed APIs, incompatible CRDs, broken webhooks, and skewed add-ons while the old version is still serving traffic. Skip it, and you discover the same facts during the drain, when half the cluster has already moved and the control plane can no longer be rolled back. This standard is mostly about doing the discovery early and keeping node, add-on, and application rollback paths open until the new version has proven itself under real traffic.

docs.title: When to Use This Standard
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Upgrade EKS, self-managed Kubernetes, or a compatible platform across a minor version.
  • Replace worker node groups as part of a platform upgrade.
  • Upgrade core add-ons (CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CSI drivers, ingress controller, cert-manager, metrics-server).

This is the upgrade standard, and it assumes a running cluster. Do not use it as:

  • A cluster-creation guide — see the Kubernetes cluster provisioning standard.
  • An application rollout procedure — see the deployment rollback runbook.
  • A substitute for a provider's exact console/CLI steps — use the provider's documented upgrade path inside this sequence.
  • A way to skip minor versions — upgrade one minor version at a time unless the platform explicitly supports a larger jump.

docs.title: Assumptions
docs.text:
This standard assumes:

  • A cluster currently in a supported version, upgrading to the next minor version only.
  • Mostly stateless workloads, with state in managed data services or in storage whose CSI driver compatibility you can verify.
  • The ability to add a new node group rather than mutate nodes in place — this is what makes node rollback "stop draining" instead of "un-upgrade a node."
  • An encrypted backup path for cluster state and secrets (the platform's process, or etcd backup for self-managed clusters).
  • An observation window after the upgrade during which old node groups, compatible add-on versions, and application rollback paths remain available. The managed control plane itself should be treated as forward-only.

If your cluster is several minor versions behind, this is not one upgrade — it is a planned sequence of them, each run end to end, because Kubernetes only supports single-minor-version jumps and skew rules apply at every step.

docs.title: Proportional Scaling
docs.text:
The full sequence below is calibrated for a production cluster carrying real traffic. Match the effort to the risk:

  • A compressible cluster has no customer traffic, no critical StatefulSet/PVC dependency, an accepted downtime or rebuild RTO, and a cluster state that can be recreated from code. You may shorten the observation window or drain in larger batches, but only after running the API-compatibility check and keeping the old node group until smoke tests and metrics pass.
  • A stateless cluster can drain more aggressively because node-migration rollback is "stop the drain, uncordon or scale the old node group, and reschedule there" — but only while that old node group still exists. Once you decommission it, rollback becomes an application redeploy on the new version, not a return to the known-good node version. Keep the old group through the observation window before you call statelessness a safety net. The batched, validated drain matters most when stateful workloads are present and a bad node migration cannot be undone by rescheduling.
  • A cluster with stateful workloads or strict SLAs needs the full model: small drain batches, validation between batches, the observation window, and rehearsed restore. Do not skip the batched drain here just because the small-cluster path allows it.

The check that never scales away is the API-compatibility pre-flight. Everything else flexes with how much an outage would cost.

docs.title: Upgrade Principles
docs.text:
1. Upgrade one minor version at a time unless the platform explicitly supports a larger jump.
  2. Treat add-ons as part of the cluster, not optional extras — version skew between control plane and add-ons is a top cause of breakage.
  3. Upgrade strictly in sequence: control plane → add-ons → nodes.
  4. Prefer new node group + drain over in-place node mutation, so node rollback is "stop draining, uncordon or scale the old group," not "un-upgrade a node."
  5. Validate both Kubernetes health and application behavior before declaring done.
  6. Define rollback triggers up front and keep rollback paths open through the observation window.

docs.title: Pre-Upgrade Assessment
docs.text:
Capture a baseline and run the compatibility checks. This is where you prevent the outage — while everything is still on the old version and reversible.

kubectl version
kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get deploy,statefulset,daemonset -A
kubectl get ingress,svc,pvc,storageclass -A
kubectl get pdb -A                 # check ALLOWED DISRUPTIONS — a PDB at 0 will stall the drain
kubectl top nodes; kubectl top pods -A

Also capture a baseline error rate, latency, and SLO burn rate from your monitoring now, while the cluster is healthy on the old version. The rollback triggers below must be pre-approved numeric thresholds or SLO deltas, not improvised during the incident.

Area Check APIs removed in the target version Find workloads using APIs removed in the target version (e.g. pluto, kubent). Block the upgrade until manifests, Helm charts, GitOps repos, and CI templates stop emitting those APIs. CRDs and conversion webhooks Confirm CRD versions, stored versions, and conversion webhooks are compatible with the target version. A broken conversion webhook can make existing objects unreadable or block controller reconciliation. Admission webhooks Validate mutating/validating webhooks, certificate expiry, timeout policy, failure policy, and API compatibility before the control plane moves. A failing webhook can block pod creation after the upgrade. Aggregated APIs and APIService Check APIService health and extension API compatibility. Broken aggregated APIs can make controllers or CLIs fail even when core Kubernetes looks healthy. Operators and controllers Confirm operator versions, controller-runtime compatibility, leader election, and RBAC against the target Kubernetes version. Controllers often surface APIs removed in the target version hours after the upgrade. Add-ons CNI, CoreDNS, kube-proxy, CSI drivers, ingress controller, cert-manager, metrics-server compatibility with the target version. Workloads PodDisruptionBudgets, probes, resource requests, priority classes, security contexts. Storage CSI driver compatibility, PVC binding, snapshot/backup support. Networking Ingress class behavior, load-balancer annotations, NetworkPolicies. Access IAM/RBAC, service accounts, and a working break-glass path.

A PDB that blocks all eviction, an API removed in the target version, or an incompatible webhook/controller will stall the upgrade midway — find these now, not during the drain.

docs.title: 1. Freeze and announce
docs.text: Pause unrelated deploys and infra changes for the window. Communicate impact, rollback criteria, and owners.

docs.title: 2. Back up cluster state
docs.text:
kubectl get all -A -o yaml > cluster-workloads.snapshot.yaml
kubectl get configmap,ingress,svc,pvc,storageclass -A -o yaml > cluster-config.snapshot.yaml

Do not export Secret values to plaintext. Back up secrets via the platform's encrypted process; for self-managed clusters, back up etcd with the recommended encrypted method (see the etcd standard).

docs.title: 3. Upgrade the control plane
docs.text:
Upgrade via the provider/cluster tool, and wait for the provider-reported upgrade-complete status (e.g. the managed cluster back to ACTIVE), not only a passing /readyz — a managed control-plane upgrade can report healthy on /readyz while the provider is still rolling it out. Then validate API server discovery, admission/conversion webhooks, aggregated APIs, and core controllers before touching add-ons or nodes.

kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose'
kubectl get --raw='/apis' >/dev/null
kubectl get apiservice
kubectl get nodes

docs.title: 4. Upgrade core add-ons to target-compatible versions
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl rollout status deployment/coredns -n kube-system
kubectl get daemonset -n kube-system

Do this before moving workloads — nodes joining on the new version need compatible CNI/kube-proxy. This is the step most often skipped and the one that produces "the control plane upgrade broke DNS" reports that are really add-on skew.

docs.title: 5. Add a new node group, drain the old gradually
docs.text:
Create a new node group on the target version, then move workloads in small batches:

kubectl cordon <old-node>
kubectl drain <old-node> --ignore-daemonsets --delete-emptydir-data --timeout=10m

Validate application health after each batch before continuing. This is the reversible model: if a batch misbehaves, stop and keep the old (still-healthy) nodes.

docs.title: 6. Validate, then decommission old nodes
docs.text: After the observation window, scale down and remove the old node group. Keep snapshots and notes.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl get events -A --sort-by='.lastTimestamp'
kubectl get ingress,pvc -A

Application validation must include: HTTP health, a core API/login smoke test, background-job processing, database connectivity, external webhooks/callbacks, and that alerting/dashboards still work. Declare success only after these pass and metrics are stable through the observation window — not when the new node group is Ready.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response API removed in target version Controllers/CI fail post-upgrade, sometimes hours later Workload, chart, CRD, webhook, or controller still uses an API removed in the target version Find with pluto/kubent before upgrading; migrate manifests and controller dependencies. Webhook/APIService incompatibility Pod creation, controller reconciliation, or CLI discovery fails Admission webhook, conversion webhook, or aggregated API is incompatible or unavailable Validate webhook certificates, failure policies, timeouts, and APIService health before node migration. Add-on version skew DNS/networking/storage flaky after control-plane upgrade Add-ons not upgraded to match Upgrade add-ons before moving nodes. Drain stalls kubectl drain hangs mid-migration PDB blocks eviction / single replica Fix PDB/replicas; never --force blindly. Pods won't schedule Pending after node migration Taints/labels/capacity mismatch on new group Match labels/taints/size; check resource requests. Storage won't attach StatefulSet pods stuck CSI driver incompatibility Verify CSI compatibility in pre-assessment; roll back nodes. Control plane not reversible Can't downgrade after issue Managed upgrades are forward-only Recover via nodes/add-ons/app rollback + backups (below).

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Managed control-plane upgrades are usually not directly reversible — so rollback is designed around the layers that still are. This is exactly why principle 4 (new node group + drain) matters: it keeps node migration reversible even though the control plane is not.

  1. Stop node migration immediately; uncordon or scale the old node group while it is still supported.
  2. Roll back add-ons to compatible versions where safe.
  3. Roll back application deployments (kubectl rollout undo).
  4. Restore stateful components from backups if data was affected.

Define rollback triggers before starting:

  • A core business smoke test fails.
  • A pre-approved SLO trigger is breached for the agreed duration, such as 5xx rate > <threshold>, p95/p99 latency > <threshold>, or burn-rate delta > <threshold> for more than <duration>.
  • Critical pods cannot schedule.
  • Storage attach/mount errors hit stateful workloads.
  • Ingress/load-balancer behavior breaks external traffic.

docs.title: Post-Upgrade Review
docs.text: Record source/target versions, add-on versions before/after, node-group changes, APIs removed in the target version, webhook/APIService findings, incidents and mitigations, and follow-up work.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never export Secret values to plaintext during backup; use encrypted/secret-manager backups.
  • Verify RBAC, IAM, and break-glass access still work on the new version before decommissioning old paths.
  • Do not publish real cluster names, account IDs, endpoints, or node names.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/cluster team.
  • Re-run checks for APIs removed in the target version, webhook/APIService compatibility, controller compatibility, and add-on compatibility before every upgrade.
  • Re-confirm rollback triggers and the backup/restore path before each upgrade window.
  • Track the Kubernetes version support window so upgrades are planned, not forced by end-of-support.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • AWS EKS Production HA Cluster Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • etcd Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines when kubeadm is an acceptable way to build a Kubernetes cluster and what must be decided before the first control-plane node is initialized.

Core judgment:

kubeadm gives you the cluster bootstrap. It also gives you the control-plane operating burden.

A kubeadm cluster is not a managed service with fewer buttons. It is a self-managed control plane. The team owns API availability, etcd recovery, certificate lifecycle, host patching, add-on compatibility, node replacement, upgrade sequencing, audit posture, and rebuild decisions.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use kubeadm when:

  • managed Kubernetes is unavailable, unsuitable, or blocked by infrastructure constraints
  • the team needs explicit ownership of control-plane hosts, PKI, networking, or etcd topology
  • the environment has network, compliance, air-gap, hardware, or locality requirements
  • operators can rehearse backup, restore, certificate rotation, upgrade, and rebuild
  • platform ownership is stable enough to carry the cluster lifecycle

Prefer managed Kubernetes when the team cannot reliably own etcd restore, certificate rotation, control-plane patching, node replacement, and incident recovery.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard is not a command transcript and does not approve publishing real kubeadm join tokens, certificate keys, kubeconfigs, internal endpoints, node names, or production CIDRs. It complements the broader Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before running kubeadm, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters API endpoint Which stable endpoint clients and nodes use? A changing API address breaks kubelets, automation, and humans. Control-plane topology Single node, stacked HA, or external etcd? The topology determines failure and restore behavior. etcd recovery How snapshots are taken, protected, restored, and tested? The cluster state is only recoverable if etcd restore is real. PKI ownership Who owns CA material, certificate expiry, and rotation? Certificate failure can become a cluster-wide outage. CIDR choices Which Pod and Service CIDRs are final? Wrong CIDRs are usually rebuild decisions, not tuneables. CNI choice Which CNI and policy model is installed first? Pod networking assumptions start at cluster init. Add-on order Which versions of CNI, DNS, CSI, ingress, metrics, and observability are approved? Unversioned add-ons create drift before workloads arrive. Host lifecycle How control-plane and worker hosts are patched and replaced? kubeadm does not manage operating systems for you. Access and audit Who can administer the cluster and how actions are logged? Self-managed clusters need explicit accountability. Rebuild boundary Which mistakes trigger rebuild instead of repair? Some bootstrap choices are cheaper to rebuild than to carry forever.

docs.title: Decide topology before initialization
docs.text:
Do not initialize a cluster and later retrofit the production story. Decide first:

  • single control plane or highly available control plane
  • load balancer or virtual IP owner for the API endpoint
  • stacked etcd or external etcd
  • control-plane host placement and anti-affinity
  • node operating-system baseline
  • supported Kubernetes minor version
  • upgrade and rollback sequence

A single-control-plane cluster can be acceptable for labs, edge cases, or explicit low-criticality environments. It should not be called production without a written exception and recovery expectation.

docs.title: Freeze irreversible bootstrap inputs
docs.text:
Treat these as design decisions:

  • Pod CIDR
  • Service CIDR
  • DNS domain
  • API endpoint name
  • CA ownership
  • etcd topology
  • CNI family

Changing them after workloads arrive is often more dangerous than rebuilding a young cluster.

docs.title: Install add-ons as the platform contract
docs.text:
Minimum sequence:

  1. OS and container runtime baseline.
  2. kubeadm, kubelet, and kubectl version alignment.
  3. Control plane initialized against the approved endpoint and CIDRs.
  4. CNI installed and validated.
  5. CoreDNS validated from a pod.
  6. Metrics server installed if autoscaling or resource visibility depends on it.
  7. CSI installed before stateful workloads.
  8. Ingress installed before publishing services.
  9. Observability and log collection installed before application onboarding.
  10. Backup and restore validation completed before critical workloads.

Applications should not be onboarded before CNI, DNS, storage, ingress, observability, and etcd restore have been validated.

docs.title: Own etcd as the cluster state boundary
docs.text:
Required etcd controls:

  • snapshot schedule
  • snapshot retention
  • snapshot encryption and access control
  • restore rehearsal in an isolated environment
  • documented restore target and expected downtime
  • alerting on snapshot failure and disk pressure
  • operator access to restore materials during an incident

If the team cannot restore etcd in a test environment, the cluster is not production-ready.

docs.title: Track certificates before they become an outage
docs.text:
Record:

  • CA owner
  • certificate expiry dates
  • renewal and rotation procedure
  • kubelet client certificate behavior
  • control-plane certificate rotation window
  • backup of CA material and access rules

Certificate material should be protected as cluster-root authority. Do not store it in public repositories, tickets, chat, or examples.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After installation, validate the operating model, not just node readiness:

kubectl get nodes -o wide
kubectl get pods -A
kubectl cluster-info
kubectl get --raw='/readyz?verbose'

Pass conditions:

  • All control-plane components report ready through the API readiness endpoint.
  • Nodes are Ready and version skew is supported.
  • CNI supports pod-to-pod and pod-to-service connectivity.
  • DNS resolves service names from a pod.
  • Services route correctly.
  • Metrics server works if autoscaling or visibility depends on it.
  • StorageClass and PVC provisioning work if stateful workloads are planned.
  • Ingress path works if services are published.
  • Observability and log collection can see control-plane and workload signals.
  • etcd snapshot and restore rehearsal has been performed.
  • Certificate expiry and rotation path are recorded.
  • API endpoint remains stable during control-plane maintenance test or documented exception.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention API endpoint improvised after init Nodes and clients depend on an unstable address. Decide endpoint and ownership before init. Pod or Service CIDR wrong Network conflicts appear after workloads arrive. Freeze CIDRs before bootstrap; rebuild young clusters when wrong. No etcd restore test Cluster state loss becomes unrecoverable. Restore snapshots in isolation before onboarding workloads. Certificate lifecycle ignored Cluster fails unexpectedly at expiry. Track, alert, and rehearse rotation. CNI selected after init Pod networking assumptions drift. Choose CNI and policy model before app onboarding. Add-ons unversioned Upgrade path becomes guesswork. Maintain add-on version matrix. Apps onboarded before observability First failure has no evidence. Install observability and logging before workloads. Host patching undefined Control-plane security or reboot work becomes ad hoc. Define host patch and replacement path.

docs.title: Rebuild or Repair Boundary
docs.text:
Prefer rebuild when:

  • Pod or Service CIDRs are wrong.
  • Control-plane endpoint design is wrong.
  • etcd topology is wrong for production.
  • CA ownership is unclear or compromised.
  • Bootstrap configuration is undocumented.
  • The cluster is young and has no critical workloads.

Prefer in-place repair when:

  • add-on configuration is wrong but data plane is recoverable
  • worker nodes need replacement
  • version skew can be corrected within supported bounds
  • ingress, storage, or observability needs reconfiguration

When critical workloads already exist, rebuild becomes migration. Define cutover, rollback, data movement, and workload validation explicitly.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Protect kubeconfig, CA material, service-account signing keys, and etcd snapshots.
  • Restrict control-plane host access.
  • Use named admin identities and least privilege where possible.
  • Keep audit logs where feasible.
  • Encrypt and restrict etcd backups.
  • Do not publish real cluster endpoints, join tokens, certificate keys, kubeconfigs, node names, or production CIDRs.
  • Treat public examples as placeholders only.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review before:

  • new kubeadm cluster build
  • Kubernetes minor upgrade
  • control-plane topology change
  • certificate rotation
  • etcd restore rehearsal
  • CNI, CSI, ingress, or CoreDNS upgrade
  • host OS patch wave
  • production workload onboarding

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • EKS and Kubernetes Upgrade Standard
  • System OS Patching Standard

docs.title: Executive summary
docs.text:
A production cluster is not a set of kubeadm init or cloud-console parameters. It is an operated platform product that must deliver version boundaries, networking and identity, failure domains, data protection, access paths, observability, upgradeability, and recovery.

mindmap root((Kubernetes Platform)) Version Control plane kubelet and kubectl Add-on matrix Cloud and account Subscription or project Quotas Cost ownership IAM Human access Workload identity Break glass Network VPC or VNet Pod and Service CIDR Egress and private endpoints CNI Routing NetworkPolicy IP capacity CSI StorageClass Topology Snapshot and restore Node pools System General Stateful Spot and GPU Failure domains Zones Capacity Disruption budgets Ingress and Gateway Load balancer TLS DNS and WAF Policy Admission Supply chain Quotas Observability Metrics Logs Events and traces Upgrade API deprecation Add-on order Node replacement Recovery etcd or provider boundary Backup restore Workload migration Cost Requests Autoscaling Idle capacity

The acceptance target is not “the cluster exists.” It is “workloads can onboard safely within explicit ownership and recovery boundaries.”

docs.title: Applicable versions and selection
docs.text:
As of July 2026, Kubernetes maintains 1.36, 1.35, and 1.34. A new cluster should use the latest patch that the provider or distribution supports and whose critical add-on matrix is closed.

Choice Decision Latest 1.36 patch Preferred for a new platform when CNI, CSI, ingress/gateway, admission, operators, and observability all support it Latest 1.35 patch Conservative choice when ecosystem certification for 1.36 is incomplete Latest 1.34 patch Only for a documented dependency blocker, with an upgrade deadline before onboarding 1.33 or earlier Not for a new production cluster

Freeze a compatibility matrix as a release artifact:

Component Current Target Official evidence Owner Order Blocker Control plane <version> <version> <link> Platform 1 kubelet/runtime <version> <version> <link> Platform 4 CNI/CoreDNS <version> <version> <link> Network 2 CSI/snapshot <version> <version> <link> Storage 2 Ingress/Gateway <version> <version> <link> Edge 3 Admission/Operators <version> <version> <link> Security/App 3 Observability agents <version> <version> <link> SRE 3

Do not upgrade when the provider has not opened the target minor, CNI/CSI support is absent, webhooks use removed APIs, CRD conversion is incompatible, or PDB/capacity prevents safe node replacement.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This standard applies to managed Kubernetes services, kubeadm-based self-managed HA clusters, production and shared pre-production platforms, and clusters rebuilt after major network, identity, or runtime changes.

It does not replace application architecture, service-mesh design, regional business disaster recovery, or provider-specific implementation guides. A self-managed control plane also needs dedicated etcd, certificate, and API-endpoint runbooks.

docs.title: Preconditions and provisioning chain
docs.text:
flowchart LR A[Organization and account] --> B[Network and address plan] B --> C[Control plane and API endpoint] C --> D[Node pools and failure domains] D --> E[CNI / CoreDNS / kube-proxy] E --> F[CSI / Snapshot] F --> G[Ingress / Gateway / DNS] G --> H[Identity / Security / Admission] H --> I[Observability] I --> J[Workload namespace onboarding] J --> K[Upgrade and recovery exercise]

Pod and Service CIDRs must be checked against VPC, VPN, peering, and on-premises networks before control-plane creation; identity must be decided before secrets enter the cluster; CSI topology and restore must be proven before stateful onboarding; ingress, DNS, certificates, and observability must be ready before traffic.

Required inputs include owners and on-call responsibility, target versions and matrix, network/DNS/NTP/registry reachability, zone quotas, data-protection targets, release and recovery objectives, and cost ownership.

docs.title: Prohibitions
docs.text:
• Do not treat all Nodes being Ready as production acceptance.
  • Do not allow Pod or Service CIDRs to overlap existing or planned networks.
  • Do not grant permanent personal or pipeline cluster-admin.
  • Do not use static cloud keys as the default workload identity.
  • Do not create the cluster first and improvise CNI, CSI, and ingress versions later.
  • Do not install competing default IngressClasses or StorageClasses.
  • Do not onboard workloads without monitoring, tested restore, and an upgrade path.
  • Do not skip minor releases or treat control-plane downgrade as recovery.

docs.title: Prefer managed control planes; prove self-management
docs.text: Self-management requires three failure-domain-aware control-plane/etcd members, API load balancing, certificate lifecycle, etcd backup and quorum recovery, component upgrades, and continuous on-call ownership. Without people, automation, and exercises, it is not justified.

docs.title: Make platform contracts governable
docs.text: Record every address range and CNI allocation model; separate human, workload, and break-glass identity; make every node pool declare workload class, labels/taints, zones, autoscaling, OS/runtime, and replacement cycle; assign owners to CNI, CSI, ingress, admission, and observability.

docs.title: Establish upgrade and recovery on day one
docs.text: Before onboarding, exercise deprecated APIs, webhooks, add-on order, PDBs, spare capacity, drain, stop conditions, and recovery in non-production.

docs.title: Design example: three-zone production cluster
docs.text:
This is a design example, not evidence from a verified production deployment.

flowchart TB U[Users] --> DNS[DNS and WAF] DNS --> LB[Regional Load Balancer] LB --> GW[Gateway / Ingress] CP[Managed multi-AZ control plane] --> NP1 CP --> NP2 CP --> NP3 subgraph AZ-A NP1[System pool A] APP1[General pool A] ST1[Stateful pool A] end subgraph AZ-B NP2[System pool B] APP2[General pool B] ST2[Stateful pool B] end subgraph AZ-C NP3[System pool C] APP3[General pool C] ST3[Stateful pool C] end GW --> APP1 GW --> APP2 GW --> APP3 ST1 --> CSI[(Regional / zonal CSI)] ST2 --> CSI ST3 --> CSI OBS[Central observability] --- CP OBS --- APP1 OBS --- APP2 OBS --- APP3

The example uses a managed multi-zone control plane, system and general pools in each zone, storage-aware stateful pools, enough spare capacity for one-zone loss, owned gateway/DNS/certificate controls, independent backup and restore beyond CSI snapshots, and GitOps with isolated production write permissions.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text: Design error Impact CIDR overlap Routing black holes and often a rebuild Single-zone capacity A zonal fault exhausts scheduling Missing add-on matrix CNI, CSI, or webhooks block an upgrade Excess default privilege Human or workload escalation Untested restore Healthy PVCs but unusable recovery Monitoring only Node Ready DNS, network, storage, and business failures stay hidden

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Retain evidence for cross-node Pods, Services, DNS, egress and NetworkPolicy; least privilege, workload identity and break-glass; zone distribution, autoscaling and node replacement; PVC remount, expansion, snapshot and restore; TLS, DNS, logs and ingress failover; injected DNS/CNI/CSI/scheduling failures and alerts; upgrade order, PDB behavior, drain and stop conditions; node-pool rebuild, data restore and workload migration.

A green CI run, Ready, or one smoke test is not enough.

docs.title: Exception handling
docs.text: Every exception records rationale, risk, owner, expiry, and exit plan. A single-zone edge site needs spare hardware and rebuild procedures; a blocked minor needs an explicit deadline; absent workload identity requires short-lived brokered credentials; absent CSI snapshots requires application-level backups and a restore exercise.

docs.title: Recovery and rollback principles
docs.text:
• Managed control-plane upgrades recover through forward repair and compatible node pools, not downgrade.
  • Node changes recover through a new pool, cordon/drain, and batched migration.
  • CNI, CSI, ingress, and admission upgrades need rollback and configuration backups.
  • Self-managed clusters need tested etcd backup and fresh-control-plane restoration.
  • Workload recovery distinguishes stateless rebuild, PVC restore, and cross-cluster migration.
  • GitOps declarative state must match runtime recovery.

docs.text:

  • Kubernetes version skew policy

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This runbook defines how to respond when a Kubernetes PersistentVolumeClaim approaches or reaches its capacity threshold.

The core judgment is:

A PVC usage alert is a data-risk alert, not a disk-cleanup ticket.

The response must identify the owning workload, confirm real filesystem usage, understand whether the data is disposable or business-critical, and choose a recovery path that does not silently delete state.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook for alerts such as:

  • KubePersistentVolumeFillingUp
  • KubePersistentVolumeUsageHigh
  • KubeletVolumeStatsUsedBytes threshold alerts
  • NFS or shared filesystem capacity alerts linked to Kubernetes PVCs
  • Application alerts that indicate failed writes caused by full storage

Recommended threshold model:

Severity Suggested Threshold Expected Response Warning 75 percent used or forecast full within 7 days Review owner and growth pattern. High 85 percent used or forecast full within 72 hours Prepare cleanup or expansion. Critical 90 to 95 percent used or writes failing Act immediately and escalate if owner is unavailable.

Adjust thresholds for workload growth rate. A slow archival volume and a rapidly growing upload volume should not share the same operational urgency.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before acting, confirm:

  • Namespace, PVC name, PV name, StorageClass, and owning workload.
  • Whether the volume is block, file, local, cloud disk, or NFS-backed.
  • Whether the data is disposable, cache-like, reconstructable, or business-critical.
  • Whether expansion is supported by the StorageClass and backend.
  • Whether backup or snapshot exists for business-critical data.
  • Whether the application owner is available.

If ownership is unknown and the alert is critical, escalate to the platform owner and incident commander before deleting anything.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
Check whether the application is already affected:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc>
kubectl -n <namespace> describe pvc <pvc>
kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -o wide | grep <workload-or-pvc-hint>
kubectl -n <namespace> get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

Look for:

  • Failed writes.
  • Pod restarts.
  • Readiness failures.
  • Application errors related to no space left on device.
  • Ingress or API errors caused by the workload.
  • Queue backlog or delayed jobs.

If user impact exists, treat the alert as an incident, not routine maintenance.

docs.title: Confirm Real Usage
docs.text:
Kubernetes PVC capacity and actual filesystem usage are not always the same signal.

For kubelet volume stats:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc> -o wide
kubectl -n <namespace> describe pod <pod-using-pvc>

From inside an approved diagnostic pod or the application pod:

df -h <mount-path>
du -xh --max-depth=1 <mount-path> 2>/dev/null | sort -h

For NFS-backed PVCs, confirm usage on the NFS server or the platform monitoring path as well. A PVC may request 100Gi, while the real shared filesystem is a larger pool with many claim directories.

Record:

  • PVC requested capacity.
  • Filesystem total, used, available, and percent used.
  • Largest directories or file classes.
  • Recent growth rate.
  • Owning workload and owner.

docs.title: Decision Tree
docs.text:
Choose the response based on data type and growth behavior:

Condition Preferred Action Disposable cache or temporary files consume space. Clean up through the application-approved path. Business-critical data is growing normally. Expand the volume or backend capacity. Business-critical data is growing unexpectedly. Expand only if needed for safety, then investigate growth source. Logs or exports are written to persistent data path. Stop the writer or change path, then clean up approved files. NFS backend pool is filling across many PVCs. Treat as shared storage incident; capacity action belongs to NFS layer. StorageClass does not support expansion. Migrate to a larger volume or new storage profile. Owner is unknown. Do not delete. Escalate and apply only non-destructive mitigation.

docs.title: 1. Identify owner and data class
docs.text:
Find the workload using the PVC:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pods -o jsonpath='{range .items[*]}{.metadata.name}{"\t"}{range .spec.volumes[*]}{.persistentVolumeClaim.claimName}{" "}{end}{"\n"}{end}' | grep <pvc>

Identify labels, deployment, StatefulSet, CronJob, or Helm release:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pod <pod> --show-labels
kubectl -n <namespace> get pod <pod> -o jsonpath='{range .metadata.ownerReferences[*]}{.kind}{"/"}{.name}{"\n"}{end}'

If JSONPath output is unclear, inspect the pod owner references and events with:

kubectl -n <namespace> describe pod <pod>

docs.title: 2. Stop unsafe growth if needed
docs.text:
If the volume is filling fast, stop the source before cleaning:

  • Pause the job or CronJob writing the data.
  • Disable the feature flag producing files.
  • Scale down a non-critical writer if approved.
  • Redirect logs or exports away from the persistent path.

Do not scale down stateful systems blindly. For databases, brokers, and queues, follow the service-specific runbook.

docs.title: 3. Clean only approved data
docs.text:
Cleanup is allowed only when the data class is understood.

Safe examples:

  • Expired temporary files owned by the application.
  • Rebuildable cache directories approved by the service owner.
  • Completed export files after retention period.
  • Duplicate diagnostic dumps after incident evidence is preserved.

Unsafe examples:

  • Database directories.
  • Queue segments.
  • User uploads.
  • Object metadata.
  • Files with unknown ownership.

Before deletion, prefer moving a small sample to a quarantine path when the application owner needs proof.

docs.title: 4. Expand when data must be retained
docs.text:
Confirm expansion support:

kubectl get storageclass <storageclass> -o yaml
kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc> -o yaml

If supported, request a larger size:

kubectl -n <namespace> patch pvc <pvc> \
  --type merge \
  -p '{"spec":{"resources":{"requests":{"storage":"<new-size>"}}}}'

Then verify:

kubectl -n <namespace> get pvc <pvc> -w
kubectl -n <namespace> describe pvc <pvc>

Check the mounted filesystem after expansion. For some drivers, filesystem resize completes only after pod restart or remount.

docs.title: 5. Migrate when expansion is not available
docs.text:
If the StorageClass cannot expand or the backend is wrong for the workload:

  • Provision a new PVC using the approved storage profile.
  • Stop writes.
  • Copy data with an approved tool and checksum or application-level validation.
  • Start the workload against the new PVC.
  • Keep the old PVC retained until validation and rollback window expire.

Do not delete the old PVC immediately after migration unless the data is explicitly disposable.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
The response is complete only when:

Check Pass Condition Usage PVC or backend usage is below the recovery threshold and no longer rising unexpectedly. Application Writes succeed and readiness is stable. Alerts Critical alert cleared or downgraded with an owner and deadline. Data safety No business-critical data was deleted without owner approval. Monitoring Dashboards show the same capacity reality as the application sees. Follow-up Growth cause, owner, and next review date are recorded.

docs.title: Rollback and Escalation
docs.text:
Cleanup rollback is only possible if files were moved or backed up before deletion. If data was deleted, recovery depends on backups or application reconstruction.

Escalate when:

  • User-facing writes are failing.
  • The PVC is above critical threshold and owner is unavailable.
  • Data class is unknown.
  • Expansion fails or stays pending.
  • Backend storage is full even after PVC-level action.
  • Multiple PVCs on the same NFS or shared storage backend are filling.

Include namespace, PVC, PV, StorageClass, workload, usage percent, growth rate, and suspected largest paths.

docs.title: Post-Incident Review
docs.text:
After recovery, answer:

  • Why was the data growth not predicted earlier?
  • Should the threshold or forecast alert change?
  • Does the workload need retention policy?
  • Should logs, exports, or temporary files move to object storage?
  • Is the StorageClass appropriate for this workload?
  • Does the owner mapping need to be added to the platform inventory?

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to design and validate Kubernetes storage and ingress before business workloads depend on them.

The core judgment is:

Storage and ingress are not add-ons. They are production contracts between the platform and every application team.

Storage decides whether state survives, expands, moves, and restores. Ingress decides how users reach services, how TLS is renewed, how traffic is logged, and where the external boundary of the cluster begins. If either is treated as a default installed component, production risk becomes invisible until the first outage.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Provisioning a new Kubernetes cluster.
  • Introducing or changing a CSI driver, StorageClass, IngressClass, or ingress controller.
  • Onboarding stateful workloads.
  • Publishing services through public or private ingress.
  • Reviewing a cluster before it becomes a shared production platform.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Full database backup architecture.
  • Service mesh traffic policy.
  • Cloud-specific load balancer billing optimization.
  • Application-level data model design.
  • Every possible ingress controller feature.

docs.title: Design Principle
docs.text:
A production platform should expose a small number of named choices, not a large pile of implicit defaults.

Application teams should be able to answer:

  • Which StorageClass should I use for normal persistent data?
  • Which StorageClass supports expansion?
  • Which storage profile supports ReadWriteMany, if any?
  • Who owns backup and restore?
  • Which IngressClass should expose public traffic?
  • Which IngressClass should expose private traffic?
  • Who owns DNS and TLS renewal?
  • What request limits and timeout defaults apply?

If these answers require reading the platform install history, the platform contract is not clear enough.

docs.title: Define storage profiles explicitly
docs.text:
At minimum, define storage profiles like this:

Profile Typical Use Access Mode Expansion Backup Expectation Notes standard-rwo Normal application persistence ReadWriteOnce Supported Application or platform policy Default only if it is safe for most apps. fast-rwo Latency-sensitive state ReadWriteOnce Supported Required Use only when performance need is proven. shared-rwx Shared files, uploads, model artifacts ReadWriteMany Platform-defined Required Often NFS or managed file storage; monitor real filesystem usage. ephemeral Disposable cache or temporary state Depends Not guaranteed None Must not hold business-critical data.

Do not make a StorageClass default until its behavior under provision, mount, expansion, deletion, and failure is known.

docs.title: Make reclaim policy a deliberate decision
docs.text:
Delete is convenient but can destroy data when a PVC is deleted. Retain protects data but creates cleanup responsibility.

Use this rule:

  • Use Delete only for disposable or easily recreated data.
  • Use Retain for business data unless a backup and restore contract says otherwise.
  • Document who can delete retained volumes and how they verify the data is no longer needed.

docs.title: Treat expansion as a runbook, not a checkbox
docs.text:
allowVolumeExpansion: true only means Kubernetes accepts the resize request. It does not prove that the underlying filesystem, CSI driver, quota, and application can safely absorb the change.

Before approving expansion for a profile, verify:

  • The CSI driver supports online or offline expansion.
  • The filesystem reflects the new size.
  • The application tolerates the resize path.
  • Monitoring follows the real filesystem after expansion.
  • Rollback expectations are clear. Shrinking a volume is usually not a safe rollback.

docs.title: Separate backup responsibility from storage provisioning
docs.text:
Provisioning a PV is not the same thing as protecting the data in it.

For every storage profile, document:

  • Whether backups are required.
  • Whether snapshots are crash-consistent or application-consistent.
  • Who owns restore testing.
  • How long recovery is expected to take.
  • Which workloads must not use the profile.

docs.title: CSI Validation
docs.text:
Validate CSI before onboarding stateful workloads:

kubectl get csidrivers
kubectl get storageclass
kubectl get pods -n kube-system
kubectl get events -A --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

Then run a real PVC test:

apiVersion: v1
kind: PersistentVolumeClaim
metadata:
  name: storage-validation
spec:
  accessModes:
    - ReadWriteOnce
  storageClassName: standard-rwo
  resources:
    requests:
      storage: 1Gi
---
apiVersion: v1
kind: Pod
metadata:
  name: storage-validation
spec:
  restartPolicy: Never
  containers:
    - name: writer
