      image: busybox:1.36
      command: ["sh", "-c", "date > /data/proof && cat /data/proof"]
      volumeMounts:
        - name: data
          mountPath: /data
  volumes:
    - name: data
      persistentVolumeClaim:
        claimName: storage-validation

Pass conditions:

  • PVC becomes Bound.
  • Pod schedules on an expected node.
  • File write succeeds.
  • Events show no CSI errors.
  • Deleting the test resources produces the intended reclaim behavior.

docs.title: Define ingress classes as exposure contracts
docs.text:
IngressClass names should communicate exposure and ownership.

Recommended pattern:

IngressClass Exposure Typical Owner Required Controls public-web Internet-facing Platform or edge team TLS, DNS, access logs, WAF or gateway review if required. private-web Internal network Platform team Internal load balancer, private DNS, access logs. partner-web Restricted external access Platform plus security Allowlist, TLS, audit, explicit approval.

Avoid leaving multiple ingress controllers active without a defaulting rule. An application should not be exposed through the wrong controller because an annotation was omitted.

docs.title: Own TLS as a lifecycle
docs.text:
TLS is not complete when the first certificate is issued.

Document:

  • Certificate issuer.
  • Renewal mechanism.
  • DNS validation owner.
  • Expiry alert.
  • Secret namespace and access policy.
  • Emergency replacement procedure.

If TLS renewal is manual, the service must have an expiry alert and a named owner.

docs.title: Standardize ingress defaults
docs.text:
Set and document defaults for:

  • Request timeout.
  • Body size.
  • WebSocket support.
  • HTTP to HTTPS redirect.
  • Access log format.
  • Real client IP handling.
  • Health check path.
  • Rate limiting or WAF boundary if used.

These values affect application behavior. They must not be hidden inside an ingress controller chart with no platform documentation.

docs.title: Ingress Validation
docs.text:
Use a small validation service before exposing real traffic:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: ingress-validation
  annotations:
    cert-manager.io/cluster-issuer: letsencrypt-production
spec:
  ingressClassName: public-web
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - example.devops.test
      secretName: ingress-validation-tls
  rules:
    - host: example.devops.test
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: ingress-validation
                port:
                  number: 80

Pass conditions:

  • Ingress is assigned to the intended controller.
  • Load balancer or gateway address appears.
  • DNS resolves to the expected target.
  • TLS certificate is valid and renewable.
  • Access logs and request metrics appear.
  • A 404, 5xx, and slow request can be observed in the expected dashboard or query path.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text: Area Check StorageClass Default is deliberate, documented, and tested. CSI Controller and node plugins are healthy across node pools. PVC Provision, mount, write, delete, and expansion behavior are known. Reclaim Data retention behavior matches workload criticality. Backup Restore owner and restore test expectation are documented. IngressClass Public and private exposure paths are distinct. TLS Issuer, renewal, expiry alert, and emergency replacement are known. Logs and metrics Storage and ingress failures are observable. Ownership Platform and application responsibilities are separate.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Symptom Prevention Missing default StorageClass PVC stays Pending. Either define a safe default or require explicit storageClassName. Wrong access mode Pod cannot mount volume or multi-writer workload corrupts data. Map workloads to approved profiles. CSI IAM or identity failure PVC never provisions, events show cloud permission errors. Validate workload identity before onboarding. Unclear reclaim policy Data is deleted too early or retained forever. Document reclaim policy and cleanup owner. NFS used as invisible capacity pool PVC appears fine while backend filesystem fills. Monitor real filesystem usage and PVC metadata together. Wrong IngressClass Service becomes unreachable or publicly exposed unintentionally. Use explicit ingressClassName and admission checks where possible. TLS renewal missing Service fails when certificate expires. Add expiry alert and named renewal owner. Controller defaults hidden Large uploads, WebSockets, or slow requests fail unexpectedly. Publish default timeout and body-size policy.

docs.title: Rollback and Change Boundary
docs.text:
Storage and ingress changes have different rollback properties.

Usually reversible:

  • Adding a new StorageClass.
  • Adding a new IngressClass.
  • Changing annotations on a validation ingress.
  • Replacing an ingress controller configuration after a canary.

High-risk or not safely reversible:

  • Changing the default StorageClass after workloads already depend on it.
  • Changing reclaim policy without a migration plan.
  • Shrinking storage.
  • Moving a stateful workload between storage backends without backup and restore proof.
  • Replacing a public ingress path without DNS, TLS, and client cache planning.

If the rollback path is "restore from backup," the change is not a normal configuration rollback. Treat it as a data recovery operation.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real hostnames, certificate details, private IPs, or customer domains in examples.
  • Restrict access to TLS secrets.
  • Avoid mounting broad cloud credentials into CSI components.
  • Use least-privilege workload identity for storage drivers.
  • Review public ingress exposure before DNS is delegated.
  • Keep access logs available for incident review, but avoid logging sensitive request bodies.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • Before a new cluster accepts workloads.
  • Before changing CSI drivers, StorageClasses, ingress controllers, or IngressClasses.
  • After any data-loss, storage saturation, certificate expiry, or public exposure incident.
  • Before onboarding a new class of stateful workload.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to manage capacity for Kubernetes PVCs backed by NFS or NFS-like shared file storage.

The core judgment is:

NFS-backed PVC capacity is a shared filesystem problem wearing Kubernetes metadata.

A PVC request tells Kubernetes what the workload asked for. It does not always tell you how much space exists on the backend filesystem, how many other PVCs share that backend, or whether the backend can enforce per-claim limits. Capacity management must join both layers.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Kubernetes PVCs are provisioned from an NFS server, managed file service, or shared filesystem.
  • Multiple PVCs map to directories under the same backend export.
  • PVC usage alerts do not match backend filesystem usage.
  • Application teams use shared files, uploads, model artifacts, reports, or batch outputs.
  • The platform needs a standard for capacity ownership and alert thresholds.

This standard is especially important when NFS is used as a convenient ReadWriteMany solution. Convenience does not remove the need for capacity isolation, monitoring, and cleanup rules.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Designing high-availability NFS from scratch.
  • Database storage tuning.
  • Replacing object storage for large immutable artifacts.
  • Full backup architecture for shared files.
  • Vendor-specific file service operations.

docs.title: Two-Layer Capacity Model
docs.text:
Manage NFS PVC capacity as two related but different layers:

Layer What It Shows What It Cannot Prove Kubernetes PVC metadata Namespace, claim name, requested size, StorageClass, PV, mount relationship. Real backend free space, shared usage, filesystem pressure. NFS backend filesystem Actual total, used, available, inode use, per-directory growth. Kubernetes owner, namespace, workload intent, data criticality.

Neither layer is enough by itself.

Kubernetes-only monitoring can miss a backend filesystem filling up across many claims. NFS-only monitoring can tell you a directory is large but not which namespace, workload, and team owns it.

docs.title: Required Inventory
docs.text:
Maintain an inventory that maps each PVC to backend reality:

Field Example Namespace payments-prod PVC uploads-data PV pvc-abc123 StorageClass shared-rwx Backend export nfs-shared-01:/exports/k8s Backend path /exports/k8s/payments-prod/uploads-data Workload payments-api Owner Platform or application team alias Data class Uploads, cache, reports, artifacts, logs, unknown Retention 30 days, owner-defined, or none Backup Required, not required, or unknown Expansion model Backend pool expansion, directory quota, or migration

If the platform cannot map a full directory to an owner, it must not delete from that directory.

docs.title: Standard Thresholds
docs.text:
Use two sets of thresholds: backend filesystem thresholds and per-PVC or per-directory growth thresholds.

Signal Warning Critical Action Backend filesystem used percent 75% 90% Review growth, plan expansion, or clean approved data. Backend inode usage 70% 85% Find small-file sources and owner. Single PVC directory growth 20% in 24h 40% in 24h Identify writer and data class. Forecast full 7 days 72 hours Prepare capacity action. Unknown owner data Any sustained growth Any critical pressure Escalate; do not delete blindly.

Tune thresholds for backend size and business risk. Large filesystems may need forecast-based alerts earlier than percent-based alerts.

docs.title: Collect Kubernetes metadata
docs.text:
Collect:

  • PVC name.
  • Namespace.
  • PV name.
  • StorageClass.
  • Requested capacity.
  • Volume mode.
  • Access mode.
  • Workload using the claim.

Kubernetes metrics alone do not always expose backend path. If the provisioner writes path information into PV attributes or annotations, ingest it into the inventory.

docs.title: Collect backend filesystem usage
docs.text:
Collect from the NFS server, managed file service metrics, or an approved observation host:

  • Filesystem total, used, available, and percent used.
  • Inode total, used, available, and percent used.
  • Per-export usage.
  • Per-directory usage where feasible.
  • Growth rate.
  • Top directories by size and file count.

For Linux NFS servers, platform-owned collection may use commands like:

df -h /exports/k8s
df -i /exports/k8s
du -xh --max-depth=2 /exports/k8s 2>/dev/null | sort -h

Do not run expensive recursive scans during peak hours on a busy file server without understanding the impact.

docs.title: Join the two layers
docs.text:
Capacity dashboards and alerts should show:

  • Backend filesystem pressure.
  • Top PVC directories.
  • Namespace and owner.
  • Workload and data class.
  • Growth trend.
  • Last review date.

The useful question is not "which disk is full?" but "which workload and data class is consuming shared capacity, and what action is safe?"

docs.title: 1. Classify data before action
docs.text:
Use this classification:

Data Class Default Action Cache Clean by approved application method or recreate. Temporary export Apply retention and move long-term artifacts elsewhere. User upload Do not delete without owner approval and backup confirmation. Application state Treat as business data; expand or migrate. Logs Move to logging pipeline or object storage; clean after retention. Unknown Escalate; no destructive action.

docs.title: 2. Choose the capacity action
docs.text: Situation Action Backend has enough capacity but one PVC directory is growing. Work with owner; enforce retention, move data, or resize claim expectation. Backend filesystem is filling because many claims share one pool. Expand backend, split tenants, or migrate high-growth claims. Inodes are filling before bytes. Find small-file workload, archive or compact, and change application behavior. NFS performance degrades under load. Reduce scan pressure, split workload, or move to a more suitable storage backend. PVC request does not match actual usage. Update inventory and alerting; do not rely on request size alone.

docs.title: 3. Expand deliberately
docs.text:
For NFS, expansion may mean increasing the backend filesystem or changing a quota, not patching an individual PVC.

Before expansion:

  • Confirm backend pool headroom or provider quota.
  • Confirm backup or snapshot posture.
  • Confirm whether the expansion affects all tenants.
  • Confirm alert thresholds after expansion.
  • Record the new capacity and expected forecast.

docs.title: 4. Migrate when NFS is the wrong abstraction
docs.text:
NFS is often misused for data that belongs elsewhere.

Consider migration when:

  • Large immutable artifacts would fit object storage better.
  • A database is placed on shared file storage without a strong reason.
  • A single tenant dominates the shared filesystem.
  • Application performance depends on filesystem semantics NFS cannot guarantee.
  • Cleanup requires risky manual file deletion.

docs.title: Cleanup Safety
docs.text:
Never delete from NFS solely because a directory is large.

Minimum deletion requirements:

  • Owner identified.
  • Data class identified.
  • Retention policy or explicit approval confirmed.
  • Backup or non-critical classification confirmed.
  • Exact path reviewed.
  • Command scoped to the intended directory.
  • Result validated.

Prefer application-level deletion over platform shell deletion. The application usually knows which files are indexed, referenced, or safe to remove.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
This standard is implemented when:

Check Pass Condition Inventory Each NFS-backed PVC maps to namespace, workload, owner, backend export, and path. Metrics Backend bytes, backend inodes, and PVC metadata are visible together. Alerts Backend pressure and rapid per-directory growth are both detected. Ownership Unknown-owner directories are tracked as risk. Response Cleanup, expansion, migration, and escalation paths are documented. Safety No destructive cleanup occurs without owner and data classification.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Monitoring only PVC requested size Backend fills while PVCs appear normal. Monitor backend filesystem usage. Monitoring only NFS server Large directory has no clear Kubernetes owner. Maintain PVC-to-path inventory. Shared pool with no tenant separation One workload consumes capacity for all workloads. Use quotas, separate exports, or separate storage classes. Manual deletion of unknown files Data loss or application corruption. Require owner and data class before cleanup. Recursive scans during peak load NFS latency worsens during investigation. Schedule scans or use lightweight metrics. No inode alerting Millions of tiny files break writes before bytes fill. Alert on inode usage and file count growth.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real export paths, server names, IPs, or customer directory names.
  • Restrict shell access to NFS servers.
  • Avoid broad read access to shared application files.
  • Treat user uploads and reports as potentially sensitive.
  • Keep cleanup commands out of public docs unless they are generic and placeholder-based.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • Before onboarding a new NFS-backed StorageClass.
  • After any NFS capacity or performance incident.
  • When a tenant becomes a top capacity consumer.
  • Before increasing backend capacity by a material amount.
  • When application data retention changes.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard

docs.title: Linux
docs.text:
Server baselines, patching, process management, disks, and LVM operations.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

3 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Linux Server Baseline Standard Standard for a safe, repeatable Linux server baseline — identity, patching, SSH hardening, kernel/limits, firewall, and validation, with no secrets in bootstrap.
  • Supervisor Process Management Standard Supervisor process management standard for bounded legacy Linux services, including ownership, restart discipline, validation, rollback, and exit criteria.
  • System OS Patching Standard System OS patching standard for Linux fleets with ownership, exposure-based urgency, maintenance waves, reboot proof, validation, rollback boundaries, exceptions, and compliance evidence.
1 document Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook Runbook for safely expanding Linux storage with LVM by proving device identity, backup status, filesystem type, forward-only risk, validation, and escalation.

docs.title: Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook
Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when a Linux volume is approaching capacity and must be expanded, typically for:

  • filesystem usage alert above the agreed threshold
  • planned growth ahead of predictable demand
  • incident response where a full filesystem causes write failures
  • database, log, registry, or application data volume expansion

Core judgment:

The risky command is rarely lvextend. The risky moment is deciding which block device is safe to touch.

Most severe failures come from acting on the wrong disk, expanding without a verified backup, or treating an online expansion as easily reversible. XFS cannot be shrunk online, and ext4 shrink is offline and risky. Treat expansion as a forward change whose rollback is usually restore.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before any destructive command, confirm:

  • verified backup exists and restore owner is reachable
  • application or service owner approves the target mount
  • exact mountpoint, logical volume, volume group, and new disk are identified
  • new disk is unused: no filesystem, no mountpoint, no partition table that belongs to another system, and not already in a VG
  • filesystem type is known: XFS and ext4 use different grow tools
  • change window or incident approval exists
  • rollback owner and storage/platform owner are reachable
  • monitoring path for disk usage is known

Do not proceed if device identity is ambiguous.

docs.title: Impact Check
docs.text:
Collect current state:

lsblk -f
blkid
df -hT
sudo pvs
sudo vgs
sudo lvs -a -o +devices
findmnt <mountpoint>

Write down:

  • target mountpoint
  • existing logical volume
  • existing volume group
  • filesystem type
  • new disk device name
  • current filesystem usage
  • expected new capacity
  • backup reference

If the host is virtualized or uses SAN/cloud disks, confirm the new device mapping with the platform that attached the disk. Device names can change across reboot; prefer stable identifiers where practical.

docs.title: Safety Gate
docs.text:
Before pvcreate, prove all of these are true:

Check Required Evidence New disk has no mountpoint lsblk -f shows no mountpoint. New disk is not already in LVM pvs does not list it. New disk has no needed filesystem blkid result is empty or approved for overwrite. Target VG/LV are correct vgs, lvs, and findmnt match the target mount. Backup exists Backup ID or restore point is recorded privately. Owner approval exists Service/storage owner agrees with target and timing.

If any item is uncertain, stop. A wrong pvcreate can destroy data faster than an incident bridge can react.

docs.title: 1. Add the new disk to LVM
docs.text:
Example shape after the safety gate has passed. Replace placeholders only after the target device has been proven:

sudo pvcreate /dev/CONFIRMED_UNUSED_DISK
sudo vgextend TARGET_VG /dev/CONFIRMED_UNUSED_DISK

Do not paste this block as-is. In production, re-check the actual device immediately before running the command.

docs.title: 2. Extend the logical volume
docs.text:
sudo lvextend -l +100%FREE /dev/TARGET_VG/TARGET_LV

Use a smaller explicit size instead of +100%FREE when the volume group also supports other volumes or when future headroom must stay reserved.

docs.title: 3. Grow the filesystem
docs.text:
Match the command to the filesystem:

# XFS: grow by mountpoint
sudo xfs_growfs /CONFIRMED_MOUNTPOINT

# ext4: grow by block device
sudo resize2fs /dev/TARGET_VG/TARGET_LV

Alternatively, lvextend -r grows the LV and filesystem in one step, but only use it when the filesystem type and target LV have been verified.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Run:

df -hT
sudo pvs
sudo vgs
sudo lvs -a -o +devices
dmesg | tail

Pass conditions:

  • target mountpoint shows expected increased capacity
  • filesystem type is unchanged
  • application can write to the expanded path
  • disk usage alert clears or reflects the new capacity
  • no filesystem, I/O, or multipath errors appear
  • backup and disk-layout documentation are updated
  • growth trend is recorded so the next expansion is planned

For application paths, validate with a safe write or application-level smoke test rather than only df.

docs.title: Rollback and Escalation
docs.text:
There is no clean online shrink for XFS, and ext4 shrink is offline and risky. Treat expansion as forward-only.

If the wrong device was modified:

  1. Stop further LVM or filesystem commands.
  2. Preserve command history and current device state.
  3. Escalate to storage/platform owner.
  4. Restore from verified backup if data is damaged.

If lvextend succeeded but filesystem grow failed, the LV is larger while the filesystem is unchanged. Do not panic-shrink. Diagnose the filesystem grow step and retry only after confirming the target.

Escalate when:

  • device identity is unclear
  • VG has no free space
  • filesystem reports errors
  • disk belongs to shared/SAN/cloud-managed storage
  • multipath or device naming is unstable
  • backup cannot be verified

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Wrong disk passed to pvcreate Data loss on another volume. Prove device identity with lsblk, blkid, pvs, and owner approval. No restore-tested backup Recovery depends on hope. Verify backup before destructive commands. Wrong filesystem grow command Expansion fails or damages confidence. Match XFS to mountpoint and ext4 to block device. +100%FREE used blindly Other volumes lose reserved headroom. Use explicit size when VG is shared. Alert not reloaded Monitoring still reports old state or misses new threshold. Validate monitoring after expansion. Documentation not updated Next incident repeats device-discovery risk. Record new layout and growth trend.

docs.title: Post-Check
docs.text:
• Capacity headroom is above alert threshold with margin.
  • Disk layout record matches current lsblk, pvs, vgs, and lvs.
  • Monitoring reflects new capacity.
  • Backup schedule still covers the expanded data.
  • Next capacity review date or growth forecast is recorded.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Linux Server Baseline Standard
  • Kubernetes PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines a safe, repeatable baseline for preparing Linux servers for production workloads: what every new server gets before it runs anything, hardened in a way you can re-apply and verify.

The core judgment of this standard:

A baseline is only useful if it is repeatable and auditable. A server configured by hand, once, drifts and cannot be trusted; a baseline that bakes a real SSH key, password, or internal mirror into a bootstrap script becomes a leaked credential the moment it is shared. Make it idempotent, and keep secrets out of it.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • New servers, rebuilds, and post-provisioning hardening.
  • Defining the baseline a configuration-management tool (Ansible, cloud-init, image build) should enforce.
  • Auditing whether an existing host meets a known-good baseline.

docs.title: Scope and Non-Goals
docs.text: Applies to common distributions (RHEL/Rocky-compatible, Ubuntu/Debian). Provider-specific build steps belong in cloud image templates, not here. This standard does not cover application deployment, container-host specifics (see the Docker host standard), or full CIS-benchmark compliance — it is the practical baseline beneath those.

docs.title: Baseline Principles
docs.text:
1. Make every step idempotent so the baseline can be re-applied safely.
  2. Keep access control separate from application deployment.
  3. Prefer SSH key authentication; disable password and root login.
  4. Record every non-default setting; an unrecorded tweak is future drift.
  5. Never embed real users, public keys, passwords, or internal mirror URLs in public scripts — use placeholders.
  6. Validate after each major block, not just at the end.

docs.title: 1. Identify the system
docs.text:
cat /etc/os-release; uname -a; hostnamectl

Record distribution, kernel, architecture, timezone, hostname, and primary interface.

docs.title: 2. Patch and install base tooling
docs.text:
# RHEL/Rocky:
sudo dnf makecache && sudo dnf update -y
sudo dnf install -y vim curl wget tar unzip lsof sysstat chrony bash-completion
# Debian/Ubuntu:
sudo apt-get update && sudo apt-get upgrade -y
sudo apt-get install -y vim curl wget tar unzip lsof sysstat chrony bash-completion

Patching is part of the baseline, not a later task — see the OS patching standard for the ongoing cadence.

docs.title: 3. Time synchronization
docs.text:
sudo systemctl enable --now chronyd 2>/dev/null || sudo systemctl enable --now chrony
timedatectl
chronyc sources -v

Clock skew breaks TLS, tokens, logs, and distributed systems; treat working time sync as mandatory.

docs.title: 4. SSH hardening
docs.text:
PermitRootLogin no
PasswordAuthentication no
PubkeyAuthentication yes
UseDNS no

Validate before reloading so a bad config never locks you out:

sudo sshd -t
sudo systemctl reload sshd 2>/dev/null || sudo systemctl reload ssh

Keep an active session open while changing SSH, and confirm a new login works before closing it. Use placeholders in any published example (example-user, ssh-ed25519 AAAA... <key-comment>) — never a real key.

docs.title: 5. Kernel parameters and limits
docs.text:
cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/sysctl.d/99-baseline.conf
vm.swappiness = 10
fs.file-max = 1048576
EOF
sudo sysctl --system

cat <<'EOF' | sudo tee /etc/security/limits.d/99-baseline.conf
* soft nofile 65535
* hard nofile 65535
* soft nproc 65535
* hard nproc 65535
EOF

Set net.ipv4.ip_forward = 1 only on hosts that actually route (gateways, container hosts) — not as a blanket default.

docs.title: 6. Firewall: default-deny inbound
docs.text:
Open only documented ports. On cloud hosts, align the OS firewall with security groups rather than running two conflicting policies.

sudo systemctl enable --now firewalld
sudo firewall-cmd --add-service=ssh --permanent
sudo firewall-cmd --reload

docs.title: 7. Data disks
docs.text: Inspect before touching any disk; only format disks explicitly approved and not mounted. See the Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook.

docs.title: Validation Checklist
docs.text:
• Packages updated; reboot taken if a kernel update requires it.
  • Time sync enabled and healthy (chronyc sources).
  • sshd -t passes; password and root login disabled; key login confirmed.
  • Firewall enabled (or deliberately delegated to cloud security groups).
  • Baseline sysctl and limits applied (sysctl -a | grep swappiness, ulimit -n).
  • Disk layout documented.
  • No plaintext credentials, real keys, or internal URLs in any bootstrap script.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Locked out of SSH Cannot log in after change Reloaded a bad sshd config / disabled key login without a working key Always sshd -t first; keep a session open; fix via console. Drift Hosts diverge over time One-off manual changes Drive the baseline from config management; re-apply idempotently. Secret leaked Credential exposed Real key/password baked into a shared script Use placeholders + secret injection; rotate the leaked secret. Clock-skew failures TLS/token/log errors Time sync not running Ensure chrony is enabled and sources are reachable. Conflicting firewalls Intermittent connectivity OS firewall vs cloud SG disagree Pick one authority; align rules.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Each block is idempotent and independently reversible; keep backups of files you modify (sshd_config, sysctl, limits).
  2. To roll back SSH changes, restore the previous sshd_config, sshd -t, reload — using a console session if locked out.
  3. For sysctl/limits, remove the baseline drop-in file and re-apply (sysctl --system).
  4. Re-run the validation checklist after any rollback.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• SSH keys only; no password or root login. Restrict who has keys.
  • Default-deny inbound; document every open port and its owner.
  • Do not store credentials, real keys, internal mirror URLs, or hostnames in public/bootstrap scripts.
  • Use centralized audit logging for regulated systems; shell history is not an audit log.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/infrastructure team.
  • Re-baseline images on a regular cadence as distributions and recommendations change.
  • Re-audit hosts against the baseline after incidents or major changes.
  • Review SSH and firewall policy when access requirements change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Linux Disk and LVM Expansion Runbook
  • Docker Host Operations Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines when Supervisor is acceptable for managing Linux processes and how to operate it safely.

The core judgment:

Supervisor is a process guardian, not a platform. It can restart a process, but it cannot provide scheduling, high availability, service discovery, secret management, deployment control, or product recovery.

Supervisor is acceptable only when the host boundary, process owner, release artifact, health check, and exit path are explicit.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use Supervisor for:

  • legacy applications that are not packaged as systemd services
  • small background workers on a single owned host
  • transitional processes before containerization or systemd migration
  • internal utilities with clear host ownership and limited blast radius
  • short-lived compatibility work where rewriting service packaging would delay safer operation

Prefer:

  • systemd for host-native long-running services
  • containers or Kubernetes for portable services that need standard deployment, scheduling, scaling, or rollback
  • managed platform jobs for work that needs queueing, retries, and multi-node execution

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not approve:

  • running critical distributed systems as ad hoc Supervisor programs
  • using Supervisor as a high-availability layer
  • hiding repeated crashes behind infinite restarts
  • storing secrets inside Supervisor configuration
  • replacing application-level health checks with PID checks

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Every Supervisor-managed process needs this contract:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Owner Who owns the process and who responds when it flaps? A process can be up and still be abandoned. Host boundary Which host is allowed to run it and what depends on that host? Supervisor does not move work away from host failure. Runtime user Which Unix user runs the process? Running as root expands blast radius. Release artifact Which versioned binary, script, or release directory is running? Manual replacement makes rollback ambiguous. Configuration source Where do config and environment values come from? Hidden shell state becomes production behavior. Restart policy Which exits are restartable and which require human action? Infinite restart loops can hide incidents. Health signal How is service behavior checked beyond PID existence? A running process can still be broken. Logs and rotation Where do logs go and how are they bounded? Local logs can fill the host. Exit criteria When should this workload move to systemd, containers, or Kubernetes? Transitional process management should not become permanent by accident.

docs.title: Use a dedicated service user
docs.text:
Do not run application processes as root unless there is a documented low-level system reason.

Use a dedicated user with:

  • minimum filesystem permissions
  • no interactive login unless required
  • read access only to required configuration
  • write access only to required data and log paths

docs.title: Keep configuration explicit
docs.text:
Use a clear program block:

[program:example-worker]
command=/opt/example/releases/current/bin/worker --config /etc/example/config.yml
directory=/opt/example/releases/current
user=example
autostart=true
autorestart=unexpected
startsecs=10
stopsignal=TERM
stopwaitsecs=30
stdout_logfile=/var/log/example/worker.log
stderr_logfile=/var/log/example/worker.err.log
environment=APP_ENV="production"

Avoid secrets in Supervisor configuration. Load secrets from the approved protected source. Avoid relying on an operator's shell profile.

docs.title: Bound restarts
docs.text:
Automatic restart is useful only when the process can safely recover.

Define:

  • expected exit codes
  • unexpected exit behavior
  • restart limit or alert threshold
  • backoff/flapping alert
  • escalation owner

If a process restarts repeatedly, the incident is not resolved. It is only being repeated faster.

docs.title: Use versioned release directories
docs.text:
Recommended layout:

/opt/example/releases/<version>/
/opt/example/releases/current -> /opt/example/releases/<version>

Supervisor should point to current, but deployment records must name the actual version. Keep the previous release through the validation window.

docs.title: Monitor behavior, not only process state
docs.text:
Minimum checks:

  • supervisorctl status
  • application health endpoint or command
  • log error rate
  • restart count
  • host disk and inode usage
  • service-specific user journey or job completion signal

Supervisor can tell you a process exists. It cannot prove the service is useful.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
supervisorctl status
supervisorctl tail example-worker
supervisorctl restart example-worker

Treat restart as an impact-bearing action, not a harmless status check. Before restarting a live program, confirm a controlled test or approved window, safe or idempotent restart behavior, no critical in-flight work, and graceful stop behavior.

Pass conditions:

  • Program runs as the intended user.
  • Command, working directory, and environment source are explicit.
  • Logs are written to known paths and rotation is configured.
  • Restart works for a controlled test or approved window after the impact check above.
  • Stop signal terminates cleanly within stopwaitsecs.
  • External monitoring confirms the service behavior, not only the PID.
  • Previous release and previous config are available for rollback.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Process runs as root Host compromise blast radius increases. Use a dedicated service user. Secrets in config Credentials leak through config backups or process inspection. Use protected secret source. Infinite restart loop Incident is hidden while host load grows. Alert on restart count, fatal exits, and flapping. No log rotation Filesystem fills and other services fail. Configure log rotation or external logging. PID-only health check Process exists but service is broken. Monitor behavior and user-facing checks. Manual binary replacement Unknown version runs in production. Use versioned releases and deployment records. Host treated as scheduler Single-host failure stops the workload. Move to systemd, containers, Kubernetes, or a job platform when availability requires it.

docs.title: Rollback and Exit
docs.text:
Rollback requires a known previous release and configuration.

Recommended rollback:

  1. Point current back to the previous release.
  2. Restore previous config if configuration changed.
  3. Restart through Supervisor.
  4. Validate service behavior and logs.
  5. Keep the failed release for investigation unless it contains sensitive material that must be removed.

Exit Supervisor when:

  • the process becomes business-critical
  • more than one host should run it
  • deployment needs artifact promotion, canary, or standard rollback
  • secrets, service discovery, or scaling need platform support
  • repeated restarts indicate missing application lifecycle design

Move to systemd for stable host-native services. Move to containers or Kubernetes for portable services that need scheduling, scaling, and platform-controlled delivery.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Restrict write access to Supervisor configuration.
  • Restrict supervisorctl usage.
  • Avoid world-readable environment files.
  • Do not log credentials, tokens, request bodies, or personal data.
  • Review processes that bind privileged ports or access host devices.
  • Keep public examples generic; do not publish real hostnames, users, paths, or internal service names.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • when onboarding a new process
  • after repeated restarts or flapping
  • before host migration
  • before privilege changes
  • before deciding whether the workload should move to systemd, containers, Kubernetes, or a job platform
  • after a disk-full, log-growth, or manual-release incident

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • Docker Compose Stack Standard
  • Application Health Check Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to patch Linux operating systems without turning routine security work into unplanned outages.

The core judgment:

OS patching is a reliability change with security motivation. The patch is not complete when packages install; it is complete when risk is reduced and the service path is still healthy.

Patching must be scheduled, observable, reversible where possible, tied to ownership, and verified after reboot when reboot is required. "Run package upgrade everywhere" is not a patching strategy.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • monthly or emergency Linux patching
  • kernel, libc, OpenSSL, OpenSSH, systemd, driver, and container-host updates
  • bastion, CI runner, monitoring, database, and platform host maintenance
  • Kubernetes node image or host patch waves
  • fleet patch compliance review
  • security exception review when a patch cannot be applied on time

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define application dependency upgrades, container image rebuild policy, managed OS patching service internals, or distribution-specific hardening benchmarks.

docs.title: Patching Contract
docs.text:
Before patching a production host class, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Owner Who owns patch approval, service validation, and rollback? Unknown-owner hosts turn patching into blind change. Host class What role does the host serve: stateless app, database, bastion, runner, monitoring, gateway, node? Different roles have different drain, reboot, and recovery behavior. Failure domain Which hosts can be patched together without losing service capacity? A safe patch window can still create an outage if all replicas move at once. Urgency Is the patch routine, expedited, or emergency based on exposure and exploitability? Severity alone does not decide timing; reachable exposure matters. Reboot boundary Which updates require reboot or service restart to become effective? Installed packages may leave the vulnerable kernel or library active. Validation path Which smoke test, metric, or owner check proves the host returned safely? "Host is up" is not the same as "service path works." Rollback path Is rollback downgrade, previous kernel boot, snapshot restore, rebuild, or service failover? Some patches are forward-only under pressure. Exception rule Who approves delayed patching and which compensating control exists? Permanent exceptions are hidden risk.

docs.title: Keep inventory useful for decisions
docs.text:
Each host or node group needs:

  • owner
  • environment
  • criticality
  • OS distribution and version
  • kernel version
  • workload role
  • exposure class
  • reboot tolerance
  • backup, snapshot, or rebuild path
  • maintenance window
  • monitoring owner
  • patch exception status

Unknown-owner hosts should not be silently patched. They should be escalated as governance risk because nobody can validate impact or approve downtime.

docs.title: Classify patch urgency by exploitability and exposure
docs.text:
Class Use when Operating mode Routine Normal security and stability updates with no urgent exposure. Scheduled maintenance window. Expedited Vulnerability is exploitable, exposed, or affects important host classes. Approved accelerated window with owners present. Emergency Active exploitation, critical remote exposure, or severe business risk. Incident-managed change with explicit communication.

Emergency patching still needs communication, rollback thinking, and post-change validation. Speed does not remove the need to know what fails if the host reboots badly.

docs.title: Patch by failure domain, not by convenience
docs.text:
Patch in waves:

  1. Non-production or canary host.
  2. One low-risk host in the same role.
  3. One host per failure domain, availability zone, node pool, or backend group.
  4. Remaining fleet after metrics stay healthy.

Do not patch every host in the same service role simultaneously unless the workload has been proven to tolerate that loss of capacity. For Kubernetes nodes, drain and uncordon one controlled unit at a time unless the node pool is designed for larger waves.

docs.title: Treat reboot as the real activation point
docs.text:
Kernel, libc, OpenSSL, OpenSSH, systemd, and driver updates may require restart or reboot. A package manager can report success while the vulnerable code remains loaded.

Before reboot:

  • drain workload if applicable
  • confirm capacity can absorb the host
  • coordinate stateful services with their runbook
  • confirm console or rebuild access
  • confirm alert silence or maintenance window
  • record the previous kernel or snapshot where available

After reboot, prove the expected kernel or service version is active.

docs.title: Manage exceptions as expiring risk
docs.text:
An exception must include:

  • owner
  • affected hosts
  • missing patch or version
  • reason patch cannot be applied
  • exposure and impact
  • compensating control
  • expiry date
  • next review date

Do not accept "application may break" as an indefinite exception. Either test the application path or classify the host as unsupported risk.

docs.title: 1. Prepare
docs.text:
uname -a
cat /etc/os-release
df -h
systemctl --failed

Confirm:

  • host owner and service owner are known
  • monitoring is healthy before the change
  • backups, snapshots, or rebuild path exist where required
  • capacity can absorb one host unavailable
  • package repository source is approved
  • reboot requirement is understood
  • validation command or smoke test is ready
  • rollback owner is present for high-risk hosts

docs.title: 2. Apply patches
docs.text:
Use distribution package managers and approved repository sources. Avoid ad hoc package downloads during production patching.

For Debian or Ubuntu style systems:

sudo apt update
sudo apt list --upgradable
sudo apt upgrade

For RHEL style systems:

sudo dnf check-update
sudo dnf upgrade

Record the package set or patch batch when the change affects important host classes.

docs.title: 3. Reboot or restart when required
docs.text:
If reboot is required, complete the drain or failover first. If a service restart is enough, still validate the service path after restart.

Do not skip reboot for kernel or core library updates and then mark the host compliant.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After patching:

uname -a
systemctl --failed
uptime
journalctl -p err -b --no-pager | tail -50

Pass conditions:

  • host is reachable through the approved management path
  • expected kernel or package versions are active
  • critical services are running
  • workload has been returned to rotation when applicable
  • monitoring reports healthy
  • no unexpected boot, driver, disk, time-sync, SSH, or service errors appear
  • application or platform smoke test passes
  • alerts are closed or explained
  • patch compliance record is updated

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Patch all nodes at once Service outage despite successful package updates. Patch by failure domain and observe metrics between waves. Skip reboot Vulnerable kernel or library remains active. Track reboot-required state and prove active version. Patch without owner Unknown service impact and no validation authority. Maintain inventory and escalate unknown-owner hosts. Repository drift Hosts receive different packages or unapproved versions. Use approved mirrors and record package set. No disk check Package install fails mid-change. Check free disk, inode, and package cache space before patching. Stateless assumption on stateful host Data service loses quorum or availability. Follow service-specific drain, failover, and recovery runbooks. No rollback boundary Failed patch becomes extended outage. Define previous kernel, snapshot, rebuild, or failover path before patching. Permanent exception Known exposure becomes normal. Require expiry, compensating control, and review owner.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Rollback depends on change type:

  • userland package downgrade may be possible when repositories still provide the previous version
  • kernel rollback may use the previous boot entry
  • snapshot restore may be appropriate for pets or stateful hosts if data consistency is understood
  • host rebuild may be safer than manual repair for cattle-style hosts
  • data services require service-specific recovery and quorum checks

If a patch breaks boot, use console access, previous kernel, snapshot restore, or image rebuild depending on host class. If a patch breaks application behavior, stop the wave, keep the last healthy hosts untouched, and choose between package rollback, application mitigation, or traffic removal.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Patch sources must be trusted repositories.
  • Do not disable security controls to force a patch.
  • Record exceptions with owner, reason, expiry, and compensating control.
  • Keep emergency patch credentials time-bounded.
  • Verify patch compliance after maintenance.
  • Do not publish real hostnames, IP addresses, package mirrors, vulnerability-exception details tied to real systems, credentials, or operational evidence.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard after emergency patching, patch-related incidents, new host classes, OS distribution support-window changes, repeated exceptions, or when patch waves repeatedly require manual recovery.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • Shell Automation Standard
  • Application Health Check Standard

docs.title: Middleware
docs.text:
Production standards for gateways, schedulers, messaging, and configuration services.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

4 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • API Gateway Standard API gateway standard for route ownership, admin-surface protection, traffic policy, validation, rollback, and observability in production.
  • DolphinScheduler Operations Standard DolphinScheduler operations standard for treating workflow scheduling as a production time-window dependency with metadata protection, worker isolation, idempotency, alerting, validation, and recovery.
  • Kafka and ZooKeeper Operations Standard Standard for operating Kafka — durability settings, version-aware metadata mode, partition and lag monitoring, safe migration, and recoverable failback.
  • Nacos Operations Standard Nacos operations standard for service discovery, runtime configuration, namespace ownership, change control, validation, backup, rollback, and access safety.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate API gateways such as APISIX, Kong, Nginx-based ingress gateways, or similar edge and internal traffic gateways.

The core judgment:

An API gateway is not a reverse proxy with plugins. It is the point where external traffic, identity, policy, application ownership, and failure behavior meet.

A gateway route can expose a service, bypass a control, amplify a dependency failure, or silently change a customer path. Treat gateway configuration as a production change surface, not as an afterthought beside application deployment.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • applications are exposed through a shared gateway
  • routes, plugins, or upstream targets are changed through GitOps, an admin API, or a dashboard
  • the gateway terminates TLS, enforces authentication, applies rate limits, or performs traffic splitting
  • multiple teams depend on the same gateway data plane or control plane
  • a failed route could affect customer traffic, internal platform APIs, or partner integrations

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not choose a gateway product, teach all plugin options, or replace application API design. It defines the operating contract for publishing and changing routes.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before a route receives production traffic, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Route owner Who owns the host, path, upstream service, policy, and rollback? A route without an owner becomes shared production risk. Exposure class Is the route public, partner-facing, internal, or admin-only? Exposure decides authentication, network boundary, rate limit, logging, and review depth. Source of truth Is route config stored in Git, Kubernetes manifests, gateway CRDs, or a controlled admin API? Operators need to know what must be reverted when live state is wrong. Admin boundary Who can change routes, plugins, certificates, and upstreams? From which network path? The admin surface can rewrite production traffic. Identity policy Which authentication and authorization controls are enforced at the gateway and which remain in the application? A gateway must not create false security by only checking transport. Traffic policy What are the timeout, retry, request-size, rate-limit, and canary rules? Defaults can turn a small upstream issue into a gateway-wide incident. Failure behavior What happens when the upstream is unhealthy, slow, overloaded, or partially deployed? Users experience this behavior, not the deployment manifest. Observability Which route-level metrics, logs, traces, and audit events prove the route is safe? Gateway incidents are hard to debug without per-route evidence. Rollback boundary Does rollback remove exposure, restore a route version, shift traffic, or revert the upstream release? Deleting a route is not the same as restoring the application.

docs.title: Keep the admin surface narrower than the traffic surface
docs.text:
The data plane may be public. The admin plane must not be.

Required controls:

  • admin API reachable only from approved networks or management workloads
  • dashboard protected by strong identity and role boundaries
  • route and plugin changes audited with actor, time, object, and diff
  • admin credentials stored in an approved secret manager
  • separate roles for route authoring, approval, certificate management, and emergency operation where scale requires it
  • no anonymous admin access
  • no wildcard admin allowlist
  • no admin token, key, or certificate material in public examples or values files

If a gateway product stores state in etcd, a database, or a controller, protect that state as part of the gateway control plane. A healthy data-plane pod is not enough if the control plane can be rewritten or lost.

docs.title: Publish routes as reviewed contracts
docs.text:
Each production route should have a route contract, even if the implementation is an Ingress, Gateway API resource, APISIX route, Kong route, or product-specific object.

Required route fields:

  • host and path
  • owning team
  • upstream service and namespace
  • exposure class
  • authentication and authorization expectation
  • TLS certificate owner and renewal path
  • timeout and retry policy
  • request-size limit
  • rate-limit or quota behavior where required
  • canary or weight rule when traffic is split
  • expected health check and readiness behavior
  • validation command or synthetic request
  • rollback owner and rollback action

Do not publish a route because the manifest applies cleanly. Publish it when the route contract can be validated from the user path to the upstream and back.

docs.title: Put policy close to the route, not in memory
docs.text:
Gateway policy is easiest to lose when it lives only in a dashboard or someone's memory.

Prefer route configuration that can be reviewed, diffed, and restored. If a dashboard or admin API is the only supported path, export the current route and plugin state after approved changes and record the recovery source.

Controls that should be explicit:

  • TLS termination and certificate reference
  • authentication mode
  • authorization boundary
  • rate limit and burst behavior
  • timeout and retry behavior
  • request and response transformation
  • allowed methods and payload size
  • header forwarding rules
  • logging and tracing settings
  • canary or weighted upstream rule

docs.title: Treat retry and timeout defaults as failure design
docs.text:
Retries can hide transient faults, but they can also multiply load on an already failing upstream. Timeouts can protect the gateway, but they can also cut off a slow dependency path before the application can degrade gracefully.

For each high-impact route, decide:

  • client timeout expectation
  • gateway timeout
  • upstream timeout
  • retry count and retryable status codes
  • whether non-idempotent methods can be retried
  • behavior when the upstream has no healthy endpoints
  • fallback or fail-closed expectation

If the route accepts writes, be conservative with retries. A duplicated mutation is often worse than a visible failure.

docs.title: Validate canary and weighted routing as traffic movement
docs.text:
Traffic splitting is a production traffic movement, not a cosmetic release feature.

Before enabling canary or weight changes:

  • prove both upstream versions are healthy
  • confirm sticky-session or state expectations
  • define the metric that blocks promotion
  • define the threshold that triggers rollback
  • confirm logs and metrics distinguish versions
  • keep a route-level rollback action ready

Do not promote a weight change when route-level errors are hidden inside aggregate gateway metrics.

docs.title: Route Example
docs.text:
Use placeholders in public examples:

apiVersion: networking.k8s.io/v1
kind: Ingress
metadata:
  name: example-api-route
  namespace: example-namespace
  annotations:
    example.gateway/owner: platform-team
    example.gateway/exposure: public
    example.gateway/timeout: "5s"
    example.gateway/rate-limit: "placeholder"
spec:
  ingressClassName: example-gateway
  tls:
    - hosts:
        - api.example.com
      secretName: example-api-tls
  rules:
    - host: api.example.com
      http:
        paths:
          - path: /v1
            pathType: Prefix
            backend:
              service:
                name: example-api
                port:
                  number: 80

The example is intentionally incomplete. A real production route also needs the route contract, admin boundary, validation evidence, and rollback action.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • gateway controller and data-plane pods are healthy
  • control-plane datastore is healthy where the gateway uses one
  • admin API and dashboard are unreachable from public networks
  • only approved roles can change production routes, plugins, certificates, and upstreams
  • route config matches the approved source of truth
  • TLS certificate is valid, renewed through the documented path, and attached to the expected host
  • synthetic request reaches the intended upstream and receives the expected status and headers
  • unauthenticated or unauthorized requests fail in the expected way
  • request-size, timeout, and rate-limit controls behave as designed
  • route-level request rate, error rate, latency percentiles, upstream health, and 4xx/5xx counts are visible
  • access logs and audit logs can identify route, upstream, actor, and decision point without leaking sensitive payloads
  • rollback was tested in the same control path used for production changes

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Admin API exposed Attackers or unapproved users can rewrite traffic. Keep admin access private, authenticated, and audited. Route without owner Nobody owns failure, stale policy, or rollback. Require owner and rollback fields before publication. Dashboard-only change Live state cannot be reviewed or restored. Use reviewed config or export controlled state after approved changes. Missing auth boundary Public traffic reaches a private service path. Classify exposure and validate auth behavior before publication. Unsafe retry on writes Duplicate mutations or amplified upstream load. Restrict retries by method, status, and idempotency. Aggregate-only metrics Route failures disappear inside gateway totals. Collect route-level metrics and version labels for canary paths. Certificate lifecycle unknown Expiry becomes an outage. Define certificate owner, renewal path, and alert. Route rollback misunderstood Exposure is removed but bad upstream release remains, or the reverse. Define whether rollback acts on route, traffic weight, or application release.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Gateway rollback can mean different actions:

  • restore the previous route definition
  • disable a new route
  • shift weight back to the previous upstream
  • remove a plugin or policy change
  • restore a certificate reference
  • revert the application release behind the route
  • temporarily fail closed for an unsafe public path

Choose the rollback action before the change.

For route changes, keep the last known-good route configuration and validate that the gateway has reconciled it. For canary changes, rollback should usually move traffic first; application rollback can follow if the bad version must be removed.

For admin-surface compromise, recovery includes credential rotation, route and plugin diff review, audit review, and confirmation that no unauthorized upstream, certificate, or policy remains.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Keep admin APIs, dashboards, and control-plane datastores off public networks.
  • Store credentials and certificate material in approved secret management.
  • Avoid broad route-edit permissions across teams.
  • Do not log sensitive headers, tokens, request bodies, or business identifiers.
  • Do not use gateway authentication as a substitute for application authorization on sensitive operations.
  • Protect metrics and logs when they reveal hostnames, route names, customer paths, or internal topology.
  • Never publish real hostnames, upstream names, credentials, customer paths, private IPs, or business-specific route evidence.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard after gateway incidents, route ownership changes, new exposure classes, plugin changes, certificate incidents, control-plane upgrades, or when a gateway begins serving additional teams or environments.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes Storage and Ingress Standard
  • Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate DolphinScheduler or a similar distributed workflow scheduler.

The core judgment:

A scheduler is not only "where jobs run." It is the system that protects business time windows. When it fails, the impact is not a failed task; it is a delayed process, stale data, missed handoff, or uncontrolled re-run.

DolphinScheduler needs metadata protection, worker capacity control, dependency isolation, retry discipline, alerting, and recovery rules before critical workflows depend on it.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • batch jobs or data workflows run through DolphinScheduler
  • failed schedules can delay a business process or downstream report
  • multiple teams share projects, workers, data sources, or shell execution
  • workflows call databases, object storage, APIs, queues, or external systems
  • operators need a controlled way to rerun, skip, pause, or recover workflows

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not teach:

  • workflow modeling
  • SQL or data pipeline design
  • DolphinScheduler installation steps
  • business SLA design
  • data quality rules inside individual jobs

It covers the operational boundary after the scheduler has been selected.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before critical workflows are onboarded, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Workflow owner Who owns success, failure, rerun, and business communication? A failed workflow without an owner becomes an invisible business delay. Metadata protection How are project definitions, schedules, state, users, and history backed up and restored? Losing metadata can erase the scheduler's memory. Worker classes Which workers run control tasks, compute tasks, data movement, and shell tasks? One noisy or risky job must not consume the whole platform. Time-window objective What is the latest acceptable finish time for important workflows? Queue health should be judged against business time, not only task count. Retry and idempotency Which tasks are safe to retry, and which need reconciliation first? Blind retries can duplicate writes or corrupt downstream state. Dependency ownership Who owns upstream and downstream dependencies? A workflow graph crosses team and system boundaries. Alert path Which failures page humans, which create tickets, and which are only reports? Failed jobs are not equal; response must match impact. Recovery rule When do operators rerun, skip, disable, restore, or escalate? Recovery without rules can make the failure larger.

docs.title: Protect metadata first
docs.text:
The metadata database contains project definitions, schedules, task state, users, permissions, and execution history. Treat it as scheduler state, not as disposable application cache.

Required controls:

  • regular backup
  • restore test
  • documented metadata database owner
  • schema/version compatibility check before upgrades
  • backup retention aligned to workflow recovery requirements
  • recovery runbook for scheduler state loss

Do not call a scheduler production-ready until metadata restore has been tested.

docs.title: Isolate worker capacity by workload class
docs.text:
Separate workers where possible:

  • lightweight control tasks
  • heavy compute tasks
  • data movement tasks
  • shell or host-sensitive tasks
  • high-priority business-window tasks

Use queues, worker groups, permissions, or deployment separation to prevent a single workflow from consuming all execution capacity.

Monitor:

  • worker slots
  • task queue backlog
  • delayed starts
  • task duration drift
  • missed workflow windows
  • failed or killed workers

docs.title: Make retries depend on idempotency
docs.text:
Each critical task should declare:

  • retry count
  • retry interval
  • timeout
  • idempotency expectation
  • external systems written to
  • upstream and downstream dependency
  • owner
  • manual reconciliation rule

Do not blindly rerun tasks that write external state. A duplicated payment export, overwritten object, or repeated API mutation may be worse than the original failure.

docs.title: Alert by business impact
docs.text:
Alert routing should consider:

  • criticality of the workflow
  • missed time window
  • downstream dependency impact
  • whether retry is still running
  • whether data has already been partially written
  • owner and escalation path

The dangerous failure is not always the first task that turns red. Sometimes it is a workflow that is still running but has already missed the handoff window.

docs.title: Control shared execution rights
docs.text:
Schedulers often become a path to shell execution, database credentials, and cross-team data movement.

Controls required:

  • project-level permissions
  • named owners
  • protected data source credentials
  • separated worker users where shell tasks exist
  • audit of workflow edits for critical projects
  • review before granting broad script or data-source access

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Master, worker, alert, API, and UI services are healthy.
  • Metadata database is reachable, backed up, and restore-tested.
  • A test workflow runs successfully on the intended worker group.
  • A controlled failed workflow sends an alert to the intended owner.
  • A controlled delayed or missed-window workflow routes to the expected owner, ticket, or report path according to criticality.
  • Queue backlog, worker slot usage, delayed starts, and missed windows are visible.
  • A known idempotent task can be rerun safely.
  • A known non-idempotent task has a documented reconciliation step before rerun.
  • Permissions prevent cross-team workflow modification where required.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Metadata database lost Workflow definitions, state, schedules, and history disappear. Back up and test restore. Worker saturation Schedules miss business windows while the platform appears "up." Monitor slots, backlog, delay, and worker classes. Unsafe retry Duplicate writes, repeated external calls, or corrupted downstream state. Record idempotency and reconciliation rules. Shared shell permissions One workflow can affect host or other teams. Isolate workers, users, and project permissions. No alert owner Failed workflows wait unnoticed. Route by owner, criticality, and time-window impact. Partial output after failure Downstream systems read inconsistent data. Mark commit points and recovery rules. Upgrade breaks metadata compatibility Scheduler starts but workflows cannot be trusted. Check version compatibility and restore path before upgrade.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery actions include:

  • restart scheduler components
  • disable a bad workflow
  • pause a project or worker group
  • restore metadata database
  • rerun idempotent tasks
  • reconcile non-idempotent task output
  • manually complete or skip a controlled step
  • notify downstream owners when time windows are missed

Do not blindly rerun a failed workflow that writes external state. First identify the last committed output and whether downstream consumers have observed it.

For scheduler upgrades, rollback requires:

  • compatible metadata backup
  • previous deployment artifact
  • known worker compatibility
  • validation workflow
  • owner approval when in-flight workflows exist

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Restrict project and workflow edit permissions.
  • Protect data source credentials in approved secret management.
  • Avoid broad shell execution rights.
  • Separate worker users or worker groups for risky tasks.
  • Audit workflow changes for critical projects.
  • Do not publish real workflow names, database names, data source names, credentials, hostnames, or business schedules.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • after missed schedules
  • after metadata incidents
  • after worker saturation
  • after unsafe retries or duplicate outputs
  • before major version upgrades
  • when onboarding new teams
  • when adding shell-heavy or high-impact workflows
  • when business time-window requirements change

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kafka and ZooKeeper Operations Standard
  • Nacos Operations Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run Kafka safely in production — including legacy ZooKeeper-backed clusters still present in the field — and how to migrate or upgrade a cluster without losing acknowledged records or unexpectedly replaying consumer work.

The core judgment of this standard:

Kafka durability is not “replication factor 3.” It is the combination of replication factor, min.insync.replicas, producer acknowledgements, leader-election policy, and the write boundary used during change.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Operate a self-managed Kafka cluster in KRaft or legacy ZooKeeper mode.
  • Need durability, monitoring, and migration practices you can defend in an incident review.
  • Plan a broker addition, rolling upgrade, metadata-mode migration, or cross-cluster migration.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Stream-processing application design such as Kafka Streams, ksqlDB, or connectors.
  • Managed streaming-service internals.
  • A complete ZooKeeper-to-KRaft migration procedure.
  • Active-active conflict-resolution design.

docs.title: Version Boundary
docs.text:
• Kafka 4.x uses KRaft metadata mode.
  • ZooKeeper-backed clusters require procedures for their exact Kafka version and supported upgrade path.
  • Do not assume a broker binary can be downgraded after metadata, feature levels, or storage formats have advanced.

docs.title: Data and Control Topology
docs.text:
flowchart LR Producers[Producers] --> Source[(Source Kafka cluster)] Source --> SourceConsumers[Source consumers] Source --> Mirror[Directional replication flow] Mirror --> Target[(Target Kafka cluster)] Target --> TargetConsumers[Target consumers] Metadata[Metadata mode: KRaft or ZooKeeper] --> Source Metadata --> Target Catalog[Topic, owner, ACL, and criticality catalog] --> Source Catalog --> Target Observe[Quorum, URP, disk, replication lag, consumer lag] --> Source Observe --> Target Observe --> Mirror Change[Change controller] --> Producers Change --> SourceConsumers Change --> TargetConsumers

The topology separates the data plane from the control plane. Producers, brokers, replication, and consumers move records; catalog, identity, observability, and change control decide whether that movement is safe.

docs.title: Cross-Cluster Cutover Flow
docs.text: flowchart TD     A[Inventory topics, partitions, ACLs, groups, and offsets] --> B[Start approved directional replication]     B --> C[Validate naming, records, lag, and offset sync]     C --> D{Target matches the declared contract?}     D -- No --> E[Stop and correct topology or replay policy]     E --> C     D -- Yes --> F[Fence source writes at a recorded position]     F --> G[Wait until target reaches final source position]     G --> H[Translate and verify consumer offsets]     H --> I[Move producers and consumers in one controlled window]     I --> J[Confirm only the target accepts new writes]     J --> K[Observe and retain source until rollback window closes]

docs.title: Product Integration
docs.text: Product capability Managed object Required behavior Asset inventory Cluster, broker, metadata mode, topic, partition, owner, criticality Make topology and failure domains visible before change. Topic and schema governance Topic config, retention, partition count, naming, compatibility Prevent unmanaged topology drift and unsafe destination naming. Identity and access SASL identity, ACL, TLS trust, replication credential Separate producer, consumer, operator, and replication permissions. Observability Offline partitions, URP, quorum, disk, replication lag, consumer lag Prove cluster health and cutover readiness. Change management Final source position, producer fence, offset map, rollback window Coordinate traffic movement as one bounded operation. Evidence store Topic inventory, offsets, lag graph, validation, reviewer Preserve the proof for cutover and failback decisions.

docs.title: Planning Checklist
docs.text: Area Questions Brokers How many brokers, on what storage class, across how many failure domains? Metadata KRaft or legacy ZooKeeper? What exact source and target versions are supported? Topics Partition count, replication factor, retention, cleanup policy, and destination naming per topic? Durability Do producer acks, min.insync.replicas, replication factor, and leader-election policy agree? Consumers Which groups move, how are offsets translated, and what duplicate or replay behavior is acceptable? Write boundary How are source producers fenced, and what is the final accepted source position? Failback Is there reverse replication, an intentional dual-write contract, or a reconciliation procedure? Security Are SASL, ACLs, TLS, and replication credentials controlled? Observability Are offline partitions, under-replicated partitions, quorum health, replication lag, consumer lag, and disk usage collected?

docs.title: Durability is a combined contract
docs.text:
For a topic that must not lose acknowledged writes:

  • Replication factor at least 3 where the platform provides independent failure domains.
  • min.insync.replicas = 2 for the intended one-broker-failure tolerance.
  • Producer acks=all.
  • Idempotent production when supported by the client and workload contract.

Replication factor 3 with acks=1 can still lose acknowledged data if the leader fails before replicas catch up. The cost of stronger durability is latency and rejected writes when ISR drops below the required minimum. Consumers may still read available data, so describing the entire topic as “read-only” is imprecise.

docs.title: Deployment
docs.text:
1. Keep broker or node identities stable.
  2. Store log segments on persistent storage sized for ingress, retention, replication, and recovery headroom.
  3. Spread brokers and KRaft controllers or ZooKeeper quorum members across failure domains; use an odd quorum count.
  4. Set retention deliberately per topic; unbounded retention eventually becomes a disk outage.

docs.title: Monitoring that matters
docs.text:
• Offline partitions and under-replicated partitions.
  • KRaft or ZooKeeper quorum health.
  • Consumer lag per group.
  • Cross-cluster replication lag and checkpoint latency during migration.
  • Broker disk usage, JVM heap, and GC.

docs.title: Migration / Upgrade
docs.text: Treat the change as a controlled operation with a defined write boundary, acceptance criteria, and rollback owner.

docs.title: Rolling broker upgrade
docs.text:
1. Confirm the official source-to-target compatibility path.
  2. Freeze incompatible configuration changes.
  3. Upgrade one broker at a time.
  4. Wait for the broker to rejoin and under-replicated partitions to return to zero before proceeding.
  5. Delay irreversible metadata or feature-level changes until the rollback boundary is understood.

docs.title: Cross-cluster migration
docs.text:
MirrorMaker 2 replication flows are directional. Two-way replication requires two explicitly enabled flows, and topic naming, offset translation, duplicate handling, and loop prevention must be tested for the selected policy.

  1. Capture topics, partition counts, replication factors, configs, ACLs, consumer groups, and source offsets.
  2. Establish the approved replication flow and secure connectivity.
  3. Validate destination topic naming, records, partition mapping, configs, replication lag, and consumer-offset synchronization.
  4. Define the final source write position and fence writes to the migrated topics.
  5. Wait until the target has replicated through that final position.
  6. Pause consumers when required by the replay contract, translate and verify their target offsets, then move producers and consumers within one controlled change boundary.
  7. Confirm that only the intended cluster accepts new writes for the migrated topics.
  8. Keep source data and replication available until validation and the rollback window complete.

There is no universal producer-first or consumer-first cutover order:

  • Starting target consumers first can drain mirrored backlog.
  • Starting target producers first can create a bounded target backlog.
  • Either can be valid when the source write fence and target consumer position are proven.
  • Neither is safe when both clusters accept uncontrolled writes and no merge contract exists.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server <broker>:9092 --describe --under-replicated-partitions
kafka-topics.sh --bootstrap-server <broker>:9092 --describe --topic example-topic
kafka-consumer-groups.sh --bootstrap-server <broker>:9092 --all-groups --describe

Confirm:

  • Zero offline partitions and no unexplained under-replicated partitions.
  • Each critical topic has the intended replication factor and min.insync.replicas.
  • Producer acknowledgement and idempotence settings match the durability requirement.
  • Consumer lag is bounded and draining, not growing.
  • Cross-cluster replication reaches the agreed cutover position.
  • Target consumer offsets resolve to the intended records.
  • Only the intended cluster accepts writes after cutover.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Acknowledged record lost Data disappears after broker failure Weak acks, ISR, or leader-election settings Align producer and broker durability settings; test broker-loss behavior. Writes rejected Producers receive insufficient-replica errors ISR dropped below min.insync.replicas Restore replica health; do not weaken durability reflexively. Consumers fall behind Growing lag Slow consumers, rebalance, partition skew, or broker pressure Investigate workload and broker capacity before adding partitions. Offset translation error Duplicate or skipped processing after migration Target offsets were not verified against records Stop cutover; map offsets to actual records and replay policy. Divergent writes Both clusters contain unique records Both clusters accepted writes without conflict handling Fence one side; preserve both histories; reconcile before routing. Disk fills Broker unavailable or cluster unstable Retention or capacity planning failed Restore capacity, then correct retention and alerting. Rolling restart causes risk URP remains above zero between brokers Next broker restarted too early Stop the rollout and restore ISR before continuing.
