Replication loop or topic collision Repeated records or unexpected remote-topic names Directional flows and naming policy conflict Correct flow topology and validate with isolated topics first.

docs.title: Rollback and Failback
docs.text:
Before target writes begin, rollback normally means leaving producers on the source, stopping the migration flow cleanly, and proving that consumer state did not advance against target-only records.

After target writes begin, safe failback requires one of these contracts:

  • verified reverse replication from target to source;
  • an intentional dual-write design with ordering, idempotency, and conflict resolution;
  • controlled export, replay, and reconciliation of target-only records.

Without one of those contracts, routing producers back to the source creates missing records or divergent histories. Stop writes, preserve both clusters, reconcile records and consumer positions, and perform a second controlled cutover. Accepting a known gap is an explicit data-loss decision requiring business authorization, not a routine rollback step.

For a failed broker upgrade, follow the supported recovery path for the active metadata and feature level. Do not assume reinstalling the previous binary is safe after an irreversible level change.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enable SASL authentication and ACL authorization; do not run an open broker.
  • Use TLS for client, inter-broker, and cross-cluster traffic where confidentiality or identity validation requires it.
  • Never publish real broker addresses, business-sensitive topic names, or credentials.
  • Restrict who can create or alter topics, ACLs, and replication flows.
  • Protect MirrorMaker and connector credentials through an approved secret-management path.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/messaging team.
  • Re-confirm durability settings when a topic's loss tolerance changes.
  • Re-confirm version, metadata mode, and migration topology for every upgrade or move.
  • Re-test broker-loss, rolling-restart, offset translation, and failback behavior after major upgrades.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Apache Kafka 4.3 Geo-Replication
  • Apache Kafka KRaft and ZooKeeper migration documentation

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Nacos as a service discovery and runtime configuration platform.

The core judgment:

Nacos is a runtime control plane. A small configuration edit or registration error can change live traffic faster than a deployment pipeline can stop it.

Nacos needs ownership, namespace boundaries, change review, service-health discipline, backup, monitoring, and rollback. Treating it as a simple key-value UI or service list creates production risk.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • applications depend on Nacos for service discovery
  • runtime configuration changes are delivered through Nacos
  • multiple teams share one Nacos cluster
  • Nacos is on the critical path for deployment, failover, feature flags, traffic routing, or startup
  • operators need a controlled way to restore config, remove bad instances, or recover namespace state

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define application framework usage, every Nacos deployment topology, or product-specific UI steps. It defines the operating contract for a shared discovery and configuration control plane.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before production systems depend on Nacos, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Namespace boundary Which environment, team, and application family does each namespace or group represent? A shared namespace can turn one team's edit into another team's outage. Config ownership Who owns each high-impact config key, default value, validation, and rollback value? Runtime config changes can bypass normal deployment guardrails. Discovery truth What makes an instance eligible to receive traffic? Discovery is dangerous when registration does not reflect readiness. Change path Which changes require review, window, approval, or emergency path? A UI edit can be faster than incident response. Access model Who can read, write, publish, delete, or administer each namespace? Shared admin access removes accountability. Backup boundary What is backed up: database, namespace mapping, config exports, access policy, cluster config? Restoring only the database may not restore an operable control plane. Client behavior How do applications behave when Nacos is slow, unavailable, or returns stale data? Startup and failover behavior can become the actual outage. Rollback rule Is rollback a config revert, namespace/group revert, metadata cleanup, or database restore? Different failures require different recovery actions.

docs.title: Separate configuration authority from discovery authority
docs.text:
Nacos often carries two different forms of authority:

  • configuration authority: what values applications read at runtime
  • discovery authority: which service instances are allowed to receive traffic

Do not manage both as an undifferentiated list of keys and instances. A configuration edit changes behavior; a discovery error changes traffic. Each needs an owner, validation path, and rollback action.

For shared clusters, require namespace and group naming that includes environment and ownership. Production and non-production values must not share a namespace unless access, naming, and approval rules make accidental cross-use unlikely.

docs.title: Control runtime configuration as a release-class change
docs.text:
Every high-impact config change should record:

  • config key and namespace
  • application owner
  • reason
  • expected behavior change
  • previous value
  • proposed value
  • validation method
  • rollback value
  • blast radius
  • approval or emergency justification
  • change window for sensitive paths

Avoid direct production UI edits without a recorded path. If emergency UI changes are allowed, export or record the resulting state after the incident so the source of truth is not lost.

docs.title: Protect discovery from false health
docs.text:
Service discovery is useful only when registered instances are eligible to receive traffic.

Applications should:

  • register only after readiness is true
  • deregister during controlled shutdown
  • avoid advertising an endpoint before dependencies required for serving are available
  • use accurate cluster, weight, and metadata values
  • avoid stale metadata that keeps traffic on the wrong version or zone

Monitor:

  • stale instances
  • unexpected instance count
  • incorrect weight
  • cluster or metadata mismatch
  • registration churn
  • discovery latency
  • client-side discovery errors

Discovery validation should ask: "Would the next request be routed to an instance that can really serve it?"

docs.title: Make access smaller than namespace scope
docs.text:
Access should follow ownership and environment, not convenience.

Required controls:

  • authentication enabled
  • no shared production admin account for routine work
  • least-privilege roles by namespace, group, and environment
  • separate read, publish, delete, and admin permissions where possible
  • audit review for production config changes
  • emergency access with expiry and review
  • no raw application secrets stored as plain config values

If a config item must reference a secret, prefer an approved secret-management integration or an indirection that does not expose the secret value in Nacos.

docs.title: Back up state that can actually restore the control plane
docs.text:
Backup should include:

  • Nacos database if externalized
  • configuration exports
  • namespace and group mapping
  • user and access-policy mapping
  • cluster configuration
  • version and schema compatibility notes
  • recovery owner and restore procedure

A backup that has never been restored is only a file. Restore must be tested in a non-production environment, including whether applications can read config and discover services after restore.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Operational checks:

curl -fsS http://<nacos-endpoint>/nacos/v1/ns/operator/metrics
curl -fsS "http://<nacos-endpoint>/nacos/v1/ns/instance/list?serviceName=<service-name>"

Pass conditions:

  • Nacos cluster nodes are healthy
  • datastore or database dependency is healthy and backed up
  • applications can read expected config
  • a controlled config change reaches clients and can be reverted
  • service instance count matches expected topology
  • unhealthy or draining instances are not served
  • namespace and group ownership is documented
  • production write access is limited and auditable
  • backup and restore have been tested
  • client-side Nacos errors, discovery latency, and config-refresh failures are observable

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Unreviewed config edit Runtime behavior changes without deployment evidence. Require change record, previous value, validation, and rollback value. Shared namespace misuse One team breaks another service or environment. Separate namespace/group boundaries and access roles. Stale instance remains registered Traffic reaches a dead or draining endpoint. Readiness-aware registration and shutdown deregistration. Wrong weight or metadata Traffic shifts to the wrong version, zone, or cluster. Validate discovery metadata and observe route distribution. Nacos unavailable during startup Services fail to start or start with unsafe defaults. Define client fallback behavior and alert on client errors. No tested restore Mistaken delete or database failure becomes prolonged outage. Back up database, config exports, namespace mapping, and access policy; test restore. Shared admin account No reliable accountability for production edits. Use named or role-based access with audit review. Secrets stored as plain config Credential exposure through UI, export, or logs. Use secret management or safe indirection.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Rollback may mean:

  • restore the previous config value
  • revert namespace or group mapping
  • remove bad service-instance metadata
  • reset incorrect weight
  • re-publish a known-good config export
  • restore database state after destructive change
  • temporarily freeze config publication while clients stabilize

For high-risk config changes, capture the previous value before change and validate rollback through the same client path used for release validation.

For discovery failures, prefer targeted metadata or registration correction before broad restarts. Restarting every client can amplify the incident if the root problem is namespace, metadata, or control-plane health.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enable authentication and avoid shared admin credentials.
  • Restrict namespace access by team and environment.
  • Audit production config changes and permission changes.
  • Do not store raw secrets in plain Nacos config.
  • Protect Nacos endpoints from public exposure.
  • Restrict database and backup access.
  • Do not publish real service names, namespace names, hostnames, internal endpoints, config values, credentials, or incident evidence.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review this standard after config incidents, discovery incidents, namespace growth, access changes, version upgrades, restore tests, or when Nacos becomes a dependency for additional teams or more critical startup paths.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Application Health Check Standard
  • Kubernetes Cluster Provisioning Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: MongoDB
docs.text:
MongoDB production requirements, validation, and recovery boundaries.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • MongoDB Operations Standard Standard for production MongoDB — replica-set availability, write-concern/durability trade-offs, restore-proven backup, safe upgrade, and rollback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run MongoDB safely in production: the topology that gives real availability, the write-concern choices that decide whether an acknowledged write can still be lost, and a backup you have actually restored.

The core judgment of this standard:

A single mongod is not "MongoDB in production" — it is a database with no failover and a write concern that can acknowledge data you are about to lose. Availability and durability in MongoDB are configuration decisions (replica set + write concern), not defaults you inherit.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run self-managed MongoDB (VM, bare metal, or container/operator).
  • Need to choose a topology and write/read concern for a given durability requirement.
  • Plan a version upgrade, host move, or migration.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Schema/document modeling and index design.
  • Managed MongoDB (Atlas-style) internals — use the provider's HA and backup contract; the write-concern and restore-validation judgment still applies.
  • Sharding design beyond noting when to consider it.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Run a replica set (odd number of voting members) in production — never a standalone for data you cannot lose.
  2. Enable authentication and role-based access; bind only to private interfaces.
  3. Choose write concern and read concern deliberately per workload; they are the durability/consistency dial.
  4. Prove backups by restoring them, and record the restore time.
  5. Monitor replication lag, connections, locks/contention, storage, and oplog window.

docs.title: Topology: replica set, not standalone
docs.text: A replica set provides automated failover (a member is elected primary if the current primary fails) and a source for backups/reads. Use an odd number of voting members (commonly 3) so elections have a quorum. Spread members across failure domains (hosts/zones).

docs.title: Durability is set by write concern
docs.text:
This is the decision most teams get wrong by leaving it default.

Write concern Acknowledged when Loss risk w:1 Primary has it (not yet replicated) A primary failover can lose the last acknowledged writes w:majority A majority of members have it Survives a single-member failover w:majority, j:true Majority and journaled to disk Strongest common durability

For data that must survive a node loss, use w:majority. Reserve w:1 for caches/derived data where loss is acceptable, and write that decision down.

docs.title: Authentication and access
docs.text:
• Enable auth; create least-privilege roles (application users get DB-scoped read/write, not cluster admin).
  • Bind to private interfaces only; never expose MongoDB to the public internet.
  • Keep credentials in a secret manager; never publish connection strings, users, passwords, or internal hosts.

docs.title: Backup and Restore
docs.text:
For small/medium datasets, logical backup is portable:

mongodump --uri="mongodb://<user>@<host>/?authSource=admin" --out /backup/mongodb
# restore:
mongorestore --uri="mongodb://<user>@<host>/?authSource=admin" /backup/mongodb

For large datasets, prefer filesystem/volume snapshots of a stopped or fsync-locked secondary, or a managed/continuous backup. Whatever the method:

  • Encrypt backups and restrict who can read them (a backup is a full copy of the data).
  • Restore-test on a schedule into an isolated instance; verify collection counts and an application-level check. Record the restore time — it is your real recovery objective.

docs.title: Upgrade and Migration
docs.text:
MongoDB upgrades must follow the supported version path and the featureCompatibilityVersion (FCV) rules — skipping versions or raising FCV prematurely can block rollback.

  1. Confirm the supported upgrade path; do not skip major versions.
  2. Take a verified backup first.
  3. For a replica set, upgrade secondaries first, then step down the primary and upgrade it last (rolling upgrade keeps the set available).
  4. Raise FCV only after the whole set is upgraded and you are confident you will not roll back.
  5. Validate (below) before considering it done.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
mongosh --host <host> --eval 'rs.status()'      # member states, who is primary
mongosh --host <host> --eval 'rs.printSecondaryReplicationInfo()'   # lag
mongosh --host <host> --eval 'db.serverStatus().connections'

Confirm:

  • One PRIMARY and healthy SECONDARY members; no member stuck in RECOVERING/DOWN.
  • Replication lag is near zero and within the oplog window.
  • The latest restore test passed within its interval.
  • Connections are well below the limit; no sustained lock contention.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Acknowledged write lost Data gone after failover w:1 on durable data Use w:majority for data that must survive failover. No failover Writes fail when a node dies Standalone, or even member count / lost quorum Run an odd-numbered replica set across failure domains. Replication falls behind Secondary lags, oplog rolls over Undersized oplog, slow disk, heavy writes Resize oplog; fix disk; reseed the secondary. Backup won't restore Restore fails / incomplete Never restore-tested Schedule restore tests; validate counts. Upgrade can't roll back Stuck after upgrade FCV raised too early Raise FCV only after committing to the version. Exposed instance Unauthorized access Bound to public interface / no auth Bind private; enable auth; restrict network.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep a verified backup and the prior binaries until post-change validation passes.
  2. For a failed rolling upgrade (before FCV raised), step down and reinstall the prior version on the affected member; let the set re-sync.
  3. If data was corrupted, restore from the pre-change backup into a clean instance and re-point the application.
  4. Rotate credentials exposed during the change.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enable auth and TLS; bind to private interfaces.
  • Use least-privilege roles; separate admin from application users.
  • Encrypt and access-control backups.
  • Never publish connection strings, credentials, or internal hostnames.
  • Restrict who can run administrative commands and trigger elections.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the database/platform team.
  • Re-confirm write-concern choices when a workload's durability requirement changes.
  • Verify a successful restore test each interval.
  • Re-confirm the supported upgrade path and FCV plan before each major upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • MySQL Operations Standard
  • Redis Operations Standard
  • MongoDB production notes

docs.title: MySQL
docs.text:
MySQL production requirements, validation, and recovery boundaries.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • MySQL Operations Standard Standard for production MySQL — account model, restore-proven backup, replication-aware migration, break-glass recovery, and data-safe failback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines safe, reusable practices for running self-managed MySQL in production: how to set up an instance, how to back it up so the backup is actually recoverable, how to migrate or upgrade without data loss, and how to recover under pressure.

The core judgment of this standard:

A backup you have never restored is a hypothesis, not a backup. A server that missed committed writes is not a rollback target until it has caught up and been validated.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run self-managed MySQL on bare metal, a VM, or a container where you own backup and recovery.
  • Plan a version upgrade, host move, or replication-based migration.
  • Need a defensible account, backup, migration, and recovery posture for an audit or incident review.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Managed MySQL internals. Use the provider's backup, failover, and point-in-time-recovery contract; this standard's account model and validation principles still apply.
  • Query and schema design, indexing, or application-level performance tuning.
  • Product-specific HA automation procedures.

docs.title: Operating Principles
docs.text:
1. Prefer a managed database service when operational maturity or on-call depth is limited.
  2. Separate administrative, migration, replication, and application accounts; grant least privilege.
  3. Never publish initial root passwords, generated credentials, or connection strings.
  4. Prove backups by restoring them on a schedule, not by checking that the backup job exited 0.
  5. Keep exactly one authoritative writer unless the application is explicitly designed for multi-writer conflict handling.
  6. Make upgrades and migrations controlled changes with a write fence, validation boundary, rollback path, and owner.

docs.title: Operating Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart LR App[Application] --> Endpoint[Database write endpoint] Endpoint --> Primary[(Authoritative writer)] Primary --> Replica[(Replica or migration target)] Primary --> Binlog[(Binary logs)] Primary --> Backup[Base backup] Binlog --> PITR[Point-in-time recovery] Backup --> Restore[Test restore environment] PITR --> Restore Monitor[Monitoring and audit] --> Primary Monitor --> Replica Catalog[Asset and ownership catalog] --> Endpoint Catalog --> Primary Catalog --> Replica

The operating model has one authoritative writer, one declared routing point, a separate recovery path, and observable replication. A replica is not automatically a valid failback target; it becomes one only after position and data validation.

docs.title: Single-Writer Cutover Sequence
docs.text: sequenceDiagram     participant Change as Change controller     participant App as Application     participant Source as Old source     participant Target as New target     participant Evidence as Evidence store      Change->>App: Fence new writes     Change->>Source: Record final GTID or binlog position     Source-->>Target: Replicate through final boundary     Change->>Target: Validate threads, position, checksums     Target-->>Evidence: Store validation result     Change->>App: Route writes to target     App->>Target: Resume writes     Change->>Source: Keep non-writable during rollback window

docs.title: Product Integration
docs.text: Product capability Managed object Required behavior Asset inventory Instance, endpoint, role, version, owner, data criticality Show the authoritative writer and every replica or recovery target. Change management Migration plan, write fence, final GTID, approval, rollback window Coordinate one controlled cutover rather than independent operator actions. Backup and recovery Base backup, binlog range, restore test, RPO/RTO result Prove recoverability and expose the latest usable recovery point. Secret and access management Application, admin, migration, replication accounts Separate identities, limit source hosts, and rotate emergency credentials. Observability Replication threads, errors, lag, connection pressure, restore status Detect when a target is not safe to promote. Evidence store Checksums, row invariants, GTID boundary, route change, reviewer Preserve the proof used to authorize cutover or failback.

docs.title: Installation Baseline
docs.text:
For self-managed MySQL:

  • Use a supported MySQL version with a defined end-of-life date.
  • Run as a dedicated mysql system user; keep data on a dedicated volume.
  • Set character set and collation explicitly (utf8mb4 unless you have a specific reason).
  • Enable slow query logging and a reviewed long_query_time.
  • Manage the service through systemd so restart and boot behavior are predictable.
[mysqld]
datadir=/var/lib/mysql
socket=/var/lib/mysql/mysql.sock
character-set-server=utf8mb4
collation-server=utf8mb4_unicode_ci
slow_query_log=ON
long_query_time=1
max_connections=500

Size max_connections to what the host can actually serve; an unreachable connection ceiling just converts a load spike into a different failure. Treat a collation change as an application-compatibility change, not as incidental migration cleanup.

docs.title: Account Model
docs.text:
Use least privilege and scope hosts tightly:

CREATE USER 'app_user'@'<app-host-or-approved-subnet>' IDENTIFIED BY '<from-secret-manager>';
GRANT SELECT, INSERT, UPDATE, DELETE ON example_db.* TO 'app_user'@'<app-host-or-approved-subnet>';
FLUSH PRIVILEGES;
  • The password placeholder must be supplied through a secret manager, never committed.
  • Application users get DML on their schema only — not ALL PRIVILEGES, not *.*.
  • Scope the host part to the narrowest approved source; do not use broad private-network wildcards as a convenience default.
  • Administrative, migration, and replication accounts are separate and used deliberately, not shared with the application.

docs.title: Backup Standard
docs.text:
Choose the backup method by dataset size and recovery objective.

Method Good for Restore characteristic Logical (mysqldump / mysqlpump) Small/medium datasets, portability, single-table recovery Slower restore; replays SQL Physical (for example Percona XtraBackup) Large datasets, fast full restore Fast restore; version/layout sensitive Binary logs Point-in-time recovery on top of a base backup Requires complete retention and tested replay

Logical backup example for transactional tables:

mysqldump --single-transaction --routines --triggers --events example_db > example_db.sql

For every backup, document: type, schedule, retention, encryption, restore-test frequency, binary-log coverage, and the owner/escalation path. Combine a periodic base backup with binary logs when you need point-in-time recovery.

docs.title: Prove the backup
docs.text:
On a schedule, restore the latest backup into an isolated instance and validate it:

  • Row counts on critical tables match expectations.
  • Application-level integrity checks or checksums pass.
  • Routines, triggers, events, users, and grants in the recovery scope are present.
  • Point-in-time replay reaches a chosen timestamp when required by the RPO.
  • Record the restore duration and compare it with the RTO.

docs.title: Migration and Upgrade
docs.text:
Before:

  • Confirm source and target versions and the supported upgrade path; do not skip unsupported jumps.
  • Check character set and collation compatibility.
  • Estimate data size, restore time, transfer time, and required downtime.
  • Validate network throughput for the transfer.
  • Freeze incompatible schema changes for the window.
  • Write the cutover and failback plan and confirm the owner.

Replication-based migration is usually safer than a stop-the-world dump: replicate to the target, let it catch up, then cut over during a controlled window.

docs.title: Replication validation
docs.text:
SHOW REPLICA STATUS\G   -- MySQL 8.0.22+
SHOW SLAVE STATUS\G     -- older versions
SELECT COUNT(*) FROM important_table;

Do not use Seconds_Behind_Source = 0 as the only cutover proof. Also confirm:

  • the I/O and SQL/applier threads are healthy;
  • Last_Error is empty;
  • the executed binary-log coordinates or GTID set cover the agreed boundary;
  • no replication filter silently excludes required data;
  • checksums or application invariants match for critical data.

docs.title: Single-writer cutover contract
docs.text:
1. Stop or fence application writes to the old source.
  2. Record the final accepted source position or GTID set.
  3. Wait until the target has applied through that boundary.
  4. Complete final integrity checks.
  5. Redirect application writes to the target.
  6. Keep the old source non-writable until the rollback window closes.
  7. Monitor write success, replication state, error logs, and application invariants.

Do not allow both servers to accept uncontrolled writes merely to shorten the window. Without conflict resolution, that creates divergent histories that cannot be repaired by changing a connection string.

docs.title: Break-Glass: Root Password Reset
docs.text:
Root password reset (--skip-grant-tables or equivalent) is a break-glass procedure, not routine maintenance. It removes authentication from the running server, so require:

  • Approval and a maintenance window.
  • Console or host-level access; never expose the bypassed server over an untrusted network.
  • An audit record of who did it and why.
  • Immediate credential rotation and a return to normal authentication afterward.

Do not publish environment-specific reset passwords or internal access paths.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Service is up via systemd; the error log shows no crash-recovery loop.
  • Replication, if used, has healthy threads, no error, and an understood executed position.
  • Only the intended server accepts application writes.
  • The most recent restore test passed within its scheduled interval and RTO.
  • Slow query logging is being written and reviewed.
  • The application connects with a least-privilege account, not an administrative account.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Backup completes but will not restore Restore fails or data is incomplete Never restore-tested; missing routines, events, users, or binlogs Restore-test the complete recovery contract. Replication stops silently Lag grows; Last_Error is set Schema drift, conflicting writes, filter error, or binlog gap Read replication status; fix the root cause; reseed if required. Replica appears caught up but data differs Lag is zero but checksum or row count differs Lag metric treated as proof; replication error or excluded data Stop cutover; verify coordinates, GTIDs, filters, and data. Split writes after cutover Source and target each contain unique rows Old source stayed writable or clients were not drained Fence one writer; preserve both histories; reconcile before failback. Connection storm Too many connections or memory pressure Leaked connections, weak pooling, or an unrealistic limit Fix pooling and size limits to host capacity. Character set corruption Mojibake or changed sort behavior Source/target charset or collation mismatch Verify end-to-end compatibility before migrating. Upgrade rollback fails Previous binary cannot open upgraded data files In-place downgrade was assumed safe Restore a compatible pre-upgrade backup or use the supported downgrade path.

docs.title: Rollback and Failback
docs.text:
Before the target accepts writes, rollback normally means leaving or returning applications to the still-authoritative source after proving that no write crossed the fence.

After the target has accepted writes, failback is a data-movement operation, not a connection-string operation:

  1. Stop or fence target writes and record the final target position.
  2. If versions and topology permit, make the old source a replica of the new source so it receives the writes it missed.
  3. Validate replication position, checksums, and application invariants.
  4. Promote the old source only through a second controlled cutover.
  5. If reverse replication is not compatible, export and reconcile target-only writes or continue forward on the target; do not silently discard them.

For a failed in-place upgrade, restore the pre-upgrade backup onto the prior supported version. Do not assume upgraded data files can be downgraded in place.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Bind to approved private interfaces; never expose MySQL directly to the public internet.
  • Encrypt backups and restrict who can read or restore them.
  • Keep credentials in a secret manager; do not pass them on a command line where they can leak into process lists or shell history.
  • Audit administrative and replication access; rotate credentials after any break-glass event.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the database/platform team.
  • Verify a successful restore test at the scheduled interval.
  • Re-confirm the supported upgrade and replication path before each migration.
  • Re-check account privileges when a new service starts using the database.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Redis Operations Standard
  • MySQL backup and recovery documentation
  • MySQL 8.4: Switching Sources During Failover

docs.title: Networking and access
docs.text:
Load balancing, TLS, VPNs, network devices, and file sharing.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

5 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • HAProxy Load Balancing Standard Standard for HAProxy load balancing — health checks that match readiness, validated reloads, L4 vs L7 choice, draining, and rollback.
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard Standard for Nginx/OpenResty reverse proxy and TLS with version-aware HTTP/2, long-lived connections, validated reloads, and rollback.
  • OpenVPN Access Standard OpenVPN access standard for bounded remote network reachability, identity ownership, route scope, DNS behavior, logging, revocation, validation, and retirement.
  • OpenWrt Network Device Standard OpenWrt network device standard for lab, edge, and small-site routers with configuration ownership, firmware control, access hardening, validation, rollback, and recovery boundaries.
  • Samba File Sharing Standard Samba file sharing standard for internal SMB/CIFS access with data ownership, permission boundaries, backup, audit, validation, and recovery.
1 document Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • TLS Certificate Expiry Runbook TLS certificate expiry runbook for ownership, renewal path, TLS termination, chain validation, client trust, alert recovery, and rollback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines safe patterns for using HAProxy to distribute TCP/HTTP traffic across backends in production.

The core judgment of this standard:

A load balancer is only as good as its health check. A check that passes when the app is not actually ready will route users straight into errors; a check that is too aggressive will flap healthy backends out of rotation. The health check is the design, not a detail.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Balance traffic across multiple backends (HTTP or raw TCP).
  • Need a single validated reload procedure and a draining model for safe backend changes.
  • Are deciding between HAProxy (connection/L4-L7 balancing) and an application proxy.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • TLS termination patterns and HTTP header handling in depth — see the Nginx reverse proxy + TLS standard; the validated-reload and certificate-lifecycle principles there apply.
  • Kubernetes Ingress controllers.
  • WAF or DDoS mitigation.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Match the health check to real application readiness, not just "port is open".
  2. Validate config (haproxy -c) before every reload; reload (not restart) to avoid dropping connections.
  3. Choose L4 (mode tcp) vs L7 (mode http) deliberately — you only get HTTP-aware features in L7.
  4. Drain backends before maintenance instead of yanking them.
  5. Restrict the stats/admin socket; never publish real backend addresses.

docs.title: Frontend, backend, and a readiness-matching check
docs.text:
frontend example_http
  bind *:80
  mode http
  default_backend example_backend

backend example_backend
  mode http
  balance roundrobin
  option httpchk GET /health           # check a real readiness endpoint
  http-check expect status 200
  default-server inter 3s fall 3 rise 2 # fail after 3 bad, recover after 2 good
  server app-a <backend-a-host>:8080 check
  server app-b <backend-b-host>:8080 check
  • option httpchk GET /health plus http-check expect status 200 means a backend is only "up" when its readiness endpoint actually returns 200 — not merely when the TCP port accepts a connection.
  • fall/rise give hysteresis so a single blip does not eject a healthy backend, and a single good response does not prematurely re-add a flapping one.

docs.title: L4 vs L7
docs.text: Mode Use for Note mode http (L7) HTTP services needing path/host routing, header logic, HTTP health checks Slightly more overhead; HTTP-aware mode tcp (L4) Databases, message brokers, TLS passthrough, non-HTTP protocols No HTTP features; check with option tcp-check

docs.title: Balancing algorithm
docs.text: roundrobin is a fine default. Use leastconn for long-lived connections (databases, websockets) where request duration varies widely. Persistent sessions (cookie/stick-table) only when the app genuinely needs affinity — sticky sessions undermine even balancing and complicate draining.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
1. Edit the config.
  2. Validate:
haproxy -c -f /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg
  1. Reload (graceful — existing connections finish):
systemctl reload haproxy
systemctl status haproxy --no-pager
  1. To take a backend out for maintenance, drain it first (set it to MAINT/drain via the admin socket or config) so in-flight requests complete, then remove it. Do not delete a live backend and reload — that drops its connections.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
haproxy -c -f /etc/haproxy/haproxy.cfg     # config valid
# via stats socket (restricted): show backend/server state
echo "show stat" | socat stdio /run/haproxy/admin.sock | cut -d, -f1,2,18

Confirm:

  • All intended servers show UP; none stuck DOWN or flapping.
  • The health check targets a real readiness endpoint and reflects app state.
  • After a reload, no connection errors or 5xx spike; traffic is distributed as intended.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Check / response Users hit errors despite "healthy" LB Health check passes before app is ready Point httpchk at a true readiness endpoint; add expect status. Backends flap in/out fall/rise/inter too aggressive, or backend overloaded Add hysteresis; investigate backend latency. Connections dropped on change Backend removed + reload instead of drain Drain to MAINT first, let connections finish, then remove. Uneven load Sticky sessions or roundrobin with long-lived conns Use leastconn; reconsider session affinity. TCP service mis-checked L4 service with HTTP check Use mode tcp + option tcp-check. Restart caused an outage Used restart, not reload Always reload for config changes.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep the previous config under version control or as a timestamped copy.
  2. Restore the prior config, haproxy -c, then systemctl reload haproxy.
  3. If a backend change went wrong, re-enable the drained/removed backend and reload; confirm it returns to UP.
  4. If a reload left HAProxy degraded and config won't validate, restore the last-known-good file before debugging further.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Restrict the stats page and admin socket to trusted operators/networks; they can change routing.
  • Do not publish real backend addresses, hostnames, or the admin socket path.
  • For TLS termination, use a managed certificate lifecycle (see the Nginx + TLS standard).
  • Keep HAProxy patched; it is on the request path and often internet-facing.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/networking team.
  • Re-validate that health checks still reflect readiness when an app's startup behavior changes.
  • Review balancing algorithm and affinity when traffic patterns change.
  • Confirm stats/admin access controls on a regular cadence.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • HAProxy configuration manual

docs.title: Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines safe patterns for deploying Nginx or OpenResty as a reverse proxy that terminates TLS in front of application upstreams.

A reverse proxy is a live production dependency. Every change must match the installed runtime, pass validation before reload, and remain reversible afterward.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
• Terminate HTTPS for application upstreams.
  • Standardize HTTP, WebSocket, or streaming proxy behavior.
  • Manage certificates and renewal.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
• Kubernetes ingress-controller implementation details.
  • WAF or DDoS policy.
  • Primary L4/TCP load balancing.
  • CDN cache strategy.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Separate listeners, upstreams, and shared proxy parameters.
  2. Use a controlled certificate lifecycle.
  3. Never commit real domains, upstream addresses, certificates, or private keys.
  4. Run nginx -t before every reload.
  5. Treat forwarded headers as a trust boundary.
  6. Configure long-lived connection behavior only where required.

docs.title: Request-Path Architecture
docs.text:
flowchart LR Client[Client] --> Edge[Optional CDN or load balancer] Edge --> Proxy[Nginx or OpenResty TLS proxy] Proxy --> Pool[Upstream pool] Pool --> AppA[Application A] Pool --> AppB[Application B] Cert[Certificate lifecycle] --> Proxy Config[Versioned proxy configuration] --> Proxy Catalog[Domain, route, owner, upstream catalog] --> Config Observe[Access logs, errors, latency, status codes, connection metrics] --> Proxy Observe --> Pool

The proxy is both a traffic path and a policy boundary. Domain ownership, certificate state, upstream membership, forwarded-header trust, and endpoint-specific timeout behavior must be managed as one product model.

docs.title: Change and Rollback Flow
docs.text: flowchart TD     A[Record nginx -V and effective configuration] --> B[Edit the smallest configuration fragment]     B --> C[Run nginx -t]     C --> D{Syntax and referenced files valid?}     D -- No --> E[Reject candidate and keep current runtime]     D -- Yes --> F[Graceful reload]     F --> G[Validate TLS, HTTP, upstreams, WebSocket, and streaming]     G --> H{Acceptance checks pass?}     H -- Yes --> I[Close change and retain evidence]     H -- No --> J[Restore last-known-good config and certificate pair]     J --> C

docs.title: Product Integration
docs.text: Product capability Managed object Required behavior Asset inventory Domain, listener, route, upstream, owner, environment Show the full request path and accountable service owner. Certificate management Certificate, private-key reference, issuer, expiry, renewal job Track lifecycle and verify the certificate served by the live endpoint. Configuration and change Config revision, runtime version, validation result, approval Bind reload to a reviewed and syntax-validated candidate. Service discovery Upstream members, ports, health contract Keep routing aligned with real application endpoints. Observability Status codes, latency, resets, active connections, upstream failures Detect behavior regressions after reload. Recovery center Last-known-good config and certificate pair Restore one atomic rollback unit and repeat acceptance checks.

docs.title: Runtime Boundary
docs.text:
Before copying TLS or HTTP/2 directives, record the actual runtime:

nginx -V
openssl version

Confirm the Nginx/OpenResty version, build arguments, linked OpenSSL, and required modules. http2 on; appeared in Nginx 1.25.1. Older supported builds commonly use listen 443 ssl http2;. TLS 1.3 also depends on the linked OpenSSL capability.

docs.title: Reverse Proxy Baseline
docs.text:
upstream example_upstream {
    server <backend-a-host>:8080 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    server <backend-b-host>:8080 max_fails=3 fail_timeout=30s;
    keepalive 32;
}

server {
    listen 80;
    server_name service.example.com;
    return 301 https://$host$request_uri;
}

Passive failure parameters do not replace application health checks.

docs.title: TLS Listener
docs.text:
server {
    listen 443 ssl;
    server_name service.example.com;

    ssl_certificate     /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.fullchain.pem;
    ssl_certificate_key /etc/nginx/certs/service.example.com.key;
    ssl_protocols       TLSv1.2 TLSv1.3;
    ssl_session_cache   shared:SSL:10m;
    ssl_session_timeout 10m;

    client_max_body_size 16m;

    location / {
        proxy_pass http://example_upstream;
        proxy_http_version 1.1;
        proxy_set_header Host              $host;
        proxy_set_header X-Real-IP         $remote_addr;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-For   $proxy_add_x_forwarded_for;
        proxy_set_header X-Forwarded-Proto $scheme;
        proxy_connect_timeout 5s;
        proxy_send_timeout    60s;
        proxy_read_timeout    60s;
    }
}

Do not paste a generic ssl_ciphers value or force server cipher preference without testing the client population, runtime, and linked OpenSSL. Nginx currently defaults ssl_prefer_server_ciphers to off; TLS 1.3 does not use the same legacy cipher-string behavior.

docs.title: HTTP/2 Syntax
docs.text:
Nginx 1.25.1 and later:

listen 443 ssl;
http2 on;

Older supported builds may use:

listen 443 ssl http2;

Use only the form that passes nginx -t on the deployed binary.

docs.title: Forwarded Header Trust
docs.text:
X-Forwarded-Proto can create redirect loops when the proxy and application disagree about the original scheme. Set it consistently and configure the application to trust only known proxies.

If Nginx is behind a CDN or another proxy, do not trust arbitrary client-provided forwarding headers. Configure explicit trusted proxy ranges and validate the derived client address.

docs.title: WebSocket
docs.text:
Place the map directive in the http context, not inside a server or location block.

map $http_upgrade $connection_upgrade {
    default upgrade;
    ''      close;
}

location /ws/ {
    proxy_pass http://example_upstream;
    proxy_http_version 1.1;
    proxy_set_header Upgrade    $http_upgrade;
    proxy_set_header Connection $connection_upgrade;
    proxy_read_timeout 3600s;
}

Scope long timeouts to the endpoint that needs them.

docs.title: Streaming Responses
docs.text:
location /events/ {
    proxy_pass http://example_upstream;
    proxy_buffering off;
    proxy_read_timeout 3600s;
}

Do not disable buffering globally. Validate connection count, memory use, timeout behavior, and cancellation.

docs.title: Change Procedure
docs.text:
1. Record runtime and effective configuration:
nginx -V
sudo nginx -T > /tmp/nginx-effective-before.txt
  1. Edit the smallest relevant fragment.
  2. Run sudo nginx -t.
  3. Reload with sudo systemctl reload nginx.
  4. Check service status, HTTP/TLS behavior, affected long-lived connections, and logs.
  5. Roll back immediately when acceptance checks fail.

docs.title: Certificate Lifecycle
docs.text:
echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -issuer -subject -dates
  • Test automated renewal before production use.
  • Serve the required certificate chain.
  • Protect the private key with restrictive permissions.
  • Reload after replacement and verify the live endpoint.
  • Alert early enough to recover from a failed renewal.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
sudo nginx -t
curl -I https://service.example.com
curl -sS https://service.example.com/health
openssl s_client -connect service.example.com:443 -servername service.example.com </dev/null

Confirm:

  • Expected certificate chain and protocol.
  • Correct redirects and forwarded scheme.
  • Reachable upstreams and expected status codes.
  • Stable WebSocket or streaming connections where used.
  • No unexplained 4xx, 5xx, or connection-reset spike after reload.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Symptom Likely cause Response 502 Bad Gateway Upstream unavailable, wrong port, or name-resolution failure Reach the upstream from the proxy host and correct the upstream definition. 504 Gateway Timeout Upstream slow or timeout too low Raise timeout only when the upstream contract requires it; otherwise fix the upstream. TLS handshake failure Wrong/expired chain, key mismatch, or unsupported protocol Inspect runtime versions, chain, and key/certificate match. http2 on rejected Runtime predates the directive or lacks the module Use version-supported syntax or upgrade deliberately. Redirect loop Proxy and application disagree about the scheme Correct forwarding and trusted-proxy handling. WebSocket disconnects Missing upgrade headers or timeout too short Add endpoint-scoped upgrade handling and a tested timeout. Streaming response arrives in bursts Proxy buffering remains enabled Disable buffering only for that location. 413 response Upload limit too small Set the limit to the largest legitimate upload. Reload succeeds but traffic fails Syntax passed but behavior was not validated Restore the last-known-good configuration and reload.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Keep the previous configuration and certificate pair as one rollback unit.
  2. Restore the last-known-good fragment.
  3. Run nginx -t before reload.
  4. Reload and repeat the complete acceptance checks.
  5. Do not leave production traffic on a failing candidate while debugging.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Protect private keys and never commit them.
  • Avoid logging credentials, session data, or secret-bearing query parameters.
  • Restrict public exposure of administrative routes.
  • Patch Nginx/OpenResty and linked libraries on a controlled cadence.
  • Reassess protocol and cipher policy when the runtime or client population changes.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: platform/networking team.
  • Revalidate against the supported Nginx/OpenResty and OpenSSL baseline.
  • Test renewal and expiry alerting quarterly.
  • Revalidate forwarded headers, WebSocket, and streaming behavior when upstream applications change.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Nginx SSL module documentation
  • Nginx HTTP/2 module documentation
  • Mozilla SSL Configuration Generator

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate OpenVPN as a bounded remote access mechanism.

The core judgment:

VPN access is privileged network reachability. It should prove who can reach which network path, why that path exists, how it is revoked, and when it should be replaced.

OpenVPN can be appropriate for small, legacy, vendor, emergency, lab, or transitional environments. It must not become a permanent shortcut around identity-aware access, bastion workflows, service-specific gateways, or least-privilege network design.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • engineers need temporary or bounded access to private systems
  • a small environment does not yet have a stronger identity-aware access layer
  • vendor or emergency access needs a time-limited network path
  • legacy systems require network-level reachability
  • lab or migration work needs controlled access before a better access model exists

Prefer identity-aware access, bastion sessions, workload-specific gateways, or application-level authorization when they provide stronger audit, narrower scope, and easier revocation.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not cover consumer VPN usage, anonymous browsing, bypassing company access controls, or approving broad network access where service-level access would be safer.

docs.title: Access Contract
docs.text:
Before issuing VPN access, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Access owner Who approves, reviews, and revokes this access? VPN access without an owner becomes permanent network reachability. Identity Which person, device, automation identity, or vendor is represented by the credential? Shared profiles remove accountability. Scope Which CIDRs, hosts, ports, or services are reachable? Which are denied? A VPN route can silently expose far more than the intended service. Environment Does the access touch production, staging, lab, or shared infrastructure? Production reachability needs stronger approval and logging. Duration Is the access permanent, temporary, vendor-bound, or emergency-only? Temporary access must expire by design. DNS behavior Which names resolve through VPN, and which stay local? Split DNS mistakes send users to the wrong endpoint. Logging Which connection and access evidence is retained? Incident review needs user, time, assigned address, and source context. Revocation How is the credential revoked and how is revocation verified? Revocation that is not tested is only an intention. Replacement path Should this access move to bastion, identity-aware proxy, or service gateway? VPN should not become the default long-term access architecture by accident.

docs.title: Keep route scope smaller than user trust
docs.text:
Do not grant broad routes because the user is trusted. Grant only the network paths required for the task.

Document:

  • approved users or groups
  • allowed CIDRs
  • allowed hosts or services
  • allowed ports where firewall rules support it
  • explicitly denied environments
  • approval owner
  • expiry date for temporary access
  • revocation method

Avoid pushing every private subnet to every VPN user. A route table is an access-control decision.

docs.title: Use one credential per identity
docs.text:
Each user, device, automation identity, or vendor should have its own credential. Shared .ovpn profiles are unacceptable for production access because they cannot prove who connected or who must be revoked.

Track:

  • identity
  • credential owner
  • device or use case
  • issue date
  • expiry date
  • approval record
  • MFA or compensating control where available
  • revocation status
  • last access review

If a shared profile already exists, treat it as a migration risk. Replace it with individual credentials and rotate shared material.

docs.title: Treat vendor and emergency access as expiring exceptions
docs.text:
Vendor and emergency access should be narrower than normal access, not broader.

Required controls:

  • named sponsor
  • named vendor identity where possible
  • specific service or network scope
  • start time and expiry time
  • approval reason
  • logging enabled before access is granted
  • revocation test after work ends
  • post-access review for production paths

Do not create a permanent vendor VPN path because re-approval is inconvenient.

docs.title: Make DNS behavior explicit
docs.text:
VPN failures often look like application failures because DNS silently points users at the wrong endpoint.

Document:

  • whether split tunnel is enabled
  • which domains resolve through VPN DNS
  • which routes are pushed
  • whether public DNS remains available
  • whether internal names can collide with public names
  • expected behavior when VPN disconnects

Validation must include both allowed and denied name resolution paths.

docs.title: Log enough to support incident review
docs.text:
At minimum, retain:

  • connection time
  • disconnect time
  • identity
  • assigned VPN address
  • source context where available
  • certificate or credential identifier
  • failed authentication attempts
  • revocation events

VPN logs do not replace host, bastion, application, or database audit logs. They only prove network reachability. Sensitive systems still need their own audit trail.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before granting or changing access:

openvpn --version

From a test client:

curl -fsS https://<internal-service>/healthz
nc -vz <internal-host> <port>

Pass conditions:

  • the user can reach only approved services or networks
  • denied networks and denied ports are unreachable
  • production and non-production reachability match the access contract
  • DNS resolves expected internal names and does not hijack unrelated names
  • logs identify the user, assigned address, and connection window
  • revocation removes access from the same client path
  • temporary or vendor access has an expiry and review owner
  • no VPN profile, private key, route table, internal hostname, or customer-specific path appears in public documentation

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Shared client profile No user accountability and broad revocation blast radius. Issue one credential per identity and rotate shared material. Overbroad route push Users reach unrelated private systems. Limit routes and firewall rules to the task. Untested revocation Former users or vendors retain access. Test CRL or credential revocation from a client path. Split DNS confusion Users reach the wrong endpoint or bypass intended controls. Document DNS scope and validate allowed and denied names. Long-lived vendor access Temporary path becomes permanent access. Require expiry, sponsor, and post-work revocation. VPN treated as audit Operators cannot prove what happened after connection. Pair VPN logs with host, bastion, application, or database audit. Private keys copied into docs or tickets Network access material leaks. Keep profiles and keys in approved secret storage only. VPN becomes the default architecture Broad network access replaces service-level access. Track replacement path and review long-lived access.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Access rollback may include:

  • revoke a user certificate
  • remove a user profile
  • remove a pushed route
  • narrow firewall rules
  • disable a vendor or emergency credential
  • rotate shared material if a shared profile existed
  • disable the VPN service only as an incident response measure

Verify from the revoked client path that access is no longer possible. For suspected credential exposure, revocation alone may not be enough; rotate shared keys, review logs, and check the systems reachable during the exposure window.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Use MFA or an identity-aware access layer where available.
  • Do not share .ovpn profiles.
  • Protect client private keys and device storage.
  • Keep VPN endpoints patched.
  • Restrict management interface access.
  • Do not expose VPN administration publicly.
  • Do not publish real routes, internal hostnames, private IPs, client names, profiles, certificates, private keys, shared secrets, or customer-specific network paths.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text: Review VPN access monthly or quarterly depending on risk, after staff changes, after vendor work ends, after network exposure incidents, before broad route changes, and whenever a VPN path becomes long-lived enough that a narrower access model should replace it.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• JumpServer and Bastion Access Standard
  • Server Baseline Standard
  • Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate OpenWrt-based network devices in lab, edge, or small-site environments.

The core judgment:

A small router is still infrastructure when traffic depends on it. The risk is not that OpenWrt is small; the risk is that its configuration is changed like a personal device while it carries shared routing, DNS, VPN, or firewall decisions.

OpenWrt can be useful in bounded environments. It must not become an undocumented production dependency.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • lab routing
  • small-office or edge routing
  • VPN gateway experiments
  • network testing
  • custom firmware evaluation
  • temporary connectivity where owner, backup, and rollback are explicit

Do not use unmanaged OpenWrt devices as hidden dependencies for production networks, remote access, DNS, VPN, or firewall enforcement.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not:

  • recommend OpenWrt as the default production router platform
  • document ISP-specific values
  • publish Wi-Fi passwords, VPN profiles, MAC lists, routes, or firewall policies
  • replace enterprise network change management
  • approve remote high-risk changes without recovery access

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before an OpenWrt device carries shared traffic, define:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Owner Who owns configuration, firmware, and incident response? Network devices without owners become forgotten failure points. Role Is the device lab, edge, VPN, DNS, wireless, or firewall? Validation depends on the role. Firmware baseline Which firmware version and packages are approved? Package drift changes routing and security behavior. Configuration backup Where are UCI exports and restore instructions stored? A bad change must be reversible. Management access Which networks may reach SSH or LuCI? WAN or broad admin exposure is a direct attack path. Trust boundaries What are the firewall zones, VPN peers, and DNS behavior? Zone mistakes silently over-trust networks. Recovery access Is there console, onsite support, or fail-open/fail-closed plan? Remote network changes can cut off the operator. Secret boundary Where are Wi-Fi keys, VPN keys, and ISP credentials stored? Router backups often contain sensitive material.

docs.title: Separate firmware, config, and secrets
docs.text:
Track these separately:

  • firmware version
  • package list
  • network configuration
  • firewall policy
  • DHCP and DNS behavior
  • VPN configuration
  • wireless settings
  • secrets and keys

Public documentation must use placeholders only. Private backups must be stored in an approved private location.

docs.title: Back up before every risky change
docs.text:
Before changing firmware, firewall, VPN, DNS, DHCP, or routing, export relevant configuration:

uci export network
uci export firewall
uci export dhcp

This example is partial. Export every affected UCI section for the change, including VPN, wireless, system, or service-specific sections when they participate in rollback. Store secret-bearing exports privately; do not put them in public docs or ordinary repositories.

Also record:

  • firmware version
  • package list
  • change owner
  • expected connectivity after change
  • rollback command or restore path

docs.title: Harden management access
docs.text:
Minimum controls:

  • change default passwords
  • restrict SSH and LuCI to approved management networks
  • disable WAN administration
  • disable unused services
  • keep firmware current within the approved baseline
  • avoid password-based remote access where keys are approved
  • log changes where practical

docs.title: Validate denied paths, not only allowed paths
docs.text:
Network validation must prove both:

  • required paths work
  • forbidden paths remain blocked

Examples:

  • management access works only from approved network
  • WAN cannot reach SSH or LuCI
  • VPN peers reach only intended routes
  • DNS resolution follows the intended resolver path
  • firewall zones deny cross-zone traffic that should not be trusted

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Use generic commands and placeholders:

uci show network
uci show firewall
logread | tail

Pass conditions:

  • Management access works from approved network only.
  • WAN admin access is blocked.
  • Required routes work.
  • DNS behavior matches the plan.
  • Firewall zones match the intended trust boundary.
  • VPN peers are limited to intended routes.
  • Backup is current and restorable.
  • Recovery access exists before high-risk remote changes.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention No config backup Device change is hard to recover. Export config before risky changes. WAN admin exposed Device can be attacked remotely. Restrict management interfaces. VPN key published Private network exposed. Keep keys out of docs and repos. Firmware drift Behavior differs from expected baseline. Record firmware and package list. Firewall zone mistake Networks over-trust each other. Validate denied paths and zone boundaries. Remote change cuts access Operator cannot repair the device. Ensure out-of-band or onsite recovery. DNS or DHCP change unreviewed Clients fail in ways that look like application issues. Treat DNS/DHCP changes as network-impacting changes.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Rollback options:

  • restore previous UCI configuration
  • revert firewall rules
  • restore previous firmware if supported
  • disable a new VPN or route
  • factory reset and reapply approved backup

For remote devices, confirm recovery access before risky changes. If recovery depends on onsite support, schedule the change when that support is available.

Do not make simultaneous firmware, firewall, VPN, and DNS changes unless the rollback plan covers all of them together.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish Wi-Fi passwords, VPN profiles, private keys, ISP account values, MAC lists, or real routes.
  • Restrict SSH and LuCI access.
  • Disable unused services.
  • Store backups privately because they may contain secrets.
  • Keep firmware within the approved baseline.
  • Review any service exposed to WAN.
  • Avoid using a lab device as an untracked production dependency.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • after firmware upgrades
  • after VPN changes
  • after firewall or zone changes
  • after DNS/DHCP changes
  • after remote-access changes
  • after network incidents
  • before a lab or temporary device becomes long-lived

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• OpenVPN Access Standard
  • Cloud Network and Object Storage Migration Standard
  • Server Baseline Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Samba file shares for internal SMB/CIFS access.

The core judgment:

A file share is a data boundary, not a folder with a network path. The share defines who can change data, how deletion is recovered, and which team owns the risk when access is too broad.

Samba can be appropriate for bounded internal file sharing. It must not become unowned application storage, a secrets dump, or an audit blind spot.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use Samba when:

  • internal users or systems require SMB/CIFS access
  • the share has a clear owner
  • access can be controlled by named users or groups
  • data class and retention are known
  • backup or explicit disposability is documented
  • capacity and access can be monitored

Prefer managed file services, object storage, application-native storage, or document management platforms when they provide stronger durability, identity integration, audit, retention, or governance.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not:

  • replace enterprise document management
  • approve unmanaged public shares
  • define application data modeling
  • cover Internet-exposed SMB
  • approve storing secrets in shared folders

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Every share needs:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Owner Who approves access, deletion, restore, and retirement? Unknown-owner shares accumulate risk. Purpose What workflow or system uses the share? Generic dumping grounds become impossible to govern. Data class Is the data public, internal, sensitive, regulated, temporary, or disposable? Backup, audit, and access depend on data class. Access model Which users or groups can read, write, administer, or delete? Broad write access weakens accountability. Filesystem boundary Which path is exported and what Unix permissions apply? Samba policy cannot fix an unsafe filesystem path. Backup and restore What is backed up and how is restore tested? Accidental deletion is common for shared storage. Capacity Which filesystem, quota, disk, and inode alerts exist? One share can fill a host. Audit Which access or change logs are required? Sensitive data needs traceability. Retirement When should the share be archived, migrated, or deleted? Old shares become unreviewed data stores.

docs.title: Use named users and groups
docs.text:
Avoid guest write access. Use named users and groups so access can be reviewed and revoked.

Required access records:

  • share owner
  • approved read groups
  • approved write groups
  • admin group
  • exception list
  • review date

docs.title: Align Samba config with filesystem permissions
docs.text:
The exported path, Unix ownership, group ownership, create mask, and directory mask must match the intended access model.

Example with placeholders:

[example-share]
  path = /srv/samba/example
  browsable = yes
  read only = yes
  valid users = @example-readers @example-writers
  write list = @example-writers
  create mask = 0660
  directory mask = 0770

Use an all-writer share only when every approved user is allowed to create, modify, and delete files. Do not rely on Samba config alone if the underlying filesystem permissions are broad.

docs.title: Back up before broadening access
docs.text:
Before granting write access to a new group, confirm:

  • data class
  • owner approval
  • backup coverage
  • restore procedure
  • expected write pattern
  • capacity impact

Shared file systems are vulnerable to accidental deletion, bulk overwrite, and ransomware-like behavior. Backup and restore are part of the access decision.

docs.title: Monitor data growth and access risk
docs.text:
Minimum monitoring:

  • filesystem usage
  • inode usage
  • backup success
  • failed authentication attempts
  • share availability
  • audit logs for sensitive shares

Where possible, set quotas or alerts per share or filesystem.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
testparm
smbclient -L //<file-server> -U <user>

Pass conditions:

  • Samba configuration validates.
  • Only approved users or groups can access the share.
  • Write behavior matches the documented policy.
  • Underlying filesystem permissions do not overexpose data.
  • Backup includes required paths.
  • Restore has been tested for representative files.
  • Disk and inode usage are monitored.
  • Logs are retained where audit is required.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Result Prevention Guest write enabled No accountability for changes or deletion. Use named users and groups. Broad filesystem permissions Users access unrelated data despite Samba rules. Restrict path ownership and masks. No backup Accidental deletion becomes data loss. Back up business data and test restore. No capacity alert Share fills underlying filesystem. Monitor bytes and inodes. Secrets stored in share Credentials spread through backups and clients. Use secret management instead of file shares. Stale owner No one can approve access or deletion. Review owner and purpose regularly. Real paths published Internal structure leaks. Use placeholders in public docs.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery may include:

  • restore deleted files from backup
  • revert Samba configuration
  • revoke a user or group
  • move a share to a larger filesystem
  • disable the share during incident response
  • restore previous filesystem permissions

Do not delete large directories until owner, data class, backup status, and retention requirements are known.

If broad access caused data modification, preserve logs and backups before cleanup.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish real share names, users, hosts, access paths, or internal directory structure.
  • Restrict admin access.
  • Use least-privilege groups.
  • Avoid storing secrets in shared folders.
  • Audit access for sensitive data.
  • Review SMB protocol exposure and firewall boundaries.
  • Do not expose SMB directly to the public Internet.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • after access changes
  • after data growth
  • after file deletion incidents
  • after backup failures
  • after share owner changes
  • before migrating data
  • before granting write access to a new team or system

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Server Baseline Standard
  • NFS PVC Capacity Management
  • OpenVPN Access Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This runbook handles TLS certificate expiry alerts for public endpoints, ingress resources, reverse proxies, or internal services with production clients.

Core judgment:

A certificate expiry is rarely an OpenSSL problem. It is usually an ownership and renewal-path problem becoming visible.

Renewing the file is only part of the response. The responder must identify the endpoint owner, TLS termination point, renewal authority, reload path, client trust boundary, validation signal, and the process gap that allowed the alert to fire.

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook when:

  • certificate expiry is below warning or critical threshold
  • a client reports certificate expired, hostname mismatch, or chain failure
  • an ingress or proxy serves an unexpected certificate
  • a renewal job failed
  • a compromised certificate or key requires replacement

Recommended thresholds:

Severity Days Remaining Response Warning 30 Identify owner and renewal path; schedule renewal. Critical 7 Treat as production risk; renew or replace before user impact. Expired 0 Incident response; restore trust path immediately.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Before changing certificates, confirm:

  • endpoint owner and rollback owner
  • exact hostname and SAN requirements
  • TLS termination point: ingress, reverse proxy, load balancer, service mesh, or application
  • certificate source: public CA, internal CA, ACME, cert-manager, cloud certificate manager, or manual issue
  • current certificate, key, and chain backup location
  • whether clients need trust-store updates
  • maintenance or reload impact

Do not print, copy, paste, commit, or attach private keys during triage.

docs.title: Initial Triage
docs.text:
Check the served certificate from the client-facing path:

echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -subject -issuer -dates -ext subjectAltName

For Kubernetes:

kubectl get ingress -A
kubectl get certificate -A
kubectl get secret example-tls -n example-namespace

Compare:

  • certificate served to clients
  • certificate stored in the expected secret or file
  • hostname/SAN coverage
  • chain order and issuer
  • renewal controller status
  • monitoring alert target

If the served certificate differs from the expected secret or file, the problem is likely the TLS termination point, reload path, ingress class, load balancer, or stale proxy process.

docs.title: Diagnosis
docs.text: Symptom Likely Cause Check Certificate near expiry Renewal job missing, failed, or unowned. Renewal controller logs, CA account, owner record. Expired certificate still served after renewal Proxy or ingress did not reload. Process reload status and served certificate. Wrong certificate served Host points to wrong ingress, secret, proxy, or load balancer listener. DNS, ingress class, listener, SNI, secret reference. Hostname warning SAN does not include requested hostname. subjectAltName and expected host list. Chain warning Intermediate missing or chain order wrong. openssl s_client -showcerts and client trust. Some clients still fail Client trust store, pinned certificate, cache, or old node path. Affected client class and trust-store version.

docs.title: Response
docs.text:
1. Identify certificate owner, service owner, and reload owner.
  2. Confirm the correct renewal method and CA path.
  3. Renew or issue the certificate through the approved process.
  4. Validate certificate, key match, SANs, issuer, expiry, and chain before installation.
  5. Update the approved secret, certificate manager object, proxy path, or load balancer certificate.
  6. Reload or roll only the TLS-terminating component where possible.
  7. Validate from at least one client-facing path.
  8. Confirm the alert recovers and the renewal path is repaired.

For Kubernetes cert-manager, prefer fixing the Certificate, Issuer, or ClusterIssuer flow instead of manually patching a secret unless incident pressure requires a temporary manual replacement.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
External validation:

curl -Iv https://service.example.com

Certificate validation:

echo | openssl s_client -servername service.example.com -connect service.example.com:443 -showcerts -verify_return_error 2>/dev/null \
  | openssl x509 -noout -issuer -subject -dates -ext subjectAltName

Representative client validation should also be performed from the same class of client that failed or will depend on the certificate:

curl --fail --show-error --location https://service.example.com/healthz

When hostname verification or trust behavior differs by client type, validate from those client classes instead of relying only on OpenSSL output.

Pass conditions:

  • Served certificate is the expected new certificate.
  • Expiry is beyond the warning threshold.
  • Hostname appears in SAN.
  • Chain is complete and trusted by intended clients.
  • Application health endpoint works through TLS.
  • Synthetic or monitoring check recovers.
  • Renewal controller, CA job, or manual ownership record is repaired.
  • Private key was not exposed during the change.

If a certificate was replaced because of key compromise, validation is not complete until the old key is revoked or destroyed according to the CA and platform policy.

docs.title: Rollback and Escalation
docs.text:
Rollback options:

  • restore the previous certificate and key when the new chain is wrong and the old certificate is still valid
  • switch traffic back to the previous TLS listener or ingress route
  • revert the secret or certificate reference
  • roll back the proxy or ingress reload if the new configuration broke routing

Do not roll back to an expired or compromised certificate unless there is no safer temporary path and an incident commander explicitly accepts the risk.

Escalate when:

  • the private key may have leaked
  • the CA account or ACME flow is broken
  • the service uses certificate pinning
  • client trust stores need coordinated update
  • the TLS termination point is unclear
  • renewal cannot complete before expiry

docs.title: Closure Criteria
docs.text:
• Certificate is valid beyond the warning threshold.
  • Alert recovered on the real monitoring path.
  • Owner, renewal method, and next expiry are recorded.
  • Renewal automation or manual calendar is fixed.
  • Any temporary manual secret or exception is removed or tracked with expiry.
  • Related runbooks or service records point to the right termination point.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Renewal owner unknown Alert waits until expiry pressure. Track owner and renewal method per hostname. Secret updated but ingress not reloaded New certificate exists but clients still fail. Validate served certificate, not only stored certificate. Missing intermediate Some clients reject the chain. Validate full chain from client-facing path. SAN mismatch Browser or client rejects hostname. Compare SANs to expected host list before install.
