Manual hotfix left in place Automation later overwrites the fix. Reconcile manual change into the renewal source of truth. Private key pasted into ticket Certificate rotation becomes credential incident. Keep keys out of public tools, chat, and tickets.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Nginx Reverse Proxy and TLS Standard
  • Harbor Certificate Rotation Runbook
  • Application Maintenance Health Check Standard

docs.title: Observability
docs.text:
Metrics, logs, traces, alerting, monitoring migrations, and instrumentation.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Understand

Build the core mental model: how the components cooperate, why the system is designed this way, and where its boundaries are.

  • Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration Deep dive on why monitoring migrations fail at alert routing and label continuity, not at moving the Prometheus, Alertmanager, and Grafana binaries.
6 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • ELFK Logging Standard Standard for an Elasticsearch/Logstash/Filebeat/Kibana logging stack — index lifecycle and retention, disk-watermark safety, structured logs, and access control.
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard Standard for adding Prometheus exporters as a controlled change — placement, least-privilege metrics access, the down/target-down distinction, and required alert coverage.
  • Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard Standard for running Loki and Alertmanager as non-Helm systemd services with safe retention, ruler, and notification-secret handling.
  • Loki Logging Standard Loki logging standard for label design, collection boundaries, retention, access, cost control, privacy, and incident validation.
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard Prometheus alerting standard for symptoms, ownership, routing, runbooks, inhibition, silence policy, and failure-path validation.
  • SigNoz Observability Standard SigNoz observability standard for using an OpenTelemetry-compatible backend without mistaking a unified UI for production observability.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run an ELFK (Elasticsearch, Logstash, Filebeat, Kibana) logging stack so that it stays available and affordable, and does not become an outage or a data-leak vector itself.

The core judgment of this standard:

A logging stack fails in two predictable ways: it fills its own disk and goes read-only (taking your logs down exactly when you need them), or it quietly indexes secrets and personal data into a searchable store. Retention/lifecycle and what-you-index are not tuning — they are the design.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Run a self-managed Elasticsearch-based logging stack (ELFK/ELK or OpenSearch-equivalent).
  • Need controlled retention, index lifecycle, and access to logs.
  • Decide what to collect and how to keep the store from filling up.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Choosing between ELFK and a metrics-style logging system (Loki) — see the Loki logging standard for that trade-off.
  • Full-text search relevance tuning or APM.
  • Cluster sizing math beyond the capacity-safety principles below.

docs.title: Core Principles
docs.text:
1. Use structured logs (JSON with consistent fields) so logs are queryable, not just greppable.
  2. Control retention with Index Lifecycle Management (ILM) — every index has a defined rollover and delete policy.
  3. Protect against the disk watermark: Elasticsearch goes read-only when disk fills, so capacity and retention must keep you below it.
  4. Restrict Kibana and the Elasticsearch API; logs are sensitive.
  5. Do not index secrets, tokens, credentials, full request bodies, or personal data.

docs.title: Structured logs and a consistent shape
docs.text: Emit JSON logs with stable fields (timestamp, level, service, env, trace id). Do the parsing/enrichment in Filebeat or Logstash, not by indexing free-form text — unstructured logs explode storage and are hard to query.

docs.title: Retention via ILM
docs.text:
Define an ILM policy so indices roll over by size/age and are deleted on schedule. Without it, indices grow until the disk watermark trips and the cluster goes read-only.

PUT _ilm/policy/logs-policy
{
  "policy": {
    "phases": {
      "hot":    { "actions": { "rollover": { "max_size": "50gb", "max_age": "1d" } } },
      "delete": { "min_age": "14d", "actions": { "delete": {} } }
    }
  }
}

Set retention from a deliberate decision (cost vs. how far back you actually investigate), not "keep everything".

docs.title: Capacity and the disk watermark
docs.text: Elasticsearch has disk watermarks (low/high/flood-stage). At flood stage it sets indices read-only — a logging outage that often coincides with the incident you are trying to debug. Keep headroom: size storage for peak ingest × retention, alert on disk usage well before the watermark, and ensure ILM deletion is actually running.

docs.title: Access control
docs.text:
• Put Kibana behind authentication and least-privilege roles; do not expose it openly.
  • Restrict the Elasticsearch HTTP API to trusted networks/clients.
  • Logs frequently contain sensitive data — treat read access as a privilege.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
curl -sS http://<es-host>:9200/_cluster/health?pretty
curl -sS http://<es-host>:9200/_cat/indices?v
curl -sS http://<es-host>:9200/_cat/allocation?v   # disk usage per node

Confirm:

  • Cluster health is green (or a justified yellow), not red.
  • ILM is rolling over and deleting old indices on schedule (_cat/indices shows bounded growth).
  • Disk usage is below the high watermark with headroom.
  • Filebeat/Logstash are shipping; no large ingest backlog.
  • Spot-check that logs do not contain secrets or full sensitive payloads.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Cluster read-only Indexing stops; read_only_allow_delete flag Disk hit flood-stage watermark Free space / delete old indices; clear the block; fix retention. Unbounded growth Disk fills steadily No ILM, or ILM delete not running Apply ILM with rollover + delete; verify it executes. Storage blowup Cost/disk spikes Indexing unstructured/high-cardinality fields Structure logs; drop noisy fields at Filebeat/Logstash. Secrets in index Sensitive data searchable Indexed request bodies/headers/tokens Filter at the shipper; purge affected indices; fix the source. Kibana exposed Unauthorized log access No auth / open network Put Kibana behind auth; restrict network. Red cluster Shards unassigned Node loss, disk, or shard allocation issue Check _cat/allocation and allocation explain; restore capacity.

docs.title: Rollback / Recovery
docs.text:
1. To recover a read-only cluster: free disk (delete or move old indices), then clear the read_only_allow_delete block; afterwards fix ILM/retention so it does not recur.
  2. To roll back an ILM/policy change, restore the previous policy; ILM applies going forward, so verify index ages against the intended retention.
  3. Keep snapshot backups of indices you must retain for compliance; restore from snapshot rather than relying on a live cluster alone.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Never index secrets, tokens, credentials, authorization headers, full request bodies, or personal data — filter at Filebeat/Logstash before they reach the store.
  • Restrict Kibana and the ES API; use TLS and authentication.
  • Do not publish real hostnames, endpoints, or index contents.
  • Treat snapshots as sensitive (they contain log data).

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/observability team.
  • Re-confirm retention vs. cost and investigation needs on a regular cadence.
  • Verify ILM rollover/delete is actually running and disk headroom holds.
  • Re-check what fields are indexed when a new log source is onboarded.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Loki Logging Standard
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Elasticsearch Index Lifecycle Management

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to add Prometheus exporters (Node Exporter, database exporters, Nginx, JVM, blackbox, etc.) so that the metrics are trustworthy, the exporter does not become its own risk, and every exporter ships with the alerts that make it useful.

The core judgment of this standard:

An exporter you can scrape is not the same as monitoring you can trust. An exporter with no alerts is decoration; and the difference between "exporter down" and "target down" is the difference between a blind spot and a real outage — your alerts must tell them apart.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when you:

  • Add Prometheus monitoring for a host, database, web server, or application runtime.
  • Standardize how exporters are deployed, labeled, and secured across services.
  • Need a minimum alert baseline so a new exporter is actually actionable.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Prometheus server sizing, federation, or long-term storage design.
  • Dashboard design beyond requiring that one exists.
  • Application-level custom metrics instrumentation (use OpenTelemetry/SDK instrumentation for that).
  • Alert routing and on-call policy (see the Prometheus alerting standard).

docs.title: Exporter Principles
docs.text:
1. Run the exporter close to the target service (same host or sidecar), so exporter health reflects target reachability locally.
  2. Use a least-privilege metrics account for database/service exporters — read-only stats access, never an admin account.
  3. Do not expose exporters publicly; restrict the metrics port to Prometheus.
  4. Add labels that identify service, environment, team, and owner so alerts are routable.
  5. Every exporter ships with a dashboard and alert rules, or it is not done.

docs.title: Exporter Coverage Checklist
docs.text: Exporter Key signals to scrape Node Exporter CPU saturation, memory pressure, disk capacity & inodes, filesystem/read-only errors, network errors, load average MySQL Exporter Up, connections vs max_connections, replication running & lag, slow queries, buffer pool Redis Exporter Up, memory vs maxmemory, evictions, clients, persistence status, replication MongoDB Exporter Connections, locks/contention, replication lag, storage Nginx Exporter Up, requests, active connections, 5xx rate (via status endpoint) JVM Exporter Heap usage, GC pause/frequency, threads, class loading Blackbox Exporter HTTP/TCP/DNS probe success, and TLS certificate expiry

docs.title: Node Exporter specifics
docs.text:
Node Exporter is the canonical host exporter; run it on every Linux host and restrict its port to Prometheus. The signals that most often catch real incidents:

  • Disk capacity and inodes (a full inode table fails writes while df still shows free space).
  • Read-only / failing filesystems.
  • Memory pressure and OOM indicators.
  • CPU saturation and sustained load average.

docs.title: Scrape Configuration Pattern
docs.text:
Attach identifying labels at scrape time so every series is routable and ownable.

scrape_configs:
  - job_name: node
    static_configs:
      - targets:
          - <exporter-host>:9100
        labels:
          service: example-service
          env: prod
          team: platform

Do not publish real internal targets or hostnames. Prefer service discovery over hand-maintained static target lists at scale.

docs.title: Required Alert Coverage
docs.text:
A new exporter is not "monitored" until these exist, as applicable:

  • Exporter down — up == 0 for the exporter job. Means you are blind, not that the service is down. Page or warn accordingly.
  • Target down — the service the exporter watches is unreachable/unhealthy. This is the real outage signal.
  • Disk capacity and inode exhaustion.
  • Memory pressure.
  • Error-rate (5xx, failed connections) above threshold.
  • Replication lag (databases).
  • TLS certificate expiry (blackbox).
  • JVM GC pressure / heap exhaustion (JVM apps).

Distinguishing exporter down from target down is the single most important design point. If they collapse into one alert, you cannot tell "we lost visibility" from "the service is failing" — and you will eventually trust a green dashboard that is green only because the exporter died.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
# Exporter serves metrics locally:
curl -sS http://<exporter-host>:9100/metrics | head

In Prometheus:

up{job="node"}                      # 1 = scraped OK, 0 = exporter unreachable
rate(node_filesystem_avail_bytes[5m]) # signal is present and changing

Confirm:

  • The target appears in Prometheus targets as UP with the expected labels.
  • Key metrics for the exporter type are present and non-stale.
  • The dashboard renders and the alert rules load without error (promtool check rules).
  • A deliberately failed scrape (stop the exporter briefly in staging) fires the "exporter down" alert.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Likely cause Correct response Green dashboard, dead service All looks fine but users report errors "Exporter down" and "target down" not separated Split the alerts; alert on up == 0 distinctly. Missing labels Alert fires but nobody owns it No service/team/owner labels at scrape Add identifying labels in scrape_configs. Exporter is a security hole Metrics reachable from untrusted networks Exporter port exposed publicly Bind to private interface; restrict port to Prometheus. Over-privileged exporter DB exporter has write/admin access Reused an admin account Create a least-privilege read-only stats user. Stale metrics Values frozen, no alert Exporter up but not collecting Check exporter logs and the target connection; alert on staleness. Cardinality blowup Prometheus memory/CPU spike High-cardinality labels (per-request IDs) Drop/aggregate high-cardinality labels at scrape.

docs.title: Rollback / Removal
docs.text:
1. Removing an exporter removes visibility — confirm nothing depends on its alerts before doing so.
  2. Remove the scrape job and the associated alert rules together; an orphaned alert rule referencing a gone job is noise.
  3. If an exporter caused load or a security concern, restrict its port or stop it first, then remove cleanly.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Exporters expose service metadata; treat the metrics endpoint as internal and restrict it to Prometheus.
  • Use least-privilege, read-only credentials for database/service exporters; store them in a secret manager.
  • Use TLS or private networking where the metrics are sensitive.
  • Avoid high-cardinality labels that can leak identifiers (user IDs, request IDs) into metrics.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform/observability team.
  • Re-check the alert baseline whenever a new exporter type is introduced.
  • Re-validate the exporter-down vs target-down split after Prometheus or exporter upgrades.
  • Review metrics-account privileges when a database exporter is added or rotated.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Production Standard
  • Redis Operations Standard
  • MySQL Operations Standard
  • Prometheus exporters documentation

docs.title: Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard
Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to run Loki as a binary systemd service, with Alertmanager-based alerting, in environments where a full Kubernetes Helm deployment is not appropriate.

It complements the Loki Logging Standard, which covers label design, retention principles, and Kubernetes collection. This page covers the operational boundary specific to a non-Helm install: service isolation, lifecycle, ruler configuration, and keeping notification secrets out of published configuration.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • You run a small or single-node environment where a binary install is simpler to operate than Helm.
  • You need log-based alerting through the Loki ruler and Alertmanager.
  • You want a controlled, reproducible systemd service rather than an ad hoc process.

Prefer the Helm deployment model (see the Loki Logging Standard) for multi-tenant, scalable, or Kubernetes-native environments.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not cover:

  • Loki label and cardinality design (see the Loki Logging Standard).
  • Microservice-mode or simple-scalable Loki topologies.
  • Grafana dashboard design.
  • Notification provider internals beyond keeping their credentials out of config.

docs.title: Service isolation
docs.text:
• Run Loki and Alertmanager under a dedicated, non-login service user, not as root and not as a human account.
  • Keep binaries under a controlled, version-pinned install path.
  • Separate the data, configuration, and rule directories so retention, backup, and permission rules can differ per directory.
  • Manage the lifecycle with systemd units, not with nohup or a terminal session.

Example directory layout:

/opt/loki/bin/            # pinned binaries
/etc/loki/                # configuration
/etc/loki/rules/          # ruler rule groups
/var/lib/loki/            # chunk and index data

docs.title: Retention and ingestion limits
docs.text:
• Set retention explicitly and validate it against the data disk capacity before enabling.
  • Set ingestion rate and burst limits so a misbehaving log source cannot fill the disk.
  • Keep compactor retention enabled when you rely on retention for cost control; retention settings without a working compactor do not actually delete data.

docs.title: Ruler and Alertmanager
docs.text:
• Configure the Loki ruler to load rule groups from /etc/loki/rules/.
  • Point the ruler at an internal Alertmanager address only. Use a generic placeholder in any published example:
ruler:
  alertmanager_url: http://<alertmanager-host>:9093
  storage:
    type: local
    local:
      directory: /etc/loki/rules

The Alertmanager v2 API is the default on current Loki, so no extra flag is needed. On much older Loki versions you may still need to opt in explicitly; verify against the version you run.

  • Keep alert routing in Alertmanager, not embedded in each rule.

docs.title: Keep notification secrets out of configuration
docs.text:
This is the highest-risk part of a binary alerting stack.

  • Never embed webhook URLs, bot tokens, chat IDs, or SMTP credentials in Loki, ruler, Alertmanager, or Grafana configuration that could be committed or published.
  • Load notification secrets from a separate file referenced by environment, a secret store, or systemd credentials — not from the tracked config.
  • In any documentation or example, use placeholders only: <webhook-url>, <smtp-host>, <alert-email>.

Example Alertmanager receiver using an environment-provided secret rather than an inline value:

receivers:
  - name: platform-oncall
    webhook_configs:
      - url: ${ALERT_WEBHOOK_URL}   # provided via systemd EnvironmentFile, not committed

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
1. systemctl status loki and systemctl status alertmanager report active services with no restart loop.
  2. The Loki HTTP endpoint responds locally: 
    curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3100/ready
curl -s http://127.0.0.1:3100/metrics | head
  3. The ruler loads a test rule group without parse errors (check /loki/api/v1/rules and service logs).
  4. A synthetic alert reaches Alertmanager through a non-secret placeholder receiver during the test.
  5. Grafana queries Loki through the internal service address; the Loki port is not exposed publicly.
  6. Disk usage trend after enabling retention matches the expected steady state.

docs.title: Known Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Cause Correct response Secret in config Webhook or SMTP credential visible in tracked config Notification credentials embedded inline Move to EnvironmentFile or secret store; rotate the exposed credential. Disk fills Loki stops ingesting or the host runs out of space Retention set without validating disk capacity, or compactor disabled Lower retention or expand disk; confirm compactor is running. Silent ruler Rules present but no alerts fire Ruler not pointed at Alertmanager, or rule parse error Check ruler logs and alertmanager_url; validate rule syntax. Public Loki port Loki API reachable from outside the trust boundary Service bound to a public interface Bind to internal interface; place access behind the platform proxy. Log volume spike Sudden storage pressure Debug logging left enabled Set log level back to info or warn before steady-state operation.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Stop the affected service with systemd; the unit file makes start and stop deterministic.
  2. Restore the previous configuration and rule directory from backup.
  3. Restart and re-run the validation steps.
  4. If a secret was exposed, rotate it regardless of rollback success.

Keep the previous configuration and binary version until the observation window passes.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Notification secrets must never live in tracked or published configuration.
  • The Loki API must stay inside the trust boundary; do not expose it directly to the internet.
  • Run services under a dedicated low-privilege user.
  • Do not log credentials, tokens, or full authorization headers into Loki itself.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
• Owner: the platform or observability team that runs the binary stack.
  • Re-validate retention against disk capacity whenever log volume grows materially.
  • Re-confirm that no notification secret has drifted into tracked configuration on each change.
  • Re-check binary and ruler compatibility on each Loki upgrade.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Loki Logging Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate Loki-compatible logging for Kubernetes and cloud-native workloads.

Core judgment:

Logs are incident evidence. Loki only helps if the evidence can be found, trusted, retained, and safely shared.

Deploying a collector and seeing lines in Grafana is not enough. The operating contract is label design, collection scope, retention, access, privacy filtering, query cost, and validation during real failure.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • deploying Loki, Promtail, Grafana Agent, Alloy, or another Loki-compatible collector
  • adding Kubernetes log collection
  • changing labels, retention, or tenant boundaries
  • migrating from ELK/EFK-style logging
  • exposing logs to multiple teams
  • investigating noisy, expensive, or unqueryable logs

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not require Loki for every environment and does not define every Helm value. It defines the logging contract that must exist before logs are treated as production evidence.

docs.title: Logging Contract
docs.text:
Before logs become a shared production surface, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters Collection scope Which namespaces, workloads, and node paths are collected or excluded? Accidental collection can leak sensitive logs or waste storage. Label model Which labels are allowed and forbidden? Loki cost and queryability are dominated by label cardinality. Tenant and access model Which teams can read which logs? Logs often contain operational and user context. Retention How long each class of log is kept? Retention is a cost, compliance, and incident-investigation decision. Privacy filtering Where secrets and personal data are masked or dropped? Once stored, sensitive logs spread into queries, screenshots, and tickets. Query budget What query limits protect the platform? Expensive queries can turn debugging into a logging outage. Incident evidence Which log signals are required during a known failure? A log pipeline that fails during incidents is not evidence. Owner Who tunes labels, retention, collector health, and access? Logging without ownership becomes a dumping ground.

docs.title: Label Design
docs.text:
Recommended low-cardinality labels:

cluster
namespace
pod
container
app
environment
team

Avoid high-cardinality labels such as:

request_id
trace_id
order_id
exception_fingerprint

These may be stored as structured fields only when they are non-sensitive, covered by retention and access policy, and needed for investigation.

Sensitive or personal values should not merely be moved from labels into the log body. Mask, drop, tokenize under an approved scheme, or require explicit data-owner approval before storage:

user_id
email
session_id
full_url
authorization_header
request_body

A useful Loki label narrows the search space; it should not create one stream per request, user, or sensitive value.

docs.title: Collection Flow
docs.text:
For Kubernetes:

  1. Deploy the log collector as a DaemonSet or approved node-level agent.
  2. Collect container logs from the node log paths.
  3. Attach Kubernetes metadata.
  4. Drop disallowed namespaces, labels, and fields.
  5. Mask or drop sensitive fields before storage when possible.
  6. Send logs to Loki through the approved endpoint.
  7. Validate queries in Grafana or the approved log UI.

Do not treat kubectl logs as the production logging system. It is useful during local triage, but it does not provide retention, access control, historical search, or cross-workload investigation.

docs.title: Deployment Model
docs.text:
Choose the deployment model deliberately.

Model Use When Watch For Single binary / monolithic Small or single-tenant environments. Local storage pressure and limited scale boundaries. Simple scalable Moderate volume with read/write separation. Object storage, gateway, query frontend, and retention configuration. Microservices High-volume or multi-tenant environments. Operational cost, component health, and upgrade complexity.

For a non-Helm systemd variant, see the Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard.

docs.title: Pin versions and own values
docs.text: Helm chart versions, image versions, and values files should be explicit and version-controlled. Do not track latest. The values file should show retention, storage, resource limits, auth mode, service exposure, and collector relabeling rules.

docs.title: Keep Grafana as a governed access path
docs.text:
Reuse the platform Grafana when it exists. Installing a second Grafana for convenience fragments access control, dashboards, and incident workflow.

Grafana data sources should use stable service names or approved internal endpoints. kubectl port-forward is a debugging convenience, not a production access pattern.

docs.title: Make retention and deletion explicit
docs.text:
Define:

  • default retention
  • extended retention for critical services
  • excluded namespaces
  • log volume budget
  • deletion process for sensitive accidental ingestion
  • who can approve retention exceptions

Retention that is never revisited will either erase incident evidence too early or turn log storage into unbounded cost.

docs.title: Protect query performance
docs.text:
Set query limits and teach query shape:

  • start with narrow time ranges
  • filter by low-cardinality labels first
  • avoid unbounded text scans across all namespaces
  • define limits for query length, result size, and parallelism
  • monitor query latency and failed queries

An observability platform should not become unavailable because one incident query scans the world.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Loki write, read, compactor or retention components are healthy for the chosen mode.
  • Collectors are running on expected nodes and shipping logs.
  • A known workload emits a log line and it appears under the expected labels.
  • A sensitive test field is dropped or masked according to policy.
  • A query for a recent error returns results within the expected time.
  • Retention is configured and observable.
  • Team access rules prevent unrelated teams from reading restricted logs.
  • Collector or Loki failure produces a platform alert outside Loki itself.
  • During a controlled application failure, logs can be found using the agreed labels.

Validation queries:

{namespace="example-namespace"}
{app="example-service"} |= "error"

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Request IDs used as labels Stream count explodes and queries slow down. Keep non-sensitive high-cardinality values in structured fields, not labels. Sensitive values moved from label to body Privacy risk remains, only less visible. Mask, drop, tokenize, or require data-owner approval before storage. No retention owner Cost grows or evidence disappears unexpectedly. Define retention by service class and review it. Sensitive logs stored raw Secrets or personal data spread into tickets and screenshots. Mask or drop at application or collector layer. Collector health not monitored Logs disappear during the incident that needs them. Alert on collector and Loki ingestion health outside Loki. Duplicate Grafana Teams look at different truths. Reuse governed Grafana where available. Port-forward becomes the access model Production investigation depends on a developer tunnel. Use stable internal service or gateway access. Labels too sparse Logs are stored but hard to find. Require namespace, app, environment, and owner labels.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not log credentials, tokens, session IDs, authorization headers, private keys, or full request bodies.
  • Mask personal data at the application or collector layer where possible.
  • Restrict log access by team, environment, and data class.
  • Treat exported log files and screenshots as sensitive artifacts.
  • Keep collector configuration out of public examples when it contains internal paths, tenants, endpoints, or credentials.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review when:

  • log volume changes sharply
  • teams cannot find logs during an incident
  • query latency or failures increase
  • new regulated or personal data may enter logs
  • retention or storage cost changes
  • a logging migration occurs
  • a collector, gateway, or Loki upgrade is planned

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Loki Binary Deployment and Alerting Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • ELFK Logging Standard
  • Application Maintenance Health Check Standard

docs.title: Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration
Core Claim
docs.text:
A monitoring migration — moving or rebuilding a Prometheus, Alertmanager, Grafana, and notification stack — is mostly a migration of labels, alert rules, and alert routing, not a migration of binaries.

The binaries are the easy part. Teams reliably get the new Prometheus, Alertmanager, and Grafana running. What breaks is subtler: alerts that silently never route, dashboards that point at the wrong data source, scrape labels that changed so old rules no longer match, and notification templates that either go quiet or leak sensitive data. The system looks migrated because the UIs load, but its job — telling humans when production is unhealthy — has quietly regressed.

docs.title: Why It Is Misunderstood
docs.text:
The migration is planned as an infrastructure task ("stand up the new servers, point things at them, decommission the old ones"), so success is measured by "the new stack is up." That is the wrong success criterion.

The real success criterion is: a real failure still produces the right alert to the right people. That property lives in labels, rule expressions, routing trees, and receiver secrets — none of which are validated by checking that pods are Ready.

Two forces make this worse:

  • Alert rules and routing are usually owned by different people than the platform doing the migration, so continuity assumptions are never written down.
  • The failure is invisible until an incident. A monitoring stack that has stopped alerting looks identical to a healthy quiet system.

docs.title: Constraints
docs.text:
Any monitoring migration operates under constraints that shape the plan:

  • The old stack must stay authoritative until the new one is proven, because you cannot detect a gap with the same system you are migrating.
  • Scrape target labels (job, instance, namespace, app, and application-specific labels) are part of the alerting contract. Changing them changes which alerts fire.
  • Alert rules and Alertmanager routing must be deployed together and version-controlled together; a rule with no matching route is silent.
  • Notification receivers carry secrets (webhook URLs, SMTP credentials, chat tokens) that must move as secrets, not as copied config.

docs.title: Evidence
docs.text:
A migration validated only by "the stack is up" repeatedly produces the same outcomes:

  • An alert that fired daily on the old stack is never seen again on the new one, because a relabel step renamed the label its expression matched on.
  • A dashboard shows data, but from the old data source, so the team believes the new Prometheus is scraping when it is not.
  • A receiver was copied without its secret, so Alertmanager logs delivery failures that nobody reads.
  • A notification template that once included raw request context is carried over and now leaks that context into a chat channel.

The common thread: every one of these passes a "does the UI work" check and fails a "does a real failure alert" check.

docs.title: Migration Checklist
docs.text:
Treat this as the migration's real definition of done:

  • Existing dashboards still answer real operational questions, not just render panels.
  • Prometheus scrape targets and labels are preserved, or changed intentionally with the dependent rules updated in the same change.
  • Alert rules and Alertmanager routing are deployed together from version control.
  • Notification templates do not leak secrets, request bodies, or personal data.
  • Webhook and SMTP endpoints are stored as secrets or platform-managed values, never inline.
  • Application exporters expose the expected application label that rules depend on.
  • The rollback path keeps the old monitoring stack running and authoritative through the observation window.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
• Prometheus target count on the new stack matches the expected inventory, not just "more than zero."
  • All alert rules load without syntax errors.
  • A synthetic alert traverses the full path and reaches the real notification channel.
  • Grafana dashboards are confirmed to use the new data source.
  • A controlled "application down" scenario produces the expected alert with the expected routing — the single most important test.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Cause Silent alert gap A known alert never fires after migration Relabeling changed a label the rule expression matched on Wrong data source Dashboards render but show stale or no data Panels still bound to the old Prometheus Dead receiver Alertmanager logs delivery errors Receiver copied without its secret Routing drop Rule fires but no human is notified Rule has no matching route in the new routing tree Template leak Sensitive data appears in a notification channel Notification template carried over unredacted

docs.title: Counterexamples
docs.text:
This claim does not mean every monitoring change is high risk:

  • Adding a new dashboard or a new exporter alongside an unchanged alerting path is a low-risk additive change, not a migration.
  • Upgrading the Prometheus binary in place, with labels, rules, and routing untouched, is genuinely an infrastructure task and can be validated as one.
  • A fully GitOps-managed stack where rules, routing, and dashboards already live in version control reduces this to a controlled apply — the routing migration risk was paid down earlier.

The risk concentrates specifically when ownership, labels, or routing change at the same time as the servers.

docs.title: Practical Judgment
docs.text:
• Plan the migration around alert routing continuity, and make "a real failure still alerts the right people" the acceptance test.
  • Run old and new stacks in parallel; let the old one stay authoritative until the new one passes a live failure test.
  • Version-control rules and routing together and migrate them as one change.
  • Treat notification secrets and templates as a security review item, not a copy step.

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Loki Logging Standard
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to write, review, route, and operate Prometheus alerts.

Core judgment:

An alert is a contract to interrupt a human, not a query that happens to be true.

Prometheus can detect many conditions. Only some deserve a notification. A useful alert has a symptom, owner, consequence, threshold, route, runbook, and validation path. Without those, it becomes noise or false confidence.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • adding a new production alert
  • changing severity, threshold, or for duration
  • routing alerts to a team or on-call path
  • migrating Prometheus, Alertmanager, or notification receivers
  • publishing reusable alert examples
  • reviewing noisy, stale, or unactionable alerts

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define every metric name for every stack. It defines the operating contract an alert must satisfy before it is allowed to interrupt people.

docs.title: Alert Contract
docs.text:
Before enabling an alert, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters Symptom What user, service, capacity, or recovery symptom is failing? Alerts on internal mechanics often miss impact or create noise. Owner Which team owns response and tuning? Unowned alerts become background radiation. Severity Is this planned action or immediate response? Critical must mean human interruption now. Threshold What risk boundary does this threshold represent? Arbitrary numbers are difficult to defend under pressure. Duration How long must the symptom persist before notification? for should filter noise without delaying real recovery. Route Which receiver should get it? A correct alert sent to the wrong team is operationally broken. Runbook What should the responder do first? Critical alerts without runbooks transfer design debt to on-call. Silence rule Who may silence it, for how long, and why? Broad silence can hide a real incident. Test signal How will the rule and route be proven? UI green status does not prove a real failure reaches a human.

docs.title: Alert on symptoms before causes
docs.text:
Prefer symptoms tied to user or system outcomes:

  • request error rate
  • latency above guardrail
  • unavailable replicas
  • storage exhaustion
  • queue backlog beyond recovery window
  • certificate or backup expiry
  • SLO burn rate

Cause alerts can be useful, but they should support diagnosis rather than replace symptom alerts. A pod restart alert is weak if users see no impact and automation recovers quickly. A missing ingress, exhausted PVC, or fast SLO burn usually deserves stronger treatment.

docs.title: Make severity carry response meaning
docs.text:
Severity Meaning Route info Needs later review, not immediate action. Dashboard, report, or low-priority channel. warning Human should plan action before risk becomes urgent. Team notification channel. critical Human action is needed now to prevent or recover user impact. On-call or incident path.

Do not use critical for everything. If every alert wakes someone, responders learn to distrust all of them.

docs.title: Use labels for ownership and routing
docs.text:
Recommended labels:

labels:
  severity: warning
  team: platform
  service: example-service
  environment: production
  runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook

Do not put secrets, tokens, real customer names, internal domains, private IPs, or business-sensitive identifiers in alert labels or annotations.

docs.title: Write annotations for action
docs.text:
Recommended annotation shape:

annotations:
  summary: "PVC usage is above warning threshold"
  description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is using {{ $value }} percent of available capacity."
  action: "Check recent growth, confirm owner, and follow the PVC usage runbook."

The annotation should help a tired responder decide what to do next. It should not be a raw expression pasted into a message.

docs.title: Example Rule: PVC Capacity
docs.text:
groups:
  - name: kubernetes-storage
    rules:
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageHigh
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 85
        for: 15m
        labels:
          severity: warning
          team: platform
          service: storage
          environment: production
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 85%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 85% usage for 15 minutes."
          action: "Review growth, confirm owner, and prepare cleanup or expansion."
      - alert: KubernetesPVCUsageCritical
        expr: kubelet_volume_stats_used_bytes / kubelet_volume_stats_capacity_bytes * 100 > 90
        for: 10m
        labels:
          severity: critical
          team: platform
          service: storage
          environment: production
          runbook: kubernetes-pvc-usage-alert-runbook
        annotations:
          summary: "PVC usage is above 90%"
          description: "PVC {{ $labels.persistentvolumeclaim }} in namespace {{ $labels.namespace }} is above 90% usage for 10 minutes."
          action: "Execute the PVC usage runbook and notify the service owner."

docs.title: Routing and Inhibition
docs.text:
Alertmanager routing is part of the alert, not an afterthought.

Required checks:

  • severity reaches the intended receiver.
  • Team labels map to the right ownership route.
  • Critical alerts are not routed only to a dashboard.
  • Maintenance silences are scoped by alert, service, environment, and time.
  • Inhibition suppresses duplicate lower-priority noise without hiding the primary symptom.
  • Receiver templates do not leak secrets, webhook URLs, customer data, or internal topology.

Public examples should use placeholder channels such as platform-alerts and never include webhook URLs.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before enabling or migrating an alert, prove:

  • The PromQL expression returns expected series in the target environment.
  • The expression handles missing metrics when absence is itself a failure.
  • Threshold and for duration are tied to a response window or capacity boundary.
  • The alert fires in a controlled test or staging scenario where practical.
  • The alert reaches the intended receiver with the expected severity.
  • The responder can open the runbook from the message.
  • The alert recovers when the condition recovers.
  • A silence with the intended scope suppresses only the intended alert.

For migrations, validation must include at least one known failure signal from metric to Alertmanager route to human receiver. Moving rules without proving the route preserves only files, not operations.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Alert on non-actionable metric On-call ignores future pages. Require symptom, owner, consequence, and action. Critical overuse Every notification feels urgent; none do. Tie severity to response expectation. Missing metric not handled Outage hides the series and the alert goes quiet. Use absent() or exporter self-health where absence is failure. Wrong route The right signal reaches the wrong humans. Validate Alertmanager route with a controlled signal. Broad silence Real incident is hidden during maintenance. Scope silence by alert, service, environment, and time. No runbook Incident response starts with archaeology. Block critical alerts without a runbook. Unsafe annotation Alert leaks sensitive details into chat or ticket systems. Sanitize message fields and use placeholders in public docs.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review alerts when:

  • a critical alert was ignored
  • an incident had no alert
  • a page reached the wrong owner
  • a rule became noisy
  • a metric name or exporter changed
  • service ownership changed
  • Alertmanager routing changed
  • a monitoring migration occurs

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Kubernetes PVC Usage Alert Runbook
  • Monitoring Migration Is a Routing Migration, Not a Server Migration
  • Application Maintenance Health Check Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to use SigNoz or a similar OpenTelemetry-compatible backend as part of a production observability system.

The core judgment:

SigNoz is not observability by itself. It is a place where telemetry can be queried. It becomes production observability only when ingestion, labels, storage, access, alerts, ownership, and failure validation are designed as an operating contract.

A unified traces, metrics, and logs interface is useful. It is also dangerous if the team treats a working UI as proof that failures will be found during an incident.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Services export OpenTelemetry traces, metrics, or logs.
  • A team needs a trace-first backend for service latency, error, and dependency analysis.
  • The platform wants one controlled telemetry entry point behind OpenTelemetry Collector.
  • The organization accepts the operational cost of running a ClickHouse-backed observability stack.
  • SigNoz is used alongside, not as an accidental replacement for, existing alerting and log standards.

SigNoz is strongest when the main reader task is "why did this request path become slow or fail?" It is weaker as the only source of infrastructure alerting, long-retention log archive, or compliance audit evidence.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not:

  • Teach OpenTelemetry instrumentation. See the OpenTelemetry and Java instrumentation documents below.
  • Replace Prometheus alerting standards for infrastructure and SLO alerts.
  • Replace Loki or another log platform when the organization needs dedicated log retention and query controls.
  • Approve sending sensitive request bodies, headers, tokens, user identifiers, or customer data into traces or logs.
  • Treat a demo deployment as production-ready.

docs.title: Decision Model
docs.text:
Decide the role before deploying. The role decides the operating contract.

Role Acceptable use Required boundary Lab tool Learn OpenTelemetry, validate instrumentation, explore traces from sample services. No production traffic, no sensitive data, disposable storage. Team analysis backend Debug selected services and correlate traces with metrics or logs. Collector boundary, access control, retention, owner, and rollback path. Production observability service Support incident diagnosis for real services. Storage lifecycle, backup or rebuild decision, alert routing, SLO impact checks, capacity planning, and failure drills.

If the team cannot name which role it is deploying, keep the deployment out of production paths.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before SigNoz is allowed to receive production telemetry, define these contracts:

Contract Required decision Why it matters Telemetry ownership Which team owns collectors, backend, dashboards, alerts, and retention? During an incident, "the UI is down" must have an owner. Collector boundary Do applications send to an OpenTelemetry Collector, or directly to SigNoz? The collector is where filtering, batching, retry, memory limits, and export routing should be controlled. Attribute policy Which resource attributes are required and which attributes are forbidden? Bad labels make search useless; unsafe attributes leak data. Storage lifecycle What retention, volume, backup, and rebuild model does ClickHouse use? A trace backend can quietly become a large stateful database. Access model Who can view traces, logs, dashboards, and alerts? Telemetry may contain production metadata and user-adjacent context. Alerting model Which alerts belong in SigNoz and which stay in Prometheus or another alert path? Tool overlap creates alert gaps and duplicate noise. Self-monitoring How do SigNoz, ClickHouse, and collector health alert through a path outside SigNoz? The observability backend cannot be the only tool that reports its own failure. Sampling and cardinality Who owns sampling, which traces must be preserved, and what cardinality is forbidden? A backend that samples away the failure or explodes on labels is not an incident tool. Failure validation What real test proves the path from application to collector to backend to human? A green pod only proves a process is running. Rollback boundary How do applications stop exporting to this backend without redeploying unrelated changes? Observability rollout must be reversible without breaking applications.

docs.title: Put OpenTelemetry Collector in front
docs.text:
Applications should send telemetry to an OpenTelemetry Collector service. The collector then exports to SigNoz.

This gives the platform one controlled place to:

  • Drop or redact sensitive attributes by default.
  • Hash only approved low-risk pseudonymous identifiers after data-classification review; never hash tokens, authorization headers, cookies, request bodies, full URLs, or secrets as a safety substitute.
  • Set memory limits, queues, retry, and batch behavior.
  • Normalize service.name, namespace, environment, team, and version attributes.
  • Route the same telemetry to another backend during migration or rollback.
  • Pause or throttle export without changing application manifests.

Do not point application agents at localhost, a developer machine, a kubectl port-forward, or an unowned endpoint. The production endpoint must be a stable service name and port owned by the platform.

docs.title: Treat ClickHouse as production state
docs.text:
SigNoz commonly depends on ClickHouse for telemetry storage. ClickHouse may not hold business records, but it still holds incident evidence, performance history, and sensitive metadata.

Production deployment requires:

  • Pinned chart and image versions.
  • Persistent volumes with known expansion behavior and reclaim policy.
  • Retention policy for traces, metrics, and logs.
  • Resource requests and limits for ClickHouse, collector, query service, frontend, and alerting components.
  • Backup or rebuild decision. If the team chooses not to back up telemetry storage, that must be an explicit loss-acceptance decision.
  • Capacity alerts for disk, ingestion rate, dropped spans, query latency, and queue pressure.

The unsafe pattern is letting telemetry storage grow until the observability system becomes the next incident.

docs.title: Separate three signal jobs
docs.text:
SigNoz can display traces, metrics, and logs, but those signals do different work.

Signal Strong use Guardrail Traces Request path, latency breakdown, dependency errors, service topology. Sampling, safe attributes, consistent service names. Metrics Service-level latency, error, throughput, and backend health. Keep SLO and infrastructure alert ownership explicit. Logs Context around selected failures and trace correlation. Do not use trace backend logging as an unmanaged long-retention log archive.

Do not enable every signal everywhere just because the UI supports it. Each signal adds ingestion cost, data risk, and operational ownership.

docs.title: Make attributes searchable before adding volume
docs.text:
The first production value of SigNoz depends less on chart installation and more on whether telemetry can be filtered during an incident.

Require safe resource attributes:

  • service.name
  • service.namespace
  • deployment.environment
  • service.version
  • k8s.namespace.name or equivalent platform namespace
  • team or owner label where the organization uses one

Avoid unsafe or high-cardinality attributes:

  • user IDs, emails, tokens, authorization headers, cookies
  • request bodies, full URLs, raw SQL, customer names
  • order IDs, request IDs, session IDs as indexed dimensions
  • internal hostnames or private topology details in public examples

Bad attributes are not a cosmetic problem. They decide whether a real incident can be narrowed down quickly and whether telemetry is safe to retain.

docs.title: Govern sampling and cardinality before rollout
docs.text:
Sampling is not only a cost setting. It decides which evidence survives an incident.

Before production traffic enters SigNoz, define:

  • Sampling owner and approval path.
  • Default sampling policy for normal traffic.
  • Preservation rule for error traces, slow traces, and explicitly marked diagnostic traffic.
  • Rollout limit for how many services or how much traffic may be added in one change.
  • Cardinality budget for resource attributes and span attributes.
  • Validation that synthetic slow requests and synthetic error requests are not sampled away.

Do not tune sampling only after ClickHouse or the collector is under pressure. By then the system may already have dropped the trace that explains the pressure.

docs.title: Kubernetes Deployment Standard
docs.text:
Before deployment:

  • Classify the role: lab, team analysis backend, or production observability service.
  • Confirm where the OpenTelemetry Collector runs and which receiver/exporter ports are used.
  • Confirm the default StorageClass supports the required volume expansion and reclaim behavior.
  • Pin the Helm chart and image versions.
  • Create a dedicated namespace.
  • Decide ingress, authentication, TLS, and network access before exposing the frontend.
  • Review resource requests and limits for ClickHouse, collector, Alertmanager or alerting service, frontend, and query service.
  • Decide retention and capacity alerts before production traffic arrives.

Deployment is not complete until a real instrumented workload can send telemetry through the intended collector path and a real operator can find it through the intended access path.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Validate in layers and stop at the first unclear layer.

Layer Evidence Stop condition Backend health SigNoz frontend, query service, ClickHouse, collector, and alerting components are ready. Any core component is unhealthy or repeatedly restarting. Storage ClickHouse tables initialize, PVCs bind, retention is configured, disk alerts exist. Storage cannot grow, retention is absent, or disk pressure has no owner. Collector path Test workload sends OTLP telemetry to the collector and collector exports to SigNoz without queue saturation or drops. Export errors, queue pressure, memory limiter drops, or protocol mismatch. Attribute quality Test traces show safe service.name, environment, namespace, version, and owner attributes. Service appears as unknown, unsafe attributes leak, or labels are too noisy to search. Sampling and cardinality Synthetic slow and error requests remain queryable, and high-cardinality attributes are blocked before broad rollout. The test failure is sampled away, or labels create unbounded series and query pressure. Access and alerting A real operator reaches the UI through the approved path and a test alert reaches the intended channel. Access depends on port-forward, shared admin access, or unowned alert routing. Self-monitoring SigNoz, ClickHouse, and collector health, drops, queue pressure, disk pressure, and query failures alert through a path that does not depend on SigNoz. SigNoz failure can only be noticed inside SigNoz itself. Incident usefulness A synthetic slow/error request can be found from service to trace to related logs or metrics. The UI has data, but the failure cannot be located by service, environment, version, or owner.

Passing pod readiness alone is not enough. The minimum useful proof is a known failure signal traveling from workload to collector to backend to human.

docs.title: Rollback and Exit
docs.text:
Rollback depends on where SigNoz sits in the telemetry path.

Situation Safer rollback Applications export to collector, collector exports to SigNoz Remove or disable the SigNoz exporter in the collector; keep application instrumentation unchanged. Applications export directly to SigNoz Change application endpoint configuration and redeploy affected workloads. This has higher blast radius. SigNoz deployment is unhealthy but telemetry is still useful elsewhere Route collector export to the alternate backend and pause SigNoz ingestion. SigNoz is being removed permanently Stop exporters first, wait for no dependent workloads, then uninstall the release. Delete PVCs only after explicit approval.

Do not delete PVCs as part of a casual rollback. They may contain incident evidence and retention-controlled telemetry.

If the stack was used only as a lab tool, deletion is acceptable after confirming no production collector exports to it.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure mode Symptom Correct response UI works but no useful diagnosis Traces exist, but service, version, environment, or owner cannot be filtered. Fix resource attributes before adding more services. Collector bypassed Applications export directly to backend, with no filtering or routing control. Move export behind OpenTelemetry Collector. ClickHouse disk pressure Ingestion slows, queries fail, or pods restart. Reduce ingestion, check retention, expand storage, and review sampling/cardinality. Sensitive telemetry leak Traces or logs contain tokens, headers, request bodies, user data, or customer context. Stop the offending export, filter at collector and application layers, and review retention. Demo access becomes production access Operators depend on kubectl port-forward or shared admin credentials. Create controlled ingress, authentication, and authorization before production use. Alerting split brain Prometheus and SigNoz both alert on similar symptoms, or neither owns them. Define alert ownership and route each symptom to one response path. Self-monitoring depends on SigNoz Backend, ClickHouse, or collector failure is only visible inside the failed stack. Alert SigNoz health, ingestion drops, queue pressure, disk pressure, and query failures through an external path. Sampling hides the incident Slow or failed synthetic requests cannot be found in traces. Preserve error/slow diagnostic traffic, lower rollout scope, and review sampling before adding services. Backend overload after rollout Dropped spans, collector queue pressure, high ClickHouse write rate. Lower sampling, pause rollout, and validate collector memory and queue limits. Logs treated as free add-on Cost and retention grow without log ownership. Keep logs in the approved log platform unless SigNoz log retention and access are owned.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Treat telemetry as production data export.
  • Restrict UI access; avoid shared admin accounts.
  • Keep backend credentials in collectors or secret management, not in application manifests.
  • Filter request bodies, authorization headers, cookies, tokens, user identifiers, and full URLs.
  • Use TLS and network controls for frontend and collector paths.
  • Limit who can change collector pipelines, retention, and alert routes.
  • Do not publish real service names, namespaces, domains, user identifiers, dashboard screenshots, or query results.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review this standard:

  • before moving from lab to team or production role
  • when enabling a new signal type, such as logs or metrics
  • when adding a high-traffic service
  • after collector drops, storage pressure, or alert-routing failure
  • during OpenTelemetry Collector, SigNoz, ClickHouse, or Kubernetes upgrades
  • when retention, access policy, or data classification changes

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Production Standard
  • Java Agent Rollout Runbook
  • Prometheus Exporter Monitoring Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard
  • Loki Logging Standard

docs.title: OpenTelemetry
docs.text:
From telemetry signals, context propagation, and auto-instrumentation to Collector architecture, production governance, and recovery.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

2 documents Understand

Build the core mental model: how the components cooperate, why the system is designed this way, and where its boundaries are.

  • What Is OpenTelemetry? Understand OpenTelemetry through the engineering problem, component boundaries, and telemetry data flow instead of mistaking it for a backend or a collection of auto-instrumentation agents.
  • Signals, Context, and Pipelines Learn how traces, metrics, logs, and Baggage divide responsibilities, how Context crosses service boundaries, and where a Collector Pipeline processes or loses data.
1 document Getting started

Start with a minimal environment and complete the first reproducible, verifiable path.

  • Generate Your First Trace Generate, receive, and verify a first trace in an isolated local environment with telemetrygen and OpenTelemetry Collector 0.156.0, including stop conditions and complete cleanup.
1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Production Standard Production admission standard for Kubernetes OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation, expressed as enforceable rules, evidence, exceptions, and rollback boundaries.
3 documents Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • OpenTelemetry Auto-Instrumentation Canary Rollout Runbook Roll out Kubernetes OpenTelemetry auto-instrumentation with an independent canary, layered validation, and explicit-false rollback.
  • OpenTelemetry Java Agent Canary Rollout Runbook Safely attach, validate, and roll back the OpenTelemetry Java agent through an independent canary on Kubernetes, VMs, or containers.
  • Troubleshooting Missing OpenTelemetry Data Trace missing telemetry hop by hop from a known request through injection, protocol, Collector, and backend, with explicit stop, rollback, and escalation conditions.
1 document Reference

Look up versions, commands, parameters, templates, and verified official facts.

  • OpenTelemetry Compatibility and Configuration Reference Reference for version evidence, OTLP protocols, runtime constraints, annotations, and configuration precedence in OpenTelemetry Operator auto-instrumentation.

docs.title: When to use this runbook
docs.text:
Use this runbook after the design has passed the Auto-Instrumentation Production Standard, for a first enablement or an Operator auto-instrumentation upgrade in Kubernetes. It does not approve namespace-wide injection and does not choose sampling, performance, or retention limits for the service.

Official facts were last verified on 2026-07-12. Recheck deployed versions against the Compatibility and Configuration Reference; this runbook makes no cross-version compatibility claim.

docs.title: Roles and change inputs
docs.text:
Name the service owner, operator, rollback owner, observability owner, and data-incident contact. The change record must contain:

  • stable and canary Deployments, image digests, runtime, and target container;
  • Kubernetes, Operator, CRD, agent image, Collector, and backend versions;
  • OTLP protocol, endpoint, TLS/authentication, and NetworkPolicy;
  • canary traffic method, minimum sample, and observation window;
  • independent SLI plus p95/p99, error, restart, startup, CPU/RSS limits;
  • Collector accepted/refused/send_failed/queue and cost limits;
  • fake marker for the sensitive-data negative test;
  • rollback patch and escalation contacts.

Stop if any input is empty.

docs.title: 1. Prove canary independence
docs.text:
The canary must be an independent Deployment or revision that can be scaled and deleted separately. Confirm that the Service or gateway can distinguish traffic by fixed percentage, request header, or dedicated path. Do not edit the stable Deployment pod template.

kubectl -n <app-namespace> get deploy <stable-deployment> <canary-deployment> -o wide
kubectl -n <app-namespace> get svc,httproute,ingress

docs.title: 2. Inventory configuration overrides
docs.text:
The Operator troubleshooting guide documents a current known issue: injection can override JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS, PYTHONPATH, and NODE_OPTIONS when they come from an image or ConfigMap. Official guidance

kubectl -n <app-namespace> get deploy <canary-deployment> -o yaml
kubectl -n <app-namespace> get configmap <runtime-config> -o yaml
kubectl -n <app-namespace> exec deploy/<canary-deployment> -c <app-container> -- env
kubectl -n <app-namespace> exec deploy/<canary-deployment> -c <app-container> -- ps -ef

If these variables carry required application flags or the merged result is unknown, stop and solve it in a pre-production image. Do not experiment in production.

docs.title: 3. Inspect the Operator webhook
docs.text:
kubectl -n <operator-namespace> get deploy,pod,svc,endpoints
kubectl get mutatingwebhookconfiguration -o yaml
kubectl -n <operator-namespace> logs deploy/<operator-deployment> --since=15m

Confirm that failurePolicy and timeoutSeconds match the change record, the CA bundle is nonempty, the certificate is not near expiry, the Service has endpoints, and logs have no TLS, timeout, or injection errors. Kubernetes explains that failurePolicy controls whether a timed-out webhook is ignored or rejects the request: Dynamic Admission Control.

docs.title: 4. Inspect the Collector boundary
docs.text:
kubectl -n <collector-namespace> get deploy,statefulset,pod,svc,pdb,networkpolicy
kubectl -n <collector-namespace> get secret <collector-client-identity> -o name
kubectl -n <collector-namespace> logs deploy/<collector-deployment> --since=15m

Prove that receiver protocol/port, TLS trust, and authentication match; Collector replicas and capacity are approved; internal telemetry has no refusals, export failures, or queue nearing its limit. Never print Secret content merely to test authentication.

docs.title: 5. Save the baseline
docs.text: From a source independent of OTel, save request count, p95/p99, business error rate, pod readiness/restarts, CPU/RSS, and startup time for the agreed window. Also save Collector receive rate, refusal rate, send failure, and queue/capacity. Stop without a baseline.

docs.title: 1. Create or update Instrumentation first
docs.text:
The following is an illustrative Java-canary resource that still requires environment-specific review, not a complete production manifest. It explicitly selects http/protobuf, so the example endpoint targets the Collector's actual OTLP/HTTP receiver. Do not reuse this block by merely swapping the language annotation: Node.js and other runtimes require an explicit protocol selected for the verified version and an endpoint matching the Collector's actual receiver.

apiVersion: opentelemetry.io/v1alpha1
kind: Instrumentation
metadata:
  name: java-canary
  namespace: <app-namespace>
spec:
  exporter:
    endpoint: https://<collector-service>:4318
  propagators: [tracecontext, baggage]
  sampler:
    type: parentbased_traceidratio
    argument: "<approved-ratio>"
  java:
    env:
      - name: OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL
        value: http/protobuf
  # Configure certificate trust, authentication, and other approved controls.

Apply Instrumentation before creating canary pods. The Operator documentation states that reversing this order prevents injection: Auto-instrumentation troubleshooting.

kubectl apply --server-side --dry-run=server -f <instrumentation-manifest>
kubectl apply -f <instrumentation-manifest>
kubectl -n <app-namespace> describe otelinst java-canary

docs.title: 2. Annotate only the canary
docs.text:
Server-side dry-run the patch first. The annotation belongs at spec.template.metadata.annotations; select the resource and container explicitly.

spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "java-canary"
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/container-names: "<app-container>"

For Python, Node.js, .NET, or Go, first create a runtime-specific Instrumentation from the verified version and the Reference, with protocol and actual endpoint stated explicitly; do not inherit the Java block above. Then use the matching annotation. Apply the reviewed patch, then:

kubectl -n <app-namespace> rollout status deploy/<canary-deployment> --timeout=<approved-timeout>
kubectl -n <app-namespace> get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
kubectl -n <app-namespace> describe pod -l <canary-selector>

docs.title: 3. Validate in layers
docs.text:
1. Pod mutation: only new canary pods are injected; target container and agent are correct; stable pods are unchanged.
  2. Process health: readiness, startup, restarts, logs, and actual process arguments meet limits.
  3. Known request: send a non-sensitive synthetic request with a unique test marker; record time and request ID.
  4. Trace: find the full trace in the expected window; verify service, environment, version, and propagation.
  5. Collector: accepted volume is plausible; refused/send_failed/queue remain within limits.
  6. Independent SLI: gateway, synthetic, or native application SLI meets its threshold.
  7. Negative test: include approved fake token, cookie, query, and body markers; prove zero matches at Collector egress and backend.

docs.title: Decision and rollback flow
docs.text: flowchart TD     A[Preconditions complete] -->|No| S[Stop before injected pods]     A -->|Yes| C[Create independent canary]     C --> M{Expected pod mutation?}     M -->|No| R[Set explicit false and replace pod]     M -->|Yes| D{Zero sensitive-marker matches?}     D -->|No| I[Stop export and escalate data event]     D -->|Yes| H{Independent SLI and Collector limits pass?}     H -->|No| R     H -->|Yes| O[Complete observation window]     O --> P{Owner approves next batch?}     P -->|No| K[Keep canary; do not expand]     P -->|Yes| N[Advance one defined batch]

docs.title: Immediate stop conditions
docs.text:
Stop traffic expansion on any of these:

  • a stable workload is injected or the webhook affects unrelated pod creation;
  • real sensitive data is found or a negative-test marker appears at egress/backend;
  • any independent SLI, resource, or Collector limit is crossed;
  • canary CrashLoop, startup/readiness timeout, or overwritten process arguments;
  • webhook certificate, CA, endpoint, or failurePolicy behavior differs from the record;
  • canary and stable traffic cannot be distinguished, or sample size is insufficient.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
If namespace injection might exist, do not only remove the annotation. Explicitly set the runtime annotation to "false" on the canary pod template. The Operator defines this as the no-injection value: Injecting Auto-instrumentation.

spec:
  template:
    metadata:
      annotations:
        instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java: "false"
  1. Reduce canary traffic to zero.
  2. Apply the explicit-"false" patch and roll the canary.
  3. Prove replacement pods have no injected init container/sidecar, agent files, or injected environment.
  4. Use the independent SLI to confirm return to baseline.
  5. Keep Instrumentation and the Operator until every dependent workload is clean; do not dismantle platform components first.
  6. Handle exported real data separately; agent rollback cannot retract it.

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text: Escalate successful receipt but missing backend data, sustained queue growth, certificate/webhook failure, or agent/runtime conflicts to platform/observability owners. Escalate sensitive-data matches to the data-security owner and an unapproved privileged Go sidecar to the security owner. Record commands, timestamps, resource versions, and non-sensitive error summaries—not credentials or real payloads.

docs.title: Completion
docs.text: Recommend the next batch only after the full observation window and sample pass, all MUST evidence is archived, rollback rehearsal succeeds, and stable service behavior is unchanged. The next batch is another controlled change; one successful canary never approves an entire namespace.

docs.title: Purpose and scope
docs.text:
This standard defines the minimum production gates for injecting Java, Node.js, Python, .NET, Go, or SDK configuration into Kubernetes workloads through the OpenTelemetry Operator. It decides whether a change may ship. Use the Auto-Instrumentation Canary Rollout Runbook for procedure and the Compatibility and Configuration Reference for runtime-specific facts.

Auto-instrumentation changes container environment, startup arguments, filesystem content, or sidecars when a pod is created. It also adds CPU, memory, and telemetry egress. It must be managed as a production code change, not as an observability switch for a namespace. The Operator documentation describes pod-creation-time injection and the requirement to create the Instrumentation resource before the application pod: Injecting Auto-instrumentation.

In this document, MUST is a release blocker, SHOULD is the default and requires a recorded reason when not followed, and MAY is optional.

docs.title: Version and fact boundary
docs.text:
• Official-source facts on this page were last verified on 2026-07-12.
  • This page does not claim that arbitrary Operator, Kubernetes, Collector, runtime, and agent versions are compatible.
  • Every change MUST record actual versions, image digests, base-image libc, protocol, Collector distribution, and the most recent pre-production test result. A missing field blocks promotion.
  • Upgrading any component invalidates historical evidence and requires a new canary.

The version record must include at least the Kubernetes minor version, Operator version, Instrumentation CRD version, auto-instrumentation image digest, application runtime version, application image digest, Collector version, and backend receiver version. Volatile constraints live in the Compatibility and Configuration Reference.

docs.title: Release rule matrix
docs.text: Rule Requirement Minimum evidence If unmet OTI-001 Versions and image digests MUST be pinned and verified; “latest” or floating tags are not approval evidence. Change-record version matrix, image digests, and pre-production test date. Block release. OTI-002 Injection MUST affect one independently controlled canary workload. Annotating every pod template in the stable Deployment is not a canary. Independent Deployment/ReplicaSet, fixed traffic share or dedicated synthetic traffic, and rollback object. Block release. OTI-003 JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS, PYTHONPATH, NODE_OPTIONS, and runtime startup arguments MUST be inventoried before the change. Review of image configuration, ConfigMaps, pod environment, and actual process arguments. Block when a conflict exists. OTI-004 OTLP protocol, endpoint, TLS, and authentication MUST be explicit; NetworkPolicy or equivalent control MUST restrict telemetry egress. Receiver summary, trust-chain test, authentication test, and allowed source/destination rules. Block release. OTI-005 The team MUST prove sensitive fields cannot leave the approved boundary. Negative test containing fake tokens, cookies, query parameters, user identifiers, and body markers; zero backend matches. Block and escalate as a data event. OTI-006 Collector capacity and availability MUST cover current load and the approved backend-outage budget. Replicas, anti-affinity/PDB, resources, queue, retry, measured capacity, and internal-telemetry dashboard. Block release. OTI-007 Admission webhook failurePolicy, timeoutSeconds, CA bundle, certificate expiry, and Service endpoints MUST be reviewed. Webhook configuration snapshot, certificate alert, and failure-drill result. Block release. OTI-008 Application health MUST be judged with an SLI independent of the new telemetry path. Baseline and thresholds from load balancer/gateway metrics, synthetic requests, native application metrics, or business success rate. Block release. OTI-009 Quantitative pass, stop, and rollback thresholds MUST be defined in advance. Observation window, sample size, p95/p99, error rate, restarts, RSS/CPU, Collector refusal/queue, and cost thresholds. Block release. OTI-010 Rollback MUST override namespace injection with explicit "false" and create clean replacement pods. Rollback patch, rehearsal output, and clean-pod evidence. Block release. OTI-011 Traces SHOULD be enabled first; metrics and logs should be separate changes. Recorded signal scope. Record deviation and added capacity/data review. OTI-012 A clearly named Instrumentation per language and namespace SHOULD be used, with the target container explicit. Resource name and container-name annotation. Record why mis-injection is impossible.

docs.title: A real canary
docs.text:
A canary must have independent scaling, rollback, and identifiable traffic. Acceptable patterns include:

  • clone the stable Deployment as example-service-otel-canary, run a small replica count, and route a fixed share or request header through the gateway;
  • expose a dedicated test path and send only non-sensitive synthetic traffic;
  • create an independently controlled canary revision in a progressive-delivery controller and prove that only selected replicas are affected.

Changing spec.template.metadata.annotations on the stable Deployment rolls every pod in that Deployment. That is a progressive full rollout, not an independent canary, and does not satisfy OTI-002.

docs.title: Quantitative change record
docs.text:
This standard must not invent universal thresholds. The service owner fills in absolute or relative limits from the pre-change baseline and records sample size and observation window.

Input Example format only Traffic and sample 5% canary traffic; at least 10,000 requests; observe 30 minutes Latency p95 increase below approved value; p99 remains within service SLO Correctness 5xx/business-failure delta below approved value Resources CPU, RSS, startup time, and GC each have an upper bound Stability no new restarts; no increase in readiness failures Collector queue/capacity, send_failed, refused, and export latency each have a limit Cost estimated and measured spans/s, bytes/s, and retention cost remain in budget Stop stop on missing MUST evidence, sensitive-data match, or independent-SLI regression

Derive the sampling ratio from ingress rate, average trace width, error/slow-request retention policy, Collector capacity, and backend budget. Neither 5% nor 100% is a safe universal default.

docs.title: Data-security gate
docs.text:
OTI-004 and OTI-005 must pass before the first injected pod is created:

  1. Application-to-Collector traffic uses organization-approved TLS and authentication; plaintext OTLP is not allowed across trust boundaries.
  2. Credentials reside in the Collector when possible, rather than being copied into every workload.
