  3. NetworkPolicy or equivalent controls allow applications to reach only approved Collectors and Collectors to reach only approved backends.
  4. Configuring a Collector filter, transform, or attribute processor is not evidence by itself. Send a request containing fake sensitive markers and prove there are zero matches at Collector egress and in the backend.
  5. If real tokens, cookies, personal data, or tenant identifiers are found, stop export, preserve minimal investigative evidence, and escalate through the organization's data-incident process. Rolling back an agent cannot retract exported data.

OpenTelemetry's security guidance explains that sensitive-data classification is organization-specific and recommends removing, hashing, or filtering at the SDK or Collector: Handling sensitive data.

docs.title: Collector and webhook gates
docs.text:
The Collector MUST at least provide:

  • no single-replica production dependency; replicas, topology distribution, and PDB align with the availability target;
  • sending queue, retry, and persistence choices derived from measured receive rate, average batch size, permitted backend outage, and memory budget;
  • memory_limiter or a validated equivalent, plus container requests and limits;
  • monitoring for otelcol_receiver_accepted_*, otelcol_receiver_refused_*, otelcol_exporter_sent_*, otelcol_exporter_send_failed_*, and exporter queue size/capacity; metric names can change by Collector version, so verify them against the deployed version's internal-telemetry documentation;
  • a backend-outage drill showing alerting before queue exhaustion and bounded recovery backlog.

Official guidance states that data can still be lost when the downstream is unavailable and the sending queue fills, and recommends capacity planning from queues, memory, and receive rate: Collector resiliency, Scaling the Collector, and Internal telemetry.

The webhook is part of the pod-creation control path. The team must deliberately choose failurePolicy: Fail or Ignore and record the security/availability trade-off; neither is universally correct. Kubernetes documents that timeout behavior is controlled by failurePolicy and that timeoutSeconds is configurable: Dynamic Admission Control. Certificate expiry, CA-bundle mismatch, missing Service endpoints, and webhook latency must be alerted.

docs.title: Independent validation and compliance decision
docs.text:
Approval requires all four evidence classes:

  1. Mutation evidence: only canary pods were injected; target container and agent version are correct.
  2. Telemetry evidence: a known synthetic request creates the expected trace, resource attributes identify it, and Collector refusals/failures do not increase.
  3. Independent health evidence: gateway, synthetic, or native application SLIs meet the change-record thresholds; the OTel path under test cannot certify itself.
  4. Negative evidence: fake sensitive markers cannot be found at Collector egress or in the backend.

If any of OTI-001 through OTI-010 is unmet, the only decision is noncompliant. A Ready pod, a service name in the backend, or absence of alerts is not sufficient alone.

docs.title: Exception mechanism
docs.text: An exception to a MUST records the rule ID, business reason, risk owner, compensating control, scope, start and expiry, rollback trigger, and approver. Every exception expires; promotion stops if it is not renewed. OTI-005 cannot be waived through a routine change exception when real sensitive data is exposed.

docs.title: Rollback boundary
docs.text: When namespace injection might be enabled, rollback must explicitly set the language annotation to "false" on the workload pod template, not merely remove it. The Operator documentation defines "false" as the valid no-injection value. New pods must then be created and shown to contain no injected init container, sidecar, agent files, or injected environment. See the Auto-Instrumentation Canary Rollout Runbook.

docs.title: Official sources
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry Operator: Injecting Auto-instrumentation
  • OpenTelemetry Operator: Auto-instrumentation troubleshooting
  • OpenTelemetry Collector resiliency
  • OpenTelemetry Collector internal telemetry
  • OpenTelemetry: Handling sensitive data
  • Kubernetes: Dynamic Admission Control

docs.title: Boundary of this reference
docs.text:
This page is a lookup surface, not a certified version list. Official facts were last verified on 2026-07-12, but the OpenTelemetry Operator, language agents, Deno, and Collector ship independently. Build compatibility evidence from the actual version's official release notes, image digest, and pre-production test. This page never implies arbitrary combinations are supported.

Complete this record on every upgrade:

Component Required evidence Kubernetes Minor version, node architecture, container runtime Operator Version, Helm chart/manifest version, feature gates CRD Instrumentation apiVersion and installed schema Auto-instrumentation Language, image tag and digest, agent/runtime version Application image Digest, CPU architecture, glibc/musl, startup arguments Collector Distribution/version, enabled components, receiver/exporter summary Backend OTLP receiver version, authentication/TLS, limits, retention Validation Environment, date, known request, performance/data-safety result

Treat any changed field as a new combination. latest, only a chart version, or “it started in test” is not compatibility evidence.

docs.title: OTLP protocols and ports
docs.text:
Transport Default when no port is configured Common endpoint form Note OTLP/gRPC 4317 https://collector.example:4317 Collector must enable a gRPC receiver. OTLP/HTTP 4318 https://collector.example:4318 Signals commonly use /v1/traces, /v1/metrics, and /v1/logs; base/signal URL construction follows the implementation and specification.

4317 and 4318 are specification defaults when no port is configured, not fixed ports. A Collector can listen elsewhere and a Service can remap ports. Inspect the actual endpoint and receiver; never infer protocol from port alone. OTLP Exporter specification

The specification defines grpc and http/protobuf, and allows an SDK to optionally support http/json. Therefore:

  • do not assume every language agent implements http/json;
  • do not rely on a language's current protocol default; set it explicitly and test end to end;
  • http:///https:// in an endpoint describes transport security, not whether the payload is OTLP/HTTP rather than gRPC;
  • across production trust boundaries, use approved TLS and authentication; never disable verification as a connectivity test.

docs.title: Operator injection entry points
docs.text:
Annotations belong at spec.template.metadata.annotations. Values can be "true" for the default resource in the current namespace, a resource name, namespace/name, or "false" to disable injection. Namespace annotations opt workloads in, so rollback should set explicit "false" at the workload. Operator auto-instrumentation

Runtime Injection annotation Primary boundary Java instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-java Check JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS overwrite and JVM/library compatibility. Node.js instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-nodejs Check NODE_OPTIONS overwrite. Python instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-python Check PYTHONPATH and glibc/musl. .NET instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-dotnet Check base image, architecture, and runtime identifier. Go instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-go eBPF sidecar, elevated privileges, target executable, single-container constraint. Deno/SDK-only instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/inject-sdk Injects SDK configuration; it does not install a full language SDK into an ordinary app.

Multi-container pods require an explicit target. For one language use instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/container-names. With the Operator multi-instrumentation feature gate enabled, use java-container-names, nodejs-container-names, python-container-names, dotnet-container-names, go-container-names, or sdk-container-names. One container cannot receive multiple language instrumentations, and Go does not support multi-container pods. Follow the deployed version's Operator documentation.

docs.title: Java
docs.text:
• Operator injection can overwrite JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS supplied by an image or ConfigMap; compare actual process arguments. Official troubleshooting
  • A manual Java agent attaches through -javaagent:<path> or dedicated JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS; it is in-process code, so pin the version and JAR checksum/digest.
  • Java agent 2.0+ currently defaults to http/protobuf, unlike the historical SDK-autoconfigure default. Explicit configuration is safer. Java agent configuration
  • Supported JVMs, libraries, and frameworks vary by agent release. Validate against that release's Supported Libraries page; “Java 8+” is not framework compatibility evidence.

docs.title: Python and musl
docs.text:
• Operator injection can overwrite PYTHONPATH supplied by an image or ConfigMap. Official troubleshooting
  • glibc is the default platform. The Operator documentation says that from v0.113.0, musl can be selected with:
instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/otel-python-platform: "musl"
  • This only proves that the Operator exposes a platform selector. It does not prove compatibility among the application's Python version, C extensions, architecture, and agent image; test the identical base image.

docs.title: Node.js
docs.text:
• Operator injection can overwrite NODE_OPTIONS supplied by an image or ConfigMap. Official troubleshooting
  • Preloaders, other APM agents, ESM loaders, or custom --require flags can compete for startup options. Compare actual process.execArgv and environment; do not assume automatic merging.

docs.title: .NET
docs.text:
• Record architecture, distribution, glibc/musl, and runtime identifier. A .NET label does not mean an auto-instrumentation binary is portable across base images.
  • Test profiler loading, startup, and native dependencies in a production-equivalent image. Roll back on failure; do not swap unknown binaries in production.

docs.title: Go
docs.text:
• The matching Operator feature gate must be enabled; verify its name and default for the deployed version.
  • Go auto-instrumentation uses an eBPF sidecar. The official example requires privileged: true and runAsUser: 0, so obtain separate security approval.
  • Supply the target executable via OTEL_GO_AUTO_TARGET_EXE or instrumentation.opentelemetry.io/otel-go-auto-target-exe.
  • Official documentation currently says multi-container pods are unsupported. A service-mesh or logging sidecar also makes a pod multi-container; use manual instrumentation or redesign rather than bypassing the check. Operator Go auto-instrumentation

docs.title: Deno
docs.text:
• The Operator uses inject-sdk and sets OTEL_DENO=true. Current Deno documentation also enables built-in OpenTelemetry with this variable and defaults to localhost:4318 over http/protobuf, so production must override the endpoint. Deno OpenTelemetry
  • Deno capabilities and startup flags evolve quickly. Some Operator-version documentation still requires --unstable-otel, while the current Deno quick start no longer uses it. Follow the actual Deno version's official documentation and startup test; do not copy either behavior across versions.
  • Deno can export console logs as well. Decide OTEL_DENO_CONSOLE behavior and run a sensitive-log negative test before enablement.

docs.title: SDK-only injection
docs.text: inject-sdk supplies common configuration and resource attributes. It does not install a missing language SDK, automatic-instrumentation library, or business spans into an ordinary application. The app must already include and initialize a compatible SDK; otherwise “environment present, no telemetry” is expected rather than a Collector fault.

docs.title: Configuration sources and precedence
docs.text:
The common environment-variable specification says implementations may provide these variables; not every implementation must implement all of them, and language agents can add precedence rules. Environment Variable Specification

Explicitly inspect:

  • OTEL_SERVICE_NAME (normally higher precedence than service.name in OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES);
  • OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL, common endpoint, and signal-specific endpoints;
  • trace/metric/log exporters and sampler;
  • propagators;
  • agent/SDK configuration-file variables and whether they override ordinary environment variables;
  • effective container environment after Operator injection, not only the CR.

Do not place customers, tenants, emails, tokens, full URL queries, or request-level identifiers in resource attributes.

docs.title: Minimum inspection commands
docs.text:
kubectl version
kubectl -n <operator-namespace> get deploy <operator-deployment> -o yaml
kubectl get crd instrumentations.opentelemetry.io -o yaml
kubectl -n <app-namespace> describe otelinst <name>
kubectl -n <app-namespace> get pod <pod> -o yaml
kubectl -n <app-namespace> exec <pod> -c <container> -- env
kubectl -n <app-namespace> exec <pod> -c <container> -- ps -ef

These commands prove only part of effective configuration. Final compatibility evidence also requires a known request, independent application SLI, Collector internal telemetry, and a sensitive-field negative test.

docs.title: Official sources
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry Operator: Injecting Auto-instrumentation
  • OpenTelemetry Operator: Auto-instrumentation troubleshooting
  • OTLP Exporter specification
  • OpenTelemetry environment variable specification
  • OpenTelemetry Java agent configuration
  • Deno OpenTelemetry

Review triggers: any Operator, CRD, agent, runtime, base image, Collector, or Kubernetes upgrade; changed facts on official pages; or discovery of a new startup-variable overwrite or data-exposure path.

docs.title: Generate Your First Trace
docs.text:
This tutorial verifies a minimal trace data path with your own evidence: telemetrygen creates spans and sends them over OTLP to a local Collector, then the Debug Exporter and zPages expose evidence that the Collector received and processed them.

This is not a production deployment. It has no persistent backend, TLS, authentication, high availability, capacity control, or sensitive-data governance. Every port binds only to 127.0.0.1. Do not copy this Collector setup, open ports, or debug output into production.

The procedure was checked on 2026-07-12 against the official Collector Quick Start and pins both Collector and telemetrygen to 0.156.0. If that image or Go Module version is unavailable for your platform, stop and select a matching Collector and generator version from official releases. Do not mix arbitrary versions.

docs.title: What you will prove
docs.text:
At the end, you should have three separate pieces of evidence:

  1. The generator reports that it produced and sent three traces.
  2. Collector logs contain received spans, including Trace ID, Span ID, and Parent ID relationships within a trace.
  3. The local zPages TraceZ page shows trace activity processed by the Collector.
flowchart LR T[telemetrygen generate test spans] -->|OTLP/gRPC 127.0.0.1:4317| C[Collector 0.156.0] C --> D[Debug Exporter terminal logs] C --> Z[zPages 127.0.0.1:55679] D --> V1{Check IDs and parentage} Z --> V2{Check receive activity}

The observations are not interchangeable. Generator success does not prove Collector receipt. Collector logs do not prove a real production backend is queryable. zPages is a local troubleshooting UI, not an observability backend.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Use a Bash-compatible terminal on macOS, Linux, or WSL with:

  • Docker or a compatible runtime able to run Linux containers;
  • Go installed, with go install able to reach public Go Modules;
  • local ports 4317, 4318, and 55679 free;
  • permission to pull otel/opentelemetry-collector:0.156.0;
  • a home directory where a temporary experiment directory can be created.

Check the tools and do not ignore errors:

docker version
go version

Stop if either command fails. Do not add sudo to bypass a Docker permission problem; fix the local container runtime first.

Check ports:

lsof -nP -iTCP:4317 -sTCP:LISTEN
lsof -nP -iTCP:4318 -sTCP:LISTEN
lsof -nP -iTCP:55679 -sTCP:LISTEN

No output means no process is listening on the corresponding TCP port. If a command returns a listener, stop and release the port. Alternatively, use another set of loopback-only ports only after understanding both Collector and client parameters.

docs.title: Create an isolated working directory
docs.text:
export OTEL_LAB_DIR="$(mktemp -d "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}/otel-first-trace.XXXXXX")"
export OTEL_CONTAINER="otel-first-trace-$(date +%Y%m%d%H%M%S)-$$"
mkdir -p "$OTEL_LAB_DIR/bin"
touch "$OTEL_LAB_DIR/.l2do-otel-lab"
cd "$OTEL_LAB_DIR"
export GOBIN="$PWD/bin"

Confirm that installation will remain in the experiment directory:

printf 'GOBIN=%s\n' "$GOBIN"

The output must end in /bin under the random experiment directory. Keep OTEL_LAB_DIR, OTEL_CONTAINER, and every later command in the same shell. Stop if either variable is missing; do not guess a path or container name.

docs.title: Install a pinned generator
docs.text:
go install github.com/open-telemetry/opentelemetry-collector-contrib/cmd/telemetrygen@v0.156.0
"$GOBIN/telemetrygen" --help

The second command should print telemetrygen help, not “file not found.” Pinning keeps the client on the same release line as the tutorial. The official Quick Start may advance to a newer release; check its current image version when running this procedure.

docs.title: Pull and identify the Collector image
docs.text:
docker pull otel/opentelemetry-collector:0.156.0
docker image inspect otel/opentelemetry-collector:0.156.0 \
  --format '{{index .RepoDigests 0}}'

Save the digest from the second command with the experiment record. The digest depends on registry and platform, so this page does not invent a fixed value. If it is empty or the pull fails, stop; do not substitute latest.

docs.title: Start the local Collector
docs.text:
if docker container inspect "$OTEL_CONTAINER" >/dev/null 2>&1; then
  printf 'Container-name collision; stop: %s\n' "$OTEL_CONTAINER" >&2
  exit 1
fi

docker run --detach \
  --name "$OTEL_CONTAINER" \
  --label "l2do.otel-lab=$OTEL_LAB_DIR" \
  -p 127.0.0.1:4317:4317 \
  -p 127.0.0.1:4318:4318 \
  -p 127.0.0.1:55679:55679 \
  otel/opentelemetry-collector:0.156.0

Inspect container state and startup logs:

docker ps --filter "name=^/${OTEL_CONTAINER}$"
docker logs "$OTEL_CONTAINER"

The container should be Up. Logs should not show configuration parsing failure, port binding failure, or immediate shutdown.

For this image version, the official Quick Start configuration exposes:

  • 4317 for OTLP/gRPC;
  • 4318 for OTLP/HTTP;
  • 55679 for zPages.

These are verified behaviors of the 0.156.0 Quick Start, not permanent defaults for every distribution or custom configuration (official procedure).

docs.title: Stop conditions
docs.text:
Do not send data if any of these is true:

  • the container is not Up;
  • Docker reports a port conflict;
  • logs report invalid configuration or Receiver/Exporter startup failure;
  • ports are not bound to 127.0.0.1;
  • the running image tag is not 0.156.0.

Perform the Cleanup procedure below, then resolve the cause.

docs.title: Generate three test traces
docs.text:
With the Collector running, execute:

"$GOBIN/telemetrygen" traces \
  --otlp-endpoint 127.0.0.1:4317 \
  --otlp-insecure \
  --traces 3

--otlp-insecure is acceptable only for this loopback, plaintext experiment. It does not verify TLS and must not become a production parameter.

The generator should report three traces generated and stop its Batch Span Processor. Timestamps, source line numbers, and IDs change with versions and executions; do not compare them character-for-character with official sample output. If the command times out or reports export failure, check the Collector and the 4317 listener instead of repeatedly generating noise.

docs.title: Verify that the Collector received spans
docs.text:
Save logs in the experiment directory:

docker logs "$OTEL_CONTAINER" > collector-output.txt 2>&1
grep -E '^Span|(Trace ID|Parent ID|ID|Name|Kind|Start time|End time|Status code)[[:space:]]*:' \
  collector-output.txt

Expect one or more Span # sections containing:

  • non-empty Trace ID and ID values;
  • an empty Parent ID for a root span;
  • a child Parent ID equal to another span's ID in the same trace;
  • Name, Kind, start time, and end time;
  • a common Trace ID for spans in the same parent-child group.

The actual IDs are random on each run. The acceptance criterion is their relationship, not a sample string.

List unique Trace IDs in this log if useful:

grep 'Trace ID' collector-output.txt | awk '{print $NF}' | sort -u

If the generator succeeds but the log has no spans, the path broke at client export, network entry, or the Collector Pipeline. Generator output alone is not Collector receipt evidence.

docs.title: Cross-check with zPages
docs.text:
Open this local URL:

http://127.0.0.1:55679/debug/tracez

Select trace activity in the table and confirm that the page is not a connection or blank error. zPages exposes a debugging view of trace activity inside the Collector. It provides no durable retention and does not prove an external backend received anything.

If logs contain spans but TraceZ is unreachable, investigate the 55679 mapping and zPages Extension. Do not discard receipt evidence already established by the Debug Exporter.

docs.title: Evaluate the result
docs.text:
Check Passing evidence Failure indicates Client generation telemetrygen reports three traces generated Generator, arguments, or local network issue Collector receipt and processing Logs contain spans with reasonable ID parentage Broken OTLP entry or Pipeline Debug UI TraceZ is reachable and shows trace activity zPages Extension or port mapping problem

Only when all three pass can you make this limited statement: test spans traveled from the local generator into and through this Collector. It says nothing about production application instrumentation, cross-service propagation, backend persistence, alerting, or capacity design.

docs.title: connection refused
docs.text:
Run:

docker ps --filter "name=^/${OTEL_CONTAINER}$"
docker logs --tail 100 "$OTEL_CONTAINER"

Confirm the container is alive, then confirm the generator targets 127.0.0.1:4317. Do not replace the endpoint with a container-private IP in this tutorial.

docs.title: Collector exits immediately
docs.text: Read the full logs. Common local causes are an existing container with the same name or an occupied port. This tutorial uses the image's configuration. If you mounted a custom configuration, you have left the experiment boundary; remove the mount and restart.

docs.title: Logs are noisy but contain no spans
docs.text: Save docker logs again after generating traces. Do not inspect only startup-era output. The Debug Exporter is intended to confirm receipt and processing in localized troubleshooting (Collector Troubleshooting).

docs.title: Cleanup
docs.text:
Whether the experiment succeeds or fails, remove only the container carrying this experiment's label and the random directory carrying this experiment's marker. If either guard fails, inspect manually; never bypass the guard with an unverified delete:

if [ "$(docker inspect "$OTEL_CONTAINER" --format '{{ index .Config.Labels "l2do.otel-lab" }}' 2>/dev/null)" = "$OTEL_LAB_DIR" ]; then
  docker rm --force "$OTEL_CONTAINER"
else
  printf 'Refusing to remove an unowned container: %s\n' "$OTEL_CONTAINER" >&2
fi

if [ -n "$OTEL_LAB_DIR" ] && [ -f "$OTEL_LAB_DIR/.l2do-otel-lab" ]; then
  cd "${TMPDIR:-/tmp}"
  rm -rf -- "$OTEL_LAB_DIR"
else
  printf 'Refusing to remove an unowned directory: %s\n' "${OTEL_LAB_DIR:-<empty>}" >&2
fi

Confirm no experiment container remains:

docker ps --all --filter "name=^/${OTEL_CONTAINER}$"

Only the table header should remain. The image stays in local cache by default. To remove it too:

docker image rm otel/opentelemetry-collector:0.156.0

Docker will refuse if another container references the image. Do not force deletion and disrupt another experiment.

docs.title: Move from the experiment to a real system
docs.text:
The next step is not to copy --otlp-insecure and the Debug Exporter into a cluster. Add real constraints one layer at a time:

  1. Replace telemetrygen with an application SDK or supported auto-instrumentation.
  2. Define stable service.name and Resource rules for each service.
  3. Verify Context Propagation across a real synchronous or asynchronous call.
  4. Add TLS, authentication, and network boundaries to Collector receivers.
  5. Add memory limiting, queueing, retry, capacity, and Collector self-observation.
  6. Connect a test backend and separately verify Collector send and backend query.

Change one assumption at a time and preserve the prior layer's evidence. That is much easier to diagnose than deploying a complete platform and then facing “there are no traces.”

docs.title: Official sources and version boundary
docs.text:
• Collector Quick Start: official basis for the experiment, ports, and observations.
  • Collector Configuration: component and activation rules for a custom Pipeline.
  • Collector Troubleshooting: intended use of the Debug Exporter and zPages.
  • telemetrygen source: generator source at the pinned version.

Review date: 2026-07-12. On upgrade, keep image, generator, command arguments, and documentation versions aligned, then revalidate output fields and zPages behavior.

docs.title: OpenTelemetry Java Agent Canary Rollout Runbook
When to use
docs.text:
Use this runbook for a production change that attaches the OpenTelemetry Java agent through -javaagent or dedicated JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS, rather than an Operator webhook. It applies to Kubernetes, containers, and virtual machines, with platform-specific command adaptation.

The Java agent modifies bytecode during class loading and runs inside the application process. It changes startup, CPU, memory, GC, networking, and data egress. Official facts were last verified on 2026-07-12; this page does not guarantee arbitrary agent/JVM/framework combinations. Revalidate every upgrade against the actual release's Supported Libraries and release notes.

docs.title: Required decisions
docs.text:
Input Required record Artifact Agent version, source URL/internal repository, SHA-256 or artifact-signing record JVM Vendor, major version, architecture, container base image, startup command Application Image digest, framework/application-server version, existing APM/agents Canary Independent instance/Deployment, traffic entry, sample size, observation window Export Signals, OTLP protocol/endpoint, TLS, authentication, NetworkPolicy/firewall Identity service.name, namespace, environment, version; no customer/tenant identity Limits Independent p95/p99, error, startup, CPU/RSS, GC, restarts; Collector queue/failures; cost Rollback Previous startup configuration, artifact, operator, and trigger

Stop if any field is missing.

docs.title: 1. Establish a real canary
docs.text: Create an independent JVM instance or Deployment/revision that receives a fixed share, request header, or dedicated synthetic traffic. Adding JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS to every replica in the stable Deployment is not a canary. Save gateway/native baselines for request count, p95/p99, error rate, startup, CPU/RSS, GC pause, and restarts.

docs.title: 2. Review agent supply chain
docs.text:
Obtain a pinned release only from the official OpenTelemetry release or an approved internal artifact repository. Verify SHA-256/signature/internal provenance and archive the result with the agent. Never run a floating URL, unchecked JAR, or file from a temporary download directory.

shasum -a 256 /opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar

Official setup guidance sources the JAR from opentelemetry-java-instrumentation releases and attaches it with -javaagent: Java agent getting started.

docs.title: 3. Inventory JVM arguments and agents
docs.text:
Record the full startup command, JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS, JDK_JAVA_OPTIONS, JAVA_OPTS, container entrypoint, application-server scripts, and existing -javaagent values. When APM, security, or profiler agents already exist, validate load order against both vendors' official compatibility guidance; a starting JVM alone is not proof.

java -version
ps -ef
env

On shared hosts, do not set JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS in the global login environment; it can affect jobs, CLIs, and unrelated JVMs. Prefer -javaagent in the one service's startup command.

docs.title: 4. Validate secure egress and Collector
docs.text: Prove that OTLP receiver protocol/port, TLS trust, client authentication, NetworkPolicy/firewall, and Collector configuration agree. Do not disable certificate checks, print tokens, or place credentials in shell history. Confirm Collector capacity and internal-telemetry limits exist.

docs.title: 1. Enable traces only
docs.text:
Start with traces; review metrics and logs separately. The following only demonstrates variable shape. Endpoint, certificates, and limits come from the change record:

export OTEL_SERVICE_NAME="<service-name>"
export OTEL_RESOURCE_ATTRIBUTES="service.namespace=<namespace>,deployment.environment.name=<environment>,service.version=<version>"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_ENDPOINT="https://<collector-endpoint>:4318"
export OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL="http/protobuf"
export OTEL_TRACES_EXPORTER="otlp"
export OTEL_METRICS_EXPORTER="none"
export OTEL_LOGS_EXPORTER="none"

Java agent 2.0+ currently defaults to http/protobuf, but explicit configuration avoids depending on version defaults. Official agent precedence is system properties, environment, configuration file, then SPI-supplied values; verify it for the deployed version: Java agent configuration. Do not define contradictory values in multiple places.

docs.title: 2. Attach on the canary
docs.text:
Prefer adding this to the canary's dedicated startup command:

java -javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar <existing-jvm-arguments> -jar <application.jar>

If the platform requires JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS, ensure the environment belongs only to the target JVM and preserve the tested prior value:

export JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS="<existing-options> -javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar"

Run a dry run or pre-production startup with the production image and startup script first. Do not guess argument order in a production shell.

docs.title: 3. Send a known request and validate
docs.text:
1. Confirm the canary JVM starts, agent logs report the expected version, and there are no transform/export errors.
  2. Compare actual process arguments: only the target JVM has one expected agent and heap/GC/application flags remain.
  3. Send a synthetic request with a unique non-sensitive marker; record time, ingress, and request ID.
  4. Find the full trace and verify service, namespace, environment, version, and propagation.
  5. Inspect Collector accepted/refused/send_failed/queue; the backend UI alone is insufficient.
  6. Verify independent gateway/synthetic/native application SLI, resources, and GC limits.
  7. Run a negative test with approved fake token, cookie, query, user ID, and body markers; require zero matches at Collector egress and backend.

docs.title: Stop conditions
docs.text:
Stop traffic and roll back on any of: JVM/framework outside the validated matrix; startup/readiness timeout; class-transform errors; independent SLI, CPU/RSS/GC/Collector/cost limit crossed; unintended JVM attached; existing-agent conflict; sensitive marker reaches egress; TLS/authentication works only after weakening security.

Agent debug output is very verbose. Permit OTEL_JAVAAGENT_DEBUG=true only briefly on the canary, after checking that logs will not expose sensitive context, with a bounded collection window; remove it immediately afterward. The official guide also warns that debug logs are verbose: Java agent getting started.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
1. Reduce canary traffic to zero.
  2. Remove -javaagent:/opt/otel/opentelemetry-javaagent.jar from the dedicated startup command, or restore the complete previous JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS; setting only OTEL_SDK_DISABLED is not an uninstall.
  3. Recreate/restart the canary from the previous immutable configuration.
  4. Prove actual process arguments no longer contain the agent and agent threads/logs/new telemetry cease.
  5. Confirm baseline with independent SLI and JVM/GC metrics.
  6. Preserve the verified artifact and configuration evidence until diagnosis, but do not leave an unapproved agent attached.
  7. If data escaped, invoke data-incident response; removing the agent does not delete backend data.

docs.title: Escalation
docs.text:
• Class transform, JVM crash, or framework conflict: service/JVM and observability owners, with minimal non-sensitive error and version matrix.
  • Collector queue/send failure: platform/backend owners; stop adding input.
  • Sensitive fields: data-security owner immediately.
  • Unknown or mismatched JAR: treat as a supply-chain event; do not run it.

docs.title: Completion
docs.text: Approve a subsequent batch only after the full observation window and sample, all limits pass, the negative test has zero matches, rollback rehearsal works, and evidence is archived. Every service/JVM/framework combination needs its own canary; one Spring Boot result does not prove compatibility for another application server.

docs.title: Official sources
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry Java agent
  • Java agent getting started
  • Java agent configuration
  • OpenTelemetry SDK environment variables
  • Handling sensitive data

docs.title: Signals, Context, and Pipelines
docs.text:
The hard part of OpenTelemetry is not memorizing four nouns. It is building one causal model: system activity produces signals, Context connects distributed activity, and a Pipeline moves the data to a queryable destination. A break in any one layer can leave you with data that still cannot explain an incident.

This page was checked against official OpenTelemetry concept and Collector configuration documentation on 2026-07-12. Signal and component maturity evolves, particularly for Profiles, Events, and Collector components; verify the target version before production adoption.

docs.title: Different information answers different questions
docs.text:
Information Basic unit Best at answering Poor at answering alone Trace Trace / Span Where one request went, its parent-child calls, and where time was spent Overall impact and long-term trend Metric Measurement in a time series How many requests were affected and whether trends or percentiles crossed a limit Why one particular request failed Log Timestamped contextual event What a code path recorded at a particular moment Cross-service causality unless correlation context is present Baggage Key-value data propagated with Context Carrying a small amount of context to downstream instrumentation Acting as a secure data store or an observation record

The official signal overview currently lists Traces, Metrics, Logs, and Baggage, while Profiles and Events are in development or proposal stages (Signals, checked 2026-07-12). It is still useful to distinguish Baggage: its primary function is propagation, and it does not automatically become a queryable Span, Metric, or Log.

docs.title: Traces reconstruct one execution
docs.text:
A Trace is a collection of Spans that share a Trace ID. A Span represents an operation with a beginning and end: receiving an HTTP request, calling inventory, or executing a database statement. In addition to time, it normally carries:

  • its Span ID and parent Span ID;
  • operation name and Span Kind;
  • status, events, and attributes;
  • the Resource that produced it, such as service name and version.

Parent-child relationships express causality, not just temporal proximity. Two operations happening at similar times do not prove that one caused the other. Only correct Context propagation and extraction lets a backend reconstruct the call tree.

Traces are strong evidence for tail latency and localized failure, but sampling means that “I cannot see this trace” does not prove the request never occurred. Use metrics or another independent data source to establish impact.

docs.title: Metrics describe aggregated behavior
docs.text:
Metrics aggregate many events into measurements over time. Counters suit cumulative quantities, gauges represent current state, and histograms describe distributions. They expose request rate, error rate, queue depth, and latency distributions without retaining every operation in full detail.

Aggregation is also where cardinality becomes dangerous. A request ID, user ID, or unconstrained URL in dimensions expands a bounded metric into a large series set, increasing memory, transport, and storage cost. An attribute that is acceptable on a span is not automatically safe as a metric dimension.

docs.title: Logs record discrete evidence
docs.text:
A Log records an event at a moment in time, either as a structured record or traditional text. Good logs describe the event, outcome, and necessary context; they do not dump entire requests, credentials, or arbitrary objects.

When a log record contains the current Trace ID and Span ID, a backend can navigate from a failed span to logs from the same execution. That correlation requires all three conditions:

  1. the correct current Context exists when the log is produced;
  2. the logging SDK or bridge adds identifiers to the record;
  3. the collection path and backend preserve and index the fields.

Missing any one step means that adopting OTel did not automatically correlate logs and traces.

docs.title: Baggage propagates data; it does not store secrets
docs.text:
Baggage is key-value data carried with Context across service boundaries. Downstream instrumentation can read it and decide which attributes to add, for example a bounded workflow category. Baggage does not automatically become a span attribute, and it should not be treated as a distributed session.

It can cross many processes and even external services. Never place passwords, tokens, personal data, or internal identifiers that downstream systems do not need in Baggage. Official security guidance calls for caution with context from untrusted sources and warns against credentials, API keys, or PII in Baggage (Context propagation security guidance).

docs.title: How Context connects services
docs.text:
Context is correlation information attached to the current execution. Propagation serializes it into a transport carrier and deserializes it at the other side. HTTP commonly uses headers; a message broker can use message properties.

sequenceDiagram participant U as Client participant A as Checkout API participant Q as Message Broker participant B as Inventory Worker U->>A: HTTP request + traceparent A->>A: extract Context<br/>create checkout Span A->>Q: publish message<br/>inject updated Context Q-->>B: deliver message + metadata B->>B: extract Context<br/>create reserve-inventory Span Note over A,B: Same Trace ID; each operation has its own Span ID

The sender injects the current Trace ID, current Span ID, and trace flags into the carrier. The receiver extracts the remote Context and uses it as the parent of a new span. OpenTelemetry's default propagator uses the W3C Trace Context traceparent header:

<version>-<trace-id>-<parent-id>-<trace-flags>

That header is correlation information, not an access credential. At external boundaries, decide whether to accept, sanitize, or continue propagating inbound context so forged data cannot pollute traces. The official Context propagation page describes the mechanism and its security boundary.

docs.title: What broken Context looks like
docs.text:
• The downstream service starts a new Trace ID: it probably did not extract context, or the transport omitted the header or message metadata.
  • The Trace ID matches but parentage is wrong: asynchronous or concurrent code may have reused the wrong current Context.
  • HTTP calls connect but messaging breaks: the HTTP library was instrumented while the messaging client or custom protocol was not.
  • Logs lack a Trace ID: no span was active when logging, or the logging bridge is not configured.

Do not use timestamps to “guess” that two partial traces are one request. Temporal proximity creates a hypothesis; it does not restore causal evidence.

docs.title: Resource and Context are not interchangeable
docs.text:
A Resource describes the entity producing telemetry, such as service.name=inventory, a release, or a Pod. It is relatively stable across many records. Context describes the current execution relationship and changes with each request and span.

A compact model is:

  • Resource answers who produced it;
  • Context answers which execution and parent operation it belongs to;
  • an Attribute answers what else characterizes this operation.

Putting a service name in Baggage, making a request ID a Resource, or making a user ID a metric dimension creates an incorrect model and expensive rework.

docs.title: How a Pipeline handles data
docs.text:
Defining a component in Collector configuration does not run it. Only components listed in service.pipelines are active. A common Pipeline is Receiver → Processor → Exporter. There can be zero or more Processors, and order changes behavior.

flowchart LR SDK1[Service A SDK] -->|OTLP/gRPC| R1[OTLP Receiver] SDK2[Service B SDK] -->|OTLP/HTTP| R1 Host[Host metrics] --> R2[Host Metrics Receiver] R1 --> ML[Memory Limiter] R2 --> ML ML --> Filter[Filter / Redaction] Filter --> Batch[Batch] Batch --> E1[OTLP Exporter] Batch --> E2[Debug Exporter\ntroubleshooting only] E1 --> Backend[Observability Backend] classDef boundary fill:#eef6ff,stroke:#3273dc,color:#172b4d; class R1,R2,ML,Filter,Batch,E1,E2 boundary;

docs.title: Receiver
docs.text: A Receiver accepts pushed data or actively collects it. It solves a protocol and entry problem; it does not prove data passed through Processors or was accepted by the backend. For network receivers, bind address, TLS, authentication, and exposure are security boundaries.

docs.title: Processor
docs.text: A Processor modifies, drops, aggregates, or batches data before export. Order changes the result and resource behavior. A configured Processor that is not referenced by a Pipeline has no effect.

docs.title: Exporter
docs.text: An Exporter sends data to a backend or another Collector. Successful sending usually means the destination accepted a request; it does not prove the data is queryable, correctly indexed, or retained. Verify the backend separately.

docs.title: Connector and Extension
docs.text: A Connector is an Exporter in one Pipeline and a Receiver in another, enabling cross-pipeline routing or signal generation. An Extension provides capabilities such as health checks and authentication without directly processing telemetry. Both must be enabled in the relevant service configuration. See Collector Configuration for component structure and activation rules.

docs.title: Where data can disappear
docs.text:
Business operation
  → never instrumented
  → dropped by SDK sampling
  → process exits before export
  → network, protocol, or authentication failure
  → Collector receive or queue refusal
  → intentionally filtered by a Processor
  → Exporter retries exhausted
  → backend rejection, throttling, or indexing failure

That chain is why “there is no trace in the backend” is not a root cause. Troubleshooting must establish evidence at each segment: the application created a span, the SDK attempted export, the Collector received it, Processors retained it, the Exporter sent it, and the backend made it queryable.

A Debug Exporter proves data reached and passed through one Collector Pipeline. It does not prove a production backend is healthy. The project positions it as a local and live troubleshooting tool (Collector Troubleshooting).

docs.title: Sampling changes the conclusions you can draw
docs.text:
Head sampling decides near the beginning of a trace. It costs less but cannot yet know whether the request will fail. Tail sampling waits for more spans in a Collector or similar component, allowing policies to retain errors or slow traces at the cost of buffering, completeness windows, and capacity.

Sampling is therefore not merely a cost dial; it changes the evidence set. If entry and downstream services disagree on the sampling decision, partial traces can result. If a tail-sampling Collector overloads while waiting, the exceptional traces you most want may be lost.

Record sampling policy, effective retention, drop location, and valid query interpretation together—not just a percentage.

docs.title: Test a design with three questions
Can you locate one execution?
docs.text: Is the Trace ID continuous from ingress through synchronous and asynchronous boundaries? Who injects and extracts at each boundary? How is context handled at an untrusted edge?

docs.title: Can you measure overall impact?
docs.text: Which metrics are independent of trace sampling? What are the cardinality limits? Do error rate and latency come from the application, a proxy, or backend-derived data?

docs.title: Can you prove the telemetry path is healthy?
docs.text:
What independent evidence exists at the application, Collector, and backend? Can you distinguish “no business event occurred” from “the telemetry path lost data”? Are Collector receive, refusal, send-failure, and queue metrics monitored through an independent path?

If these questions have no answer, more auto-instrumentation usually creates more unexplained data.

docs.title: Next step
docs.text: Complete Generate Your First Trace. You will observe telemetrygen producing spans, a Collector receiving and processing them, the Debug Exporter printing them, and zPages presenting Collector activity. The goal is not to install a platform; it is to practice verifying each data-path segment.

docs.title: Official sources and version boundary
docs.text:
• Signals: signal categories and current status.
  • Context propagation: Context, inject/extract, W3C Trace Context, and security boundaries.
  • Collector Configuration: Receiver, Processor, Exporter, Connector, Extension, and Pipeline activation.
  • Collector Troubleshooting: troubleshooting uses of Debug Exporter, internal telemetry, and zPages.

This page was reviewed on 2026-07-12. Port defaults, component availability, and stability must be checked with the target distribution's components output and component README.

docs.title: Trigger and objective
docs.text:
Use this runbook when a backend cannot find a service or known request, a trace is broken, metrics/logs disappear, or an Operator annotation exists but the pod is not injected. The objective is not to make some data appear in a UI. Find the first evidence-backed break, restore one known request end to end, and do not expand data exposure or application risk.

Official facts were last verified on 2026-07-12. Runtime defaults and Operator behavior can change; record actual versions first, then consult the Compatibility and Configuration Reference.

docs.title: Protect the service first
docs.text:
If application errors, latency, startup, or resources have regressed, use the Auto-Instrumentation Canary Rollout Runbook to roll back. Do not delay service recovery to preserve telemetry. If real sensitive fields appear, stop the relevant export and escalate a data event; agent rollback cannot retract exported data.

Do not jump to OTEL_TRACES_SAMPLER=always_on, full debug logs, disabled TLS/authentication, or console payload output in production. These shortcuts can create capacity or data incidents.

docs.title: Incident record
docs.text: Record first detection, affected signals, service/namespace/version, last known good time, known request ID and window, Operator/agent/Collector/backend versions, recent changes, and independent business SLI. If there is no known request, send one non-sensitive synthetic request first; do not infer from random traffic.

docs.title: Locate the break hop by hop
1. Is the backend query correct?
docs.text:
Query with an explicit time range, service.name, environment, version, and known request ID. Verify timezone, tenant/project, signal type, and sampling policy. For a partial trace, record the last upstream span and expected downstream service; the fault may be propagation, downstream instrumentation, or sampling rather than the whole Collector.

Continue only after excluding a query error. Otherwise correct the query and stop.

docs.title: 2. Did the request reach the intended instance?
docs.text:
Use gateway access logs, native request counters, or a synthetic probe to prove the request reached the target canary/pod. If it did not, repair routing rather than OTel.

kubectl -n <app-namespace> get pod -l <workload-selector> -o wide
kubectl -n <app-namespace> logs deploy/<deployment> -c <app-container> --since=<window>

Logs may be sensitive; retrieve only the approved window and necessary fields.

docs.title: 3. Was the pod injected as intended?
docs.text:
kubectl -n <app-namespace> get deploy <deployment> -o yaml
kubectl -n <app-namespace> describe pod <pod-name>
kubectl -n <app-namespace> get events --sort-by=.metadata.creationTimestamp
kubectl -n <app-namespace> describe otelinst <instrumentation-name>

Check that the annotation is in spec.template.metadata.annotations; language, resource name, and container name are correct; Instrumentation existed before the pod; and the created pod contains the expected init container/sidecar/environment. The Operator troubleshooting page documents the same order and checks: Auto-instrumentation troubleshooting.

When namespace and workload annotations interact, use actual pod mutation as evidence. Deployment YAML alone does not prove injection.

docs.title: 4. Was startup configuration overwritten?
docs.text:
Compare image declarations, ConfigMaps, pod spec, and the container's effective environment/process arguments:

kubectl -n <app-namespace> exec <pod-name> -c <app-container> -- env
kubectl -n <app-namespace> exec <pod-name> -c <app-container> -- ps -ef

Focus on JAVA_TOOL_OPTIONS, PYTHONPATH, and NODE_OPTIONS. The official guide records that injection can currently override these when set in images or ConfigMaps: configuration-source limitation. If required application flags disappeared, roll back immediately; do not concatenate untested values live.

docs.title: 5. Does the SDK/agent create the signal?
docs.text:
Use a known synthetic request and bounded agent diagnostics/application logs. Raise diagnostic level only on the canary, for a short approved window, and restore it afterward. Check runtime/library support, disabled instrumentation, sampling that may drop the request, proxy removal of propagation headers, and an unexpected service.name.

If manual SDK initialization and an automatic agent both configure a provider, check the language's official coexistence guidance; never assume they compose safely.

docs.title: 6. Do protocol, endpoint, and network agree?
docs.text:
Inspect the effective OTEL_EXPORTER_OTLP_PROTOCOL and endpoint; do not infer protocol from port. The OTLP specification defines 4317/4318 as defaults when no port is configured, while endpoints can use other ports. http/json is an optional form an SDK may implement, not a universal capability: OTLP Exporter specification.

Check DNS, NetworkPolicy, Service endpoints, TLS SNI/CA, client authentication, and Collector receiver. Do not disable certificate verification to prove reachability.

kubectl -n <collector-namespace> get svc,endpoints,networkpolicy
kubectl -n <collector-namespace> logs deploy/<collector-deployment> --since=<window>

docs.title: 7. Where does the Collector drop data?
docs.text:
Within the pipeline, compare receiver accepted/refused, processor drops, exporter sent/send_failed, and queue size/capacity. Metric names vary by version; first consult Internal telemetry for the deployed Collector.

  • receiver does not increase: agent, network, authentication, or receiver break;
  • accepted increases but exporter sent does not: processor/filter, memory limiter, routing, or pipeline binding;
  • send_failed or queue grows: backend authentication, throttling, network, or capacity;
  • queue full: stop adding input; a full sending queue still loses data, as documented in Collector resiliency.

Do not remove sensitive-data filters merely to make data flow. Follow a unique, non-sensitive marker through the pipeline.

docs.title: 8. Did the backend receive but fail to index/query?
docs.text: Inspect backend receiver errors, tenant routing, indexing lag, retention, throttling, and clock skew. Compare Collector exporter sent with backend accepted. If the Collector proves successful send but the backend has no record, escalate the evidence to the backend owner instead of repeatedly restarting applications.

docs.title: Symptom to action
docs.text: Evidence Likely break Action No pod mutation Resource order, annotation location/name, webhook Correct canary configuration and recreate the pod. Pod mutated, application startup bad Version/architecture incompatibility or environment override Roll back with explicit "false" immediately. Agent connection/TLS/auth error Endpoint, protocol, trust, NetworkPolicy Correct approved connectivity; do not weaken security. Receiver accepted is zero Agent-to-receiver path Validate DNS, Service, NetworkPolicy, TLS, and auth in order. Receiver accepted grows, exporter sent does not Processor/pipeline Review filters, routing, memory limiter, and pipeline binding. send_failed/queue grows Backend or Collector capacity Stop rollout, protect the queue, escalate backend/platform. Root span exists, downstream absent Context propagation or downstream instrumentation Check proxy headers, downstream sampling, and agent. Trace exists under wrong service Resource configuration Correct explicit resource attributes; do not delete old data and claim repair.

docs.title: Stop, rollback, and escalation
docs.text:
Roll back immediately when independent application SLI crosses a limit, process arguments are overwritten, the pod CrashLoops, unrelated pods are injected, or diagnostics cannot be bounded. Set the language annotation to "false" and create replacement pods; deleting the annotation is insufficient when namespace injection may exist.

Escalate immediately to data security when real sensitive data or a negative-test marker reaches an unapproved location. Escalate to platform when the webhook blocks unrelated pod creation, certificate/CA fails, or Collector queue approaches exhaustion. Escalate to backend when the Collector has successful-send evidence but data cannot be queried.

docs.title: Closure evidence
docs.text: Before closure, save the first broken hop, root cause, version matrix, known request end-to-end evidence, independent SLI, Collector metrics, repair/rollback diff, and proof that temporary diagnostics were removed. If the data path is still not explainable, status is “mitigated, unresolved,” even if a little data reappears in the UI.

docs.title: What Is OpenTelemetry?
docs.text:
OpenTelemetry (OTel) is an open set of specifications, APIs, SDKs, semantic conventions, and tools for producing, correlating, transporting, and processing telemetry. It does not primarily answer “which dashboard should we use?” It addresses a layer closer to the application: how data from different languages, frameworks, and runtime environments can leave a process with consistent meaning and remain correlatable, governable, and portable before it reaches a backend.

This page was checked against the official OpenTelemetry documentation on 2026-07-12. Collector and component maturity changes over time. Before deployment, verify the selected distribution's component inventory and stability; do not treat version status on this page as a permanent promise.

docs.title: Start with the problem
docs.text:
Suppose a checkout request passes through a gateway, an order service, an inventory service, and a database. The customer sees only “checkout timed out,” while an engineer needs to answer three different questions:

  • What happened? Which request failed, and at which call did the failure occur?
  • How large is the impact? Did error rate, latency percentiles, or affected request volume exceed the service objective?
  • Why did it happen? Does a timeout log belong to the same execution as a slow span, a particular release, and a particular Kubernetes Pod?

Collecting logs, metrics, or traces separately does not automatically answer those questions. The hard problems are consistency and causality: do services use the same service and attribute semantics, does context survive each call boundary, and can the collection path process, filter, and route data without putting backend-specific code in every application?

OpenTelemetry provides a common language for that infrastructure. It does not decide whether the business is healthy, but it lets application activity be recorded and related in a form that backends can understand.

docs.title: See its place in one diagram
docs.text:
flowchart LR subgraph Runtime[Application runtime] Code[Business code] Lib[Frameworks and libraries] API[OpenTelemetry API] SDK[SDK / auto-instrumentation agent] Code --> API Lib --> SDK API --> SDK end subgraph Collection[Collection and governance] Receiver[Receiver accept] Processor[Processor batch, filter, transform] Exporter[Exporter send] Receiver --> Processor --> Exporter end subgraph Backend[Observability backends] TraceDB[Trace backend] MetricsDB[Metrics backend] LogDB[Log backend] end SDK -->|OTLP or another protocol| Receiver Exporter --> TraceDB Exporter --> MetricsDB Exporter --> LogDB

The diagram contains three boundaries that must remain distinct:

  1. Instrumentation boundary: APIs, SDKs, auto-instrumentation, and library instrumentation decide what data is produced.
  2. Collection boundary: the Collector decides what is received, processed, and sent where.
  3. Storage and analysis boundary: backends decide how data is indexed, queried, alerted on, and displayed.

OpenTelemetry provides open interfaces and implementations for the first two boundaries, but it is not a complete storage, query, or alerting product. The project defines the Collector as a vendor-neutral implementation for receiving, processing, and exporting telemetry, not as an observability backend (Collector overview).

docs.title: Specification, API, and SDK
docs.text:
The specification defines behavior and data models that should remain consistent across languages, including the API, SDK, OTLP, and semantic conventions. It answers what an implementation is supposed to mean.

The API is a lightweight interface that application and library code can depend on. A library can create a span or record a measurement without forcing its user to choose a particular SDK or backend. With an API but no configured SDK provider, calls generally do not become exportable telemetry.

The SDK implements sampling, processing, resource detection, and export. It is where runtime policy enters the application process: which traces are sampled, when spans are batched, and which OTLP endpoint receives the data.

Separating API from SDK is an architectural choice. Application code states that a meaningful operation occurred; the deployment environment controls how to record and export it. The official component documentation defines these boundaries (OpenTelemetry Components).

docs.title: Instrumentation methods
docs.text:
The common instrumentation sources are complementary:

Method What it can see Main advantage Main boundary Manual instrumentation Business stages, results, and important attributes Closest to real business meaning Code must be changed, reviewed, and maintained Library instrumentation Common HTTP, database, and messaging calls Reusable and maintained with a framework Does not know business intent Zero-code / auto-instrumentation Supported framework and library calls in a runtime Fast initial coverage Constrained by language, version, and startup method Collector receivers Host, cluster, or existing protocol data Not all data must originate in an SDK Cannot recreate business meaning never expressed by the application

Auto-instrumentation and manual instrumentation are not alternatives. A reliable progression is to establish a call skeleton with auto-instrumentation, then add manual spans and attributes for important operations such as payment authorization, inventory reservation, and access decisions.

docs.title: Resources and semantic conventions
docs.text:
A span named only GET is almost impossible to assign. A Resource describes who produced telemetry, such as service.name, service version, Pod, and namespace. Semantic conventions define consistent names for HTTP, database, and messaging attributes.

These are prerequisites for cross-team queries, not decorative metadata. Service-name drift splits one service into separate time series. User IDs in dimensions create cardinality and privacy risk. Inconsistent error attributes let alerts and traces report conflicting outcomes.

docs.title: OTLP
docs.text: OTLP is OpenTelemetry's telemetry transport protocol. It provides a common data model and transport for traces, metrics, and logs, commonly over gRPC or HTTP. OTLP reduces coupling between application and Collector, and between Collector and backend, but it is not a reliability guarantee. Timeouts, queues, retries, TLS, authentication, and backend capacity still require explicit design.

docs.title: Collector
docs.text:
The Collector is a separately deployable telemetry data plane. A Pipeline normally contains a Receiver, zero or more Processors, and an Exporter. A component is active only when referenced by service.pipelines. The Collector also supports Connectors that join pipelines and Extensions that provide non-data-path capabilities such as health checks and authentication (Collector configuration).

Its value is more than forwarding. It centralizes credentials, batching, sensitive-data filtering, attribute normalization, and multi-backend routing instead of repeating those concerns in every process. Centralization also creates a failure domain: insufficient capacity, an incorrect filter, or a blocked destination can affect many services at once.

docs.title: Kubernetes Operator
docs.text:
The OpenTelemetry Operator can manage Collector custom resources and mutate supported workloads to inject auto-instrumentation. It is a control-plane component: it determines how a Pod is changed. The SDKs, agents, and Collectors that produce and transport telemetry are the data plane.

Consequently, a healthy Operator does not prove traces reach a backend, and a Ready Collector does not prove a workload was correctly injected. Verify the two paths independently. The official documentation scopes the Operator to managing Collectors and workload auto-instrumentation (Kubernetes Operator).

docs.title: How a trace is formed
docs.text:
A call does not become one trace merely because every service has an agent. The path must continuously satisfy these conditions:

  1. The entry service extracts upstream context or creates a new root span.
  2. The current operation creates a child span and injects context into the downstream call.
  3. The downstream service extracts that context and uses the received span as the parent of its span.
  4. Each SDK exports spans; a Collector receives, processes, and sends them onward.
  5. The backend reconstructs the call tree from the shared Trace ID.

If one protocol boundary fails to propagate context, the backend sees two unrelated traces. The data may still exist, but causality has been lost. Signals, Context, and Pipelines examines this process in detail.

docs.title: What it solves—and what it does not
Engineering problems it can solve
docs.text:
• Give polyglot applications a common telemetry model and propagation protocol.
  • Reduce direct coupling between applications and a particular backend.
  • Reuse instrumentation for common library calls across services.
  • Centralize batching, filtering, routing, and attribute governance.
  • Connect signals with Trace ID, Span ID, and Resource attributes.

docs.title: Problems it does not solve automatically
docs.text:
• It does not define service objectives: having data does not define health.
  • It does not guarantee completeness: sampling, queue overflow, network failures, and configuration errors lose data.
  • It does not invent business semantics: auto-instrumentation cannot know that inventory reservation matters more than an ordinary SQL call.
  • It does not remove cost: more attributes, higher sampling, and duplicate export consume resources and storage.
  • It does not automatically protect sensitive data: attributes, log bodies, and Baggage can carry data that must not leave a boundary.
  • It does not replace a backend: queries, retention, alerts, and visualization still belong to a backend.

docs.title: “Installing an agent completes observability”
docs.text: An agent observes only supported runtime and library calls. Asynchronous task boundaries, internal business stages, custom protocols, and unsupported frameworks can remain blind spots. Acceptance should ask whether predefined questions can be answered, not whether injection files exist in a Pod.

docs.title: “The Collector is a stateless pipe, so it needs no capacity design”
docs.text: A Collector can batch, queue, and retry in memory or storage. Even without long-term retention, it has a memory limit, a CPU saturation point, and downstream backpressure. Production design must observe received, refused, failed-send, and queue metrics and define behavior during backend failure.

docs.title: “Signals correlate automatically when attributes have the same text”
docs.text: Matching strings do not necessarily identify the same entity. Reliable correlation needs stable Resource rules, correctly propagated Trace/Span context, controlled attribute cardinality, and backend support for the correlation.

docs.title: “Vendor-neutral means zero migration cost”
docs.text: OTLP and semantic conventions reduce coupling at data production, but query languages, alert models, dashboards, and vendor-specific processors can still lock a system in. Portability comes from explicit boundaries and migration exercises, not from a protocol name.

docs.title: When adoption is appropriate
docs.text:
OpenTelemetry is usually a good foundation when an organization has inconsistent polyglot instrumentation, applications directly connected to multiple backends, broken cross-service causality, or a need to govern telemetry at a common exit.

If the system is one small monolith, an existing backend SDK already answers the required questions, and the team cannot operate a Collector, staying simple can be more reliable. The decision should not be “everyone uses OTel.” It should be whether OTel removes current coupling and correlation gaps and whether the team accepts responsibility for a new data plane.

docs.title: Learning path
docs.text:
1. Read Signals, Context, and Pipelines to understand how causality survives process boundaries.
  2. Complete Generate Your First Trace to verify the client, Collector, and debug output path yourself.
  3. Then move to auto-instrumentation, production architecture, security, capacity, and recovery. Do not begin learning by injecting production workloads.

docs.title: Official sources and version boundary
docs.text:
• OpenTelemetry Components: specification, API, SDK, Collector, and Operator boundaries.
  • OpenTelemetry Signals: supported signal categories and current status.
  • OpenTelemetry Collector: Collector role, distributions, and component maturity entry point.
  • Collector Configuration: actual pipeline and component activation rules.

This page was reviewed on 2026-07-12. Profiles, Events, language SDKs, auto-instrumentation, and Collector component stability are volatile facts; check the official status pages and component README for the target version.

docs.title: Production operations
docs.text:
Application maintenance, health checks, and routine production controls.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

1 document Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Application Maintenance Health Check Standard Application maintenance health-check standard for baselines, layered validation, rollout gates, rollback triggers, observation windows, and closure evidence.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to validate application health before, during, and after maintenance, deployment, patching, scaling, failover, or infrastructure change.

Core judgment:

A health check is a decision gate, not a green endpoint.

The question is not "does /healthz return 200?" The question is whether the service can still perform the user and system work that the change could break, and whether the team knows when to stop, roll back, or continue observing.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard for:

  • deployment windows
  • OS patching or host reboot
  • Kubernetes node drain or upgrade
  • database, cache, queue, or middleware maintenance
  • network, ingress, certificate, or DNS changes
  • capacity scaling
  • failover or recovery validation
  • post-incident return-to-normal checks

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define every application's business logic. It defines the health contract that each application must map to its own checks before production change.

docs.title: Health Contract
docs.text:
Before maintenance begins, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters Service owner Who decides whether the service is healthy enough to continue? Platform signals alone cannot prove product recovery. Baseline Which metrics describe normal behavior before change? Without baseline, teams debate whether symptoms are new. Critical journeys Which user or machine paths must work? Endpoints can be green while important flows fail. Dependency checks Which dependencies must be reachable and correct? A process can be alive while database, cache, queue, or API access is broken. Rollback triggers Which conditions stop the change? Deciding during pressure delays recovery. Observation window How long must health remain stable? Delayed failures often appear after the command succeeds. Alert handling Which alerts are silenced, routed, or expected? Broad silences hide incidents. Closure evidence What proof lets the team close the change? Completion should be based on evidence, not command success.

docs.title: Health Layers
docs.text:
Validate in layers:

Layer Question Example Signal Process Is the process, pod, container, or service running? systemd, Supervisor, pod, container state. Readiness Can it accept traffic safely? Readiness endpoint, load-balancer target health, queue consumer state. Dependency Can it reach required dependencies? Database, cache, queue, object storage, third-party API. Data path Can it read and write the data it needs? Read/write smoke, migration guard, background job result. User journey Does a critical journey work end to end? Login, create, read, search, upload, checkout, API transaction. Guardrail Is it within reliability limits? Error rate, latency, saturation, SLO burn, backlog. Recovery Can the team revert or reduce impact if needed? Rollback command, route switch, previous config, capacity restore.

Do not rely on process health alone. Process health is a starting line, not a finish line.

docs.title: 1. Capture baseline
docs.text:
Before maintenance, record:

  • current version, config, or host state
  • request rate
  • error rate
  • latency
  • saturation
  • queue backlog
  • dependency health
  • current alerts
  • available capacity
  • known degraded conditions that predate the change

docs.title: 2. Define gates
docs.text:
Use three gates:

Gate Question Start gate Is baseline understood and rollback ready? Continue gate Are signals still inside the agreed guardrail while the change proceeds? Close gate Has the service stayed healthy for the observation window?

If the start gate is not true, the change should not begin unless an incident commander accepts the risk.

docs.title: 3. Define rollback triggers
docs.text:
Examples:

  • error rate exceeds guardrail for the observation window
  • latency exceeds user-impact threshold
  • readiness stays below minimum capacity
  • critical user path fails
  • dependency connection errors increase
  • queue backlog grows beyond recovery threshold
  • alerts fire outside the expected maintenance scope
  • rollback owner loses confidence in the current state

Ambiguity should not always mean "keep going." If symptoms appear after the change and the team cannot quickly prove they are unrelated, rolling back is often the safer diagnostic step.

docs.title: 4. Monitor during change
docs.text:
Watch both platform and application signals:

curl -fsS https://<service-endpoint>/healthz
curl -fsS https://<service-endpoint>/readyz

For Kubernetes:

kubectl -n <namespace> get deploy,pod
kubectl -n <namespace> get events --sort-by=.lastTimestamp

For service behavior, prefer a known smoke test or synthetic transaction over a raw endpoint check.

docs.title: 5. Validate after change
docs.text: Run the agreed smoke test and observe for the required window. The service is not healthy just because the change command returned successfully.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Health and readiness endpoints return expected status.
  • Critical user or API journey succeeds.
  • Error rate, latency, saturation, and backlog are within baseline or planned range.
  • Dependencies are reachable and behaving normally.
  • No unexpected restart, crash loop, or queue stall appears.
  • Alerts are clear, expected, or acknowledged with owner and deadline.
  • Rollback path remains available until the observation window passes.
  • Change record includes baseline, validation result, owner, and closure evidence.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Only checks /healthz Broken dependency or user path goes unnoticed. Add readiness, dependency, data-path, and user-journey checks. No baseline Team cannot tell whether the change worsened service. Capture metrics before change. No rollback trigger Recovery decision stalls. Define thresholds before change. Smoke test too shallow Important user path remains broken. Include the critical product or API journey. Alerts silenced too broadly Real incident hidden during maintenance. Scope maintenance silence by alert, service, and time. No post-window observation Delayed failure missed. Require observation duration before close. Rollback path removed too early Team cannot recover after delayed failure. Keep previous config/version/capacity until stable.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Rollback to the previous safe state when rollback triggers fire and the current change is the likely cause.

Rollback may mean:

  • revert deployment
  • restore previous config
  • stop maintenance
  • re-enable previous route, DNS, or load balancer target
  • re-add drained capacity
  • restore host, node, or dependency state
  • pause a migration or background job

If rollback cannot recover because data, schema, or external clients changed, escalate to the service owner and incident commander. Record the forward-recovery plan explicitly.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not put sensitive details in public health endpoints.
  • Protect admin and diagnostic endpoints.
  • Do not expose dependency credentials through health responses.
  • Maintenance access should be time-bounded and audited.
  • Synthetic test accounts and test data should be scoped and safe to reuse.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review after:

  • failed maintenance
  • unclear rollback decision
  • false-green health check
  • missing critical user journey
  • broad silence hiding an alert
  • new dependency or route
  • new critical API or product flow

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Deployment Rollback Runbook
  • System OS Patching Standard
  • Prometheus Alerting Standard

docs.title: Platform tools
docs.text:
Production operations for GitLab, bastions, and analytics platforms.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

3 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • GitLab Operations Standard GitLab operations standard for source control, CI/CD, runner trust, release authority, backup, restore, audit, and incident recovery.
  • JumpServer and Bastion Access Standard JumpServer and bastion access standard for privileged operations, asset scope, approval, session recording, emergency access, and review.
  • Redash Governance Standard Redash governance standard for internal BI query surfaces, covering data-source credentials, read boundaries, query impact, sharing, cached results, metadata backup, audit, and rollback.

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate GitLab as a source-control and delivery platform.

Core judgment:

GitLab is not only a repository server. It is a production change authority.

When GitLab controls source code, CI/CD, protected branches, approvals, artifacts, registry, tokens, and infrastructure pipelines, a GitLab weakness becomes a production-change weakness. Treat it as a governance system for change, not a place where code happens to live.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • GitLab hosts production source code or infrastructure code.
  • GitLab CI/CD can deploy workloads or mutate infrastructure.
  • Runners can access cloud, Kubernetes, registry, package, signing, or secret systems.
  • Multiple teams use GitLab groups for release governance.
  • GitLab outage would block emergency change or rollback.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not define every GitLab feature, UI option, or licensing tier. It defines the operating contract for GitLab as a production change authority.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before GitLab becomes the source of production change, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters Platform owner Who owns GitLab service health, upgrades, backups, and incidents? GitLab outages can block deployment, rollback, and audit. Change authority Which projects, branches, tags, and pipelines can affect production? Production mutation must not be hidden in ordinary repository activity. Group ownership Who owns each group and project? Orphaned groups become unreviewed release authority. Identity lifecycle Which SSO, MFA, group sync, dormant-user, key, and offboarding controls govern human access? A former maintainer, old SSH key, or unmanaged token can still change production. Protected refs Which branches and tags require review, status checks, or approvals? One unprotected ref can bypass release governance. Runner trust Which runners can see secrets or deploy? Runner compromise is often more dangerous than repository read access. Secret boundary Where CI/CD variables, deploy tokens, and access tokens live and rotate?
