CI credentials frequently become production credentials. Artifact and registry retention What build, release, package, and image evidence is retained? Recovery and audit depend on more than Git history. Backup and restore What is backed up, how often, and how restore is proven? A backup without restore proof is not a recovery plan. Emergency release How production changes happen if GitLab is down? Incident response cannot depend on a single unavailable authority. Audit review Which events require review? Change authority needs evidence, not trust in memory.

docs.title: Protect production refs as release authority
docs.text:
Define protection for:

  • default branches
  • release branches
  • production tags
  • infrastructure repositories
  • deployment manifests
  • CI/CD templates
  • group-level variables

Require appropriate review, status checks, and approval for production-impacting refs. Emergency bypass should be named, logged, time-limited, and reconciled afterward.

docs.title: Separate build trust from deploy trust
docs.text:
Runner access is the most common hidden trust boundary.

Runner Type Use Rule Shared low-trust runner Build and test unprivileged code. No production credentials or protected variables. Project runner Project-specific pipelines. Scoped credentials only. Protected runner Deploy from protected branches or tags. Runs protected jobs only. Privileged runner Container build, Docker-in-Docker, special operations. Isolated, minimized, patched, and audited. Production deploy runner Mutates production or infrastructure state. Dedicated, protected, and tied to release approval.

Do not let untrusted merge requests run jobs with deployment credentials, protected variables, privileged runners, or cloud/Kubernetes access.

docs.title: Govern secrets and tokens as production credentials
docs.text:
Record and review:

  • CI/CD variables
  • deploy tokens
  • project access tokens
  • group access tokens
  • personal access tokens
  • SSH keys and deploy keys
  • runner registration tokens
  • runner authentication tokens
  • robot accounts
  • service accounts
  • OAuth or active sessions where supported
  • signing keys
  • registry credentials
  • webhooks and outbound integrations

Every token should have owner, scope, expiration or review date, rotation path, and incident response owner. Masked variables are not a security boundary by themselves; scoping and protected job access matter more.

docs.title: Govern human access as release authority
docs.text:
Human access is part of the production-change surface. Define:

  • authoritative identity provider or local-account exception
  • MFA scope for privileged users
  • group sync or membership ownership
  • owner and maintainer role review
  • dormant-user detection
  • SSH key and deploy-key review
  • personal access token expiration and scope review
  • offboarding test that proves access disappears from Git, UI, CI/CD, registry, and runner paths

Every production-impacting group and project needs a current accountable owner. Access exceptions should have an expiry date, revocation owner, and evidence that the exception still exists for a production reason.

docs.title: Back up the whole change authority
docs.text:
Backups must cover:

  • repositories
  • database
  • GitLab configuration
  • secrets file
  • CI/CD variables and protected settings
  • group and project membership
  • merge-request and approval metadata
  • container registry if used
  • package artifacts if required
  • CI artifacts needed for release evidence

Restore testing must prove more than "the UI loads." It should prove repository access, protected branch rules, runner registration or replacement, CI/CD variable restoration, registry or artifact access, and admin review state.

GitLab backup and restore media are sensitive. They may contain repositories, secrets, CI/CD variables, registry references, membership state, and release evidence. Encrypt backups, restrict access, handle isolated restore environments as sensitive systems, and rotate exposed tokens or keys if backup media is suspected compromised.

docs.title: Define GitLab-down behavior before the incident
docs.text:
If GitLab is unavailable during a production incident, teams need a controlled path for:

  • emergency rollback
  • emergency config change
  • disabling compromised credentials
  • freezing deployments
  • communicating source-of-truth status
  • reconciling changes back into GitLab

The emergency path should be narrower than the normal path. If the fallback is broader than the primary path, GitLab governance evaporates exactly when pressure is highest.

A minimum emergency packet should include:

  • incident ID
  • named approver
  • allowed repository, artifact, manifest, or infrastructure target
  • allowed action and denied actions
  • source of truth for the artifact or configuration being applied
  • time-boxed credential or runner path
  • evidence capture method if GitLab is unavailable
  • post-use credential rotation
  • reconciliation or back-merge proof after GitLab returns

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Regular checks:

gitlab-rake gitlab:check
gitlab-rake gitlab:doctor:secrets
gitlab-ctl status

Pass conditions:

  • Web, Git SSH or HTTP, registry, package, and CI services are healthy.
  • Production-impacting branches and tags are protected.
  • Required review, status checks, and approval match release policy.
  • Untrusted merge requests cannot access protected variables or deploy runners.
  • Runner fleet is online, scoped by trust level, and patched.
  • Production deploy runners are protected and auditable.
  • Backups complete and restore has been tested in an isolated environment.
  • Restore test proves repositories, protected refs, CI/CD variables, runners, registry or artifacts, and admin state.
  • Admin membership, group owners, deploy tokens, and project access tokens are reviewed.
  • Production-impacting groups and projects have accountable owners, no orphaned owner or maintainer authority, and reviewed access exceptions with expiry and revocation owner.
  • Offboarding tests prove former users lose Git, UI, CI/CD, registry, token, SSH key, and runner-mediated access.
  • Emergency release and rollback path has an owner and reconciliation step.
  • Pipelines, runners, or protected deploy jobs are re-enabled after compromise only after release authority, owner approval, and blast radius are revalidated.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Protected branch disabled Unreviewed code or manifests can deploy. Enforce protected refs and audit changes. Runner credentials overbroad CI compromise becomes production compromise. Separate runner trust levels and secret scopes. Untrusted MR reaches protected secrets External or low-trust code can exfiltrate deployment credentials. Block protected variables and protected runners from untrusted jobs. Token without owner or expiry Old credentials keep production access. Track owner, scope, review date, and rotation. Orphaned owner or maintainer role A former or unknown actor can change production. Review group/project ownership, dormant users, and offboarding evidence. No restore test Outage recovery fails when GitLab is needed most. Test restore to isolated environment. Backup media exposed Repositories, variables, tokens, and membership state may leak. Encrypt, restrict, audit, and rotate credentials after suspected exposure. Artifact retention unmanaged Storage fills or release evidence disappears. Define artifact, registry, and package retention. Shared admin account No accountability for release-authority changes. Use named admins, MFA, and break-glass process. GitLab-down path undefined Incident rollback is blocked or improvised. Define emergency release and reconciliation flow.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery options include:

  • restore GitLab from a tested backup
  • disable compromised runners
  • rotate CI/CD variables, deploy tokens, and access tokens
  • revoke or disable group/project membership, owner and maintainer roles, personal access tokens, SSH keys, deploy keys, service accounts, OAuth sessions, webhooks, and compromised integrations
  • revoke runner registration tokens
  • rotate runner authentication tokens when runners may be compromised
  • freeze deployments by disabling protected deploy jobs
  • revert protected branch or tag changes
  • restore repository from backup or mirror
  • restore registry or release artifacts
  • reconcile emergency changes back into GitLab after recovery

If a runner, token, protected ref, or human access path is suspected compromised, stop deployment jobs first, preserve audit evidence, revoke access, rotate credentials, and then re-enable only after the owner verifies scope, blast radius, release authority, and reconciliation evidence.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enforce MFA for privileged users.
  • Use SSO or another governed identity source where available; document local-account exceptions.
  • Keep admin membership small and reviewed.
  • Review dormant users, owner and maintainer roles, SSH keys, deploy keys, personal access tokens, and service accounts.
  • Separate build, test, and deploy credentials.
  • Protect deploy tokens, project access tokens, group tokens, and runner tokens.
  • Review webhook targets and outbound integrations.
  • Do not store secrets in repositories, issue comments, job logs, or artifacts.
  • Encrypt and restrict GitLab backups and restore media.
  • Keep GitLab and runners patched.
  • Treat CI job logs and artifacts as potential secret-exposure surfaces.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review after:

  • runner incident
  • access or group owner change
  • identity provider, MFA, group sync, SSH key, deploy key, or personal access token policy change
  • protected branch or release-policy change
  • GitLab upgrade
  • backup failure or restore test
  • deploy token or project access token rotation
  • registry or artifact retention change
  • release governance change
  • GitLab outage during an incident

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• Jenkins to Kubernetes Pipeline Standard
  • Argo CD GitOps Standard
  • Redash Governance Standard
  • Shell Automation Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to operate JumpServer or a bastion platform as the controlled entry point for privileged infrastructure access.

The core judgment:

A bastion is not a login shortcut. It is the audit boundary for privileged operations.

If engineers use it to reach servers, databases, Kubernetes nodes, network devices, or internal consoles, the bastion must preserve the answer to five questions:

  • Who operated?
  • Which asset was touched?
  • What privilege was used?
  • What evidence was recorded?
  • How is access revoked under pressure?

A bastion that only centralizes SSH traffic but leaves direct paths open, shared accounts active, sessions unreviewed, and temporary users permanent is not an access-control system. It is a more convenient bypass with a nicer login page.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Engineers need SSH, RDP, database, or web console access to private assets.
  • Direct access to servers should be removed or reduced.
  • Operations require session recording.
  • Vendors or temporary users need controlled access.
  • Break-glass access must exist without destroying accountability.
  • Asset owners need evidence for access review and incident investigation.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text: This standard does not approve shared root access, unmanaged direct SSH, permanent vendor access, or using a bastion as a substitute for least-privilege cloud IAM. It also does not define every JumpServer feature. The standard defines the access-governance contract that a bastion must satisfy before it is trusted for production assets.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before onboarding production assets, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters Asset owner Who owns each server, database, cluster, network device, or console? Access approval without an asset owner has no authority. Identity source Which identity system is authoritative for users and groups? Local orphan users weaken offboarding and incident response. Access class Which access is routine, privileged, vendor, or emergency? Review frequency and evidence requirements differ by class. Privilege boundary Which commands, accounts, databases, or consoles can each role use? A login path is not the same as controlled privilege. Direct path policy Which direct SSH, RDP, database, or console paths are closed or exception-approved? Open direct paths bypass bastion evidence. Session evidence What must be recorded, retained, protected, and reviewable? An audit boundary needs protected evidence, not only logs. Temporary access Who approves vendor or time-bound access and how expiry is enforced? Temporary access becomes permanent unless expiry is automatic or reviewed. Break-glass path How emergency access works when JumpServer or identity is down? Incidents need access, but the fallback must be narrower than the normal path. Revocation owner Who can revoke access, rotate credentials, and disable sessions quickly? Slow revocation turns access governance into paperwork. Platform recovery How JumpServer configuration, recordings, keys, and asset mappings recover? Losing the bastion can also lose evidence and access inventory.

docs.title: Treat assets as governed inventory
docs.text:
Each asset needs:

  • owner
  • environment
  • asset type
  • criticality
  • allowed access method
  • approved user group
  • privileged account mapping
  • emergency owner
  • review cadence
  • direct-path exception status
  • decommission owner

Unknown-owner assets should not be exposed through the bastion as a convenience.

docs.title: Close or classify direct paths
docs.text:
A bastion only works as an audit boundary when ordinary access flows through it.

For each asset class, decide whether direct access is:

Path Default Required Evidence SSH/RDP from engineer networks Closed Network policy, security group, firewall, or VPN rule. Database client access Closed or explicitly scoped Source ranges, users, roles, timeout, and query logging. Kubernetes node access Closed except break-glass Owner, approval, incident link, and post-action reconciliation. Network-device admin access Restricted Approved management network and emergency path. Cloud console or internal console Identity-governed MFA, role mapping, and audit log retention.

If a direct path must remain open, record the owner, reason, scope, expiration or review date, compensating evidence, and revocation method. A permanent undocumented exception is a bypass, not a control.

docs.title: Separate roles and review duties
docs.text:
Minimum role model:

  • Platform administrator.
  • Auditor.
  • Asset owner.
  • Operator.
  • Temporary vendor or emergency user.

Administrators should not be the only people who can review their own sessions. The person who grants access should not be the only person who decides whether the recorded operation was acceptable.

Access should map to named identities. Shared privileged accounts may still exist on old systems, but the bastion must bind their use to a named human, an approval context, and a recorded session.

docs.title: Record and review sessions
docs.text:
Privileged sessions should be logged and recorded. If law, policy, or tooling prevents full recording for a high-risk path, access should fail closed unless an approved alternate evidence path exists: command audit, database audit log, incident or change ticket, independent reviewer, and retained proof that the operation matched the approval.

Protect recordings and audit logs as evidence. They should have retention, access control, integrity protection where available, backup, and deletion rules. A recording that any platform admin can edit or delete without evidence is not reliable audit material.

Review triggers:

  • Production root or sudo access.
  • Database administrative access.
  • Kubernetes node access.
  • Firewall, route, VPN, DNS, or load-balancer changes.
  • Emergency access.
  • Vendor access.
  • Failed login bursts.
  • Access outside maintenance window.
  • Session recording failure.
  • Direct-path exception use.

docs.title: Govern temporary and vendor access
docs.text:
Temporary access should have:

  • requester
  • business or incident reason
  • approving asset owner
  • exact asset scope
  • allowed protocol and account
  • start and end time
  • session recording requirement
  • revocation owner
  • review evidence after use

Do not rely on a calendar reminder as the control. Use expiry, group removal, or a scheduled review where missed or rejected review removes access by default.

docs.title: Keep break-glass narrower than normal access
docs.text:
Break-glass exists for incidents, not convenience. It should define:

  • who can invoke it
  • what condition permits it
  • which assets it reaches
  • which credentials or path it uses
  • how the action is recorded if the bastion is unavailable
  • who is notified
  • how credentials rotate afterward
  • how changes are reconciled after recovery

The emergency path should be smaller than normal standing access. If break-glass gives wider, quieter, or longer-lived access than the bastion, it will become the real access model during stressful work.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Before onboarding an asset:

  • Asset owner, criticality, environment, and review cadence are recorded.
  • User can access only approved assets and accounts.
  • Direct access path is closed or exception-approved.
  • Session recording works and the recording is retrievable by an auditor.
  • Command audit works where supported.
  • Revocation removes access from the bastion and from the underlying account where applicable.
  • Temporary access expires without manual cleanup.
  • Emergency path is documented, tested, and reconciled after use.
  • Audit logs and recordings survive platform restart and backup/restore testing.

Operational tests should cover each approved access method:

Access Method Test SSH or RDP Access through bastion succeeds; direct path is denied or exception-approved; session evidence is retrievable. Database access Approved database role works only through the approved path; direct client access is denied or scoped; database audit evidence exists. Kubernetes node access Routine node access is denied; break-glass path requires approval, evidence, and reconciliation. Network device access Management path is restricted to the approved network or bastion flow; config-changing session evidence is retained. Web or cloud console Identity, MFA, role mapping, and audit log entries match the approved access class. Revocation Removing the user or group removes bastion access and underlying asset access where applicable.

Use placeholder hostnames and asset identifiers in public docs. Do not publish real asset names.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Shared privileged account No accountability. Map access to individual identity. Direct SSH remains open Bastion audit is bypassed. Restrict direct network paths. Direct database or console path remains open Sensitive operations happen outside recorded sessions. Close paths or record exception scope, owner, and expiry. Session recording can be edited or deleted silently Evidence cannot be trusted during incident review. Protect recording storage and audit administrative access. No session review Recording exists but risk is not managed. Schedule and trigger reviews with named reviewers. Vendor access never expires Temporary access becomes permanent. Enforce expiry and owner approval. Break-glass wider than normal access Incidents bypass governance exactly when risk is highest. Keep emergency scope narrow and reconcile afterward. Bastion unavailable Operators lose controlled emergency access. Test break-glass and recovery path. Asset owner unknown Access approval has no authority. Maintain asset inventory. Identity offboarding not connected Former users retain access through local accounts or groups. Integrate identity lifecycle and run revocation tests. Platform backup omits recordings or asset mappings Recovery restores login but loses evidence or scope. Back up configuration, mappings, keys, and evidence metadata.

docs.title: Rollback and Revocation
docs.text:
To remove access:

  • Remove user from role or group.
  • Revoke asset authorization.
  • Disable active sessions when risk is immediate.
  • Rotate shared asset credentials if they were used or exposed.
  • Disable temporary account.
  • Remove direct-path exceptions if the reason no longer exists.
  • Review past sessions if access was risky or unauthorized.
  • Preserve audit evidence before deleting accounts or mappings.

For bastion platform failure, use break-glass access only with an incident record. After recovery, reconcile what changed, rotate emergency credentials, attach evidence, and close the fallback path.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Enforce MFA for privileged users.
  • Restrict admin access to JumpServer itself.
  • Protect session recordings and audit logs.
  • Avoid storing unmanaged shared credentials.
  • Review sudo and root access separately.
  • Review database administrative access separately from ordinary read access.
  • Keep asset-to-owner mappings current.
  • Do not expose the bastion admin interface publicly without additional controls.
  • Patch the bastion platform and its dependencies.
  • Treat bastion backup data as sensitive because it may contain credentials, recordings, asset names, and access mappings.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review after:

  • staff change or team transfer
  • vendor onboarding or offboarding
  • privileged-access incident
  • failed recording or audit-log gap
  • direct-path exception creation or expiry
  • bastion upgrade
  • identity-provider change
  • asset criticality change
  • emergency access use
  • backup or restore test

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• OpenVPN Access Standard
  • Server Baseline Standard
  • GitLab Operations Standard

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
This standard defines how to deploy and operate Redash or a similar internal BI query surface.

Core judgment:

A BI tool is not a dashboard application. It is a governed data access surface.

Redash can make operational and business data easier to inspect. It can also turn one broad credential into hundreds of informal readers, cached result copies, exported files, shared links, and queries that stress production databases. The deployment is acceptable only when access, data class, query impact, sharing, retention, and recovery are governed as one contract.

docs.title: When to Use
docs.text:
Use this standard when:

  • Internal teams need SQL-based dashboards or ad hoc queries.
  • Data sources can be exposed through read-only accounts, replicas, warehouses, or curated views.
  • Query and dashboard access can be governed by group, data source, and data class.
  • Sharing, export, and cached result retention have an owner.
  • Metadata backup and restore are required because queries and dashboards are operational assets.

Do not connect Redash directly to production databases with write, owner, schema-change, replication, or administrative privileges.

docs.title: Non-Goals
docs.text:
This standard does not define:

  • data warehouse architecture
  • analytics modeling
  • business metric definitions
  • dashboard design style
  • public BI publishing
  • a replacement for data-governance approval

It covers the platform and security contract around the BI query surface.

docs.title: Production Preconditions
docs.text:
Do not treat Redash as production-governed until these controls exist:

  • approved identity boundary such as SSO, IdP integration, or an equivalent centrally managed access process
  • MFA for privileged users
  • group-based provisioning and deprovisioning
  • named admins, with local admin limited to controlled break-glass use
  • supported-version or approved risk-exception posture
  • patch and CVE monitoring owner
  • upgrade rollback plan
  • restore-tested metadata backup before upgrades

If any of these are missing, the page may still guide a lab deployment, but the system is not ready to hold broad internal data access.

docs.title: Production Contract
docs.text:
Before Redash is used by more than one team, define:

Contract Required Decision Why It Matters Platform owner Who owns Redash service health, upgrades, backups, and incidents? A broken BI tool can block operational decisions. Identity boundary Which SSO, IdP, group, or controlled access process governs users and admins? Revoked staff must lose query, dashboard, export, and admin access. Maintenance posture Which version is approved, how are CVEs handled, and how are upgrades rolled back? A stale BI surface becomes a persistent data-access risk. Data-source owner Who approves each data source and credential? Platform admins should not silently become data owners. Data class What kind of data can appear in queries, results, dashboards, exports, and cache? Access decisions depend on data sensitivity, not only database hostnames. Credential scope Which account is used, what can it read, and what is denied? A broad credential turns BI compromise into data compromise. Query impact Which database path absorbs long or expensive queries? Dashboards must not become production load tests. Sharing model Who may publish dashboards, create public links, invite users, or export results? Sharing bypasses the original query permission model. Result retention How long cached query results and exports survive? Revoking access does not automatically erase copied results. Audit and review Which changes are logged and reviewed? Sensitive access needs evidence, not memory. Recovery How metadata, credentials, and data-source definitions are restored or revoked? Losing Redash metadata can erase operational dashboards and incident evidence.

docs.title: Control identity lifecycle before adding data sources
docs.text:
Identity is the first production boundary. Before broad data-source onboarding, define:

  • how users are provisioned
  • how users are deprovisioned
  • which groups map to query, dashboard, data-source, and admin rights
  • which accounts are break-glass only
  • how MFA is enforced for privileged roles
  • how access review evidence is retained

Validation must prove a revoked user loses query, dashboard, export, sharing, and admin paths. Deprovisioning that only removes the user from a chat group or mailing list is not access removal.

docs.title: Maintain the platform as a data-access surface
docs.text:
Redash needs an explicit maintenance owner. Record:

  • approved version
  • patch and CVE monitoring source
  • upgrade owner
  • upgrade test path
  • metadata backup taken before upgrade
  • downgrade or rollback path
  • user-facing impact window

If the project version is unsupported or difficult to upgrade, record a risk exception and compensating controls before expanding data access.

docs.title: Separate Redash from production write paths
docs.text:
Production data sources must use one of these patterns:

  • read replica
  • data warehouse
  • curated view layer
  • read-only service account with narrow schema permissions
  • approved analytics endpoint

If a production primary must be queried, treat it as an exception with an owner, timeout, concurrency limit, query review, and rollback plan. Redash should never be a path for modifying production data.

docs.title: Treat each data source as an access grant
docs.text:
For every data source, record:

  • data-source owner
  • business purpose
  • data class
  • credential storage location
  • read scope and explicit denied scope
  • network access path
  • query timeout and concurrency limit
  • allowed user groups
  • dashboard sharing rule
  • export rule
  • review date

Avoid shared credentials that cannot be rotated, attributed, or scoped. If the account can read more data than the Redash users should see, the data source is over-privileged even if the UI permissions look tidy.

docs.title: Govern query authorship and dashboard publishing separately
docs.text:
Query authors, dashboard publishers, data-source creators, and admins are different powers.

Define:

  • who can create data sources
  • who can write or edit queries
  • who can publish dashboards
  • who can export results
  • whether public links are disabled
  • how sensitive dashboards are reviewed
  • who can add users or groups
  • who can rotate credentials

Dashboards with sensitive or regulated data should have a named owner and review cycle. A dashboard without an owner is a data leak waiting for a calendar change.

docs.title: Control cached results and exports
docs.text:
Redash permissions protect the UI path. They do not fully protect:

  • cached query results
  • downloaded CSV files
  • screenshots
  • embedded dashboards
  • public or secret links
  • copied SQL queries
  • notification payloads

Define retention for cached results and exports. If a user loses access, assess whether cached results, shared links, or exported files must also be removed.

docs.title: Limit query impact
docs.text:
Set guardrails before broad rollout:

  • query timeout
  • result size limit
  • concurrency limit
  • warehouse or replica preference
  • slow-query owner
  • database-side resource guard where available
  • emergency disable path for a data source

The right validation is not "the dashboard loads." It is that a slow or expensive query cannot quietly damage the database path other services depend on.

docs.title: Back up and test Redash metadata restore
docs.text:
Redash metadata contains queries, dashboards, users, groups, data-source definitions, schedules, alerts, and sometimes sensitive connection context. Backups must be protected and restore-tested.

Restore testing should prove:

  • dashboards and queries return
  • data-source definitions are restored without exposing secrets in plaintext
  • credentials can be reattached or rotated after restore
  • sharing settings remain controlled
  • admin users remain reviewed

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Pass conditions:

  • Redash web service, workers, scheduler, and metadata database are healthy.
  • Identity integration, MFA for privileged roles, and group provisioning are enforced or have an approved equivalent control.
  • A deprovisioning test proves a revoked user loses query, dashboard, export, sharing, and admin paths.
  • Metadata backup is current and restore-tested.
  • Patch/CVE monitoring has an owner, and upgrade rollback is documented.
  • Each data source has an owner, data class, read scope, and review date.
  • Data-source credentials are read-only or have an approved exception.
  • A write or schema-change attempt through the BI credential is denied.
  • Test queries succeed within timeout on the intended replica, warehouse, or governed path.
  • Slow, large, or concurrent queries are limited or routed away from production primaries.
  • Public links are disabled by default or require review.
  • Dashboard export and cached-result retention are documented.
  • Admin users, data-source creators, and dashboard publishers are reviewed.
  • Credential rotation has a tested path.

docs.title: Failure Modes
docs.text: Failure Mode Result Prevention Weak identity lifecycle Former users or stale groups keep data access. Use SSO or equivalent controls, MFA, group review, and deprovisioning tests. Unsupported or unpatched Redash The BI surface accumulates known vulnerabilities. Maintain version posture, CVE monitoring, and upgrade rollback. Broad production credential BI compromise exposes or changes production data. Use read-only scoped accounts and denied-scope checks. UI permission hides over-broad database access A user can query data the group should not see. Scope credentials and views to the data class. Public or secret links spread Sensitive dashboards bypass normal access review. Disable or tightly review public sharing. Cached results outlive access revocation Formerly authorized data remains visible. Define cache, link, and export cleanup. Long-running queries hit production primary Dashboard use degrades customer-facing systems. Use replicas, warehouses, timeouts, and concurrency limits. Metadata backup is not restorable Queries, dashboards, schedules, and access records vanish. Test metadata restore and credential reattachment. Shared admin access No accountability for data-source or sharing changes. Use named admins, MFA, and audit review. Credentials leak through exports or screenshots Data leaves the governed surface. Restrict exports and review sensitive dashboards.

docs.title: Rollback and Recovery
docs.text:
Recovery may include:

  • disable a data source
  • revoke or rotate a credential
  • disable sharing links
  • remove cached results or exports when feasible
  • restore the metadata database
  • reattach or rotate restored credentials
  • route users back to the previous BI tool
  • notify data owners when sensitive results may have spread

If a credential is suspected leaked, rotate it before restoring access. If a dashboard exposed sensitive data, treat link removal as only the first step; review cached results, exports, screenshots, notification payloads, and downstream copies.

Escalate immediately when:

  • a data-source credential may be leaked
  • a dashboard, link, export, or cached result exposes sensitive data
  • a query affects a production primary or shared database path
  • metadata restore fails
  • unauthorized sharing or admin activity is detected

The platform owner coordinates Redash service action. The data-source owner decides data-source disablement, credential scope, and re-enable approval. DBA or SRE owns database-impact response. Security or privacy owners join when credentials, regulated data, customer data, or unauthorized sharing may be involved.

Before re-enabling a disabled data source, collect evidence that the credential is rotated or still valid, access groups are reviewed, cached or shared exposure is contained where feasible, query impact is under control, and the data-source owner has approved re-enable.

docs.title: Security Notes
docs.text:
• Do not publish query results, customer data, internal table names, dashboard screenshots, data-source names, database hosts, or credential examples.
  • Protect data-source credentials in approved secret storage.
  • Use least-privilege database accounts.
  • Keep Redash behind approved access controls; do not expose it to the public internet without additional controls.
  • Require MFA for privileged users and controlled break-glass admin handling.
  • Keep the Redash version, dependencies, and security patches under review.
  • Review public links and exports as data-sharing events.
  • Audit admin, data-source, permission, sharing, and credential changes.
  • Do not use Redash as an emergency database console.

docs.title: Review Cycle
docs.text:
Review Redash when:

  • a new data source is added
  • a data source changes owner or data class
  • a new team receives query access
  • public sharing or export rules change
  • a dashboard becomes operationally critical
  • credential rotation occurs
  • metadata restore is tested
  • a slow query affects a production path
  • a security incident or suspected data exposure occurs

docs.title: Related Documents
docs.text:
• MySQL Operations Standard
  • JumpServer and Bastion Access Standard
  • Standard Document Template

docs.title: Redis
docs.text:
Runtime behavior, memory, persistence, backups, AOF recovery, and failover.

This topic follows the path from mental model to verified practice, then production design, operations, and recovery.

3 documents Production design

Turn architecture, capacity, security, availability, and release gates into testable production requirements.

  • Redis Memory and Availability Standard Redis memory, eviction, replication, and availability standard.
  • Redis Production Operations Standard Minimum Redis production standard covering data role, memory, persistence, availability, security, monitoring, change, and recovery boundaries.
  • Redis Persistence and Data Protection Standard Redis RDB, AOF, multipart AOF, and data protection standard.
2 documents Operations and recovery

Execute changes, validation, stop decisions, rollback, and incident recovery in a safe order.

  • Redis AOF Inspection and Recovery Runbook Runbook for evidence preservation, AOF file-set inspection, isolated validation, repair, and Redis recovery after AOF load failure.
  • Redis Migration and Failover Runbook Runbook for Redis host migration, version migration, and planned role changes.

docs.title: Redis AOF Inspection and Recovery Runbook
docs.text:
Never run redis-check-aof --fix against the production directory, the only copy, or the evidence copy. The default path is to find a more complete recovery source, not to repair AOF.

flowchart TD A[Redis cannot load AOF] --> B{Healthy replica exists} B -- Yes --> C[Verify replication position and data] C --> D[Rebuild from healthy replica] B -- No --> E{Qualified backup within RPO} E -- Yes --> F[Restore and validate backup] E -- No --> G[Read-only AOF inspection] G --> H{Only final tail can be truncated} H -- No --> I[Stop and escalate data-recovery incident] H -- Yes --> J[Approve estimated loss] J --> K[Repair only repair_dir] K --> L[Isolated load and business validation] mindmap root((Redis AOF recovery model)) Preferred sources Healthy replica Qualified backup AOF candidate Evidence preservation Read-only source Evidence checksums Logs and configuration Limited repair Read-only recovery copy Repair copy only RPO estimate Isolation boundary Separate directory Separate port Replication and Cluster disabled No production network Acceptance Keys and TTLs Business samples Single write authority Separate cutover plan

docs.title: Trigger
docs.text:
Use this runbook only when Redis cannot start because of an AOF, manifest, or format error; a failed node leaves an AOF candidate to assess; a single-file AOF appears truncated at the tail; Redis 7/8 multipart AOF relationships are inconsistent; or an interrupted rewrite leaves the authoritative file set uncertain.

Use a product-specific recovery process for Redis Cloud/Software, an Operator that hides the file layer, full Redis Cluster disaster recovery, vendor encryption/modules/storage, or any case where a healthy primary/replica or qualified backup is available.

docs.title: Risk
docs.text:
This is high-risk data recovery. An unsafe action can truncate valid writes, select an older replica as authority, destroy the only evidence, reconnect an isolated instance to the original topology, misread commands because of version/modules/ACL/TLS differences, or create two writable authorities.

Every --fix, candidate promotion, and production cutover requires data-owner approval. verification_status: not-verified means this runbook has not been executed in the user's environment.

docs.title: Required permissions
docs.text: Require read-only access to original configuration, logs, and data; access to replica, backup, replication, and business evidence; controlled root/platform access; temporary capacity for at least three copies; an isolated host/container/network namespace; written RPO and cutover approval; and Redis tools matching the source major/minor release.

docs.title: Establish role, traffic, and better candidates
docs.text:
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> ROLE
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> INFO replication
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> INFO persistence
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> CONFIG GET dir
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> CONFIG GET appendonly
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> CONFIG GET appendfilename
redis-cli -h <host> -p <port> CONFIG GET appenddirname

Determine whether the node is primary, replica, or standalone; whether clients still write; whether Sentinel/Cluster/systemd/orchestration will restart it; which replica is more complete; whether a tested backup exists; and the approved maximum RPO. Do not stop an authoritative writer before traffic is contained.

docs.title: Record version and file facts
docs.text:
redis-server --version
redis-check-aof --version 2>/dev/null || true
find <source-dir> -maxdepth 3 -type f -printf '%TY-%Tm-%Td %TH:%TM:%TS %s %p\n' | sort
df -h <source-dir>

Record redis_conf, source_dir, appendfilename, appenddirname, deployment type, and role. Stop when modules, ACL, TLS, encryption, compression, or the file system cannot be reproduced.

docs.title: Build four directories and checksum evidence
docs.text:
flowchart LR S[source_dir<br/>original scene] -->|read-only copy| E[evidence_dir<br/>immutable evidence] E -->|complete copy| R[recovery_dir<br/>read-only candidate] R -->|copy after approval| P[repair_dir<br/>only --fix target] R --> I[load unrepaired candidate in isolation] P --> I2[load repaired candidate in isolation] 
case_id="redis-aof-$(date -u +%Y%m%dT%H%M%SZ)"
work_root="/var/tmp/$case_id"
evidence_dir="$work_root/evidence"
recovery_dir="$work_root/recovery"
repair_dir="$work_root/repair"
sudo install -d -m 0700 "$evidence_dir" "$recovery_dir" "$repair_dir"
sudo cp -a --reflink=auto "<source-dir>/." "$evidence_dir/"
sudo cp -a --reflink=auto "$evidence_dir/." "$recovery_dir/"
sudo find "$evidence_dir" -type f -print0 | sort -z | xargs -0 -r sha256sum | sudo tee "$work_root/evidence.sha256" >/dev/null
sudo find "$recovery_dir" -type f -print0 | sort -z | xargs -0 -r sha256sum | sudo tee "$work_root/recovery-before.sha256" >/dev/null

source_dir, evidence_dir, and recovery_dir are immutable. Only repair_dir, copied after approval, may be changed.

docs.title: Identify Redis 6.2 and Redis 7/8 structures
docs.text:
flowchart TB A[Redis 6.2] --> A1[appendonly.aof<br/>single file] B[Redis 7 / 8] --> B1[appendonlydir] B1 --> B2[appendonly.aof.manifest] B1 --> B3[base AOF or RDB] B1 --> B4[incremental AOF files] B2 --> B3 B2 --> B4

Use the actual configuration and manifest. For Redis 7/8, confirm every referenced base and incremental file exists, ordering is plausible, and files from different rewrite cycles are not mixed. Do not hand-splice missing or internally damaged files.

docs.title: Run a read-only check with matching tools
docs.text:
redis-check-aof "$recovery_dir/<appendfilename>" 2>&1 | tee "$work_root/redis-check-aof-readonly.log"
redis-check-aof "$recovery_dir/<appenddirname>/<appendfilename>.manifest" 2>&1 | tee "$work_root/redis-check-aof-readonly.log"

Load a valid recovery copy without repair. Consider repair only when the final command or final incremental tail is truncated. Stop for internal corruption, missing manifest/files, unrecognized format, or candidates with unique writes.

docs.title: Estimate loss before repair
docs.text: The valid offset and file size estimate byte truncation, not the business time window. Correlate Redis logs, replication offset/backlog, application write audit, backup timestamps, and business records. File mtime is supporting evidence only. Record bytes, files, possible commands, whether the time range is known, approver, and maximum RPO. Do not run --fix when loss cannot be bounded.

docs.title: Repair only a fresh repair copy
docs.text:
sudo rm -rf "$repair_dir"
sudo install -d -m 0700 "$repair_dir"
sudo cp -a --reflink=auto "$recovery_dir/." "$repair_dir/"
sudo find "$repair_dir" -type f -print0 | sort -z | xargs -0 -r sha256sum | sudo tee "$work_root/repair-before.sha256" >/dev/null
redis-check-aof --fix "$repair_dir/<appendfilename>" 2>&1 | tee "$work_root/redis-check-aof-fix.log"
redis-check-aof --fix "$repair_dir/<appenddirname>/<appendfilename>.manifest" 2>&1 | tee "$work_root/redis-check-aof-fix.log"
sudo find "$repair_dir" -type f -print0 | sort -z | xargs -0 -r sha256sum | sudo tee "$work_root/repair-after.sha256" >/dev/null
diff -u "$work_root/repair-before.sha256" "$work_root/repair-after.sha256" | tee "$work_root/repair-files.diff" || true

Do not repeatedly experiment on one repair copy. Discard it and recopy from recovery after any failed attempt.

docs.title: Start inside a real isolation boundary
docs.text:
flowchart LR P[Original production topology] -. no connection .-> I[Isolated recovery instance] I --> D[Separate data directory] I --> PORT[Separate port 16379] I --> NET[No production egress] I --> REP[Replication disabled] I --> CL[Cluster disabled] V[Validator] -->|localhost or controlled tunnel| I I --> LOG[Separate log and PID]

Copy the original configuration to preserve module/ACL/encoding behavior, then override bind, port, directory, log, PID, replication, and cluster-enabled no. Loopback binding alone is not isolation; use a disconnected host, container, or network namespace.

Standalone candidates can load independently. A primary requires checking for a more complete replica. A replica must not become authority without offset/data comparison. Full Cluster recovery is out of scope because slot/node configuration and cross-shard consistency remain unresolved. Reproduce required modules; never delete unknown commands merely to start Redis.

docs.title: Expected results
docs.text: Stage Expected result Candidate selection Replica, backup, and AOF completeness/RPO compared Preservation Source unchanged and evidence checksums stored Read-only inspection Recovery copy unchanged; tool and entry point known Approval Truncation and business loss bounded and approved Limited repair Only repair copy changed as expected Isolated load Separate directory/port with no topology or production connection Data validation Keys, TTLs, types, scale, and business samples acceptable Cutover readiness Separate change, backup, monitoring, and rollback plan exists

docs.title: Stop conditions
docs.text: Stop when the four directories are confused; authority cannot be determined; tool versions mismatch; a manifest/base/incremental file is missing; corruption is not at the final tail; loss exceeds RPO or cannot be assessed; dependencies cannot be reproduced; the candidate reaches the old topology; critical data cannot be parsed; or unique writes require merging candidates.

docs.title: Success validation
docs.text:
redis-cli -p 16379 PING
redis-cli -p 16379 ROLE
redis-cli -p 16379 INFO persistence
redis-cli -p 16379 INFO replication
redis-cli -p 16379 INFO keyspace
redis-cli -p 16379 DBSIZE
redis-cli -p 16379 CONFIG GET dir
redis-cli -p 16379 CONFIG GET appendonly
redis-cli -p 16379 CONFIG GET appendfilename
redis-cli -p 16379 CONFIG GET appenddirname
redis-cli -p 16379 CONFIG GET cluster-enabled

Confirm no production upstream/downstream, correct directory and AOF entry point, no continuing format/module/permission error, readable keys/TTLs/types/business samples, plausible scale against an authoritative source, approved RPO, application parsing in isolation, and a separate production cutover with one writer.

docs.title: Failure handling
docs.text: For an unrecognized format, recheck release, manifest, and entry point. For excessive loss, discard repair and prefer replica/RDB/backup. For startup failure, retain logs and recopy from recovery. Reproduce missing modules. Do not onboard after failed samples. Escalate multiple unique writers into data merge and business adjudication.

docs.title: Rollback and escalation
docs.text: Rollback here means discarding recovery/repair candidates and returning to immutable evidence, not editing production. Production cutover keeps the old node and evidence frozen, builds a new instance from the accepted candidate, ensures one writable authority, restores clients gradually with monitoring, and returns to a healthy replica or verified backup on failure. Preserve logs, manifests, file listings, checksums, approvals, and estimated loss.

docs.title: Related documents
docs.text:
• Redis persistence and data protection standard
  • Redis production operations standard
  • Redis migration and failover runbook

docs.title: Official references
docs.text:
• Redis persistence
  • Redis Open Source version management
  • redis-check-aof source

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines Redis capacity, eviction, and availability boundaries. The objective is predictable behavior during peak load and background work.

docs.title: Memory model
docs.text:
maxmemory must not equal total host memory. Capacity planning must include the dataset, peak growth, process overhead, allocator fragmentation, replication buffers, client output buffers, persistence copy-on-write, the operating system, and co-located agents.

Some memory used for replication or persistence is excluded from eviction accounting, so host-level headroom is still required.

docs.title: Eviction policy
docs.text:
• Rebuildable caches may use an allkeys-* policy suited to their access pattern.
  • State that must not disappear silently normally uses noeviction.
  • Do not mix evictable cache data and non-evictable state in one instance.
  • Applications must handle key eviction or rejected writes.
  • Do not rely on the operating-system OOM mechanism for Redis capacity control.

At minimum, monitor used_memory, used_memory_rss, used_memory_peak, mem_fragmentation_ratio, mem_not_counted_for_evict, and evicted_keys.

docs.title: Replication boundary
docs.text:
Redis replication is asynchronous. A connected replica is not necessarily caught up, and an automated role change does not guarantee preservation of every acknowledged write. The business RPO must determine whether a lagging replica is acceptable.

Where the RPO justifies it, test min-replicas-to-write, min-replicas-max-lag, and client-side WAIT. These controls can reduce or bound exposure, but they do not turn Redis into a strongly consistent CP system or eliminate failover loss.

docs.title: Sentinel
docs.text: Sentinel provides primary discovery and role change for non-Cluster topologies. Deploy at least three Sentinel instances on separate hosts or failure domains. Validate quorum, majority authorization, and client discovery, and exercise the failover path periodically.

docs.title: Redis Cluster
docs.text: Use Cluster only when horizontal sharding or a Cluster-native topology is required. Deploy at least three primaries; production commonly starts with one replica per primary. Cover all 16384 slots, distribute primaries and replicas across failure domains, and validate client redirection and topology refresh.

docs.title: Acceptance criteria
docs.text:
• The capacity model covers peak load and background work.
  • Eviction matches the data role.
  • The application handles eviction or rejected writes.
  • Replication lag has an explicit threshold.
  • Sentinel or Cluster matches the failure-domain design.
  • Client behavior during role change has been tested.
  • Memory, replication, and client metrics have alerts.

docs.title: Official sources
docs.text:
• Redis Key Eviction
  • Redis Replication
  • Redis Sentinel
  • Redis Cluster

docs.title: Purpose
docs.text:
Use this runbook for host moves, version upgrades, planned primary-replica changes, Sentinel-managed changes, and maintenance of one Redis Cluster shard.

Core principle:

Only one side may be authoritative for production writes during the migration.

If the target has not accepted production changes, the original topology can remain authoritative. Once the target has accepted production changes, failback becomes a new migration.

docs.title: Preconditions
docs.text:
Confirm before starting:

  • source and target versions, replication mode, and persistence format are compatible;
  • target capacity, network, ACL, persistence, and monitoring are validated;
  • an external data copy meets the current RPO;
  • the application has a maintenance window or traffic-control mechanism;
  • clients support the target topology;
  • business checks, change owner, and fallback owner are defined.

Stop and reassess if the current primary is unknown, the target already contains independent business changes, replication is persistently unstable, clients do not support the target topology, or an unknown dual-primary state exists.

docs.title: Baseline capture
docs.text:
Record:

Redis version
current role
replication ID and offset
replica connection state
persistence state
memory and key count
application error rate and latency
critical business invariants

DBSIZE is supporting information only; it does not prove business consistency.

docs.title: Planned migration flow
docs.text:
1. Create the target and configure network, ACL, persistence, monitoring, and capacity boundaries.
  2. Attach the target as a replica and wait for initial synchronization to complete.
  3. Observe replication progress, latency, memory, and persistence under real load.
  4. Enter the maintenance window and pause sources of new business changes.
  5. Record the source final replication position and confirm that the target reaches it.
  6. Complete the topology-specific role change: use coordinated flow for normal replication, validate quorum and majority authorization for Sentinel, and use a planned replica change for Cluster.
  7. Confirm that the former primary is no longer in the production write path.
  8. Update clients, service discovery, proxies, or connection configuration.
  9. Validate reads first, then restore production writes gradually.
  10. Enter the observation window and continue checking application and Redis metrics.

Do not default to options that skip synchronization or cluster agreement. For Cluster, CLUSTER FAILOVER FORCE skips coordination with the primary, while CLUSTER FAILOVER TAKEOVER also bypasses cluster consensus. Use non-coordinated paths only during an incident after impact assessment and owner approval.

docs.title: Normal replication
docs.text:
• The target is a replica of the current primary.
  • Replication progress meets the agreed threshold.
  • The former primary becomes a replica or leaves the service path.
  • Other replicas reconnect to the new primary.

docs.title: Sentinel
docs.text:
• Multiple Sentinel instances agree on the current primary.
  • Quorum and majority authorization are valid.
  • The candidate replica is healthy.
  • Clients can obtain the new primary address through Sentinel.

docs.title: Redis Cluster
docs.text:
• cluster_state is healthy.
  • All slots are covered.
  • No slot movement remains incomplete.
  • The target is a replica of the relevant primary shard.
  • Client topology caches can refresh.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
Prove that:

  • the new primary is the only production write endpoint;
  • the former primary is a replica or outside the production path;
  • clients connect to the correct target;
  • replicas reconnect and continue advancing;
  • RDB/AOF state is healthy;
  • keys, TTLs, and critical data structures are correct;
  • application reads, writes, error rate, and latency are healthy;
  • Sentinel or Cluster converges on the new topology.

docs.title: Target has not accepted production changes
docs.text: Return clients to the source, keep the source authoritative, and rebuild the target.

docs.title: Target has accepted production changes
docs.text:
Do not only reverse the client address. Pause new changes, preserve both sides, identify target-only data, establish a new single-writer boundary through reverse synchronization or offline reconciliation, and then perform a second planned migration.

If target-only data cannot be synchronized safely, continue operating on the target and escalate the decision rather than silently discarding changes.

docs.title: Closure criteria
docs.text:
• The new primary is unique.
  • The former primary role is explicit.
  • Client connections are correct.
  • Redis and application validation passed.
  • Replication and persistence are stable.
  • No new critical alert is active.
  • The observation window completed.
  • Change records and follow-up actions are updated.

docs.title: Official sources
docs.text:
• Redis Replication
  • FAILOVER
  • Redis Sentinel
  • CLUSTER FAILOVER

docs.title: Redis Production Operations Standard
docs.text: Redis replication is asynchronous by default. Replicas, AOF, Sentinel, Cluster, and WAIT do not automatically guarantee zero data loss. Production design must begin with the amount of data loss and recovery time the business can tolerate, then select the configuration.

docs.title: Scope
docs.text:
This standard applies to self-managed Redis 6.2 and later, including standalone instances, primary-replica deployments with Sentinel, and Redis Cluster.

It does not replace managed-service constraints and does not assume Redis is the source of truth for every workload. Cache, session, queue, ranking, and critical business state require different decisions.

docs.title: Prerequisites
docs.text:
Before production use, record:

  • the data role: cache, reconstructable state, or authoritative data;
  • the source of truth and reconstruction method;
  • RPO, RTO, and acceptable degradation;
  • peak data volume, write rate, and growth estimate;
  • owner, on-call responsibility, and escalation contact;
  • Redis version, deployment mode, and persistence directory.

Unknown answers are not acceptable as “deploy first and observe later.”

docs.title: Memory and eviction
docs.text:
• Set maxmemory and reserve space for replication buffers, client buffers, fork, and the operating system.
  • Select maxmemory-policy from the data role; authoritative data must not depend on uncontrolled eviction.
  • Monitor used_memory, used_memory_rss, fragmentation, evicted keys, and blocked clients.
  • A container memory limit must not equal maxmemory, or the process may be OOM-killed before Redis reaches its own threshold.

docs.title: Persistence and data protection
docs.text:
• A cache may disable persistence only when reconstruction is proven.
  • When recovery is required, define RDB, AOF, or combined mode and test an actual restore.
  • Redis 7+ multipart AOF must be managed as one file set containing the manifest, base, and incremental files.
  • Backups must be copied to an independent failure domain and restored on a schedule.

docs.title: Availability and failover
docs.text:
• Sentinel and Cluster provide automated role election or shard availability; they do not provide synchronous replication.
  • Before failover, check replication state, lag, backlog, and persistence health.
  • After failover, confirm client routing, the active write target, and isolation of the former primary.
  • Failback is a new migration. Reconfirm data direction, write fencing, and validation before changing roles again.

docs.title: Security
docs.text:
• Bind only to controlled interfaces and restrict source networks with security groups or firewalls.
  • Use ACLs to separate application, operator, and monitoring permissions.
  • Do not expose credentials in documentation, shell history, or pipeline logs.
  • Use TLS across hosts or untrusted networks.
  • Production changes require auditable identity and least privilege.

docs.title: Monitoring and change control
docs.text:
At minimum, monitor availability, command latency, memory, connections, replication, persistence, role changes, rejected connections, and slow commands.

Upgrades, configuration changes, scaling, failover, and data migration require a baseline, impact scope, validation signals, stop conditions, and a rollback or recovery plan.

docs.title: Validation
docs.text:
After deployment or a major change, run at least:

redis-cli INFO server
redis-cli INFO memory
redis-cli INFO persistence
redis-cli INFO replication
redis-cli ACL WHOAMI

The evidence must show that:

  • the application reaches the intended instance and role;
  • memory headroom covers fork and peak load;
  • persistence has no errors and recent save or AOF rewrite state is healthy;
  • replica lag and link state remain within the agreed boundary;
  • a backup can be restored in an isolated environment;
  • alerts fire and close through the normal path.

docs.title: Exceptions
docs.text:
Every exception requires a business reason, risk statement, compensating control, owner, and expiry date.

For example, a cache-only instance may omit persistence only when origin recovery, rate limiting, and cache rebuild are proven. A temporary test environment may reduce availability requirements, but it must not use production credentials or production data.

docs.title: Rollback
docs.text:
Configuration changes must retain the previous configuration and startup parameters. Version upgrades must retain the previous artifact and compatibility evidence.

When data direction changes, do not treat “point clients back to the old primary” as a simple rollback. Fence writes, identify the authoritative data set, measure divergence, and then choose failback, compensation, or restoration.

For focused recovery work, use the Redis AOF Inspection and Recovery Runbook.

docs.title: Redis Persistence and Data Protection Standard
Purpose
docs.text: This standard defines the selection and validation boundaries for RDB, AOF, and external data copies. Persistence reduces loss after process or host failure; it does not replace business consistency design.

docs.title: Persistence selection
docs.text:
Mode Behavior Appropriate boundary No persistence Empty after restart Fully rebuildable cache RDB Periodic point-in-time snapshot Data that can lose changes between snapshots AOF everysec Records writes and normally syncs once per second Short-lived state requiring a lower RPO RDB + AOF Redis loads the more complete AOF at restart; RDB remains useful as a point-in-time artifact Lower restart-loss exposure plus separately retained RDB snapshots

When RDB and AOF are both enabled, Redis reconstructs the dataset from AOF at restart. It is incorrect to describe this as starting from RDB and then applying AOF. An RDB file on the same host and failure domain is not an independent backup until it is retained elsewhere.

docs.title: AOF sync policy
docs.text:
• appendfsync always: highest sync frequency and highest latency or throughput cost.
  • appendfsync everysec: common compromise; a failure can lose roughly one second of changes.
  • appendfsync no: delegates flushing to the operating system and is unsuitable for state with an explicit low RPO.

No policy guarantees zero business data loss. Test latency and throughput with the real workload.

docs.title: Redis 7+ multipart AOF
docs.text:
Redis 7.0 and later use a set containing:

  • at most one base AOF or RDB file;
  • one or more incremental AOF files;
  • a manifest that records the files and load order.

The files reside in appenddirname and must be managed as one complete set. Retaining a single AOF file can produce an incomplete dataset.

docs.title: Data protection requirements
docs.text:
A running replica is not an independent data copy because incorrect deletes, writes, and expiry changes propagate through replication.

Minimum requirements:

  • store retained data outside the Redis host and failure domain;
  • record Redis version, configuration, creation time, and file digest;
  • protect sensitive data with appropriate storage and access controls;
  • retain points that meet the business RPO;
  • validate periodically in an isolated environment;
  • check important keys, TTLs, data structures, and business invariants;
  • alert on stale or failed data-copy jobs.

docs.title: Operational monitoring
docs.text:
Monitor at least:

rdb_last_bgsave_status
rdb_last_bgsave_time_sec
aof_enabled
aof_last_write_status
aof_last_bgrewrite_status
aof_rewrite_in_progress
aof_rewrite_scheduled

When capturing Redis 7+ AOF files, avoid an AOF rewrite window. The official process disables automatic rewrite temporarily, confirms that no rewrite is in progress, captures the complete appenddirname, and then restores the original setting.

docs.title: Acceptance criteria
docs.text:
• The persistence mode matches the data role and RPO.
  • The latest RDB save or AOF rewrite is healthy.
  • The Redis 7+ file set contains every file referenced by the manifest.
  • External data copies include version, time, and digest metadata.
  • The latest isolated validation passed.
  • Stale or failed data-copy activity has alerts.

docs.title: Official source
docs.text: • Redis Persistence
